Toxic units (TUs), including estimation of PM_{2.5}-induced OS and CT potency values, can be estimated as follows: TUOS_{j,s} = \frac{1}{(\mathrm{EC}_{1.5})_{j,s}} and TUCT_{j,s} = \frac{1}{(\mathrm{IC}_{20})_{j,s}}, where j and s denote sample (j) and industry source (s), respectively, EC_{1.5} is the endpoint of the PM_{2.5}-induced OS potency, indicating the concentration causing a 1.5-fold increase in intracellular ROS formation relative to that in the control group, IC_{20} is the endpoint of the PM_{2.5}-induced CT potency, indicating the concentration causing 20% inhibition of the cell viability relative to that in the control group. This passage unambiguously defines TU as an inverse mapping from an endpoint concentration (EC\_1.5 or IC\_20) to a “toxic unit.” Once TU is defined as 1/EC or 1/IC, any aggregation across processes or samples that aims to represent a mixture or a sector should be done in TU-space (i.e., by combining 1/EC values with appropriate weights). Otherwise, the aggregation no longer corresponds to the stated definition of TU and will systematically deviate whenever EC/IC varies across components. Because the paper’s central premise is “unequal toxicities,” substantial variation is expected, making this discrepancy practically important rather than a negligible algebraic detail. The rest of the analysis (toxic equivalents, TPAE, and control rankings) depends on these aggregated toxicity metrics, so any bias here propagates directly into headline results. The average provincial TUs of industrial PM_{2.5} emissions can be estimated as follows: TUOS_{i,s} = \frac{1}{\sum (\mathrm{EC}_{1.5})_{j,s} \times \mathrm{WF}_{j,s}} and TUCT_{i,s} = \frac{1}{\sum (\mathrm{IC}_{20})_{j,s} \times \mathrm{WF}_{j,s}}, where WF is the weighing factor, estimated based on the mass fraction of PM_{2.5} emissions of each individual manufacturing process (or each individual fuel-fired unit) to the total PM_{2.5} emission amount of all manufacturing processes (or all fuel-fired units). This formula computes TU as the reciprocal of a weighted *arithmetic mean* of EC\_1.5/IC\_20, which is generally not equal to the weighted mean of the reciprocals (the TU values) implied by the earlier TU definition. The difference can be large under heterogeneity (e.g., mixing a highly potent component with a weakly potent one), exactly the regime the paper emphasizes. Because the weighting factor WF is emission-mass-based, the natural toxicity-weighted mixture metric—consistent with a linear “per mass” potency framing—would be Σ WF·(1/EC) rather than 1/Σ WF·EC. The paper does not justify why the nonlinear reciprocal-of-mean is the correct mixture rule for toxicity units, nor does it provide a sensitivity analysis showing the ranking is invariant to this choice. As a result, the quantitative toxic equivalents and exposure-risk reductions can be artifacts of this aggregation choice rather than robust reflections of underlying toxicity measurements. TPAE<sub>OS</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> were estimated as TPAE<sub>OS</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × OS<sub>i,s</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × CT<sub>i,s</sub>, respectively, where OS<sub>i,s</sub> and CT<sub>i,s</sub> are the toxic equivalent values of the PM<sub>2.5</sub>-induced OS and CT potency, respectively, originating from each industry sector (s) in each geographic unit (i). This shows that the toxic equivalent values (OS\_{i,s}, CT\_{i,s}) are direct multiplicative factors in the paper’s population-relevant risk index (TPAE). Therefore, any bias in TU aggregation that feeds into OS/CT equivalents will proportionally bias TPAE and any reported “times higher” reductions. Because the core policy recommendation is based on relative reductions in population-weighted TPAE under alternative sector priorities, even moderate systematic distortion of OS/CT equivalents can flip or exaggerate the comparison. The formulation also implies a linear scaling of “risk” with these equivalence factors, so the exact construction of OS/CT equivalents is not a cosmetic methodological choice but a structural determinant of results. The paper provides no validation that its particular TU aggregation rule preserves ordering under realistic intra-sector variability, which undermines confidence in the downstream comparisons. The toxic equivalent (TEQ) is operationally defined by the sum of the products of the concentration of each congener multiplied by its TEF value. This statement describes the standard logic of toxicity-equivalency aggregation: overall mixture toxicity is represented as a *linear* sum of component contributions in the form Σ(concentration × equivalency factor), rather than by applying a nonlinear reciprocal transformation after averaging. While the paper’s pollutants and endpoints differ (industrial PM vs. dioxin congeners), the key methodological parallel is the mixture-combination principle used to build a single index from heterogeneous components. If the paper intends TUs/TEs to play an analogous “equivalency factor” role, a linear mass-weighted combination is the default and widely used approach in regulatory and risk-assessment settings. The paper’s reciprocal-of-weighted-mean construction deviates from this norm without justification, which is especially concerning because it can systematically downweight extreme toxicities. This mismatch strengthens the concern that the paper’s aggregation step is not only internally inconsistent but also out of line with established equivalency frameworks. [\[3\]](#ref3)

This paper makes strong policy claims about “health-oriented” control and “exposure risks” using toxic potency-adjusted exposure (TPAE) that is built from in vitro A549 oxidative-stress/cytotoxicity endpoints measured on extracted primary stack PM, without demonstrating that TPAE is a valid surrogate for real-world human health outcomes or that its assumed linear scaling supports sector-priority decisions. Compared with the current mass-based control strategy (prioritizing implementation of ultralow emission standards in the power sector), the proposed health-oriented control strategy (priority control of the ISI sector) could generate 5.4 times higher reduction in population-weighted toxic potency-adjusted PM<sub>2.5</sub> exposure among polluting industries in China. This sentence elevates “toxic potency-adjusted PM\_2.5 exposure” to the central outcome for comparing policies and motivates a sector-priority shift. However, “toxic potency-adjusted exposure” is not a standard epidemiologic effect metric (e.g., attributable mortality or morbidity), and the paper does not show how a given percentage reduction in TPAE maps to an expected reduction in health outcomes. Without such calibration, the “5.4 times higher reduction” is only meaningful within the authors’ constructed index, not necessarily for human health. The claim is especially consequential because it frames existing regulations as mis-prioritized and proposes an “urgently required” alternative; that level of prescription typically requires outcomes that are validated against human data or at least mechanistically linked through well-supported dose–response relationships. As written, the paper risks over-interpreting an index built from in vitro endpoints as if it were a health-impact function. Human epithelial A549 cell lines were adopted to examine PM<sub>2.5</sub>-related biological effects, including PM<sub>2.5</sub>-induced oxidative stress (OS) potency and cytotoxicity (CT) potency. This establishes that the toxicity signal driving the policy analysis is derived from a single in vitro cell line assay platform, rather than from in vivo or epidemiologic evidence. A549 assays are useful for mechanistic screening, but translating their dose–response endpoints to population-level exposure risk is nontrivial because real exposures involve complex deposition, clearance, metabolism, and systemic responses not represented in monoculture assays. The paper also uses solvent extraction (“sonicated in methanol and ultra-purified water”) before testing, which can change the bioavailable fraction of components relative to inhalation exposure and complicate interpretability across sources. Critically, the paper does not demonstrate that the relative ranking of source toxicities in A549 assays persists under more realistic exposure models or across multiple relevant endpoints (e.g., inflammation, cardiovascular pathways). Because TPAE is built from these endpoints, the absence of validation threatens the robustness of the downstream sector-priority recommendation. TPAE<sub>OS</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> were estimated as TPAE<sub>OS</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × OS<sub>i,s</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × CT<sub>i,s</sub>, respectively, where OS<sub>i,s</sub> and CT<sub>i,s</sub> are the toxic equivalent values of the PM<sub>2.5</sub>-induced OS and CT potency, respectively, originating from each industry sector (s) in each geographic unit (i). This construction implicitly assumes that “health-relevant risk” scales linearly with modeled concentration (C\_{i,s}) and a single scalar toxic-equivalence multiplier (OS\_{i,s} or CT\_{i,s}) derived from in vitro endpoints. In practice, health impact assessment typically requires an exposure–response function (often nonlinear and outcome-specific) and baseline risk information; a simple product index can be informative for ranking hypotheses but is not automatically a valid risk surrogate. The paper does not provide any empirical evidence that a unit change in OS-equivalent or CT-equivalent translates into proportional changes in morbidity/mortality, nor does it evaluate whether the chosen endpoints represent the dominant pathways of PM\_2.5 health effects. Because the policy comparisons and cost-effectiveness conclusions are made “per unit of toxic potency-adjusted mass,” any misspecification of this index can materially alter which sector appears most beneficial to control. The linear product form therefore requires external validation that is not shown in the manuscript. We point out here the potential dangers of exclusive use of an in vitro screening approach in view of experience with other particles, suggesting that particles can cause a rather narrow range of effects on cells in culture which does not reflect the range of different pathogenic effects they cause in vivo. This review directly cautions against using in vitro oxidative-stress-type screens as a stand-alone basis for hazard ranking across particle types, because similar cellular oxidative stress responses can correspond to very different in vivo pathologies. That warning applies to this paper’s strategy of using A549-based OS/CT potency endpoints as the core toxicity input for nationwide policy ranking. Even if the assays are executed carefully, the quote highlights that the mapping from in vitro response to in vivo disease outcomes can be weak or ambiguous without supporting toxicokinetic and in vivo evidence. The paper’s central claim is not merely mechanistic (that industrial PM differs in potency), but prescriptive (that control priorities should change to protect health), which requires stronger validation than a screening endpoint alone. The external critique therefore strengthens the concern that the paper’s “health-oriented” index may not reliably predict actual health benefits from sector-specific controls. [\[1\]](#ref1) Health impact assessments typically use a single log-linear CR function per health outcome to estimate counts of avoided human health effects resulting from air quality improvements. This statement summarizes a widely used practice in policy-relevant health impact assessment: converting exposure reductions into avoided health outcomes through concentration–response (CR) functions grounded in epidemiology. In contrast, the paper’s TPAE does not specify a CR function relating its toxicity-weighted exposure to concrete outcomes (e.g., mortality), and therefore cannot be directly interpreted as “health effects” avoided. Because the paper claims “cost-effective reductions in particulate exposure risks,” readers would reasonably expect an outcome-linked risk model or at least a demonstration that TPAE is predictive of outcome changes. The absence of such linkage means that the comparative effectiveness ratios (e.g., “5.4 times higher reduction”) may not correspond to proportional differences in avoided disease burden. This discrepancy undermines the robustness of the conclusions for real-world health-oriented regulation, even if TPAE is a useful exploratory index for hypothesis generation. [\[5\]](#ref5)

This paper’s sector-contribution estimates and “health-oriented” control prioritization are potentially biased because they explicitly omit source-specific secondary aerosol formation and fugitive emissions from key industrial processes and, in attribution scenarios, remove only primary PM<sub>2.5</sub> emissions; this can materially change both exposure burdens and the ranking of which sector should be prioritized. Source-specific secondary and fugitive aerosols emission from manufacturing iron/steel and cement processes were not considered, which could result in underestimation of the adverse impacts associated with exposure to specific source-emitted aerosols. This admission directly affects the paper’s central comparison of sector priorities because excluding secondary and fugitive components changes both the magnitude and spatial distribution of exposure attributable to each sector. Industrial sectors differ substantially in precursor emissions (e.g., SO\_2, NO\_x, VOCs, NH\_3 interactions) and in fugitive dust contributions; omitting these components can therefore alter relative burdens, not just absolute totals. The claim that ISI should be prioritized over power based on toxic potency-adjusted exposure reductions depends on accurate sector-attributable concentrations, which are compromised if key aerosol pathways are missing. Importantly, the limitation is not a marginal refinement: secondary formation often dominates ambient PM\_2.5 composition in many regions, and industrial precursors are major drivers of that formation. Without quantifying the direction and magnitude of this omission, the policy ranking is not reliably supported. Emission inventories excluding primary PM<sub>2.5</sub> emissions from power, cement, and ISI sectors, as obtained with the baseline scenarios, were used to simulate the NoELE, NoCI, and NoISI scenarios, respectively. This sentence indicates that sector attribution is performed by “zeroing out” only primary PM\_2.5 emissions, not the gaseous precursors that form secondary PM\_2.5 attributable to the same sectors. As a result, the computed “ambient PM\_2.5 concentration attributable to the power, cement, and ISI sectors” will systematically miss secondary contributions from those sectors and can misrepresent sector responsibility for population exposure. This is particularly problematic for comparing sectors because power and heavy industry have distinct precursor profiles and stack heights, affecting downwind secondary production differently. The paper’s toxicity-weighting then multiplies these potentially incomplete concentrations by toxicity equivalents, compounding errors in both concentration and toxicity dimensions. Therefore, the attribution approach is not aligned with the paper’s stated goal of assessing health-relevant impacts of industrial emissions in the atmosphere. As for the population-weighted TPAE, we limited our uncertainty analysis to the emission inventory owing to the near-linear response of the ambient PM_{2.5} concentration to the primary PM_{2.5} emission. This sentence reflects an analytical framing in which primary PM\_2.5 emissions are treated as the dominant driver of the modeled concentration responses relevant for TPAE. However, secondary aerosol formation is explicitly acknowledged elsewhere as omitted, so assuming a near-linear response to primary PM\_2.5 is not a safe basis for concluding that ignoring these pathways does not affect rankings. Even within primary PM\_2.5, the relationship between emissions and population-weighted concentration can be nonlinear due to meteorology, plume rise, and chemistry affecting particle dynamics, which would be handled by the chemical transport model rather than assumed. By tying both attribution and uncertainty choices to primary PM\_2.5 only, the paper structurally limits its ability to support conclusions about comprehensive industrial PM\_2.5 exposure burdens. This weakens the validity of using the resulting TPAE reductions as the decisive basis for sector-priority policy. Secondary fine particles - Fine particles that form in complicated reactions in the atmosphere of chemicals, such as sulfur dioxides and nitrogen oxides, that are emitted from power plants, industries, and automobiles. This definition highlights that secondary fine particles arise from atmospheric reactions of emitted precursor gases and that power plants and industries are explicitly among the sources of those precursors. Therefore, a sector-attribution framework that removes only primary PM\_2.5 emissions will, by construction, omit an important class of PM\_2.5 that is causally attributable to the same sectors through precursor emissions. Because the paper’s objective is “health-oriented air pollution control,” omitting secondary PM\_2.5 undermines the completeness of exposure assessment and may skew which sectors appear most beneficial to control. The quote reinforces that secondary PM\_2.5 is not an exotic niche component but a recognized and policy-relevant pathway for how industrial activities create fine-particle exposures. Consequently, the paper’s conclusions about optimal sector prioritization are not adequately supported without incorporating (or bounding) secondary PM\_2.5 contributions. [\[2\]](#ref2)

This paper’s main quantitative results depend on an updated ABaCAS emission inventory and WRF–CMAQ simulations plus additional toxicity-weighting and cost calculations, yet the provided code/materials do not include the CMAQ modeling system, model configurations, the ABaCAS inventory data, or the scripts needed to reproduce TPAE and cost-effectiveness estimates. Ambient PM2.5 concentrations were simulated with the WRF-CMAQ model. The simulation period covers the entirety of 2019. The WRF-CMAQ model with the updated ABaCAS emission inventory was adopted for baseline scenario simulation. This is the computational backbone of the paper: sector-specific concentration changes and subsequent TPAE calculations depend on the exact WRF–CMAQ setup, emission processing, chemistry options, boundary conditions, and evaluation choices. However, the manuscript refers readers to supplementary notes/tables for evaluation, without providing the runnable model configuration that would allow an independent team to replicate the 2019 baseline and scenario simulations. For coupled meteorology–chemistry modeling, small configuration differences (e.g., grid definition, meteorological nudging, chemical mechanism, aerosol module settings) can materially affect source attribution and exposure estimates. Because the paper’s main claims are framed as quantitative multipliers (e.g., several-fold differences), reproducibility of the modeled concentration deltas is essential for verifying conclusions. Without the exact configuration and inputs, the reported results cannot be independently reproduced or audited. ```text WRF Model Version 3.9 (April 17, 2017) http://wrf-model.org/users/users.php ``` The provided codebase corresponds to WRF (meteorology) version 3.9, but the paper’s analysis requires the *coupled* WRF–CMAQ modeling chain and an “updated ABaCAS emission inventory.” Supplying only the WRF source code does not enable reproduction of the air quality (CMAQ) component, the emissions processing, or the sectoral sensitivity simulations described in the Methods. Moreover, the code repository as provided does not contain scripts for constructing the ABaCAS inventory, implementing the ELE\_noULE/CI\_ULE/ISI\_ULE scenarios, calculating TPAE from modeled concentrations and toxicity equivalents, or producing the cost-per-toxicity-adjusted-mass results. This gap prevents readers from verifying whether modeling choices (e.g., treatment of primary vs. secondary PM, plume rise, temporal profiles) drive the reported sector rankings. Therefore, the code provision does not meet the minimum bar for reproducible computational claims in this manuscript. For 2019, the detailed location, fuel type, energy consumption, product yields, and end-of-pipe control information of 5508 power plants, 742 BFs, 658 BOFs, 124 EAFs, and 1939 cement plants were collected for emission calculation and Weather Research and Forecasting−Community Multiscale Air Quality (WRF-CMAQ) simulation purposes, as in our previous study43. This sentence indicates that the analysis relies on extensive plant-level input data that determine emissions and modeled concentrations, yet these data are not included in the provided materials in a form that enables replication. Even if confidentiality restricts raw plant identifiers, reproducibility normally requires at least a shareable processed inventory (or a clearly documented procedure and access path) sufficient to rerun the model and recover the paper’s aggregate outputs. The inability to access or reconstruct these plant-level inputs means that independent checks of sectoral emission totals, spatial allocation, and control-technology assumptions are not possible. Because the paper’s main conclusions compare sectors at national scale, the representativeness and correctness of these inputs are crucial to the validity of the conclusions. The current artifact set does not allow verification of these foundational inputs. Last, but not least, all input data, scripts, versions, parameters, and intermediate results should be made publicly and easily accessible. This reproducibility guideline captures what is missing for this manuscript’s computational pipeline: the specific inputs, scripts, and parameters needed to regenerate the core results. The paper’s claims are inherently computational (inventory + CTM + toxicity weighting + cost accounting), so independent assessment requires access to the full analysis workflow, not just a subset of upstream model code. Without the emission inventory files, scenario definitions, and post-processing scripts that compute TPAE and unit costs, readers cannot reproduce figures/tables or test sensitivity to key assumptions. This is not a minor documentation issue: it prevents the community from checking whether the main “health-oriented” policy ranking is robust to plausible methodological variations. Thus, the paper falls short of common standards for reproducible computational research, weakening its methodological credibility. [\[4\]](#ref4)

The study incorrectly applies the toxic potency measured from primary PM2.5 emissions to the total ambient PM2.5 concentration attributable to each industrial sector, which includes a significant fraction of secondary inorganic aerosols (sulfate and nitrate) that have much lower toxicity. "TPAE_OS = C_i,s × OS_i,s" This equation multiplies the total ambient concentration attributable to a sector ($C_{i,s}$) by the oxidative stress potency of the primary emissions ($OS_{i,s}$). This assumes that all PM2.5 originating from a sector (including secondary aerosols formed in the atmosphere) has the same toxicity as the primary particles emitted from the stack. "The differences in the simulated concentrations between the baseline scenario and the NoELE, NoCI, and NoISI scenarios were employed to estimate the ambient PM2.5 concentration attributable to the power, cement, and ISI sectors, respectively." The "zero-out" method in WRF-CMAQ captures the total contribution of a sector, which includes both primary PM and secondary PM formed from precursor emissions (SO2, NOx). For industrial sectors like power and iron/steel, secondary sulfates and nitrates often constitute a large portion of their total ambient PM2.5 contribution. "while many of the major PM components, including inorganic sulfate and nitrate, are commonly considered innocuous species" The authors themselves acknowledge in the Introduction that secondary inorganic components are less toxic. However, their methodology treats these components as having the same high toxicity as the metal-rich primary particles from the Iron and Steel Industry (ISI). "secondary inorganic components, crustal material and secondary biogenic organic aerosols control the mass concentration of particulate matter... [but] anthropogenic secondary organic aerosols and metals from non-exhaust traffic emissions and biomass burning are the main drivers of the oxidative potential" [1] Literature shows that while secondary inorganics drive mass, they do not drive oxidative potential. By applying the high toxicity of primary ISI particles (rich in metals) to the secondary sulfate/nitrate fraction, the study vastly overestimates the health risk of ISI emissions and the benefits of their control. "Extensive animal and human toxicology data show no significant effects for particles consisting only of sulfate and nitrate compounds at levels in excess of ambient concentrations." [4] This further supports that sulfate and nitrate, which are major components of the ambient PM2.5 attributed to industrial sources in the model, have negligible toxicity compared to the metal-rich primary particles. Treating them as equally toxic to primary ISI emissions is a critical methodological flaw. References: [1] Daellenbach, K. R., et al. (2020). Sources of particulate-matter air pollution and its oxidative potential in Europe. *Nature* 587, 414-419. (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-020-2902-8) [4] Reiss, R., et al. (2007). Evidence of health impacts of sulfate-and nitrate-containing particles in ambient air. *Inhalation Toxicology* 19(5), 419-449. (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17365047/)

The paper frames the "current strategy" as prioritizing power plants and compares the proposed strategy against a scenario where power plants have no ultralow emission (ULE) standards, despite the fact that ULE retrofitting in the power sector was largely completed before the study's base year. "Compared with the current mass-based control strategy (prioritizing implementation of ultralow emission standards in the power sector), the proposed health-oriented control strategy... could generate 5.4 times higher reduction" This statement implies that the "current strategy" is an active choice between Power ULE and ISI ULE. However, Power ULE is largely a completed action, not a competing alternative for future implementation. "By the end of 2017, all CFPP units had already satisfied the emission standards (GB 13223-2011)⁴, while 72.3% of all CFPPs in mainland China had met the ULE standards" The Supplementary Material confirms that the "ELE_noULE" scenario represents a counterfactual past (pre-2017 status), not the current policy landscape. Comparing the potential benefits of a future action (ISI ULE) against the benefits of a past action (Power ULE) is valid for retrospective analysis but misleading when framed as a guide for immediate future strategy selection. "The differences between the baseline and ELE_noULE scenarios constitute the influence of the ULE standard for PM control implemented at power plants since 2015 in China." The authors explicitly state that this scenario represents the influence of past implementation. The abstract should clearly reflect that the comparison is between a proposed future strategy and a historically implemented one, rather than implying a trade-off between two current options.

The use of methanol and water extraction for toxicity assays likely underestimates the toxicity of metal-rich particles from the Iron and Steel Industry, as many relevant metal species (e.g., iron oxides) may be insoluble in these solvents. "The PM2.5 filter samples were sonicated in methanol and ultra-purified water, and the obtained PM2.5 extracts were then dried under purified nitrogen." This extraction method recovers only soluble components. While methanol extracts organics, water extracts soluble ions. Many transition metals found in industrial emissions, particularly from high-temperature processes like steelmaking, exist as insoluble oxides or bound in aluminosilicate matrices. "Fe dominated the mass concentrations, with relative concentrations ranging from 62.6 − 98.3%... in the selected metals within in PM2.5 emitted from the ISI" Since iron is the dominant metal in ISI emissions and is often present as insoluble oxides (e.g., hematite, magnetite), a significant portion of the metal mass—and potentially its surface-mediated toxicity—may be excluded from the liquid extract used for cell exposure. This could lead to an underestimation of the absolute toxicity of ISI particles, although it does not necessarily invalidate the relative ranking if ISI is still found to be the most toxic. "Some results do suggest a degree of differential toxicity, namely more consistent associations with traffic-related PM emissions, fine and ultrafine particles." [2] Literature emphasizes that particle size and surface area (often associated with insoluble cores) are determinants of toxicity. By using only extracts, the study neglects the physical effects of particles and surface-bound reactivity of insoluble fractions. "Total iron solubility fractions measured in the dark after 12 h ranged between 2.9 and 74.1% of the initial iron content for the combustion-derived particles... in acidic media" [3] Iron solubility is highly dependent on pH and is generally low in neutral water. Since the study used water/methanol extraction (likely neutral pH), the dissolution of iron oxides would be minimal compared to acidic conditions (mimicking lysosomal fluid) or total digestion. This confirms that the extraction method likely missed a large fraction of the iron. "The surface of the particles... generates ROS... transition metals on the surface of particles... are responsible for the formation of hydroxyl radicals" [5] The surface reactivity of insoluble particles is a key mechanism of toxicity. By removing the solid particles and testing only the extract, the study ignores this crucial pathway, potentially underestimating the toxicity of the metal-rich ISI particles. References: [2] Kelly, F. J., & Fussell, J. C. (2012). Size, source and chemical composition as determinants of toxicity attributable to ambient particulate matter. *Atmospheric Environment* 60, 504-526. (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.atmosenv.2012.06.039) [3] Chen, H., et al. (2012). Solubility of iron from combustion source particles in acidic media linked to iron speciation. *Environmental Science & Technology* 46(20), 11171-11178. (https://pubs.acs.org/doi/abs/10.1021/es302558m) [5] Valavanidis, A., et al. (2008). Airborne particulate matter and human health: toxicological assessment and importance of size and composition of particles for oxidative damage and carcinogenic mechanisms. *Journal of Environmental Science and Health, Part C* 26(4), 339-362. (https://doi.org/10.1080/10590500802494538)

Lines 70-71: Why did the authors select the iron and steel industry (ISI), power industry, and cement industry as the targets of industrial sectors in this study? What contribution do these industrial sectors make to ambient air pollution or PM2.5 levels?

Lines 172-175: The results indicate that the PM2.5-induced oxidative stress (OS) potency could mainly be attributed to PM2.5-bound toxic metals. Calculating PM2.5-induced OS potency for selected metals and PAHs is based on reference models, which can be found in the study by Jin et al. (2019). That study provided EC1.5 and REP for valence metals. However, ICP-MS does not analyze metal valence. The authors should clarify the OS potency concentrations from metals and make assumptions if necessary. In addition, the calculations of OP are not transparent the authors only presented references.
Have any prior experimental studies investigated the toxic effects of industrial PM2.5? It’s necessary to compare the findings with earlier research.

Numerous studies in environmental epidemiology have investigated the impact of PM2.5 compositions on public health. It is essential for authors to analyze and contrast the variations between their study and previous epidemiology research in this field.

Line 62-63 “while many of the major PM components, including inorganic sulfate and nitrate, are commonly considered innocuous species17.”, the author should double check the sentence. The reference is out of date and there are many epidemiology studies found the toxic of inorganic sulfate. Modifying the acidity and alkalinity levels could potentially impact the toxicity of metals present in PM2.5.

Line 140-142 “Here, the targeted chemicals are focused on metals and PAHs, which are commonly regarded as key toxic components associated with the biological effects of PM2.5.”, please explain why black carbon or elemental carbon is not a targeted chemicals in this studies?
It is recommended to include the proportion of certain key constituents, such as inorganic sulfate and nitrate, in Supplementary Table 4.
What methodology did the author employ to compute the information presented in Supplementary Figure 7a?

While I agree with the authors that the toxicity of particulate matter varies with source, the sampling collection and handling methods used in this work are not appropriate for the toxicity tests they perform. The sampling methods documented in the supplementary materials fails to collect the condensible particulate matter, which is where much of the most toxic components result in the ambient air. What is required is a dilution sampling system to allow the reaction and condensation closer to what happens once the particles, vapors, and gases are released into the atmosphere. One appropriate method is described here: England GC, Watson JG, Chow JC, Zielinska B, Chang MC, Loos KR, Hidy GM. Dilution-based emissions sampling from stationary sources: Part 1--Compact sampler methodology and performance. J Air Waste Manag Assoc. 2007 Jan;57(1):65-78. doi: 10.1080/10473289.2007.10465291. PMID: 17269232.

A second problem with the methods is that no mention is made of post collection sample handing to protect the sample acidity from neutralization. A proper storage would place samples collected into containers with citric acid coated material to prevent neutralization of acids, such as sulfuric acid, until use in the toxicological tests, also under ammonia free conditions. This is especially important in fossil fuel combustion particulate matter, high in acidic sulfur, which is known to activate transition metals by making them bioavailable (e.g., see: Fang, T.; Guo, H.; Zeng, L.; Verma, V.; Nenes, A.; Weber, R.J. Highly Acidic Ambient Particles, Soluble Metals, and Oxidative Potential: A Link between Sulfate and Aerosol Toxicity. Environmental Science & Technology 2017, 51, 2611-2620, doi:10.1021/acs.est.6b06151).

The role of sulfates is dismissed as relatively non-toxic by the authors in their discussion, but this is not a full consideration of its role. In isolation, it is true that its toxicity is low, but in combination with metals (as always occurs in the real world) acidic sulfur has an important role, as indicated by the epidemiological evidence, which the authors fail to acknowledge. For example, see: Weichenthal S, Lavigne E, raub A, Umbrio D, You H, Pollitt K, Shin T, Kulka R, Stieb DM, Korsiaik J, Jessiman B, JBrook JR, Hatzopoulou M, Evans G, Burnett RT, Association of Sulfur, Transition Metals, and the Oxidative Potential of Outdoor PM2.5 with Acute Cardiovascular Events: A Case-Crossover Study of Canadian Adults. Environmental Health Perspectives. Environ Health Perspect 129(10). (2021). doi: 30 10.1289/EHP9449.

Overall, it is unfortunate that the stack sampling and sample handling Methodology is so flawed, as the type of toxicological tests the authors performed might have provided much needed new insights into the relative toxicities of differing particles.

The paper describes a confidence-based ensemble weighting formula (Equation 3) that is mathematically inconsistent with the actual implementation in the provided code, making the methodology non-reproducible from the paper description alone. "Each subnetwork of the ensemble network calculates the average confidence level of one predicted instance as: c = (1/N) * sum(|P_i - 0.5| + 0.5), where P_i represents the predicted value of pixel i from the subnetwork, which is binarized to be either 0 or 1" According to this description, if P_i is truly binarized to values of 0 or 1 as stated, then |P_i - 0.5| would always equal 0.5 for every pixel. This means the confidence c would always equal (1/N) * sum(0.5 + 0.5) = 1.0 for all predictions, regardless of the actual prediction values. This would make the confidence measure completely uninformative and unable to distinguish between high-confidence and low-confidence predictions, which contradicts the stated purpose of confidence-based ensemble weighting. The code implementation in model_cotrain.py (lines 17-25): ```python def confidenceFactor(arr): median = 0.5 confList = [] for i in range(0, arr.shape[0]): conf = np.sum(np.abs(arr[i] - median)*1.8 + 0.1) / arr.shape[-1] confList.append(conf) meanConf = np.mean(confList) return confList, meanConf ``` The actual implementation uses different coefficients (multiplying by 1.8 and adding 0.1) compared to the paper's formula (multiplying by 1 and adding 0.5). Furthermore, the code operates on raw continuous predictions (arr[i]) before binarization, not on binarized values as the paper explicitly states. This represents a fundamental discrepancy between the published methodology and the actual implementation, which affects the reproducibility of the work. A verification script confirms that with truly binarized inputs, the paper's formula produces a constant value of 1.0 regardless of prediction quality.

The paper characterizes the approach as "self-supervised dynamic learning" in the title and abstract, but the method requires a substantial amount of initially labeled data (20,000 samples) for pre-training, which is inconsistent with the standard definition of self-supervised learning. "In this paper, we present a self-supervised dynamic learning approach that achieves long-term, high-fidelity transmission of arbitrary optical fields through unstabilized MMFs." The paper's title and abstract explicitly claim a "self-supervised" approach. However, self-supervised learning in the machine learning community typically refers to methods that learn representations from unlabeled data using pretext tasks (e.g., predicting missing parts, contrastive learning) without requiring any labeled data for the primary task. As defined in the literature, "Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a type of unsupervised learning that helps in the performance of downstream computer vision tasks... the motivation behind SSL is to learn useful depictions of the data from unlabeled data by making use of the self-supervision concept" [[4]](#citation-list). The method described in this paper is more accurately characterized as semi-supervised learning with pseudo-labeling, which is a fundamentally different paradigm. "At the preparation stage of data transmission, a sequence of known patterns is transmitted, and the MMDN is statically trained on the initial instances." The paper acknowledges that an initial training phase with known (labeled) patterns is required. This contradicts the self-supervised characterization, as true self-supervised methods do not require such labeled initialization. From the code MMDN.py (line 20): `parser.add_argument("--sizeOfPretrain", type=int, default=20000, help="number of pre-training dataset")` The default configuration requires 20,000 labeled samples for pre-training. This is a substantial amount of labeled data that is far from self-supervised. The paper's claim in the abstract that "network parameters are dynamically updated over time in a self-supervised manner, by learning from currently predicted pseudo-labels" conflates pseudo-label self-training (a semi-supervised technique) with true self-supervised learning. According to standard terminology, as explained in ["Semi-supervised learning, i.e. jointly learning from labeled and unlabeled samples"](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1908.02983) [[1]](#citation-list), pseudo-labeling is a form of semi-supervised learning, not self-supervised learning. References: [1] Arazo, E., Ortego, D., Albert, P., O'Connor, N. E., & McGuinness, K. (2020). ["Pseudo-Labeling and Confirmation Bias in Deep Semi-Supervised Learning."](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.02983) IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), 2020. [4] Jaiswal, A., Babu, A. R., Zadeh, M. Z., Banerjee, D., & Makedon, F. (2021). ["A Survey on Contrastive Self-Supervised Learning."](https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.00362) Technologies, 9(1), 2.

The paper's experimental comparisons are limited to a "StaticNN" baseline (which is the same architecture without dynamic updates) and lack direct comparison with other state-of-the-art methods for handling perturbations in multimode fiber imaging, weakening the paper's claims of comprehensive improvement. "The static neural network (StaticNN) trained on the first 500-second data and tested on the following 500-second is taken as the baseline method." The primary comparison throughout the paper is against StaticNN, which is essentially the same architecture without the dynamic update mechanism. While this demonstrates the benefit of dynamic updates, it does not establish how the proposed method compares to other existing approaches that address similar challenges. "We compared MMDN with previous approaches in terms of time duration, reconstruction quality, and transmission distance, etc., and demonstrated a comprehensive improvement of our approach (see Supplementary Fig. 7)." The paper claims comprehensive improvement but references a supplementary figure for these comparisons. There is no direct experimental comparison in the main text with methods like the "configuration training" approach by Resisi et al. (2021) [[2]](#citation-list), who demonstrated that deep learning can generalize over strongly perturbed fibers by training on multiple fiber conformations. Their work achieved a factor of ~3.5 improvement in reconstruction fidelity using a U-Net architecture with configuration training, which represents a relevant baseline for handling fiber perturbations. "Another inspiring approach is to use the mixture-of-expert framework... This architecture has been successfully applied for image denoising and phase retrieval to improve reconstruction accuracy and robustness to noise." The paper cites the Dynamic Synthesis Network (DSN) approach by Tahir et al. (2022) [[3]](#citation-list) as reference [18] and [25], which uses a similar multi-network ensemble for adaptive descattering. However, no direct experimental comparison with DSN is provided. Given that DSN also addresses dynamic adaptation to scattering media variations through an ensemble of networks, a direct comparison would significantly strengthen the validation of the claimed improvements. References: [2] Resisi, S., Popoff, S. M., & Bromberg, Y. (2021). ["Image Transmission Through a Dynamically Perturbed Multimode Fiber by Deep Learning."](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/lpor.202000553) Laser & Photonics Reviews, 15(10), 2000553. [3] Tahir, W., Wang, H., & Tian, L. (2022). ["Adaptive 3D descattering with a dynamic synthesis network."](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41377-022-00730-x) Light: Science & Applications, 11, 42.

The paper acknowledges the challenge of error accumulation in pseudo-label-based learning but does not describe or implement explicit mechanisms to mitigate confirmation bias, which is a well-documented problem in pseudo-labeling approaches. "The dynamic learning strategy for MMF dynamics modeling intuitively raises a critical demand for accurate inference on current batch, so as to provide reliable training sets for the subsequent update. However, two main factors exist that could hinder its naïve implementation from practical usage. First of all, the inevitable inference error for previous batch induces a bias to the model update, which will accumulate and largely deteriorate the network effectiveness after a long period." The paper explicitly acknowledges the problem of error accumulation and bias in the pseudo-label learning approach. However, the proposed solution (multi-scale memory ensemble) addresses this primarily through model ensembling rather than through established techniques specifically designed to prevent confirmation bias in pseudo-labeling. "Models trained using self-generated pseudo-labels generally suffer from confirmation bias, where the model keeps amplifying its own errors." — from Arazo et al. (2020) [[1]](#citation-list) The semi-supervised learning literature has extensively documented confirmation bias in pseudo-labeling approaches. Arazo et al. demonstrated that without proper regularization techniques such as mixup augmentation, minimum labeled samples per mini-batch, and dropout, pseudo-labeling approaches suffer from severe confirmation bias. The MMDN method relies solely on confidence-based weighting and forgetting mechanisms for short-term networks, without incorporating these established techniques for bias mitigation. From model.py (lines 5-21), the network architecture: ```python def createCNN(outsize, input_shape): model_input = Input(shape=input_shape) x = Conv2D(8, kernel_size=(3, 3), activation='relu')(model_input) x = BatchNormalization(axis=-1)(x) x = Dropout(0.4)(x) x = Conv2D(8, kernel_size=(3, 3), strides=2, activation='relu')(x) x = BatchNormalization(axis=-1)(x) x = Dropout(0.4)(x) x = Flatten()(x) x = Dense(outsize, activation='sigmoid')(x) cnn = Model(inputs=model_input, outputs=x) return cnn ``` While the code includes dropout (0.4), the paper does not discuss whether dropout is disabled during pseudo-label generation, which is critical for preventing noisy pseudo-labels. Furthermore, the paper does not employ data augmentation or mixup during training, which have been shown to be effective regularizers against confirmation bias. The absence of these techniques raises concerns about the long-term stability of the self-training loop, particularly for the claimed >1000 second operation. References: [1] Arazo, E., Ortego, D., Albert, P., O'Connor, N. E., & McGuinness, K. (2020). ["Pseudo-Labeling and Confirmation Bias in Deep Semi-Supervised Learning."](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.02983) IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN), 2020.

This paper’s confidence-based ensemble algorithm (the “Confidence-based ensemble algorithm” equations for computing confidence and weights) is specified in a way that becomes degenerate/undefined for the stated binarized predictions, calling into question the validity of the core MMDN ensembling mechanism that underpins the reported long-term accuracy. “Each subnetwork of the ensemble network calculates the average confidence level of one predicted instance as:” $$\begin{array}{c}c=\frac{1}{N}\sum _{i=1}^{N}\left(\left|{P}_{i} -0.5\right|+0.5\right) ,\#(3.)\end{array}$$ “where ${P}_{i}$ represents the predicted value of pixel $i$ from the subnetwork, which is binarized to be either 0 or 1, and $N$ is the spatial resolution of the input patterns.” If $P_i$ is binarized to 0 or 1, then the term $|P_i-0.5|+0.5$ equals 1 for every pixel, making $c=1$ for every instance regardless of model quality. A confidence metric that is constant cannot discriminate between experts, so it cannot serve as a gating signal for adaptive ensembling. This is not a cosmetic issue: the ensemble gate is the paper’s main mechanism for robustness to drift and jitter, so a degenerate confidence definition undermines the method’s stated rationale. Moreover, the text explicitly ties the method’s adaptability to this grading module, so the correctness of this definition is central to the paper’s validity. Without a non-degenerate confidence, the method reduces to either a fixed average or an undefined weighting. The paper does not provide an alternative, non-binarized formulation in the main manuscript. “The confidence-based weight of each subnetwork is defined as:” $$\begin{array}{c}{w}_{k}=\text{exp}\left(\frac{1-{c}_{k}}{\left(1-{c}_{1}\right)+\left(1-{c}_{2}\right)+\left(1-{c}_{3}\right)}\right). \#(4.)\end{array}$$ With $c_k$ forced to 1 by the paper’s own binarization statement, the denominator $(1-c_1)+(1-c_2)+(1-c_3)$ becomes 0, so the exponent is undefined (division by zero). That implies the weighting rule, as written, cannot be executed in the regime the paper claims to use (binary spatial patterns with binarized outputs). Even if one assumes non-binarized $P_i$, the paper does not clarify how $c_k$ is kept away from 1 to avoid numerical instability, which is essential for a stable online system. Because these weights directly determine the final prediction $P$, any undefined or unstable gating would directly impact the reported “~100% accuracy throughout the 1000s period.” The manuscript does not acknowledge this degeneracy or provide implementation details that resolve it. This creates a gap between the mathematical description and an operational algorithm, which is a major methodological flaw. ```python # return confidence level, 0.1~1.0 def confidenceFactor(arr): median = 0.5 confList = [] for i in range(0, arr.shape[0]): conf = np.sum(np.abs(arr[i] - median)*1.8 + 0.1) / arr.shape[-1] confList.append(conf) meanConf = np.mean(confList) return confList, meanConf # confidence based weight def weightFactor(conf1, conf2, conf3): w1 = np.exp(10 * (2 - conf2 - conf3) / (3 - conf1 - conf2 - conf3)) w2 = np.exp(10 * (2 - conf1 - conf3) / (3 - conf1 - conf2 - conf3)) w3 = np.exp(10 * (2 - conf1 - conf2) / (3 - conf1 - conf2 - conf3)) return w1, w2, w3 ``` The released code implements a different confidence computation (continuous-valued predictions relative to 0.5, scaled into roughly [0.1, 1.0]) and a different weight function (with a factor of 10 and a distinct functional form) than the paper’s equations. This discrepancy matters because the gate’s behavior (how sharply it selects experts, and how it behaves near “high confidence”) is highly sensitive to the chosen mapping and scaling. The code also avoids the “binarized $P_i$” degeneracy by operating on raw predicted probabilities rather than binarized values, contradicting the manuscript’s textual definition. Since the paper’s central claim is that adaptive ensembling stabilizes self-supervised updates over long times, the lack of agreement between the written method and the executable method undermines interpretability and reproducibility of the main result. At minimum, readers cannot determine which algorithm produced the reported figures without additional clarification. This is a validity issue because it is unclear which procedure is being evaluated.

This paper claims online self-supervised learning from unlabeled streaming data, but the provided implementation performs per-update training with labeled validation data and early stopping on validation loss, which is incompatible with the stated unlabeled setting and can inflate reported performance. “The MMDN model will be dynamically updated by online training on the unlabeled streaming data using predicted input images as labels, hence working in a self-supervised architecture.” This sentence asserts that streaming data are unlabeled and that the model updates rely on pseudo-labels, which implies that ground-truth labels are not available during online operation. In such a regime, the update procedure must be specified so it can run without any access to the true $y$ for the incoming batches. The manuscript does not describe using labeled validation data or any proxy criterion for early stopping or update scheduling. Because the entire contribution is framed around long-term deployment in an “unstabilized” channel, whether updates require labels is central to validity, not an implementation detail. If labels are required to tune or stop updates, the method is no longer self-supervised in the stated sense. This mismatch affects how credible and transferable the reported long-term performance is to real-world settings. ```python def get_callbacks(): early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', min_delta=5e-6, patience=10, verbose=2, mode='min') # min_delta=5e-6 csv_logger = CSVLogger('./log.csv', append=True) tensorboard = TensorBoard(log_dir='./logs', histogram_freq=0, write_graph=False) return [tensorboard, csv_logger, early_stopping] ``` Monitoring `val_loss` for early stopping requires access to ground-truth labels for the validation set, which contradicts the claimed “unlabeled streaming data” online update scenario. In an online self-supervised setting, validation labels for each incoming time window typically do not exist, so an algorithm must rely on unsupervised consistency criteria or other proxies rather than supervised validation loss. Using labeled validation for early stopping is not just a convenience: it changes the training dynamics by adaptively selecting the amount of optimization based on ground truth. That can substantially improve apparent performance and stability compared to a truly unlabeled update loop. Since the paper’s key claim is “self-supervised dynamic updating,” this code-level dependence on supervised validation indicates the reported procedure is not the one described. The manuscript does not explain how early stopping would be done without labels. ```python elif flag_dynamic_learning: print("="*10 + " Network update " + "="*10) print("-"*4 + "> model 3") clf3.fit(x_labeled[-sizeOfBatch:], y_labeled[-sizeOfBatch:], batch_size=batch_size, epochs=int(epochs), callbacks=get_callbacks(),validation_data=(x_fortest, y_fortest)) if flag_multi_model: print("-"*4 + "> model 2") clf2.fit(x_labeled[-sizeOfBatch:], y_labeled[-sizeOfBatch:], batch_size=batch_size, epochs=int(epochs), callbacks=get_callbacks(), validation_data=(x_fortest, y_fortest)) else: clf2 = clf3 if flag_multi_model: print("-"*4 + "> model 1") clf1.fit(x_labeled[-sizeOfBatch:], y_labeled[-sizeOfBatch:], batch_size=batch_size, epochs=int(epochs), callbacks=get_callbacks(), validation_data=(x_fortest, y_fortest)) else: clf1 = clf3 ``` The implementation repeatedly supplies `(x_fortest, y_fortest)` as validation data at every online update step, implying that the true labels for each time batch are known at update time. This contradicts the manuscript’s description of unlabeled streaming adaptation and changes the problem from self-supervised adaptation to a supervised (or semi-supervised) online learning setting. Because the callback includes early stopping on `val_loss`, these ground-truth labels can influence when training stops, which is a form of label leakage into the update schedule. Additionally, the same `y_fortest` is later used for reporting accuracy on that batch, so the evaluation is not cleanly separated from update-time model selection decisions. In optics/communication experiments, such leakage can materially overestimate real deployment performance, especially in long horizons where stopping criteria strongly affect drift. The paper does not report results for an update loop that removes this dependency. “On ImageNet, we first train an EfficientNet model on labeled images and use it as a teacher to generate pseudo labels for 300M unlabeled images. We then train a larger EfficientNet as a student model on the combination of labeled and pseudo labeled images.” [4](#ref4) This description exemplifies a standard self-training setup where unlabeled inputs receive pseudo labels generated by a teacher model and are then used for training, keeping the unlabeled portion label-free by definition. The key point for this paper is that self-training does not assume ground-truth labels are available for the unlabeled stream; evaluation and selection are typically handled separately. In contrast, the reviewed implementation injects per-batch ground-truth labels via `validation_data` and then uses those labels for early stopping, meaning the online procedure is not operating in the same information setting described in the manuscript. While using labeled validation is legitimate in a supervised online benchmark, it should be stated explicitly because it changes the claim from self-supervised to supervised adaptation. Without such clarity, the reader cannot assess whether the reported long-term performance would hold when labels are truly absent. This weakens the validity of the paper’s central claim about self-supervised deployment.

The provided code and documentation do not enable reproduction of the experimental results because critical datasets are not specified/provided in a reproducible way and the code contains an import that is incompatible with standard scikit-learn, preventing execution. ~~~~markdown ## Usage ``` $ python MMDN.py ``` ~~~~ The code documentation provides only a one-line invocation and does not specify the required Python/TensorFlow/scikit-learn versions, the expected dataset format, or how to obtain the experimental data. For computational imaging papers, reproducibility typically requires (at minimum) a concrete data download/preprocessing recipe and an environment specification so that a third party can run the pipeline. Here, there is no description of where the required `.npy` files come from, how they relate to the experiments in Fig. 2–5, or what license/availability constraints apply. This omission is material because the reported results are quantitative and depend on long temporal sequences of speckle/pattern pairs. Without clear data provenance and setup instructions, reviewers cannot verify the claimed “>99.9% accuracy… over 1000 seconds.” The minimal README therefore falls short of common reproducibility practice. ```python parser = argparse.ArgumentParser() parser.add_argument("--datafolder", type=str, default="../Data/Testdata_bin32") parser.add_argument("--saverootfolder", type=str, default="../Data/Testdata_bin32/result") parser.add_argument("--use_model", type=str, default="pretrain", help="pretrain model prefix") parser.add_argument("--modelfolder", type=str, default="../Data/Testdata_bin32/model") parser.add_argument("--configFile", type=str, default="./config.yaml") ``` ```python # load target pattern dataLabels = np.load(os.path.join(datafolder, 'pattern.npy')).astype(np.uint8) outsize = dataLabels.shape[1] # load input speckle dataValues0 = np.load(os.path.join(datafolder, 'speckles.npy')).astype(np.uint8) ``` The main script hard-codes a default data path (`../Data/Testdata_bin32`) and requires `pattern.npy` and `speckles.npy` to exist in that folder. These files are not part of the provided `preprint/code/` directory structure, and the paper does not provide a reproducible procedure to generate them from publicly available sources. Because the results depend on temporal drift and jitter behavior, substituting arbitrary data is not sufficient to verify the claims; the dataset must correspond to the described unstabilized 100 m and 1 km MMF measurements. In addition, there is no schema description (shape, dtype, normalization) beyond the `astype(np.uint8)` casting, which is insufficient for replicating preprocessing choices. This creates a direct barrier to reproducing any figure or table quantitatively. Consequently, the methodological reporting is not transparent enough to enable independent verification. ```python from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, compare_mse ``` This import makes the code non-executable in standard environments where `sklearn.metrics` does not expose a `compare_mse` symbol. Because this import occurs at module import time, it prevents running any part of the pipeline even if the MSE path is never used (e.g., `graylevel=2`). This is not a minor dependency mismatch: it blocks the primary entry point (`python MMDN.py`) from running at all without manual code edits. A reproducible release should include a pinned environment and tested imports, especially given the paper’s emphasis on a deployable dynamic learning pipeline. The presence of such a blocking import indicates the released code was not packaged in a way that supports third-party reproduction. This directly affects confidence in the reported experimental outcomes. [5](#ref5) ```python from sklearn.metrics._regression import ( d2_absolute_error_score, d2_pinball_score, d2_tweedie_score, explained_variance_score, max_error, mean_absolute_error, mean_absolute_percentage_error, mean_gamma_deviance, mean_pinball_loss, mean_poisson_deviance, mean_squared_error, mean_squared_log_error, mean_tweedie_deviance, median_absolute_error, r2_score, root_mean_squared_error, root_mean_squared_log_error, ) ``` The scikit-learn project’s public `sklearn.metrics` initializer lists many exported regression metrics (including `mean_squared_error`) but does not export `compare_mse` in the same manner. This supports the claim that `compare_mse` is not part of the standard `sklearn.metrics` public API, so the paper’s code will fail for typical scikit-learn installations. In a reproducibility context, relying on non-existent public symbols without documenting a custom fork or a specific historical version is a major issue. It forces reviewers to guess how to patch the code (e.g., replacing with `mean_squared_error` or a different library), which can change evaluation behavior. Because the paper reports extremely low error rates (down to 2e-6), even small metric/threshold differences can matter. The paper does not document any such required patching, reinforcing that the released materials are insufficient for faithful reproduction. References: [1] [Shachar Resisi, Sebastien M. Popoff, and Yaron Bromberg. “Image Transmission Through a Dynamically Perturbed Multimode Fiber by Deep Learning.” *Laser & Photonics Reviews* 15, 2000553 (2021).](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2021LPRv...1500553R/abstract)

The paper’s main claims (abstract and discussion) overstate the scope of what is demonstrated, because the described experimental setup and learning objective target binary amplitude pattern recovery rather than arbitrary optical fields (e.g., complex field transmission) or direct high-resolution video transmission, weakening the robustness of the paper’s conclusions. “In this paper, we present a self-supervised dynamic learning approach that achieves long-term, high-fidelity transmission of arbitrary optical fields through unstabilized MMFs.” “Arbitrary optical fields” is a strong claim in fiber transmission, because an optical field generally refers to a complex-valued amplitude (magnitude and phase) rather than a binary amplitude pattern. The rest of the manuscript, however, describes an amplitude-only modulation and an intensity-speckle measurement pipeline paired with binary classification-style losses, which is a narrower problem. Without measuring phase or explicitly representing complex fields, the method cannot be assumed to transmit “arbitrary optical fields” in the broader sense used in wave optics. This matters for validity because the claimed application space (general remote imaging/communication of optical fields) is broader than what is experimentally validated. Readers may interpret the claim as complex field recovery, which is not supported by the provided methodological description. The paper does not delimit the claim to binary amplitude patterns in the abstract. “We used a binary SLM as the amplitude modulator to encode the spatial information, and the output speckles are captured by the monochromatic CMOS.” “Our research employed arbitrary random binary images, as well as natural scene images including the Fashion-MNIST dataset, Latin letters from the E-MNIST dataset, and handwritten digitals from the MNIST dataset. The input images tested in the experiments are of 16×16-pixel and 32×32-pixel resolution.” These statements indicate the demonstrated system encodes binary amplitude patterns and evaluates recovery on low-resolution binary images (and binarized versions of standard datasets). This is materially different from demonstrating recovery of arbitrary grayscale amplitudes or complex optical fields, both of which are common interpretations of “arbitrary optical fields.” The limitation is not just resolution; it is also the signal alphabet and modulation model, which constrains what kinds of “fields” are being transmitted and recovered. While binary pattern transmission is still valuable for communications, the manuscript’s broad framing suggests a more general optical-field capability than is actually tested. This gap affects how the results should be interpreted and compared to other optical-field transmission approaches. The paper does not provide experiments that vary gray level or recover complex-valued fields. ```python clf1 = createCNN(outsize=outsize, input_shape=input_shape) clf1.compile(loss=keras.losses.binary_crossentropy, optimizer=ada) ``` ```python x = Flatten()(x) x = Dense(outsize, activation='sigmoid')(x) ``` The use of `binary_crossentropy` and a `sigmoid` output layer is consistent with per-pixel Bernoulli (binary) prediction rather than regression of continuous-valued amplitudes or complex fields. This reinforces that the implemented learning objective is tailored to binary pattern recovery, aligning with the manuscript’s “random binary patterns” experiments. A binary objective can yield high “accuracy” even when subtle analog distortions exist, which is relevant when the paper extrapolates to high-fidelity transmission more broadly. If the goal is truly “arbitrary optical fields,” the method would need to specify a representation and loss appropriate for complex amplitudes (or at least continuous intensities) and demonstrate performance accordingly. Without that, the conclusion should be scoped to binary amplitude pattern transmission. The mismatch between broad abstract claims and the binary-coded implementation weakens the conclusions. “We experimentally measure the monochromatic transmission matrix (TM) of an optical multiple scattering medium using a spatial light modulator together with a phase-shifting interferometry measurement method.” [3](#ref3) This statement illustrates that approaches aimed at controlling or characterizing an optical medium at the “field” level often require phase-sensitive measurement (e.g., interferometry) to recover complex transmission properties. It highlights that “optical field” problems in scattering media are typically formulated in terms of complex amplitudes, not only binary intensity patterns. The reviewed paper’s setup is explicitly amplitude-to-amplitude with binary modulation and intensity speckle capture, which is a different and more restricted measurement model. Therefore, the manuscript’s wording “arbitrary optical fields” risks conflating a binary amplitude communication task with broader complex-field transmission/control tasks. This matters because the claimed significance depends on breadth of applicability. The paper should either justify why its demonstrated setting suffices for “arbitrary optical fields” or narrow the claim. “We use Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) to classify and reconstruct a large database of handwritten digits from the intensity of the speckle patterns that result after the images propagated through multimode fibers (MMF).” [2](#ref2) This prior work explicitly frames the deep learning task as reconstructing images from intensity-only speckle patterns, which is closely aligned with the reviewed paper’s measurement model. It also implicitly scopes the reconstruction target to intensity images (e.g., handwritten digits) rather than arbitrary complex optical fields. By comparison, the reviewed manuscript’s abstract elevates the claim to “arbitrary optical fields,” which is broader than what intensity-only speckle inversion typically supports without additional assumptions or measurements. The quote helps contextualize what is commonly demonstrated in deep-learning-based MMF image reconstruction: intensity image recovery under certain conditions. It therefore underscores that the reviewed paper’s experimental evidence (binary amplitude patterns and intensity speckle) supports a narrower claim than stated. This discrepancy weakens the conclusions’ generality.

The paper’s conclusion that MMDN provides a “comprehensive improvement” and robust long-term operation is not sufficiently supported by the comparison design, which uses a limited static-network baseline and omits comparisons to established strategies that explicitly address dynamic/perturbed MMF transmission. “The static neural network (StaticNN) trained on the first 500-second data and tested on the following 500-second is taken as the baseline method. The MMDN was dynamically updated at each 5-second interval.” This comparison contrasts an updating method against a non-updating method, so it primarily demonstrates that online adaptation can help under drift—something that is plausible but not, by itself, evidence of broad superiority over alternative dynamic-handling strategies. In dynamic MMF settings, common baselines include models trained to generalize across perturbations, periodic recalibration strategies, or adaptive synthesis approaches; without such comparisons, it is difficult to attribute gains specifically to the proposed multi-timescale memory ensemble design. Additionally, training StaticNN for 500 seconds and then freezing it is a particular baseline choice; other static baselines (e.g., shorter, more recent training windows) could be closer to the current channel state and reduce the apparent advantage. Because the paper claims near-perfect performance over long durations, conclusions depend on whether MMDN’s advantage persists against stronger baselines, not just a single frozen model. The manuscript does not report such comparisons, limiting the robustness of its conclusions. This affects perceived significance and the strength of evidence for the stated claims. “We compared MMDN with previous approaches in terms of time duration, reconstruction quality, and transmission distance, etc., and demonstrated a comprehensive improvement of our approach (see Supplementary Fig. 7).” The manuscript asserts a comprehensive improvement over “previous approaches,” but the main paper’s quantitative comparisons presented in the provided text focus on StaticNN versus MMDN. Without explicit head-to-head evaluation against representative prior dynamic methods under matched conditions, the strength of this claim cannot be assessed from the main document. This is particularly important because the field contains methods tailored to perturbations (e.g., training across multiple conformations) that may offer robustness without online label-dependent tuning. A reader needs transparent details of which baselines were implemented, their hyperparameters, and how their data regimes match MMDN’s. Referring to an external supplementary figure without providing the methodological comparison details in the main text reduces verifiability of the “comprehensive improvement” conclusion. As a result, the conclusion appears stronger than what the disclosed evidence supports. “To overcome this challenge, a deep learning approach that generalizes over mechanical perturbations is presented.” [1](#ref1) This sentence highlights an established alternative framing for dynamic MMF channels: training a model to generalize across perturbations rather than relying on online self-updating. Because the reviewed paper’s main experimental baseline is a frozen static model, it does not directly test whether MMDN outperforms a generalization-trained approach on comparable perturbation/drift conditions. Including such a baseline is important to substantiate novelty and superiority claims, since both directions (generalization across perturbations vs. online adaptation) aim to handle channel variability. Without direct comparison, the manuscript’s claim of comprehensive improvement over prior work is not convincingly supported by the presented evidence. This does not negate the possibility that MMDN is better, but it means the conclusion is not robustly demonstrated. Therefore, the paper’s comparative claims are currently under-evidenced.

The paper claims to use a "self-supervised" method where the model is updated using "predicted input images as labels". However, the implementation uses ground truth labels (`y_fortest`) for validation and early stopping during the online adaptation phase. This invalidates the claim of self-supervision as it relies on ground truth information that would not be available in a real deployment scenario. "The MMDN model will be dynamically updated by online training on the unlabeled streaming data using predicted input images as labels, hence working in a self-supervised architecture." The authors explicitly claim that the method is self-supervised and uses predicted labels for training. This implies that no ground truth is needed during the adaptation phase. ```python clf1.fit(x_labeled[-int(sizeOfPretrain/3*2):], y_labeled[-int(sizeOfPretrain/3*2):], batch_size=batch_size, epochs=int(2*epochs), callbacks=get_callbacks(), validation_data=(x_fortest, y_fortest)) ``` In `code/MMDN.py`, the `fit` function is called with `validation_data=(x_fortest, y_fortest)`. `y_fortest` contains the ground truth labels for the current batch. This shows that the training process has access to the ground truth for validation. ```python def get_callbacks(): early_stopping = EarlyStopping(monitor='val_loss', min_delta=5e-6, patience=10, verbose=2, mode='min') # min_delta=5e-6 csv_logger = CSVLogger('./log.csv', append=True) tensorboard = TensorBoard(log_dir='./logs', histogram_freq=0, write_graph=False) return [tensorboard, csv_logger, early_stopping] ``` In `code/model.py`, the `get_callbacks` function defines an `EarlyStopping` callback that monitors `val_loss`. Since `val_loss` is calculated using the ground truth `y_fortest` passed in `fit`, the model uses ground truth to decide when to stop training. This optimization based on ground truth is not possible in a true self-supervised setting and constitutes a significant methodological flaw (data leakage).

The paper claims "high-fidelity transmission of arbitrary optical fields", but the experiments are limited to low-resolution (32x32) binary patterns. This resolution is significantly lower than standard image transmission benchmarks (e.g., 128x128 or higher grayscale/color images) and does not demonstrate the capability to transmit "arbitrary" fields, especially complex-valued or high-resolution natural scenes. "We demonstrate >99.9% accuracy in the transmission of 1024 spatial degree-of-freedom... The long-term high-fidelity capability enables compressive encoded transfer of high-resolution video..." The claim of "high-fidelity" is based on the transmission of 1024 pixels (32x32 resolution). While the bit error rate is low (>99.9% accuracy), calling 32x32 binary transmission "high-fidelity" is misleading in the context of imaging, where high fidelity usually implies high resolution and accurate intensity/phase reconstruction. "Our research employed arbitrary random binary images... The input images tested in the experiments are of 16×16-pixel and 32×32-pixel resolution." The experiments are restricted to 32x32 binary patterns. This contradicts the abstract's broad claim of transmitting "arbitrary optical fields", as binary patterns are a specific and easier subset of arbitrary fields (which include grayscale and complex-valued fields). "We used a binary SLM as the amplitude modulator to encode the spatial information..." The use of a binary SLM further confirms that the system is designed for and tested on binary data. The ability to transmit binary data does not automatically generalize to "arbitrary optical fields" with continuous amplitude and phase.

The proposed self-training method relies on pseudo-labels generated by the model itself. The paper does not adequately address the risk of error accumulation (model collapse) in the absence of ground truth (assuming the leakage issue is fixed). The confidence metric used is simplistic (based on binarization) and may not prevent confident errors, leading to confirmation bias. "The network parameters are dynamically updated over time in a self-supervised manner, by learning from currently predicted pseudo-labels to synthesize the optimal inverse transmission model..." The method relies on its own predictions to update the model. This creates a feedback loop where errors can be reinforced. The paper does not provide theoretical or empirical analysis on how the method prevents model collapse, a common failure mode in self-training (as discussed in TENT [1] and SHOT [2]). ```python def confidenceFactor(arr): median = 0.5 confList = [] for i in range(0, arr.shape[0]): conf = np.sum(np.abs(arr[i] - median)*1.8 + 0.1) / arr.shape[-1] confList.append(conf) meanConf = np.mean(confList) return confList, meanConf ``` In `code/model_cotrain.py`, the confidence metric is calculated based on the distance from 0.5. This measures how "binary" the output is, not how correct it is. A model can be confidently wrong (e.g., predicting a perfect "1" when the true label is "0"), especially under severe channel distortions. Using this metric to weight the ensemble does not guarantee robustness against error propagation. References: [1] Wang, Dequan, et al. "Tent: Fully test-time adaptation by entropy minimization." International Conference on Learning Representations (2021). https://openreview.net/forum?id=uXl3bZLkr3c [2] Liang, Jian, et al. "Do we really need to access the source data? Source hypothesis transfer for unsupervised domain adaptation." International Conference on Machine Learning (2020). https://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/liang20a.html

The paper only compares the proposed method with a static neural network baseline. It does not compare with other adaptive methods or state-of-the-art MMF transmission techniques, such as those using physics-based constraints or other test-time adaptation strategies. "The static neural network (StaticNN) trained on the first 500-second data and tested on the following 500-second is taken as the baseline method." The comparison is limited to a static baseline, which is known to fail in dynamic environments. This sets a weak baseline. "Image transmission through a dynamically perturbed multimode fiber by deep learning [3]" The paper cites Resisi et al. [3] which proposes a deep learning approach for dynamically perturbed MMFs. However, it does not compare the proposed method against this relevant competitor. "Adaptive 3D descattering with a dynamic synthesis network [4]" The paper cites Tahir et al. [4] which also addresses adaptive descattering. A comparison with such dynamic methods is necessary to demonstrate the significance of the proposed approach. References: [3] Resisi, Shachar, et al. "Image transmission through a dynamically perturbed multimode fiber by deep learning."
