Laser & Photonics Reviews 15.10 (2021): 2000553. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/lpor.202000553 [4] Tahir, Waleed, et al. "Adaptive 3D descattering with a dynamic synthesis network." Light: Science & Applications 11.1 (2022): 42. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41377-022-00716-6

The most important part of the reported neural network, the self-supervised training, lacks essential details or explanations. (a) It is not clear how the loss function is explicitly defined, and what regularization the authors used in network training. (b) The convergence of the reported self-supervised training framework is questionable. The authors proposed to regard the previous network output as the label for current input data. But for the reported network architecture frequently updating by the input sequences, one can easily figure a trivial solution, in which the network produces constant for any input. The proof of correct convergence for the reported self-supervised training framework must be provided.

As a dynamic neural network, the update mechanism was hardly explained in the manuscript. How does the proposed CNN update its memory by its input sequence? What is the difference between S1, S2, and L3? What is the updating frequency for these three modules?

The experimental setup seems too ideal for validation of real-world applications. The inputs and the targets of the network were both binary images, which greatly reduces the difficulty of this reconstruction task and hinders the potent of the reported method for practical applications. The authors should at least show results on non-binary images, e.g., 2-bit, 4-bit images.
In addition to the static neural network baseline, I also wonder how conventional algorithms would perform against the reported method. Benchmarking would be good to include.
The specs of the MMF used in the experiment are not listed.
In line 219, please give a reference to AdaDelta paper.
The number of trainable parameters, and the training time on the given machine are not mentioned in the Methods section.
For Fig. 1b, the pink texts on the left, what is “self-s update”?

Could the authors explain why they design a multi-memory network with two branches representing interleaved short-term modeling and one branch for long-term modeling, instead of one short-term and one-long-term structure?
Could the author discuss more on the choice of self-supervised model updating interval? Is it relevant to the MMF characteristics only or also affected by other factors (e.g., imaging rate)?

In Fig. 2 and Fig. 4, the time axis of transmission accuracy trace starts from 500s. I understand that this is for comparison with the StaticNN baseline, but this could be a little bit misleading and incomplete. Could the authors reorganize their presentation (for example, add the first 500-second results as a Supplementary figure)? In addition, are the averaged spatial accuracy maps for MMDN calculated on 500s or 1000s duration?

What is the beam size of the incident light on the SLM and onto the entrance of the MMF? The authors have experimented on MMFs with different diameters. Is the beam size the same in all experiments and does the fill factor influence on the transmission performance?
I would suggest the authors to provide their code to facilitate the audience.
the calibration of TM with huge number of -> the calibration of TM with a huge number of after long time-> after a long time
with several orders throughput enhancement -> with several orders of throughput enhancement
For 100m transmission cases, MMDN even reduce the averaged error rate -> For 100m transmission cases, MMDN even reduces the average error rate

Would this work be of significance to the field? Currently shown evidence is not sufficiently convincing. A comparison with single-mode fiber (SMF), which is widely used in optical communication, could further underscore the significance of your findings. Evaluating the potential benefits of integrating the MMDN with MMFs in contrast to SMFs would be insightful. Specifically, if the SLM or DMD's speed is limited to certain kHz, how would the bandwidth of MMFs compare with that of SMFs?

Needs more convincing demos than binary input. While the video streaming experiment has been demonstrated to showcase its potentials to optical telecommunications, its impact on other applications such as remote sensing and endoscopy is unclear to me. It seems to me that MMDN is designed for decoding binary input patterns, while the images obtained in remote sensing and endoscopy are normally non-binary. Besides, MMDN requires real-time model correction via inspecting images at the distal end, which is arguably only possible with some types of “guidestars” as a feedback signal and thus substantially restricts its use scenario. On the other hand, data streaming via binary pattern decoding limits the operation of MMF at its maximum possible throughput, where a continuous level (or at least gray level) streaming is available. To validate its broad impact and applicability, it would be valuable to see some non-binary decoding results. Also, discussions on some real-world scenarios in which MMDN is viable would be helpful.

Needs more clarity on how it works. The primary merit of MMDN is its self-supervised adaptive learning capability. However, how the online learning works is neither elucidated in detail nor accompanied with proper reference. Without knowing the exact working principle and how the unseen unlabeled data are utilized to update the model, it is not clear whether MMDN serves as an efficient solution to perturbation-robust data transmission in MMFs.

Another concern is the amount of training data required for the initial model and each model update. First, 20,000 paired data for the initial model are not viable in most of the real applications in remote sensing and endoscopy even in a static system, which is probably even more challenging in a dynamic system as considered in the manuscript. In addition, it’s not clearly stated in the manuscript how many patterns are needed for each update. Given the relatively small number of input modes and the development of efficient calibration methods (e.g., Li, S., Saunders, C., Lum, D.J. et al. Compressively sampling the optical transmission matrix of a multimode fibre. Light Sci Appl 10, 88 (2021).), if the number of patterns is comparable to the number of input modes, MMDN might not provide an edge on calibration efficiency, i.e., the number of data acquisitions required for each calibration. It would be useful to include more demonstration and discussion on the possibility of using a smaller training dataset and its calibration efficiency compared to conventional methods such as transmission-matrix-based method.

The statement "We experimented on an amplitude-to-amplitude optical setup" requires clarification. i. Is "amplitude-to-amplitude" synonymous with "intensity-to-intensity"? ii. How does the SLM plane relate to the fiber input plane? Is the SLM on the Fourier plane of the objective lens, and are these two planes conjugate or Fourier related?

The characterization of the MMFs as "highly variable" in the experiment involving two different MMF channels needs to be better defined. As per Supplementary Figure 2, these MMF channels seem to fall under "medium instability".

The paper's central claim linking transposon abundance to susceptibility to environment-induced heritable changes is based solely on correlational evidence from only 6 genotypes, without experimental validation of a causal mechanism. "To explore which factor predicted the susceptibility of a genotype to environmental induction, we regressed the occurrence frequency of genotypes against different explanatory variables (climate of origin, methylation levels, and transposon abundance) and found that the abundance of transposons in the Gypsy (r = -0.840) and MuDR (r = 0.815) superfamilies explained a significant proportion of the variation (Fig. 3b, Extended Data Fig. 3b)." The correlations reported (r = -0.840 and r = 0.815) are calculated from only 6 genotypes, as stated later in the Methods. With such a small sample size, statistical power for correlation analysis is severely limited, and the correlations are highly susceptible to being driven by one or two outlier genotypes. According to standard statistical guidelines, a sample size of n=6 provides 80% power to detect only extremely large correlations (r > 0.80 at α = 0.05), and the resulting confidence intervals would be extraordinarily wide (approximately 0.33 to 0.99 for r = 0.9), providing minimal useful information about the true effect size [[1]](#ref1). "These data were available for seven genotypes, including Abd-0, Ang-0, Col-0, Dja-1, Sap-0, Tol-0, and Ws-2 (Supplementary Table 1). The transposon data of Col-0 were not included in the subsequent analysis because Col-0 is the reference genome and shows biased (much more detailed) annotation of transposons compared to other genotypes." The exclusion of Col-0 reduces the already limited sample to just 6 genotypes for the transposon correlation analysis. This sample size is far below the minimum recommended for correlation studies. Standard guidelines suggest sample sizes of at least 30-50 for moderate correlations, and this limitation severely undermines the reliability of the paper's key finding that transposon abundance predicts susceptibility to environmental induction [[2]](#ref2). "These results indicate that transposons play a central role in mediating environment-induced changes, which could occur through epigenetic modifications of transposons (i.e., DNA methylation and RNA interference) that simultaneously regulate the expression of downstream genes or through de novo transposon insertions that alter gene expression patterns." This statement suggests potential mechanisms but none were experimentally tested. The paper provides no evidence of differential DNA methylation at transposon sites, no measurement of siRNA changes, and no detection of de novo transposon insertions in the experimental lines. Without mechanistic validation, the correlational evidence cannot support the strong conclusion that transposons "play a central role" in mediating these effects. References: [1] [Sample Size and Power for Correlation Analysis - Portland State University](https://web.pdx.edu/~newsomj/mvclass/ho_sample%20size.pdf) [2] [An elaboration on sample size determination for correlations based on confidence interval estimation](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11148401/)

While the paper claims that environment-induced heritable changes are common and implicates epigenetic mechanisms, no direct measurements of DNA methylation or histone modifications were performed on the experimental plant generations, leaving the mechanistic basis for inheritance entirely untested. "The raw methylation data of 14 genotypes were obtained from published sources." The DNA methylation data used in this study were not generated from the actual experimental plants that underwent environmental treatments, but rather from previously published data for the original genotypes. This means the study cannot determine whether epigenetic marks actually changed in response to ancestral environments or whether such changes were transmitted to offspring generations. This is a critical limitation because demonstrating transgenerational epigenetic inheritance requires showing that epigenetic marks change in response to environmental treatment and persist across generations [[3]](#ref3). "Our study thus challenges the classic view that the environment only participates in the selection of heritable variation and suggests that the environment can broadly contribute to the generation of heritable variation." This strong conclusion about the environment generating heritable variation implies an epigenetic mechanism, yet the study only measured phenotypes and gene expression, not the epigenetic modifications themselves. As emphasized in the literature on transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, establishing causality requires direct measurement of epigenetic marks and demonstration that they escape meiotic reprogramming to be transmitted across generations [[4]](#ref4). "These de novo heritable variations may arise from the mechanisms discussed earlier, including epigenetic changes (DNA methylation, histone modifications, and small interfering RNAs) or de novo insertion of transposons." The paper lists multiple possible mechanisms but tests none of them directly. Without measuring DNA methylation, histone modifications, siRNAs, or transposon insertions in the actual experimental lines across generations, the study cannot distinguish between true epigenetic inheritance and other explanations such as cryptic genetic variation, maternal provisioning effects, or environmental microvariation [[5]](#ref5). References: [3] [Transgenerational Epigenetic Inheritance: myths and mechanisms - Cell](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4020004/) [4] [Molecular mechanisms of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance - Nature Reviews Genetics](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41576-021-00438-5) [5] [A critical view on transgenerational epigenetic inheritance in humans - Nature Communications](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-018-05445-5)

The paper defines heritable differentially expressed genes (DEGs) as those differentially expressed "for at least two generations," but this definition does not distinguish true transgenerational epigenetic inheritance from intergenerational effects, genetic changes, or maternal effects. "A gene was considered to be a heritable DEG if it was significantly differentially expressed for at least two generations." This definition is problematic because expression changes detected in two consecutive generations (F1 and F2) could result from intergenerational effects (where the F2 germ cells were directly exposed to the ancestral environment in the developing F1 embryo) rather than true transgenerational inheritance. The field distinguishes between intergenerational effects (affecting F1 and potentially F2) and transgenerational effects (appearing in F3 or beyond for exposures during pregnancy), as the germline of F2 offspring was present in utero during the original F1 exposure [[6]](#ref6). "Transgenerational Epigenetic Inheritance (TEI) describes the phenomenon that phenotypic traits... can be transferred to offspring generations via epigenetic mechanisms." According to established definitions in the field, true transgenerational epigenetic inheritance requires transmission of epigenetic information through the germline across generations that were never directly exposed to the original stressor [[6]](#ref6). For plants where seeds develop on the stressed maternal plant, F1 seeds are directly exposed, and the germ cells that will form F2 are exposed during F1 development. Thus, effects must persist to F3 or beyond to be considered truly transgenerational, making the "two generation" threshold used in this study insufficiently stringent. "We found that all environmental treatments caused heritable phenotypic and gene expression changes, with a substantial proportion stably transmitted over all observed generations." Without ruling out alternative explanations such as de novo genetic mutations, standing genetic variation inadvertently selected by the treatments, or maternal provisioning effects (seed quality/size differences), the claim that these changes represent environment-induced heritable variation through epigenetic mechanisms is not adequately supported. The paper acknowledges seed-mediated effects are possible but does not control for them [[7]](#ref7). References: [6] [Present and future challenges for the investigation of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance - Environment International](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0160412023000491) [7] [Effects of Multi-Generational Stress Exposure and Offspring Environment on the Expression and Persistence of Transgenerational Effects in Arabidopsis thaliana - PLOS ONE](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0151566)

The paper acknowledges significant phenotypic variation between offspring generations grown in different batches under ostensibly uniform conditions, creating a confounding factor that undermines the interpretation of transgenerational effects in the primary experimental data (Test I). "why did some significant phenotypic changes disappear in one generation but reappear in the next (Fig. 2b), and why did the occurrence frequency of induced changes vary between offspring generations (Fig. 3a)? A potential explanation is that the growth conditions differed between offspring generations, as evidenced by significant phenotypic variation between generations in the control group (Extended Data Fig. 2c, Supplementary Table 3)." The authors acknowledge that control plants showed significant phenotypic variation between generations, indicating that growth conditions were not truly uniform across the different time periods when F1-F4 plants were grown. This environmental heterogeneity directly confounds the ability to attribute observed phenotypic differences to transgenerational inheritance rather than to differential responses to varying growth conditions. "To eliminate the influence of variable growth conditions, we conducted a second test of ancestral treatment effects (Test II), where we selected a subset of 10 genotypes and five treatments and grew F1 to F4 plants together under the same conditions." While Test II was designed to address this limitation, it used only a subset of genotypes (10 of 14) and treatments (5 of 10), meaning the primary conclusions of the paper still rely heavily on Test I data, which is compromised by environmental confounding. The paper does not quantify how much of the variance in Test I could be attributed to environmental variation versus true transgenerational effects. "Such variation may also contribute to the inconsistent effect sizes observed between generations (significant G × A.E × F, Supplementary Table 4)." The significant three-way interaction between genotype, ancestral environment, and generation (G × A.E × F) could be interpreted as evidence for complex transgenerational effects, but it could equally reflect differential genotype responses to the environmental variation between generations. Without a properly controlled experimental design where all generations are grown simultaneously from the start, distinguishing these explanations is not possible [[8]](#ref8). References: [8] [Transgenerational response to stress in Arabidopsis thaliana - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3115178/)

The analysis of transposon enrichment near heritable DEGs relies exclusively on the Col-0 reference genome annotation, which the authors acknowledge may not accurately represent transposon distributions in other genotypes, potentially biasing the enrichment results. "We adopted the transposon annotation of TAIR10 and performed the analysis using a customized script coded with R package 'GenomicRanges' v.1.42.0. Notably, our study adopted Col-0 as the reference, and genotype-specific genome assembly is necessary for a comprehensive understanding of this nonrandom phenomenon." The authors use the TAIR10 transposon annotation (based on the Col-0 reference) to analyze transposon enrichment near DEGs across all four genotypes (Abd-0, Ang-0, Tol-0, TRE-1) in the RNA-seq analysis. Transposon insertion polymorphisms are extensive among Arabidopsis accessions, with different genotypes harboring unique insertions not present in Col-0 and lacking insertions present in Col-0. Using the Col-0 annotation for non-Col-0 genotypes will systematically miss genotype-specific transposons and incorrectly assign Col-0-specific transposons to regions where they may not exist [[9]](#ref9). "The transposon data of Col-0 were not included in the subsequent analysis because Col-0 is the reference genome and shows biased (much more detailed) annotation of transposons compared to other genotypes." This statement acknowledges that transposon annotation quality differs between the reference (Col-0) and other genotypes, yet the same reference annotation is used for the DEG-transposon enrichment analysis. The inconsistency in annotation quality across genotypes could create spurious enrichment signals or mask true associations, undermining the conclusion that heritable DEGs have higher transposon abundance in their upstream and gene body regions. "We found that the abundance of members of several superfamilies, including Gypsy, HAT, Mariner, and Helitron, upstream and body of these susceptible genes was significantly higher than that of background genes (Extended Data Fig. 10b)." This finding relies on accurate mapping of transposons to gene regions across different genotypes. Given that the analysis uses only Col-0 transposon coordinates while the DEGs were identified from four different genotypes (Abd-0, Ang-0, Tol-0, TRE-1), the enrichment calculation conflates genomic coordinates that may not be equivalent across accessions due to structural variation. Genotype-specific genome assemblies, as the authors note would be needed, are essential to accurately test this hypothesis [[10]](#ref10). References: [9] [Genetic and environmental modulation of transposition shapes the evolutionary potential of Arabidopsis thaliana - Genome Biology](https://genomebiology.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13059-021-02348-5) [10] [Transgenerational epigenetic inheritance in plants - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4359895/)

This paper overstates a causal conclusion that ancestral environments broadly generate heritable (including *de novo*) variation, because the experimental design and measurements do not rule out DNA-sequence-based explanations (cryptic genetic variation, selection among residual variants, or stress-induced mutations/TE insertions) for the observed multigenerational differences. “Our work challenges the textbook understanding that environments are only involved in the selection of heritable variation\(^{3}\) and suggests that environments are broadly involved in the generation of heritable variation.” This sentence makes a strong causal claim about the *origin* of heritable variation, not merely about transgenerational plasticity or parent-of-origin carryover. Demonstrating such a claim typically requires separating environmentally induced *non-genetic* inheritance from DNA sequence changes and selection on standing variation, which is difficult in multigenerational experiments. The manuscript primarily reports phenotype and expression differences, but it does not provide direct evidence that treated lineages remain genetically identical to controls across generations. Without genotyping/whole-genome sequencing (or other strong genetic controls) it is not possible to attribute persistent differences uniquely to environment-induced heritable (epigenetic) mechanisms. This matters because the paper explicitly discusses genetic mechanisms (e.g., TE insertions) that would change DNA sequence and therefore are not distinguishable from selection/mutation without genomic assays. The conclusion therefore exceeds what the described measurements can validate. “*de novo* heritable variations caused by environmental changes can serve as another primary source for rapid adaptation.” Framing the observed effects as *de novo heritable variations* implies new heritable variants are being created by the treatments, which is a much stronger claim than “ancestral environments influence offspring phenotypes.” The manuscript does not present direct evidence of new variants (e.g., newly arising TE insertions, de novo mutations, or stable epialleles that are shown to be independent of DNA sequence). In the absence of genetic validation, persistent lineage differences could reflect selection among any residual within-accession variation or rare mutations accumulating over seven generations, especially under stressful treatments. This is particularly relevant because the paper later proposes *de novo* TE insertions as a plausible mechanism, which would require genomic evidence to support. Common practice in epigenetic-inheritance work is to explicitly exclude DNA sequence change when making strong claims about epigenetic causality. As written, the paper’s causal interpretation is underdetermined by the presented evidence. “In the second generation, seeds of the four resulting plants were pooled for each genotype to establish the ancestral generation.” Pooling seeds across multiple parental plants creates a mixture of lineages at the start of the “ancestral generation,” which can complicate causal attribution if any residual heterogeneity (or contamination) exists within the accession seed stock. Even in a predominantly selfing species, residual heterozygosity and within-stock variation are not impossible, and pooling makes it harder to ensure strict genetic identity across subsequent treated and control lines. If the ancestral treatments alter survival or fecundity, selection could act on whatever variation exists in the pooled population, producing heritable differences that are genetic rather than epigenetic. The manuscript does not report genotyping to confirm that treated and control descendants remained genetically matched. This weakness directly impacts the interpretation that the environment *generated* heritable variation, rather than sorting it. The design choice therefore creates a plausible alternative explanation that is not excluded. “In the ancestral generation, 26 out of 1008 (2.58%) lines died before seed production, and their offspring generation was replaced with seeds of a randomly selected replicate from the same genotype and treatment.” Replacing dead lines with seeds from other replicates breaks strict line continuity and can blur the mapping between a particular ancestral individual and its descendants. Even if the replacement rate is relatively small, it introduces an additional mechanism by which “line” identities (and thus heritable trajectories) can be reassigned, which complicates claims about stable transmission within a lineage. This matters because the paper interprets persistence across generations as evidence of heritability and stability, but the replacement procedure can effectively merge replicate histories. Such merging can inflate apparent persistence (by substituting a successful replicate’s seed for a failed replicate) and complicate statistical independence assumptions in downstream analyses. The manuscript does not quantify how these replacements affect specific treatment–genotype combinations, which could be nonrandom under stress. Without careful accounting, this weakens causal claims about stable inheritance arising from the treatment itself. “However such studies often concern inter- rather than trans-generational effects (Figure 1) and rarely exclude DNA sequence changes as the underlying cause for heritability.” [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4020004/) This statement from a widely cited review highlights a core methodological standard: strong claims of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance require excluding DNA sequence changes as the cause. The paper under review makes broad claims about the environment’s role in generating heritable variation, but it does not provide genetic evidence to exclude DNA-sequence-based mechanisms. The review also emphasizes the frequency of confounding in studies that do not isolate truly transgenerational effects or that do not control for alternative explanations. Because the present manuscript explicitly invokes mechanisms that include *de novo* TE insertions, the need to separate sequence change from epigenetic inheritance is especially acute. Without such controls, the causal direction (“environment generates heritable variation”) remains ambiguous. This directly challenges the validity of the manuscript’s strongest conclusion. References: [1] [Heard E, Martienssen RA. Transgenerational Epigenetic Inheritance: myths and mechanisms. *Cell* (2014).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4020004/)

This paper’s key conclusion that environment-induced heritable phenotypic changes are common across environments is not statistically robust because “occurrence” is defined by repeated nominal significance tests (P<0.05) converted into binary outcomes without a multiple-comparisons strategy, creating a high risk of false positives in a very large testing problem. “we transformed the quantitative estimates of effect sizes into qualitative assessments, defining an environment-induced change as occurring when the effect size significantly deviated from zero.” This sentence shows that the central “occurrence” metric is driven by whether each estimated effect size crosses a significance threshold. In a setting with many genotype × treatment × generation × trait combinations, repeated hypothesis testing at a nominal alpha produces false positives by construction. The manuscript does not describe a correction across the full family of tests used to declare changes, even though the downstream narrative relies on counting how often “significant” effects occur across generations and environments. Converting continuous effect estimates into binary significance further amplifies sensitivity to arbitrary thresholds and sample-size differences, which can distort prevalence claims. Because the conclusion “common across various environments” is built on these counts, statistical calibration of the error rate is essential. Without it, the reported prevalence can be substantially inflated. “According to these significances, we converted the effect sizes to qualitative occurrence data, where significant effect sizes were assigned a value of one, and nonsignificant effect sizes were assigned a value of zero.” This explicitly confirms the binarization pipeline: multiple model coefficients are tested and then discretized into 0/1 outcomes. Such discretization throws away magnitude information and makes the analysis hinge on whether P-values fall just above or just below 0.05, which is known to be unstable across repeated sampling. More importantly, the manuscript does not specify any global false-positive control across the many coefficients tested across traits, treatments, and generations. In multigenerational, multi-factor designs, it is standard to either pre-specify a limited set of primary tests or apply family-wise/FDR control for discovery-style counting arguments. Here, the occurrence frequency and claims about “all environments” depend on aggregating many thresholded tests, so multiplicity control is not optional. The lack of such control undermines the reliability of the main prevalence conclusion. ```r # Create a binary variable indicating whether Pvalue < 0.05 ef.size$value_sig <- ifelse(ef.size$Pvalue < 0.05, 1, 0) ``` The provided analysis code operationalizes “occurrence” as a raw P-value cutoff at 0.05, consistent with the manuscript text, and it does so without adjustment for the number of tests. This makes the prevalence of “environment-induced changes” sensitive to the sheer number of coefficients tested, rather than solely to reproducible biological signal. Because the code converts every tested coefficient into a binary “significant/not” indicator, the downstream generalized linear mixed model is effectively modeling a discovery process that is known to contain false positives at roughly the nominal rate under null effects. If thousands of tests are performed, the expected number of false positives can become large even with modest dependence among tests. The code also suggests that significance is assessed per-coefficient rather than via a hierarchical model that would partially pool estimates and reduce spurious extremes. This code-level evidence therefore supports the critique that the paper’s “common” claim may be an artifact of uncorrected multiplicity. “It is often the case that thousands of features in a genomewide data set are tested against some null hypothesis, where a number of features are expected to be signiﬁcant.” [[2]](http://engr.case.edu/ray_soumya/mlrg/Storey03.pdf) This sentence captures a key statistical reality in large-scale testing: with many tests, a nontrivial number of apparently “significant” findings will arise even in the absence of true effects. The paper under review uses a similarly large-scale testing and counting approach (across genotypes, treatments, generations, and traits) but does not describe an approach to calibrate the expected false discovery burden. Standard practice in high-dimensional inference is to control a relevant error rate (e.g., FDR) when interpreting counts of significant results as evidence of prevalence. Without such calibration, “significant” does not translate into “common” or “pervasive” in a statistically meaningful way. The quotation therefore directly supports the claim that nominal significance thresholds are insufficient for robust prevalence conclusions. This is not a minor reporting issue; it affects the manuscript’s central quantitative conclusion. References: [2] [Storey JD, Tibshirani R. Statistical significance for genomewide studies. *PNAS* (2003).](http://engr.case.edu/ray_soumya/mlrg/Storey03.pdf)

This paper’s conclusion that genotype susceptibility to environmental induction is predictable from transposon abundance is not well supported because the regression uses transposon copy-number data for only a subset of genotypes (effectively n≈6 after excluding Col-0) and does not account for population structure/relatedness, making the reported correlations unreliable and non-generalizable. “we regressed the occurrence frequency of genotypes against different explanatory variables (climate of origin, methylation levels, and transposon abundance) and found that the abundance of transposons in the Gypsy (r = -0.840) and MuDR (r = 0.815) superfamilies explained a significant proportion of the variation” This sentence presents strong correlation-based predictability claims, but correlation coefficients can be unstable and misleading in very small samples. The manuscript text does not state the sample size used for the transposon regressions in this Results paragraph, which is crucial for interpreting r and P-values. Moreover, the analysis appears to test multiple predictors (climate variables, methylation contexts, multiple TE superfamilies), which increases the risk that some predictors appear “significant” by chance without appropriate multiplicity control. Even if one or two correlations survive nominal thresholds, they may reflect shared ancestry or geographic structure rather than a mechanistic TE effect. The paper interprets these correlations mechanistically (“susceptibility… can be predicted”), which is much stronger than the evidence supports. This undermines the strength and generality of one of the headline claims. “These data were available for seven genotypes, including Abd-0, Ang-0, Col-0, Dja-1, Sap-0, Tol-0, and Ws-2 (Supplementary Table 1). The transposon data of Col-0 were not included in the subsequent analysis…” This methods statement implies that the TE-based predictability analysis is conducted on at most six genotypes once Col-0 is excluded. With n≈6, any fitted regression is severely underpowered, and effect estimates (including r) are highly sensitive to single data points. In such a setting, model diagnostics, robustness checks (e.g., leave-one-out sensitivity), and explicit reporting of uncertainty become essential, but the Results paragraph reports the correlations as substantive predictors. Small-sample correlations are also especially vulnerable to confounding by population structure and relatedness, which are known to be strong in global Arabidopsis panels. The limited genotype coverage also conflicts with the broad framing that susceptibility is a general property “of a genotype” across the 14-genotype experiment. This is therefore a major limitation on the validity of the predictability claim. “A potentially serious obstacle to association mapping is confounding by population structure. It is well known that population structure may cause spurious correlations, leading to an elevated false-positive rate” [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1779303/) This quotation states a general and well-established problem: correlations between genomic features and phenotypes can be spurious when individuals are related or structured. Although the paper under review is not doing GWAS, it is performing genotype-level regressions of “occurrence frequency” against genomic predictors (TE abundance), where population structure among accessions is a plausible confound. Because the manuscript does not model relatedness (e.g., via kinship/mixed models) in these regressions, it cannot distinguish TE-driven predictability from ancestry-driven correlation. This is especially concerning given the extremely small n implied by the TE-data availability. The external source underscores that “significant” correlations under structure should be treated cautiously and ideally supported by independent evidence. The manuscript does not provide such independent validation for the TE predictability. “However, genetic association studies in inbred model organisms are confronted by the problem of inflated false positive rates due to population structure and genetic relatedness among inbred strains…” [[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2278096/) This statement emphasizes that population structure and relatedness can inflate false positives even in inbred model-organism panels, directly relevant to Arabidopsis accessions. The manuscript’s TE predictability analysis uses simple regressions and reports nominal significance, but does not incorporate relatedness corrections that are standard when comparing genotype-level predictors across structured panels. Without such correction, the reported TE–susceptibility correlations can be artifacts of shared background rather than causal predictors. This limitation is amplified by the fact that TE copy number and many other genomic properties can covary with geography and demography. Therefore, even if the reported correlations are numerically large, they do not constitute reliable evidence of predictability. The quotation supports the critique that methodological safeguards against inflated false positives are necessary for the manuscript’s claim. References: [3] [Zhao K, Aranzana MJ, Kim S, et al. An *Arabidopsis* Example of Association Mapping in Structured Samples. *PLOS Genetics* (2007).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1779303/) [4] [Kang HM, Zaitlen NA, Wade CM, et al. Efficient Control of Population Structure in Model Organism Association Mapping. *Genetics* (2008).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2278096/)

The paper’s transcriptomic conclusions about heritable DEGs and downstream enrichment/predictability are not reproducible as written because the manuscript specifies an adjusted-P and |log2FC| threshold that differs from the code’s actual filtering (raw p-values and a different fold-change cutoff), which can substantially change DEG sets and any claims derived from them. “the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were filtered by | log2FoldChange | >1.5 and adjusted \( P < 0.05 \).” This sentence specifies a stringent DEG definition using multiple-testing-adjusted P-values and a relatively high fold-change cutoff. Because the manuscript’s downstream results depend on the number of DEGs, the number of “heritable DEGs,” and overlap-based enrichment analyses, the exact DEG calling criterion is central to the validity of conclusions. If the analysis actually used raw P-values rather than adjusted P-values, the DEG list could contain many more false positives, especially across many genes and contrasts. Likewise, changing the fold-change threshold from 1.5 to 1 would materially increase the number of called DEGs and could inflate the number of genes appearing “heritable” across generations by chance. The paper does not provide sensitivity analyses showing robustness to these threshold choices. Therefore, inconsistency at this step compromises reproducibility of the gene-expression claims. ```r for (m in dds.name) { dds.result <- results(dds2, name=m) resOrdered <- dds.result[order(dds.result$pvalue),] resSig <- subset(resOrdered,pvalue<0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1) k=gsub('treat_','',m) k=gsub('_vs_control','',k) write.csv(as.data.frame(resOrdered),file=paste0(i,'/DEG/',i,'.',j,'.',k,'.all.csv')) write.csv(as.data.frame(resSig),file=paste0(i,'/DEG/',i,'.',j,'.',k,'.remove.csv')) } ``` The code applies a raw `pvalue < 0.05` threshold and `abs(log2FoldChange) > 1`, which directly contradicts the manuscript’s stated `adjusted P < 0.05` and `|log2FoldChange| > 1.5` criteria. This is not a cosmetic discrepancy: DEG lists can change dramatically when switching from adjusted to unadjusted P-values in RNA-seq, and those lists are then re-used to define “heritable DEGs” and to drive functional enrichment and TE analyses. Because the manuscript reports large ranges for numbers of DEGs and heritable DEGs (and interprets these counts biologically), readers must be able to reproduce exactly which genes were called. The mismatch also raises uncertainty about which pipeline generated the figures and extended-data results described in the manuscript. If the published results were produced with different thresholds than those described, then the reported statistics (e.g., counts and overlaps) are not verifiable from the provided code. This is a major reproducibility gap affecting key conclusions. “Rather, 0.013 is the expected proportion of false positives incurred if we call MSH2 significant.” [[2]](http://engr.case.edu/ray_soumya/mlrg/Storey03.pdf) This sentence explains why using unadjusted thresholds in large-scale testing problems changes interpretation: calling items “significant” carries an expected false-positive burden that must be managed explicitly. In RNA-seq, where thousands of genes are tested per contrast, DEG calling conventionally relies on multiple-testing-adjusted measures specifically to control false discoveries. The manuscript claims to use adjusted P-values, which would align with this principle, but the code uses raw p-values, which does not. Because the paper’s “heritable DEG” definition requires significance in multiple generations, uncorrected p-values can still generate false persistence when the same genes are repeatedly tested. This makes any downstream biological interpretation of DEG sets and their enrichment sensitive to the mismatch. The quotation supports why the code–methods discrepancy is not just a documentation error but a validity risk. References: [2] [Storey JD, Tibshirani R. Statistical significance for genomewide studies. *PNAS* (2003).](http://engr.case.edu/ray_soumya/mlrg/Storey03.pdf)

The paper’s mechanistic inference that transposons are enriched around repeatedly induced (“susceptible”) genes is not robust because it relies on Col-0/TAIR10 reference gene and TE annotations despite known TE presence/absence variation across accessions, which can bias overlap/enrichment estimates when applied to non-reference genotypes. “we identified all heritable DEGs shared between genotypes or treatments (Extended Data Fig. 10a) and found that the abundance of members of several superfamilies, including Gypsy, HAT, Mariner, and Helitron, upstream and body of these susceptible genes was significantly higher than that of background genes (Extended Data Fig. 10b).” This statement ties a central mechanistic claim (“nonrandom” genomic features) to an enrichment test that depends on accurate TE annotations and gene coordinates for the studied genotypes. However, the experiment uses multiple Arabidopsis accessions, and TE landscapes are known to vary substantially among accessions, so “upstream TE abundance” is genotype-dependent. If the enrichment is computed using a single reference annotation, it can misrepresent the true promoter and gene-body TE content in non-reference genomes. Because the paper interprets the enrichment as evidence that transposons mediate susceptibility, any systematic reference bias can create or erase apparent enrichment. The manuscript does not demonstrate that the same enrichment holds when using accession-specific TE calls or assemblies. Therefore, the mechanistic interpretation rests on an analysis that is potentially biased by construction. “We adopted the transposon annotation of TAIR10… Notably, our study adopted Col-0 as the reference, and genotype-specific genome assembly is necessary for a comprehensive understanding of this nonrandom phenomenon.” This passage acknowledges that the analysis is performed in a reference coordinate system and that genotype-specific assemblies would be needed for a comprehensive interpretation. Given that the paper’s discussion elevates transposons to a “central role” and uses these enrichment results to motivate mechanistic explanations, this limitation is not peripheral—it directly affects the key mechanistic claim. If TE insertions/deletions or structural variants differ across accessions, overlap-based enrichment using a reference may systematically miscount TEs in promoters and gene bodies for many genotypes. The analysis therefore risks conflating reference-annotation completeness with biology, especially since the manuscript elsewhere notes that Col-0 has “biased (much more detailed) annotation of transposons.” Without repeating the enrichment with genotype-specific TE maps, the strength of the inference remains uncertain. This weakens confidence in the claimed genomic-feature nonrandomness. “Variation in the presence or absence of transposable elements (TEs) is a major source of genetic variation between individuals.” [[5]](https://elifesciences.org/articles/20777) This quote establishes that TE presence/absence variation is pervasive and materially contributes to between-individual genomic differences in Arabidopsis. If TE content varies widely across accessions, then any enrichment analysis that uses a single reference TE annotation risks mischaracterizing TE neighborhoods for non-reference genotypes. The paper under review examines multiple accessions and uses TE-related features to explain susceptibility, so the existence of widespread TE PAV is directly relevant. A robust enrichment claim would ideally be evaluated using accession-specific TE calls (or at least sensitivity analyses) rather than relying on TAIR10 alone. The external evidence therefore supports why a reference-only TE enrichment test may not be sufficient. This is especially important when the enriched TE classes are used to motivate mechanistic pathways of induction. “It is challenging to identify the structural changes in the genome caused by TE mobilization using current short-read sequencing technologies as these reads are typically mapped to a reference genome, which has the effect of masking structural changes that may be present.” [[5]](https://elifesciences.org/articles/20777) This sentence highlights a key technical limitation: mapping and annotation relative to a reference can mask structural variation such as TE insertions/deletions. The paper’s enrichment analysis is performed relative to TAIR10/Col-0 and then interpreted as reflecting genomic features of “susceptible” genes across genotypes. If structural differences are masked, then the computed TE overlaps around genes can be systematically biased toward the reference genome’s TE landscape. This bias is not merely theoretical; it is a known limitation in TE-variant discovery and interpretation in Arabidopsis populations. Because the manuscript’s central mechanistic conclusion depends on TE abundance estimates, the analysis needs to explicitly address this limitation empirically. The quoted literature supports that reference-based approaches can miss exactly the kinds of TE differences the manuscript invokes. “we found that, at heterozygous SNPs, there was a significant bias toward higher mapping rates of the allele in the reference sequence, compared with the alternative allele.” [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2788925/) Although this quote concerns SNPs and allele-specific mapping, the broader point is that reference-based mapping can introduce systematic bias that favors the reference sequence. In the present manuscript, both gene expression quantification and TE overlap analyses rely on mapping reads and features to a single reference genome (TAIR10/Col-0). Sequence divergence and structural differences among accessions can therefore alter mapping rates and apparent signal, potentially affecting which genes are called differentially expressed and which regions appear TE-rich. This is relevant because the enrichment test is performed on the set of “heritable DEGs,” which itself depends on mapping and DEG calling. If reference bias influences DEG selection, it can indirectly influence TE enrichment as well. The external evidence supports why reference-based analyses across divergent genotypes require careful bias mitigation and validation. References: [5] [Stuart T, Eichten SR, Cahn J, et al. Population scale mapping of transposable element diversity reveals links to gene regulation and epigenomic variation. *eLife* (2016).](https://elifesciences.org/articles/20777) [6] [Degner JF, Marioni JC, Pai AA, et al. Effect of read-mapping biases on detecting allele-specific expression from RNA-sequencing data. *Bioinformatics* (2009).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2788925/)

The paper fails to correct for multiple comparisons in the analysis of phenotypic changes, leading to a high likelihood of false positives and inflating the perceived prevalence of "heritable" variations. ```r # Create a binary variable indicating whether Pvalue < 0.05 ef.size$value_sig <- ifelse(ef.size$Pvalue < 0.05, 1, 0) ``` This code snippet from `glm.R` demonstrates that the authors define a significant effect based on a raw P-value threshold of 0.05. Given that the study involves 14 genotypes, 10 treatments, 4 generations, and 5 traits (totaling thousands of statistical tests), using an uncorrected P-value will result in a large number of false positives by chance alone. Standard statistical practice requires correcting for the False Discovery Rate (FDR) using methods like Benjamini-Hochberg [1] when performing such a large number of simultaneous tests. ```r Calculate_ef_size <- function(matr, treat_list, gen_list, trait_list) { result_list <- lapply(trait_list, function(trait) { trait_results <- lapply(treat_list, function(treat) { # ... (looping through treatments) result <- lapply(gen_list, function(gen) { # ... (looping through generations) mod <- lmer(formu, na.action = na.omit, data = matr.sub[matr.sub$Generation == gen, ]) coef_summary <- coef(summary(mod)) data.frame(treat = treat, gen = gen, trait = trait, coef_summary) }) # ... }) # ... }) # ... } ``` This code from `Effect_size.R` shows that the authors fit separate linear mixed models for each combination of trait, treatment, and generation, and extract the raw P-values (`coef(summary(mod))`) without any adjustment for the multiplicity of testing. The subsequent analysis of "occurrence frequency" and "susceptibility" relies entirely on these uncorrected P-values, rendering the conclusions about the prevalence and predictability of heritable changes statistically invalid. "The statistics showed that the genotype, environmental treatment and their interaction significantly affected all phenotypes measured (except for the interaction effect on flowering time, Supplementary Table 2)." This statement in the Results section implies robust statistical significance, but the underlying methodology reveals that these "significant" effects are likely inflated by the lack of multiple testing correction. Without proper FDR control, it is impossible to distinguish true biological signals from stochastic noise, undermining the central claim that environment-induced heritable variations are "common". References: [1] Benjamini, Y., & Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the false discovery rate: a practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. Journal of the Royal statistical society: series B (Methodological), 57(1), 289-300.

The code used for differential expression analysis contradicts the Methods section and uses invalid statistical criteria (unadjusted P-values), rendering the gene expression results and subsequent transposon enrichment analysis unreliable. ```r resSig <- subset(resOrdered,pvalue<0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1) ``` This line from `transcriptom analysis.R` shows that the authors filter differentially expressed genes (DEGs) using a raw `pvalue < 0.05` and a log2 fold change greater than 1. In RNA-seq analysis, it is standard practice to use adjusted P-values (FDR) to control for the thousands of gene tests performed [2]. Using unadjusted P-values will yield a massive number of false positive DEGs, which invalidates the claim that "heritable gene expression changes were common". "The expression matrix was normalized by function vst in package “DESeq2”. The difference in gene expression was estimated with function DESeq, and the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were filtered by | log2FoldChange | >1.5 and adjusted \( P < 0.05 \)." This sentence from the Methods section explicitly claims that an adjusted P-value threshold and a stricter fold change cutoff (>1.5, approx. 2.8-fold) were used. The discrepancy between the described methodology and the actual code (unadjusted P < 0.05, >2-fold change) is a serious issue that misrepresents the rigor of the analysis and casts doubt on the validity of all downstream analyses, including the functional enrichment and transposon correlation results. References: [2] Love, M. I., Huber, W., & Anders, S. (2014). Moderated estimation of fold change and dispersion for RNA-seq data with DESeq2. Genome biology, 15(12), 550. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13059-014-0550-8

The definition of "heritable" as changes occurring in at least two out of four generations is insufficiently rigorous and likely captures stochastic variation rather than stable inheritance. "Importantly, a substantial proportion (36.8% 10.4%, and 7.5%) of these changes occurred in two, three, and four offspring generations, suggesting that these environment-induced changes were heritable." The authors classify any change that is significant in at least two generations as "heritable". However, true transgenerational inheritance typically implies stable transmission across generations or a consistent decay. A change that appears in F1, disappears in F2, and reappears in F3 (which would count as "heritable" under their definition) is more likely due to experimental noise or stochastic plasticity than stable epigenetic inheritance. Only 7.5% of changes persisted across all four generations, suggesting that stable inheritance is actually rare, contradicting the paper's title and main conclusion. "In summary, parental environmental effects are common and often strong in A. thaliana, but they are genotype-dependent and difficult to predict." [3] Previous work by Latzel et al. (2023) found that while parental effects are common, they are unpredictable and often unstable. The current paper's loose definition of heritability inflates the apparent stability of these effects, potentially masking the stochastic nature of the phenomenon observed in other studies. References: [3] Latzel, V., Fischer, M., Groot, M. P., Giseke, D., & van Kleunen, M. (2023). Parental environmental effects are common and strong, but unpredictable, in Arabidopsis thaliana. New Phytologist, 237(3), 1014-1023. https://doi.org/10.1111/nph.18573

The failure of the controlled experiment (Test II) to improve the reproducibility of effect sizes compared to the heterogeneous experiment (Test I) suggests that the observed variations are likely due to stochastic noise or experimental error rather than robust biological effects. "Surprisingly, the repeatability of the effect sizes between generations in Test II did not increase compared to that in Test I (Fig. 3d, Extended Data Fig. 4a)." The authors designed Test II specifically to eliminate environmental heterogeneity between generations, which they hypothesized was the cause of variation in Test I. The fact that reproducibility did not improve implies that the variation is intrinsic or due to measurement error, rather than environmental noise. This finding undermines the claim that these are robust, biologically meaningful heritable changes, and instead points towards stochasticity or experimental artifacts. "The plot showed considerable variation in the magnitude and direction of the phenotypic changes." The observation that phenotypic changes vary in both magnitude and direction across generations and genotypes further supports the idea that these are not stable, adaptive responses. If the direction of the effect flips between generations (e.g., larger plants in F1, smaller in F2), it is difficult to argue for a consistent heritable mechanism or adaptive value.

The correlation between transposon abundance and "susceptibility" is based on a small sample size and potentially spurious metrics derived from uncorrected P-values, and lacks direct mechanistic evidence. "Intriguingly, the susceptibility of a genotype to environmental inductions could be predicted based on the transposon abundance in the genome." This correlation is based on a sample size of only 14 genotypes (or 10 in Test II). With such a small sample size and high-dimensional genomic data (multiple transposon superfamilies), high correlations can easily arise by chance or be driven by outliers. Furthermore, the "susceptibility" metric itself is derived from the "occurrence frequency" of significant changes, which, as noted in Item 1, is flawed due to the lack of multiple testing correction. If the "susceptibility" scores are largely noise, any correlation with genomic features is likely spurious. "Our study... suggest that the environment can broadly contribute to the generation of heritable variation... transposons play a central role". The paper claims a central mechanistic role for transposons but provides only correlational evidence based on baseline transposon abundance. There is no direct evidence presented (e.g., bisulfite sequencing of treated lines, transposon mobilization assays) to show that transposons are actually moving or changing their methylation state in response to the environmental treatments in the offspring generations. Without such mechanistic validation, the link remains speculative.

Provide more detailed information (maybe I missed that) on the transposon occupancy in different cultivars, by type and genomic size. Show correlation for other transposons, for which the correlation was not as convincing.
“..The frequency of occurrence of fruit number was…” – delete “number”; is it actually just seed amount? “the frequency of occurrence of fruit” sounds weird – sounds like some plants had no fruits.
“the number of genes showing heritable expression changes can be predicted by the abundance of transposons in the genomes.” – I would use the word “correlate”
“It was not very clear why you grown it for 7 generations and tested after 4th.”
Please elaborate on pooling F1 – F4 seeds together for the Test II. Seeds from how many plants were pooled? By weight or by number?
A better Discussion about the role of transposons in the evolution is needed; there are reports demonstrating that transposons are activated by stress and genomic changes prevail in transposons.

By such paper, the authors show that different types of environmental stress are capable to induce heritable phenotypic variation. Interestingly, it has also shown that such heritable changes are related to the abundance of transposons. Such data are interesting and significant together with other previous data, obtained on different organisms, that are changing the classical view on the environment as a major player only in selecting heritable changes. The very interesting point is that transposable elements are significant mediators in heritable changes induce by environmental stress. Such notion has been experimentally explored also in animal organisms. Among the several papers on such topic, I suggest to read some the following ones just to have a more complete view about the mechanisms involved on heritable changes produced by the environment through the activation of transposable elements: Specchia V., L. Piacentini, P. Tritto, L. Fanti, R. D’Alessandro, G. Palumbo, S. Pimpinelli and MP. Bozzetti (2010). HSP90 prevents phenotypic variation by suppressing the mutagenic activity of transposons. Nature 463: 662-665. Piacentini L., Fanti L., Specchia V., Bozzetti MP., Berloco M., Palumbo G., Pimpinelli S. (2014) Transposons environmental changes and heritable induced phenotypic variability. Chromosoma. 123(4):345-54. Fanti L, Piacentini L, Cappucci U, Casale AM, Pimpinelli S. (2017) Canalization by Selection of de Novo Induced Mutations. Genetics. 206(4):1995-2006. Cappucci U, Noro F, Casale AM, Fanti L, Berloco M, Alagia AA, Grassi L, Le Pera L, Piacentini L, Pimpinelli S. (2019) The Hsp70 chaperone is a major player in stress-induced transposable element activation. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A. 116(36):17943-17950. Pimpinelli S, Piacentini L. (2020) Environmental change and the evolution of genomes: Transposable elements as translators of phenotype plasticity into genotypic variability. Functional Ecology 34 (2), 426-441.

The paper claims to discover 130,657 novel deletions and 197,008 novel duplications not present in the existing 1000g-SV catalogue, with high validation rates (0.87 and 0.85 respectively). However, the validation methodology is not clearly described, and the validation appears to be performed on the same 34 HGSVC2 samples used for training the machine learning model, potentially creating circularity in the evaluation. "Figure 7: Comparison between the 1000g-SV and SurVIndel2 datasets... Between parentheses, the validation rates of calls in samples with long reads." (Figure caption, line 139) The figure caption indicates that validation rates are computed only from "samples with long reads," which are the 34 HGSVC2 samples plus HG002. The paper does not clarify how many of the 130,657 and 197,008 unique variants actually fall within these 35 samples or how representative this subset is of the full 3,202-sample catalogue. "The machine learning model of SurVIndel2 was trained using the 34 samples in HGSVC2 sampled by the NYGC." (Methods, line 104) The same samples used for training the random forest classifier are also used for validation. While leave-one-out cross-validation addresses sample-level independence, variants at the same genomic loci appear across multiple samples, potentially creating data leakage. Additionally, the validation rates reported for novel calls are based on this same benchmark, creating potential circularity. "Furthermore, SurVIndel2 predicts 130,657 deletions not present in 1000g-SV, with high validation rate (0.87)." (Results, line 144) The paper does not explain how a validation rate was computed for these 130,657 deletions. If the validation uses the HGSVC2 benchmark as a reference, the variants would need to exist in the benchmark to be validated. Truly novel variants by definition would not exist in HGSVC2, raising questions about what exactly is being measured. Literature on SV validation emphasizes that "[l]ong read SV calls are ideal for confirming SV events with accurate breakpoint resolution" [[1]](#citation-list), but the paper does not describe whether re-alignment of long reads or de novo calling was performed for validation. References: [1] [All of Us Short-Read Structural Variant Quality Report - Research All of Us](https://support.researchallofus.org/hc/en-us/articles/27496716922900-All-of-Us-Short-Read-Structural-Variant-Quality-Report-Archived-C2022Q4R9-offcycle-CDRv7)

The random forest classifier was trained exclusively on samples sequenced by a single center (NYGC) using a single platform (NovaSeq 6000) at similar coverage (~30x), yet the paper claims the model generalizes well based on testing with only one sample (HG002) sequenced under different conditions. "The datasets used for training the model are very homogeneous: they were all sequenced by one sequencing center (NYGC), with the same platform (Illumina NovaSeq 6000), with the same sequencing depth (30x, except for one which was 60x), which can lead to overfitting." (Results, line 110) The authors acknowledge this limitation themselves. Sequencing center effects, platform-specific error profiles, and coverage-dependent features could all be learned by the random forest, potentially limiting generalization to data from other sources. "However, the HG002 sample confirms that this is not the case for our model. HG002 was sequenced by a different entity (GIAB Consortium) using a different platform (Illumina HiSeq 2500) and its depth is different (50×)." (Results, line 110-112) Relying on a single sample to demonstrate generalization is insufficient. N=1 provides no estimate of variance or reliability across different sequencing conditions. Machine learning literature indicates that leave-one-out cross-validation can exhibit distributional bias [[2]](#citation-list), and demonstrating generalization typically requires held-out test sets that are truly independent and representative. "The filtering model we provide was trained on the CNVs called from the 34 human samples. We call this the default model. For this reason, we are interested in (i) how SurVIndel2 performs on different organisms using the default model..." (Section 2.4, line 124) The authors test cross-organism generalization extensively (Arabidopsis, cattle, mouse, rice) but do not similarly test cross-platform or cross-center generalization within humans. Given that the tool is intended for use on diverse human datasets, this represents a gap in the evaluation. References: [2] [Distributional bias compromises leave-one-out cross-validation - arXiv](https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.01652)

The paper uses a random forest classifier with approximately 50 features but does not report feature importance analysis, making it unclear whether hidden split reads, statistical tests, or other features are the primary drivers of improved performance. * Quote (from code, features.py, lines 7-57): ```python feature_names = [ 'svtype', 'source', 'svlen', 'median_depths[0]', ... 'ks_pval', ... 'clipped_reads[0]', 'clipped_reads[1]', ... ] ``` The code reveals that approximately 50 features are used, including depth-based features, discordant pair features, Kolmogorov-Smirnov p-values, clipped read counts, and junction scores. Without feature importance analysis, readers cannot assess which features contribute most to classification decisions. "Additionally, we use the distribution of the insert sizes of the read pairs around the CNV to calculate a confidence interval for its size, as well as a p-value estimating the probability that the CNV is a false positive." (Methods, line 91) The KS test p-value is mentioned as a feature, but without feature importance analysis, it remains unclear whether this statistical feature contributes meaningfully to the classification, especially given that the statistical tests are not applied to duplications. "The second tests employs a Kolmogorov-Smirnoff test to calculate a p-value estimating whether C and D are significantly different." (Supplementary Methods, line 425) The statistical test features are described in detail, suggesting they are important to the methodology. However, for the random forest to learn useful patterns from these features, they must provide discriminatory power. Feature importance would reveal whether the claimed methodological innovations (hidden split reads, statistical testing) actually contribute to performance, or whether simpler features like depth or read counts dominate. This is a standard practice in machine learning-based bioinformatics tools [[3]](#citation-list). References: [1] [All of Us Short-Read Structural Variant Quality Report - Research All of Us](https://support.researchallofus.org/hc/en-us/articles/27496716922900-All-of-Us-Short-Read-Structural-Variant-Quality-Report-Archived-C2022Q4R9-offcycle-CDRv7) [3] [Understanding overfitting in random forest for probability estimation - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11437774/)

The paper compares SurVIndel2 primarily against Manta, Delly, Lumpy, and the original SurVIndel, but does not include more recent state-of-the-art methods such as DRAGEN (a widely-used commercial solution) in the main benchmarking, limiting the assessment of SurVIndel2's relative position in the field. "We use these datasets to benchmark the performance of SurVIndel2, and three highly cited methods that performed well in a recent benchmarking analysis [4]: Delly [19], Lumpy [12]... and Manta [5]." (Methods, line 102) While these methods are indeed widely cited, they are not necessarily the current state of the art. Recent benchmarking studies have shown that DRAGEN outperforms many open-source tools for SV detection [[4]](#citation-list), yet DRAGEN is not included in the main comparison. From the published Nature Communications version (not in the preprint, but from search results): "Furthermore, we compared SurVIndel2 to the SV calling module in DRAGEN... Our benchmarks demonstrate that although DRAGEN is a marked improvement over Manta, it does not achieve the same level of sensitivity and precision as SurVIndel2." [[5]](#citation-list) The published version includes DRAGEN comparison in supplementary materials, but the preprint under review does not include this comparison. For a comprehensive evaluation of the method's contribution, comparison against the current commercial state of the art should be presented. "DRAGEN also reports CNVs, which include larger deletions and duplications... For CNVs with lengths in the range of 1–5 kbp and 5–10 kbp, DRAGEN performed substantially better, with F-measures of 92.60%... and 96.60%..." [[6]](#citation-list) Recent literature demonstrates that DRAGEN achieves very high F-measures for CNV detection. Without direct comparison in the main results, readers cannot assess whether SurVIndel2's improvements represent a substantial advance over current best practices or are incremental gains over older methods. References: [4] [A Hitchhiker Guide to Structural Variant Calling - PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12383524/) [5] [SurVIndel2: improving copy number variant calling from next-generation sequencing using hidden split reads - Nature Communications](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-024-53087-7) [6] [Comprehensive genome analysis and variant detection at scale using DRAGEN - Nature Biotechnology](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41587-024-02382-1)

This paper’s conclusion that SurVIndel2 yields a substantially more complete 1000 Genomes CNV catalogue with “high validation rate” is insufficiently supported because the validation is reported only “in samples with long reads” that overlap the HGSVC2 cohort used to train and benchmark SurVIndel2’s filtering model, making the validation non-independent and potentially optimistic. “The machine learning model of SurVIndel2 was trained using the 34 samples in HGSVC2 sampled by the NYGC. For this reason, when testing them, we use a leave-one-out strategy.” Leave-one-out within the same 34-sample cohort reduces direct per-sample label leakage, but it does not provide an independent external validation of the learned filtering behavior. The training and evaluation data share the same sequencing center, platform, library preparation, and mapping pipeline characteristics, which the paper itself notes can create homogeneity and risk overfitting. When the same cohort is then used to compute “validation rates” for catalogue-level claims, the evaluation can become circular: the model is tuned to maximize agreement with long-read-derived calls in this cohort, and the reported validation is measured against those same long-read resources. This can inflate validation metrics relative to genuinely independent samples or platforms and makes it hard to extrapolate to the full 3,202-sample catalogue. A stronger design would validate the 1000 Genomes discoveries on long-read samples not used in model training/tuning, ideally sequenced and processed independently. Without that, “high validation rate” is not necessarily evidence of generalizable correctness. “Between parentheses, the validation rates of calls in samples with long reads.” This captioned evaluation explicitly restricts validation-rate reporting to a subset of samples with long-read data, rather than validating across the full cohort. If the long-read subset is largely the HGSVC2 set (as implied by the repeated use of HGSVC2 for benchmarking and validation throughout), then the validation statistic is computed on the same distribution used to build and evaluate the classifier. Even if many calls are rare, the selection of validated samples and genomic contexts can materially bias estimated validation upward or downward. For a population-scale callset claim, readers need to know how validation varies across populations, sequencing batches, and mapping contexts, not only within a curated long-read subset. The paper does not provide uncertainty estimates or stratified validation for the novel-event component (e.g., by repeat context, size, or allele frequency) in an independent validation cohort. As a result, the catalogue-level completeness claims are less robust than they appear. “Furthermore, SurVIndel2 predicts 130,657 deletions not present in 1000g-SV, with high validation rate (0.87). … SurVIndel2 predicts an additional 197,008 duplications, with high validation rate (0.85).” These large absolute numbers are a central conclusion of the paper, so the evidentiary standard should be particularly high. The validation rates are presented as though they generalize to the catalogue’s novel events, but the text ties validation to “samples with long reads,” which are a small fraction of the 3,202 genomes. If validation is computed only where long reads exist, it may systematically miss cohort-specific artifacts (e.g., platform- or mapping-related false positives) present elsewhere in the 3,202 sample set. Additionally, because the classifier was trained on the HGSVC2 cohort, agreement with long-read truth in those individuals is partly an expected consequence of the training objective, not an external check. Common practice in benchmarking emphasizes using test sets that were not used to train or optimize pipelines to avoid optimistic estimates of precision/recall. Without an external validation cohort, the conclusion that these hundreds of thousands of additional events are genuinely high-confidence remains insufficiently substantiated. “Additionally, the pipelines that generated the variant calls could be tuned to HG001, which, in many situations, was used to train or optimize pipelines.” ([[1]](#ref1)) This observation from benchmarking best practices highlights a well-known pitfall: performance estimates can be inflated when a benchmark sample (or closely related sample cohort) is used to tune or train methods. The same risk applies here at the cohort level: SurVIndel2’s filtering model is trained on HGSVC2 samples, and catalogue validation appears to be judged using long-read data from the same or closely related HGSVC2 cohort. Even with leave-one-out, repeated model development decisions (feature design, filter thresholds, and model structure) can be influenced by aggregate performance on the benchmark cohort. This makes catalogue-level validation less independent than readers might assume. Independent validation (different sequencing centers/platforms, different individuals with orthogonal long-read truth) is typically needed to establish that high validation rates are not a byproduct of benchmark-specific tuning. The paper’s current evidence does not clearly meet that standard for the “hundreds of thousands” novel-event claim. References: [1] [Krusche et al., “Best Practices for Benchmarking Germline Small Variant Calls in Human Genomes,” *Nature Biotechnology* (2019)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6699627/)

This paper’s cross-organism performance claims are weakened because its non-human “benchmark catalogues” are defined as Sniffles2 callsets on PacBio HiFi reads without demonstrating that these callsets constitute a high-confidence truth set with defined benchmark regions, which threatens the validity of sensitivity/precision comparisons. “We generated a benchmark dataset by using Sniffles2 [24] on PacBio HiFi reads for the same samples.” Running a single SV caller on long reads is a reasonable way to obtain candidate variants, but it is not equivalent to a benchmark truth set unless additional integration/adjudication steps are performed. Long-read callers—including state-of-the-art tools—still have non-trivial error modes in repetitive contexts, for duplications, and for complex events, and the paper does not describe any multi-method integration, manual curation, or orthogonal validation for these non-human truth sets. Without defining confident regions (where extra calls are presumed false positives) and without assessing false discovery in the “truth,” computed precision and sensitivity for short-read callers can be biased. In particular, methods that differ from Sniffles2’s representation, breakpoint placement, or SVTYPE conventions may be penalized even when they are correct, while methods that match Sniffles2 may appear better by construction. This issue is amplified when making broad claims that the default (human-trained) model “works well for every tested organism.” A more robust evaluation would use integrated truth sets (multiple callers/technologies) or assembly-based validation, and would specify benchmark regions where false positives can be identified reliably. “Benchmark catalogues were obtained by running Sniffles2 on PacBio HiFi reads.” This statement makes explicit that the “benchmark” is not an experimentally validated reference but the output of one algorithm. Because the same paper is comparing SurVIndel2 (a short-read caller) against other short-read callers, any incompleteness or bias in Sniffles2’s callset will propagate into all reported metrics and may reverse method rankings in certain contexts. For example, if Sniffles2 under-calls certain tandem duplications or has systematic breakpoint biases in repeats, then all tools will appear to have low sensitivity or precision in those categories irrespective of true performance. The paper does not report independent verification of Sniffles2’s correctness in these organism-specific benchmarks, nor does it quantify uncertainty due to truth-set error. As a result, the conclusion that SurVIndel2 “performs better for every organism” is not adequately supported by a benchmark of known accuracy.
