The quoted sentences show that voiding efficacy and sensing repeatability are assessed under physiological conditions, which directly tests the implant-environment coupling that benchtop balloons omit. The contrast matters because the reviewed paper makes similarly application-level claims about bladder monitoring and closed-loop treatment but presents a balloon-based validation instead. This does not mean the reviewed approach is invalid, but it shows the current evidence is not at the same validation level as closely related peer-reviewed demonstrations. The comparison therefore supports the critique that the reviewed paper’s bladder-implant conclusions are premature. A more rigorous validation path (in vivo or at least ex vivo) is necessary for comparable claims. Here, we report that GI parasite–inspired active mechanochemical therapeutic grippers, or theragrippers, can reside within the GI tract of live animals for 24 hours by autonomously latching onto the mucosal tissue. ([4](#ref4)) This literature illustrates that residence claims in the GI tract are typically validated by measuring retention time in live animals, because clearance by motility is the main failure mode. The reviewed manuscript uses similarly strong language (“prolonged residence in the stomach”) but does not present analogous retention measurements, instead emphasizing PBS immersion release results. The comparison highlights that “residing” is not only a design intent but an experimentally verifiable property requiring time-resolved in vivo evidence. Without such evidence, claims of prolonged gastric residence and associated drug delivery remain speculative. This directly challenges the robustness of the reviewed paper’s ingestible-robot conclusions. Aligning claim strength to the validation depth would materially improve the paper’s credibility.

This paper draws long-term biocompatibility and safety conclusions for implantable robots from limited short-duration assays and small-sample histology, which is insufficient to justify “long-term operation” and implant safety claims across the diverse device use-cases (thermal actuation, wireless power transfer, electrodes, and hydrogel adhesion). The as-fabricated soft robot can form a highly conformal interface with diverse biological surfaces, indicating its inherent mechanical softness and high biocompatibility (Fig. 2 F, Fig. 2 G, and Fig. S2C-E). A conformal interface is an important mechanical design goal, but conformality alone does not establish biocompatibility, especially for chronically implanted devices. Biocompatibility depends on the material chemistry, leachables, mechanical wear, and the biological response over the intended exposure duration. This sentence explicitly uses interface conformality as evidence (“indicating”) of “high biocompatibility,” which is an inference not directly supported by the stated observation. In implantable-device evaluation, a conformal fit can coexist with adverse foreign body response, fibrosis, or tissue injury from thermal/electromagnetic exposure. The paper’s use of “high biocompatibility” at this stage therefore over-interprets what the cited evidence can show. Stronger evidence (endpoint-specific testing and longer follow-up) is needed to justify the conclusion. Here, mouse 3T3-J-2 cells exposed to complete soft robotic devices with constituent materials including pure PNIPAM hydrogel and functional nanocomposites (e.g., AgNWs, RGO, PEDOT:PSS & MXene), remain robust healthy and maintain stable viability (Fig. 6 A, Fig. 6 B, Fig. S53 & Fig. S54) [108,109]. Additionally, histological analysis reveals that the soft robots implanted inside chest chamber of mice induce no observable inflammation or other adverse effects to surrounding tissues, indicating good biocompatibility for long-term operation (Fig. S55 & Fig. S56). The in vitro cell exposure and a limited in vivo implantation histology are useful screening steps, but they do not, on their own, justify a general conclusion of “long-term operation” safety for diverse implant configurations and functions. The cell study duration is short (the supplementary captions specify 48 hours), which cannot capture chronic inflammatory processes, fibrotic encapsulation, or degradation-driven toxicity. The in vivo histology is described qualitatively (“no observable inflammation”), without specifying the full endpoint set needed for the various tissue contacts and exposure modes the paper proposes. The statement also jumps from local observations in a mouse chest chamber to “long-term operation” broadly, which is a stronger claim than the evidence supports. For devices that include wireless power transfer and electrothermal actuation, safety must consider not only material toxicity but also temperature rise and electromagnetic exposure. Therefore, the conclusion is overstated relative to the duration and scope of testing shown. Fig. S53. Cell viability assessment of soft robotic implants. (A-H) Confocal microscope images of 3T3-J-2 cells before (control) and after exposure to various functional nanocomposites, including PNIPAM hydrogel, AgNWs, RGO, PEDOT:PSS and MXene-based composites, as well as as-prepared soft robotic devices integrated with an e-skin layer and PNIPAM hydrogel-muscle layer, incubated for 48 hours. This caption confirms that the main cell viability evidence is a 48-hour exposure window, which is short relative to typical “long-term” implant claims. Many clinically relevant failure modes (e.g., delamination, leachables accumulating, mechanical fatigue, and chronic immune response) manifest over weeks to months, not 48 hours. Short-term viability also does not guarantee that devices remain stable and non-toxic under repeated actuation cycles or under physiological protein adsorption and biofouling. Moreover, viability assays do not address organ-specific risks such as hemocompatibility for cardiovascular use or mucosal irritation for GI use. As a result, these data are best interpreted as preliminary screening rather than evidence supporting long-term implant biocompatibility. The manuscript should therefore temper its long-term safety claims or add longer-term, endpoint-specific evidence. Fig. S55. Histological analysis (H&E staining) of biological tissues after thermal stimulation using a robotic implant. Histological analysis of lung tissue (A-C), heart tissue (D-F), spleen tissue (G-I), and liver tissue (J-L) after thermal stimulation at a power of 0.1 W, with durations of half an hour and one hour, respectively. (H&E staining, N=3). This experiment probes acute tissue response to a specific thermal stimulation regime (0.1 W for 0.5–1 hour), but it does not establish safety across the operating envelope described elsewhere (e.g., ~1 W harvested power for actuation) or under repeated/chronic exposure. The sample size is small (N=3) and the endpoint is limited to H&E morphology, which may miss subtle immune activation, fibrosis, or functional impairment. Acute histology after short stimulation also cannot address cumulative thermal damage or immunological response to recurrent heating. For an implantable robotic platform, the intended exposure may be prolonged and intermittent over days to months, so acute tests are necessary but not sufficient. Additionally, the experiment does not explicitly connect to the device’s most demanding conditions (wireless actuation and in-tissue operation). Therefore, these data cannot support the broader conclusion of long-term safety. Fig. S56. Histological analysis (H&E staining) of the cardiac tissue after implantation of a hydrogel-based thera-gripper. (A) Day 1. (B) Day 3. (C) Day 7. (D) Day 14. (H&E staining, N=3). A 14-day follow-up provides more relevant evidence than acute studies, but it remains limited for “long-term operation” claims, particularly for chronic implants where foreign body response and encapsulation can evolve over longer periods. The endpoint is still limited to H&E staining, and the sample size remains small (N=3), which limits confidence and generalizability. The caption does not indicate whether actuation, pacing, or wireless power transfer occurred chronically during the implantation period, which would be critical for assessing real-use safety. Furthermore, a thera-gripper implanted in the chest chamber does not necessarily generalize to other anatomical sites or to GI mucosal contact or vascular contact use-cases. Thus, while suggestive of short-term tolerability, these data do not justify broad long-term biocompatibility conclusions for the platform. More comprehensive, function-under-use and longer-duration evaluations would be needed. An assessment of potential biocompatibility risk should include not only chemical toxicity, but also physical characteristics that might contribute to an unwanted tissue response. These characteristics can include surface properties, forces on surrounding tissue (e.g., mechanical, thermal, electromagnetic), geometry, and presence of particulates, among others. ([1](#ref1)) FDA’s guidance explicitly notes that biocompatibility risk is not limited to chemical toxicity and that physical characteristics—including thermal and electromagnetic forces—can drive unwanted tissue responses. This is directly relevant because the reviewed platform relies on electrothermal actuation and RF wireless power transfer, both of which impose thermal and electromagnetic stresses on surrounding tissue. The manuscript does not present a structured evaluation of these risks under realistic implant conditions (e.g., tissue temperature rise maps, induced-current considerations, or failure-mode analysis). Instead, it largely extrapolates safety from short-term cell viability and limited histology, which do not address these physical-hazard mechanisms. Because thermal/electromagnetic hazards are central to the device’s mode of operation, omitting endpoint-specific evaluation weakens the validity of the safety conclusion. The paper should either provide such analyses or substantially narrow its safety claims. ISO 10993-1 helps determine whether a device’s materials are safe for their intended use, considering both composition and exposure duration. ([5](#ref5)) This ISO overview statement captures a core principle of medical-device biological evaluation: exposure duration is a central determinant of what evidence is required. The reviewed paper frequently uses language consistent with long-duration implantation (e.g., “long-term operation,” “prolonged residence”), but the presented testing is mostly short-term and does not clearly match to intended exposure durations per use-case. When exposure duration changes (e.g., transient vs. long-term), the required endpoints and confidence level change substantially. Therefore, citing limited short-term tests as support for long-term claims is inconsistent with standard biocompatibility evaluation practice. This reinforces the critique that the manuscript’s long-term safety conclusions are overstated. A clearer mapping from intended use to duration-dependent evaluation would be required for robust claims.

This paper claims the wireless power transfer and electrothermal actuation strategy is safe for implant use (minimal heat damage and reduced tissue damage/infection), but it does not present tissue-relevant dosimetry and thermal safety evidence, and its simulations use non-physiologic material assumptions. Wireless operation of actuation as well as therapeutic and/or diagnostic functions is essential for implantable robots to minimize tissue damage and implant infection [67,69–71]. However, existing schemes for wireless operation of robots mostly require sophisticated circuitry for energy harvesting and storage, which may introduce non-negligible heat (~ 80°C), unfavored space occupation, system complexity, limited lifetimes, and high cost [72,73]. These sentences motivate the proposed wireless approach partly on safety grounds, implying reduced tissue damage/infection compared to alternatives. Yet the manuscript does not provide a direct safety comparison (e.g., temperature profiles in tissue, thermal dose, or adverse event rates) to substantiate this claim. Raising the concern of “~80°C” heat in prior schemes heightens the expectation that the current method will be evaluated against clinically relevant thermal injury thresholds. Without tissue-relevant measurements, readers cannot assess whether the proposed system truly avoids hazardous heating, especially when delivering substantial power to heaters. Moreover, implant infection risk is not solely a function of tethering; it depends on surgical approach, encapsulation, and chronic tissue response, none of which are quantitatively addressed here. Therefore, the safety rationale is not convincingly supported by corresponding evidence. This undermines validity for the safety-related conclusions. Figure 4 I shows an infrared image of the robotic finger highlighting concentrated heat on the electrical heater and minimum heat on the receiving inductor, which collectively ensures a stable power supply and minimum heat damage to surrounding bio-environments in potential usage as implants. Figure 4 K shows the output power as a function of frequency. The wireless power transfer system achieves the highest harvested power ~ 1.05 W at a frequency of 15 MHz. The paper infers “minimum heat damage” from an infrared image and coil/heater temperature distribution, but IR thermography typically measures surface temperatures in air and does not substitute for temperature rise in perfused tissue. The harvested power level (~1.05 W at 15 MHz) is substantial for an implantable heater, and safety depends on how much heat is deposited into surrounding tissue and for how long, not merely that the receiving inductor itself is cooler. Additionally, “minimum heat damage” is a biological claim that requires either histology/function endpoints under the relevant operating conditions or a validated thermal/dosimetric model in tissue. The evidence presented here does not quantify tissue temperature rise, spatial thermal gradients in vivo, or exposure duration under closed-loop operation. Without those, the conclusion that the method avoids heat damage in “bio-environments” is not supported. This leaves a critical safety and feasibility gap for implant translation. The default conductivity, permittivity, and susceptibility of the copper and air derived directly from the materials library of the software were used in the simulations. This sentence indicates that the electromagnetic simulations for power transfer are performed with copper and air as the primary material environment, rather than tissue-representative dielectric and conductive properties. In implant use, surrounding media are conductive, lossy, and heterogeneous (skin, fat, muscle, blood), and these properties strongly affect coupling efficiency, induced currents, and heating. Modeling in air may therefore overestimate performance and underestimate tissue heating risks, which are central to safety claims. Because the paper uses simulation results to argue about parameter effects and “evading possible risks to living organisms,” the underlying material assumptions become a validity-critical methodological issue. The absence of tissue-equivalent modeling or validation in phantoms makes the translation of these results uncertain. Consequently, the claims about safe WPT behavior in vivo are insufficiently supported. From a health risk perspective, we are generally interested in how much EMF power is absorbed by biological tissues, as this is largely responsible for the heating effects described above. This is typically described as a function of a relevant dosimetric quantity. For example, below about 6 GHz, where EMFs penetrate deep into tissue (and thus require depth to be considered), it is useful to describe this in terms of “specific energy absorption rate” (SAR), which is the power absorbed per unit mass (W kg−1). ([2](#ref2)) ICNIRP’s guidelines explicitly identify heating as the substantiated health-relevant effect of RF exposure and emphasize dosimetric quantities like SAR for assessing absorbed power in tissue. This is directly relevant to a 15 MHz (i.e., well below 6 GHz) wireless power system intended to operate near or in the body, where tissue penetration and bulk heating can occur. The reviewed paper does not report SAR estimates, tissue temperature rise modeling, or compliance to RF exposure limits for the operating regime and geometry. Instead, it argues safety primarily from coil/heater IR observations and air-based simulation assumptions. Given ICNIRP’s emphasis on tissue heating and SAR as a measurement framework, the omission of dosimetry leaves the safety claim unsupported by standard practice. Therefore, the manuscript’s assertions about “minimum heat damage” in implant usage are not adequately justified.

This paper’s claim that the robotic implant quantifies cardiac contractility is not convincingly supported because the reported measurements are strain-sensor readouts without sufficient calibration to established cardiac physiology metrics (and the manuscript sometimes interprets strain amplitude as myocardial strength/force). Demonstrations range from a robotic cuff encircling a blood vessel for detecting blood pressure, to a robotic gripper holding onto a bladder for tracking bladder volume, an ingestible robot residing inside stomach for pH sensing and on-site drug delivery, and a robotic patch wrapping onto a beating heart for quantifying cardiac contractility, temperature and applying cardiac pacing, highlighting the application versatilities and potentials of the nature-inspired soft robots. This sentence explicitly claims “quantifying cardiac contractility” as a demonstration outcome, not just measuring strain or ECG. However, the later results primarily describe strain sensor signals on different cardiac chambers and interpret amplitude changes as “contractility patterns” and “myocardium strength.” Contractility in cardiovascular physiology is a loaded concept, often distinguished from deformation/strain and requiring careful control of loading conditions. Because the paper frames this as a key demonstration, the burden is on the manuscript to show how its sensor outputs map quantitatively to recognized indices of contractile function. Without a clear calibration/validation framework, the conclusion risks over-interpreting what the strain sensors actually measure. This weakens the reliability of the contractility-related conclusions. Figure 6 M& N show the contractility patterns of the right and left atria (RA and LA), and the right and left ventricles (RV and LV) under normal and MI conditions, respectively, measured by an implanted thera-gripper where the output features of the sensors are determined by their experienced strain that significantly correlates to their positions on epicardial surface. The S4 (LV) displays the largest amplitude due to a maximum experienced strain, indicating the intrinsic highest myocardium strength in the LV chamber. Figure 6 I reveals the area of infarction two weeks after the MI surgery. The results in Fig. 6 N demonstrate that the LV outflow obstruction changes patterns of ventricular contraction. The sensors S2 (RV) and S4 (LV) experience a reduced strain change due to the consequent loss of contractile myocardium, which, correspondingly, reduces force of myocardial contractility and decreases heart rate. The quoted text equates measured strain patterns to “contractility patterns” and further interprets larger strain amplitude as higher “myocardium strength,” then infers reduced “force of myocardial contractility” from reduced strain change. Strain is a deformation measure that depends on both intrinsic myocardial properties and loading/boundary conditions; without calibration, strain amplitude alone is not a direct force/contractility measurement. The manuscript does not present a quantitative mapping from sensor strain signals to pressure-based or load-independent indices (e.g., pressure-volume analysis) that would justify the force/strength interpretation. While it references echocardiographic fractional shortening changes, it does not report a direct correlation between its strain-sensor outputs and validated contractile metrics under controlled conditions. This leaves room for alternative explanations (changes in contact mechanics, sensor placement, or loading) for amplitude differences. Consequently, the contractility conclusions are not as robust as the language implies. With increasing consideration for rigor and reproducibility, the goal of this guidelines review is to provide best-practice information regarding how to measure accurately cardiac physiology in animal models. ([6](#ref6)) This guideline underscores that cardiac physiology measurement requires rigor and best practices, which is relevant when a new sensing modality claims to quantify contractility. The reviewed manuscript introduces a novel strain-sensing implant, but it does not provide the kind of validation detail (calibration, controls for anesthesia/heart rate/loading, and cross-method comparisons) that best-practice discussions emphasize. Because “contractility” is sensitive to measurement conditions, a high bar for validation is typical when proposing new implantable measurement approaches. The guideline thus supports the critique that stronger methodological grounding is necessary before asserting quantification of contractility, rather than deformation proxies. The paper’s current evidence is promising for strain sensing but does not meet the validation expectation implied by its contractility claims. This affects the robustness of the conclusions. Dimensions and FS (these indexes can be reported but are fraught with error in this model), volumes, EF (if possible, 3D reconstruction), wall thickness ([6](#ref6)) This guideline text specifically cautions that dimension-based indexes like fractional shortening (FS) are “fraught with error” in myocardial infarction models, which are central to the reviewed paper’s MI demonstration. The reviewed manuscript uses FS decrease (75–15%) and echocardiographic strain imaging as contextual evidence of dysfunction, and then interprets its strain-sensor signals as contractility/force changes. If the contextual reference metrics are themselves error-prone in this model, that raises the need for careful cross-validation and robust measurement protocols when drawing contractility inferences. The paper does not show that its sensor-derived “contractility patterns” track a gold-standard or less load-dependent measure in MI (e.g., pressure-volume loops). Therefore, the guideline reinforces that the manuscript should be more conservative in interpreting MI-related contractility from strain readouts. This weakens confidence in the contractility-specific interpretation.

The proposed actuation mechanism requires high power (~1 W) and elevated temperatures (40°C), which poses a severe risk of thermal damage to surrounding tissues, violating safety standards for implants. "The PNIPAM hydrogel can undergo a notable volumetric change about 90% as the temperature change from 23°C to 40°C" This indicates that the device must be heated to 40°C to achieve full actuation. Given that the normal body temperature is 37°C, the margin for safety is extremely narrow (only 3°C). Local tissue heating to 40°C or slightly above can cause thermal damage or necrosis, especially if sustained for prolonged periods as required for drug delivery or monitoring. "The saturation temperature of AgNW/PI nanocomposite heater increases with the supplied power as more Joule heat is generated, and the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of PNIPAM hydrogel at 34°C can be obtained at low input power (< ~1W)" The statement implies that up to 1 W of power is required to reach the actuation temperature. Dissipating 1 W of power as heat within a small implantable device inside the body is highly unsafe. For context, safety standards for active implantable medical devices (e.g., ISO 14708-1) typically limit the temperature rise of the outer surface to 2°C above body temperature, which corresponds to much lower power dissipation (typically in the milliwatt range) [1]. "The wireless power transfer system achieves the highest harvested power ~ 1.05 W... which is sufficient to trigger the heater" This confirms that the system is designed to handle and dissipate around 1 W of power. Such high power dissipation in a confined space will inevitably lead to significant local heating of the surrounding tissue, posing a severe risk of thermal injury [2]. References: [1] ISO 14708-1:2014 Implants for surgery — Active implantable medical devices — Part 1: General requirements for safety, marking and for information to be provided by the manufacturer. https://www.iso.org/standard/61603.html [2] Yarmolenko, P. S., Moon, E. J., Landon, C., Manzoor, A., Hochman, D. W., Viglianti, B. L., & Dewhirst, M. W. (2011). Thresholds for thermal damage to normal tissues: an update. International Journal of Hyperthermia, 27(4), 320-343. https://doi.org/10.3109/02656736.2010.534527

The reported wireless power transfer efficiency of 80% is unrealistically high for an implantable system and likely represents an idealized benchtop measurement rather than a practical in vivo scenario. "the power efficiency reaches around 80%, which is sufficient to trigger the heater for raising local temperature to drive motions of the soft robot" Achieving 80% power transfer efficiency for an implantable inductive link is exceptionally rare and contradicts established literature. Typical efficiencies for implantable wireless power transfer systems are significantly lower (often below 50%) due to factors such as low coupling coefficients, tissue absorption, and misalignment between coils [3]. The paper likely reports the coil-to-coil efficiency in air under ideal alignment, which is misleading for an implantable application where the receiver is embedded in lossy tissue and subject to movement. "Fig. 4 K and Fig. 4 O exhibit a reduction in transmission efficiency resulting from decreased mutual inductance between the transmission and the receiving coils due to shape deformation." While the authors acknowledge a reduction in efficiency due to deformation, the baseline claim of 80% remains unsubstantiated for a realistic in vivo setup. Without specifying the conditions (e.g., distance, medium) under which this efficiency was measured, the claim exaggerates the system's performance and feasibility for deep-tissue implants [4]. References: [3] Zargham, M., & Gulak, P. G. (2012). Maximum achievable efficiency in near-field coupled power-transfer systems. IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Circuits and Systems, 6(3), 228-245. https://doi.org/10.1109/TBCAS.2011.2174794 [4] Ho, J. S., Kim, S., & Poon, A. S. (2013). Midfield wireless powering for implantable systems. Proceedings of the IEEE, 101(6), 1369-1378. https://doi.org/10.1109/JPROC.2013.2251851

The reliance on thermal actuation of bulk PNIPAM hydrogels inherently results in slow response times, which contradicts the paper's claims of "real-time" control and "spatiotemporal precision" for applications like cardiac pacing or bladder voiding. "The PNIPAM hydrogel can undergo a notable volumetric change about 90% as the temperature change from 23°C to 40°C" The volume change of bulk PNIPAM hydrogels is governed by the diffusion of water molecules, which is a slow process. While the paper claims "spatiotemporal precision," the response time for such bulk hydrogels is typically in the range of seconds to minutes, not milliseconds [5]. This slow response makes the device unsuitable for applications requiring rapid actuation, such as cardiac pacing or reacting to sudden changes in blood pressure. "Fig. S18C, the flexible electrical heater exhibits steady heating and cooling cycles" Although the figure is not provided here, the description of "steady heating and cooling cycles" implies a relatively slow thermal time constant. The cooling phase, in particular, relies on passive heat dissipation to the surrounding tissue, which is inherently slow and uncontrollable. This limits the frequency of actuation and the ability to perform rapid, repeated movements required for dynamic physiological environments [6]. References: [5] Schild, H. G. (1992). Poly (N-isopropylacrylamide): experiment, theory and application. Progress in Polymer Science, 17(2), 163-249. https://doi.org/10.1016/0079-6700(92)90023-R [6] Zhang, X. Z., Xu, X. D., Cheng, S. X., & Zhuo, R. X. (2008). Strategies to improve the response rate of thermosensitive PNIPAAm hydrogels. Soft Matter, 4(3), 385-391. https://doi.org/10.1039/B713803M

While the paper claims biocompatibility, the use of Silver Nanowires (AgNWs) and PNIPAM raises long-term safety concerns due to potential leakage and cytotoxicity, which are not adequately addressed by the short-term experiments. "mouse 3T3-J-2 cells exposed to complete soft robotic devices... remain robust healthy and maintain stable viability" The biocompatibility tests presented are short-term (likely days to weeks) and do not account for the long-term effects of implantation. Silver nanowires (AgNWs) are known to be cytotoxic and can cause oxidative stress if released into the body [7]. "curing process fully buries all the nanomaterials inside the PI matrix... This prevents separation of the nanomaterials from the polymeric matrix" While the authors claim that the PI matrix prevents separation, the device is subjected to repeated mechanical deformation (bending, twisting, expanding). Over time, fatigue can cause micro-cracks in the PI matrix, leading to the leaching of AgNWs into the surrounding tissue. The paper does not provide long-term mechanical fatigue data combined with leaching tests to substantiate the safety of the encapsulation under chronic in vivo conditions [8]. References: [7] AshaRani, P. V., Mun, G. L. K., Hande, M. P., & Valiyaveettil, S. (2009). Cytotoxicity and genotoxicity of silver nanoparticles in human cells. ACS Nano, 3(2), 279-290. https://doi.org/10.1021/nn800596w [8] De Jong, W. H., & Borm, P. J. (2008). Drug delivery and nanoparticles: applications and hazards. International Journal of Nanomedicine, 3(2), 133. https://doi.org/10.2147/IJN.S596

Despite the claim of "softness" and "tissue-matching", the use of Polyimide (PI) as the matrix for the e-skin introduces a significant mechanical mismatch with biological tissues, potentially causing stress concentrations and tissue damage over time. "flexible nanocomposite layer... incorporated within a polymer matrix (e.g., polyimide)" Polyimide (PI) has a Young's modulus in the GPa range (~2.5 GPa), which is orders of magnitude higher than that of soft biological tissues (kPa range) [9]. Even if the PI layer is thin and flexible, its high intrinsic stiffness creates a mechanical mismatch at the tissue-device interface. "The as-fabricated soft robot can form a highly conformal interface with diverse biological surfaces, indicating its inherent mechanical softness" The claim of "inherent mechanical softness" is misleading because it conflates structural compliance (bending stiffness) with material softness (Young's modulus). While the device can bend to conform to surfaces, the high modulus of the PI matrix means that any local deformation or friction can generate high stresses on the tissue, leading to inflammation or fibrosis over long-term implantation [10]. References: [9] Lacour, S. P., Courtine, G., & Guck, J. (2016). Materials and technologies for soft implantable neuroprostheses. Nature Reviews Materials, 1(10), 1-14. https://doi.org/10.1038/natrevmats.2016.63 [10] Minev, I. R., Musienko, P., Hirsch, A., Barraud, Q., Wenger, N., Moraud, E. M., ... & Lacour, S. P. (2015). Electronic dura mater for long-term multimodal neural interfaces. Science, 347(6218), 159-163. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1260318

For the LiH crystal, the paper uses a HF-based finite-size correction from a 2×2×2 to extrapolate thermodynamic properties, but performs the larger 3×3×3 calculation at only a single geometry (equilibrium lattice constant) and at the Γ-point only, without twist averaging. Standard practice in QMC studies of solids typically employs twist-averaged boundary conditions (TABC) for 3D systems to reduce finite-size errors, as the authors themselves use for graphene. "For further validation, we have also computed directly the 3 × 3 × 3 supercell of LiH at its equilibrium length 4.061Å, which contains 108 electrons." The 3×3×3 calculation is performed at only a single lattice constant, making it impossible to assess how the method performs across the equation of state for this larger system. The comparison "also very close to the experimental value −4.759 eV" relies heavily on the accuracy of the HF-based finite-size correction rather than demonstrating convergence with system size through twist averaging as done for graphene. "The calculations are performed on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error." The paper applies TABC for graphene but not for LiH, despite LiH being a 3D system where finite-size errors are typically more severe. A recent transferable neural wavefunction study [[2]](#citation-2) demonstrated that for LiH specifically, joint optimization across multiple twists and system sizes significantly reduces computational cost while improving accuracy. The absence of TABC for LiH in this work represents an inconsistency in methodology across systems. "Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a 2 × 2 × 2 supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell" The finite-size correction methodology relies on HF calculations rather than using neural network calculations at multiple supercell sizes to extrapolate to the thermodynamic limit. While this is computationally pragmatic, it introduces uncertainty about how much of the reported cohesive energy accuracy is attributable to the neural network versus the HF correction. Standard QMC practice often involves calculating multiple system sizes to directly quantify and extrapolate finite-size effects [[3]](#citation-3). References: [2] Gerard, L., Scherbela, M., Sutterud, H., Foulkes, W. M. C. & Grohs, P. Transferable neural wavefunctions for solids. [Nat. Comput. Sci. 5, 1147–1157 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00872-z). [3] Chiesa, S., Ceperley, D. M., Martin, R. M. & Holzmann, M. Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions. [Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006)](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404).

The paper does not provide clear information about the computational resources required (GPU hours, wall-clock time) for the calculations presented, making it difficult to assess the practical applicability of the method and compare with other approaches. Given that subsequent work has noted the high computational cost of the approach, this omission is significant for reproducibility and assessment of the method's practical value. "Concrete code of this work is developed on Github at https://github.com/bytedance/DeepSolid." While code availability is commendable, the paper lacks quantitative information about computational resources needed for each system studied. The acknowledgments thank "ByteDance AML team specially for their technical and computing support," suggesting substantial computational resources were used, but these are not quantified in the paper or supplementary materials. * Quote from Supplementary Table 2: "Homogeneous electron gas: (256, 32), 3 layers, 1 determinant, Batch size 4096, Training steps 3e5" While hyperparameters are provided, the actual wall-clock time and hardware used are not specified. A subsequent study [[2]](#citation-2) noted that "DeepSolid required over 80,000 GPU hours to study a single system," highlighting the significant computational burden that is not explicitly communicated in the original paper. This information is critical for readers to assess the practical utility of the method. "All the expectation values for distribution |Ψ|² are evaluated via the Monte Carlo approach, and then the energy and wavefunction is optimized using the modified KFAC optimizer" The KFAC optimizer and 200,000-400,000 training steps combined with batch sizes of 4096-8192 imply substantial computational requirements. Without explicit reporting of GPU hours or comparison to training costs of alternative methods, readers cannot properly evaluate the cost-accuracy tradeoff of this approach relative to alternatives such as traditional QMC or other neural network architectures. References: [2] Gerard, L., Scherbela, M., Sutterud, H., Foulkes, W. M. C. & Grohs, P. Transferable neural wavefunctions for solids. [Nat. Comput. Sci. 5, 1147–1157 (2025)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43588-025-00872-z).

The paper reports statistical uncertainties for energy values using parenthetical notation (e.g., "-0.551677(1)"), but does not discuss the variance of the local energy, which is an important metric for assessing wavefunction quality in variational Monte Carlo. High variance can indicate poor optimization or wavefunction quality issues that are not captured by the mean energy alone. From Supplementary Table 3: "-0.551677(1)" for H10 chain energy The statistical uncertainties reported are very small (on the order of 10⁻⁶ Hartree), but without reporting the variance of the local energy, it is unclear whether these small error bars reflect a truly converged high-quality wavefunction or simply long sampling runs. The zero-variance principle states that the exact ground state wavefunction has zero variance in the local energy; reporting variance would provide important information about wavefunction quality. "Besides energy minimization, stochastic reconfiguration optimization [37] has also been widely adopted and proved to be much more efficient" While stochastic reconfiguration is mentioned as the optimization method, there is no discussion of convergence metrics beyond energy. In neural network VMC studies, reporting both the mean local energy and its variance (or the ratio of variance reduction compared to Hartree-Fock) is standard practice to demonstrate wavefunction quality [[4]](#citation-4), [[5]](#citation-5). The absence of variance reporting makes it difficult to assess whether the wavefunction has reached a high-quality local minimum. "Training curves of the 2 × 2 × 2 LiH crystal is plotted in Supplementary Fig. 4." The supplementary figures show training curves for energy convergence but do not include variance plots. Standard practice in neural network QMC papers typically includes both energy and variance convergence plots to demonstrate wavefunction quality. For instance, the FermiNet paper [[4]](#citation-4) explicitly discusses variance reduction as a measure of wavefunction quality. This omission makes it harder to fully assess the quality of the converged wavefunctions. References: [4] Pfau, D., Spencer, J. S., Matthews, A. G. D. G. & Foulkes, W. M. C. Ab initio solution of the many-electron Schrödinger equation with deep neural networks. [Phys. Rev. Res. 2, 033429 (2020)](https://journals.aps.org/prresearch/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevResearch.2.033429). [5] Hermann, J., Schätzle, Z. & Noé, F. Deep-neural-network solution of the electronic Schrödinger equation. [Nat. Chem. 12, 891–897 (2020)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41557-020-0544-y).

The manuscript’s headline accuracy claims for *cohesive energies* (graphene and LiH) are not fully supported because the key isolated-atom reference energies are imported from an external neural-QMC work rather than computed within the paper’s own method/code, which undermines attribution of the reported agreement to the proposed periodic ansatz and weakens reproducibility of the cohesive-energy results. “Graphene is arguably the most famous two-dimensional system (Fig. 2c) receiving broad attention in the past two decades for its mechanical, electronic, and chemical applications [24]. Here we carry out simulations to estimate its cohesive energy, which measures the strength of C-C chemical bonding and long-range dispersion interactions. The calculations are performed on a \( 2 \times 2 \) supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error. The calculated results are plotted in Fig. 2d along with the experimental value [21], and it shows that our neural network can deal with graphene very well, producing a cohesive energy of graphene within 0.1 eV/atom to the experimental reference (see Supplementary Table 6). We also plotted the results with PBC, namely the Γ point only result, which deviates from the experiment data by 1.25 eV/atom.” This is a strong quantitative conclusion (“within 0.1 eV/atom”) that depends on how the cohesive energy is constructed, not just on the bulk/supercell total energy. Cohesive energies are differences between large total energies, so the atomic reference must be consistent and clearly attributable to the proposed method to justify the claim. Here, the paper presents the cohesive-energy agreement as evidence that “our neural network can deal with graphene very well,” which implicitly attributes the result to the proposed periodic ansatz. If the atomic reference energy is taken from a different method/pipeline, the claim no longer isolates the contribution of the proposed approach. The reader cannot tell whether the agreement is driven primarily by the graphene calculation, the external atomic reference, or cancellation between them. This weakens the inferential link from the graphene cohesive-energy number to the paper’s core methodological contribution. “The final results are listed in Supplementary Table 6. The energy of an isolated carbon atom is taken from Ref. [2], \( E = -37.84471 \) Hartree.” This sentence explicitly confirms that a crucial input to the graphene cohesive energy (the isolated-atom energy) is not produced by the present work’s workflow, but copied from another paper. While using an external benchmark value can be reasonable for contextual comparison, it materially changes what is being validated: the reported cohesive energy is no longer an end-to-end result of the proposed periodic neural network ansatz. Because the paper’s conclusion is phrased as the method “producing” an accurate cohesive energy, the computational provenance of all terms in the energy difference matters. In particular, any mismatch in Hamiltonian conventions, numerical settings, or physical corrections between the external atom calculation and the present periodic calculation would directly propagate into the cohesive energy. The manuscript does not provide a justification that such mismatches are negligible at the claimed 0.1 eV/atom level. “The energy of \(2 \times 2 \times 2\) LiH is listed in Supplementary Table 8. The energy of an isolated lithium atom is taken from Ref. [2], \(E = -7.47798\) Hartree.” The same pattern occurs for LiH: a cohesive-energy-related reference (the isolated Li atom) is imported from an external work rather than computed with the proposed approach. This makes it difficult to assess the method’s capability on an equal footing, because the cohesive energy is sensitive to the accuracy of *both* the bulk and atomic calculations. Importing atomic energies also impedes reproducibility: a reader re-running the provided DeepSolid configurations would not reproduce the reported cohesive energies without separately sourcing the same atomic numbers from elsewhere. More importantly, the manuscript uses the LiH cohesive-energy agreement as a validation highlight (“very close to the experimental value”), but that agreement is partly contingent on a value computed outside the presented methodology. The paper should either compute these atomic references with the same code path or clearly reframe the claim as “bulk energies + external atomic benchmarks,” not as an end-to-end cohesive-energy prediction. “C -37.84471(5)” [[1]](#ref1) This entry from FermiNet’s Table I matches the carbon atomic energy reused by the reviewed manuscript for its graphene cohesive-energy calculation. It demonstrates that a key term in the reported cohesive energy comes from an external neural-network VMC workflow rather than from the reviewed paper’s periodic-solid method. That matters because it changes what is being validated: the cohesive-energy agreement partially inherits accuracy from a separate implementation with its own training, sampling, and numerical choices. Even if the external atomic energy is highly accurate, the resulting cohesive energy is not an end-to-end prediction attributable solely to the proposed periodic ansatz. In addition, any mismatch in physical conventions or numerical corrections between the two workflows would directly enter the energy difference. Therefore, the cohesive-energy benchmark provides weaker evidence for the proposed method than implied by the manuscript’s interpretation. “The determination of the cohesive energy is obtained as being the difference between the energy of the isolated nickel and that of the bul.k per unit atom.” [[4]](#ref4) This sentence states the standard definition of cohesive energy as a difference between an isolated-atom energy and a bulk per-atom energy, highlighting that the atomic reference is an essential part of the reported quantity. Because cohesive energies are differences between large numbers, careful control of consistency between the atomic and bulk computations is needed to avoid spurious agreement. In the reviewed manuscript, the isolated-atom terms are not computed within the same reported workflow, so the cohesive energies are not fully “closed” results of the proposed method. The citation also underscores why importing atomic energies is not a minor bookkeeping choice: it can dominate the error budget for the derived quantity. Therefore, presenting cohesive-energy agreement as a primary accuracy demonstration requires more methodological closure than the paper currently provides. This gap materially weakens the strength of the paper’s validation evidence. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf) [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [4] E. H. Megchiche, C. Mijoule, M. Amarouche, and J.-C. Barthelat. *First-principles calculations of the vacancy formation energy in nickel* (Algerian Journal of Advanced Materials, 2006). [https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document](https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document)

The finite-size control for key solid benchmarks (graphene and LiH) is not validated via systematic convergence studies (supercell size and twist/k-point sampling), yet the manuscript draws strong conclusions about near-experimental accuracy; this is a major methodological gap because residual finite-size errors in Coulomb systems can be comparable to (or larger than) the claimed error bars. “The calculations are performed on a \( 2 \times 2 \) supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error.” This sentence shows that the graphene cohesive-energy claim is based on a single, small supercell (2×2) augmented by correction schemes. For graphene-like systems, finite-size effects can be subtle because the system is not a simple gapped insulator and long-range Coulomb correlations remain important. Merely stating that TABC and an S(k) correction are used does not establish that the residual finite-size bias is below 0.1 eV/atom. The manuscript does not report a convergence check with respect to supercell size (e.g., 2×2 vs 3×3 vs 4×4) or twist-mesh density. Without such a study, the magnitude of the remaining finite-size error is unknown, making the subsequent accuracy conclusion fragile. Given that the main comparative statement is “within 0.1 eV/atom,” the absence of a demonstrated finite-size error bound is a significant methodological omission. “A 3×3 Monkhorst-Pack mesh in the first Brillouin zone of the supercell reciprocal space with Γ point centered is used to approximate the twist average integral, which reads” This indicates that twist averaging is approximated with a modest 3×3 mesh for the supercell. For high-accuracy claims, the adequacy of the twist mesh should be justified by explicit convergence tests, because twist integration error is a controlled numerical approximation. The supplement gives a specific quadrature rule, but does not report how the result changes with a denser mesh (e.g., 4×4 or 6×6) or with different symmetry handling. In addition, for quantities like cohesive energy, the twist-integration error propagates into an energy difference, which may amplify small biases. Without reporting the mesh-convergence behavior, it is not possible to know whether the reported “finite-size error corrected” cohesive energy is stable at the claimed accuracy. This undermines the credibility of the graphene validation as presented. “Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a \( 2 \times 2 \times 2 \) supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell (see Supplementary Fig. 5).” This sentence shows that LiH results are primarily based on a 2×2×2 QMC supercell, with an a posteriori Hartree–Fock-based correction rather than an explicit many-body size extrapolation. In Coulomb systems, the leading finite-size effects include both single-particle shell effects and many-body long-range correlation contributions; HF corrections address only part of the problem. The paper does not show QMC energies for multiple LiH supercell sizes (beyond a single 3×3×3 point at one lattice constant) to empirically demonstrate the corrected equation-of-state curve is converged. Because the Birch–Murnaghan fit and derived properties (bulk modulus, cohesive energy) are sensitive to small systematic shifts, the lack of a multi-size convergence assessment is a substantial gap. As a result, the reported agreement with experimental thermodynamic quantities cannot be robustly attributed to the method rather than finite-size modeling choices. “Twist averaging serves to make the decay more regular but does not reduce its overall magnitude that is determined by a charge interacting with the correlation hole of its own image in the nearby supercell.” [[2]](#ref2) This statement from the foundational TABC literature emphasizes that twist averaging primarily regularizes shell effects but does not, by itself, eliminate the dominant long-range Coulomb finite-size contributions. This is directly relevant because the reviewed manuscript leans on TABC (graphene) and HF-based corrections (LiH) to claim near-experimental accuracy from small supercells. The quote highlights that additional many-body finite-size corrections (beyond twist averaging) are typically required to control the Coulomb-image/correlation-hole contributions. While the manuscript mentions an S(k) correction for graphene, it does not provide a quantitative residual-error analysis or show that the correction is sufficient for the claimed accuracy. Therefore, the literature suggests that the paper’s finite-size treatment may be incomplete without further evidence. This undermines the strength of the conclusions drawn from the finite supercell calculations. “Finite-size corrections, both on kinetic and potential energy, can be calculated within a single simulation.” [[3]](#ref3) This quote reflects a well-established viewpoint in QMC finite-size analysis: both kinetic and potential energy contributions can require explicit many-body finite-size corrections grounded in long-wavelength physics. The reviewed manuscript does include some correction ideas (TABC, structure factor correction, HF empirical correction), but it does not demonstrate that these corrections collectively bound residual finite-size error below the levels needed for its strongest claims. In particular, for LiH the manuscript emphasizes HF-based correction, which is not the same as a many-body correction of both kinetic and potential contributions. For graphene, the small twist grid and single supercell size leave open whether both classes of finite-size errors are controlled. The quoted literature makes clear that robust finite-size handling is a central methodological requirement, not an optional detail. Without convergence evidence, the manuscript’s accuracy claims remain under-justified. References: [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [3] S. Chiesa, D. M. Ceperley, R. M. Martin, and M. Holzmann. *Finite-Size Error in Many-Body Simulations with Long-Range Interactions* (Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404). [https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404)

The manuscript reports many high-precision quantitative outcomes (e.g., “within 0.1 eV/atom,” “error of less than 1%”) without clearly defining statistical uncertainties, their estimators, or how uncertainties propagate through derived metrics like cohesive energies and “correlation error,” which weakens the reliability of the conclusions. “Supplementary Figure 2 | \(2 \times 2\) Graphene training curve. For clarity, at each iteration number, we show the median energy per primitive cell over the last 10% of iteration.” Reporting a “median … over the last 10%” is a visualization choice that does not, by itself, provide a statistically interpretable uncertainty estimate for the final energies. Medians suppress outliers but do not quantify sampling variance, autocorrelation effects, or residual optimization drift, all of which are central in VMC-style estimations. Because the paper’s main claims hinge on small energy differences (sub-eV per atom) and on “outperforming” baselines, the uncertainty model needs to be explicit and standard. The paper does not accompany these median curves with defined error bars or confidence intervals. Without an explicit estimator (e.g., mean ± standard error with reblocking), a reader cannot assess whether apparent improvements exceed stochastic noise. This directly weakens confidence in the reported accuracy margins. ```html -76.15588(6) -76.24949(5) -76.26314(5) ``` The parenthetical notation strongly suggests an uncertainty is being reported, but the manuscript does not define what “(6)” or “(5)” means (e.g., one standard error of the mean, one standard deviation, or an error from fitting). In QMC practice, different conventions exist, and the distinction matters when comparing to claimed sub-0.1 eV/atom accuracy. Moreover, twist averaging and structure-factor corrections introduce additional sources of uncertainty and bias beyond the raw Monte Carlo sampling noise. The table as presented also does not state whether these uncertainties are computed after equilibration, reblocking, or accounting for autocorrelation. Without an explicit definition, the reported precision cannot be reliably interpreted. This is a significant issue because the paper repeatedly uses precise numerical comparisons to support strong conclusions. “Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11).” This claim depends on a “correlation error” metric defined using multiple energies (HF, a DMC reference, and the network’s estimate). Such derived ratios can be sensitive to small denominators (e.g., at high density where correlation energies are small) and to uncertainties in each contributing term. The manuscript does not provide a propagation-of-uncertainty analysis for this percentage, nor does it state whether the “<1%” bound holds within statistical confidence. If the underlying energies have correlated Monte Carlo noise (common in reusing walkers, or in shared correction schemes), naive error reporting can be misleading. In addition, finite-size and fixed-node biases can dominate over sampling error for some regimes, but these are not separated. As a result, the “<1%” statement is hard to interpret as a robust quantitative guarantee. This weakens the reliability of the conclusions drawn from the HEG comparison. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` After network optimization, we run O(105) MCMC steps and calculate the mean local energy every 10 steps. The energy and associated standard error are estimated using a standard approach to account for correlations. 60 ``` This quote exemplifies common practice in neural-network VMC papers: energies are reported with a clearly stated “standard error” estimate that accounts for correlated samples. The reviewed manuscript does not provide a comparable definition of its parenthetical uncertainties, nor does it state how autocorrelation is handled in its estimates. Because QMC sampling is correlated by construction, omission of correlation-aware error estimation can materially understate uncertainty. The quote is not presented to require identical methods, but to illustrate the field’s baseline expectation for uncertainty reporting when making strong quantitative claims. Without similarly explicit uncertainty definitions, the reviewed paper’s precision claims are less credible. This affects both interpretability and reproducibility. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)

The manuscript’s formal description of the solid wavefunction ansatz is internally inconsistent between the main-text expression and the provided algorithm, making it difficult to verify correctness (e.g., spin structure and determinant factorization) and undermining reproducibility of the central methodological contribution. “\(\Psi(\mathbf{r}) = \mathrm{Det}_1[e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r}_1}u_{mol}^\uparrow(d)]\mathrm{Det}_1[e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r}_1}u_{mol}^\downarrow(d)] .\)” This equation indicates a product of (at least) two determinants associated with spin-up and spin-down channels, aligning with standard QMC antisymmetry enforcement. However, the notation is ambiguous (both are labeled “Det\_1”), and it does not clearly specify whether the determinants are block-diagonalized by spin or full determinants over all electrons. In periodic systems with complex-valued orbitals, these details matter for both the correctness of antisymmetry constraints and the implementation of phase factors. Because the method’s novelty is in extending molecular neural wavefunctions to periodic boundary conditions, the wavefunction definition must be unambiguous. Any inconsistency in determinant structure directly affects the interpretation of how fermionic antisymmetry is ensured. This ambiguity is magnified because subsequent algorithmic details are presented as a concrete implementation. “28: \( \Psi = \mathrm{Det}[\phi^\dagger] \mathrm{Det}[\phi^\dagger] \)” The algorithmic line shown here appears to form the wavefunction by multiplying two identical determinants, without specifying distinct spin channels. Taken literally, this differs from the main-text description of separate spin-up and spin-down determinants and would be conceptually incorrect for a typical spin-separated ansatz. Even if this is intended as shorthand, the manuscript does not clarify how spin is represented in \(\phi\) or why the product involves two identical terms. Since Algorithm 1 is presented as the “overall sketch of the neural network,” readers should be able to map it directly to an implementable procedure. Internal inconsistencies in the method specification impede verification by other groups and weaken confidence that the written method matches what was executed. Given that the wavefunction definition is the core of the paper, this is a significant reproducibility and correctness concern. ```python def logdet_matmul(xs: Sequence[jnp.ndarray], w: Optional[jnp.ndarray] = None) -> jnp.ndarray: """Combines determinants and takes dot product with weights in log-domain. Args: xs: FermiNet orbitals in each determinant. Either of length 1 with shape (ndet, nelectron, nelectron) (full_det=True) or length 2 with shapes (ndet, nalpha, nalpha) and (ndet, nbeta, nbeta) (full_det=False, determinants are factorised into block-diagonals for each spin channel). Returns: sum_i w_i D_i in the log domain, where w_i is the weight of D_i, the i-th determinant (or product of the i-th determinant in each spin channel, if full_det is not used). """ ``` The released implementation explicitly distinguishes two cases: a single full determinant over all electrons or a product of spin-channel determinants when factorized. This illustrates that the determinant factorization and spin handling are subtle and explicitly parameterized in code. When contrasted with the manuscript’s Algorithm 1 line that multiplies two identical determinants, it reinforces that the paper’s written description does not clearly convey the actual determinant structure needed for correctness. Moreover, because the paper positions its architecture as an extension of FermiNet to periodic systems, readers expect consistency between described mathematical form and code-level semantics. If the manuscript does not precisely describe whether it uses “full_det” or spin-block determinants in each experiment, reproducing and comparing results becomes harder. This is not merely a presentation issue: determinant structure can change expressivity, optimization behavior, and even numerical stability. The mismatch therefore undermines the clarity and verifiability of the central methodological claim. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` Eq. 7 uses the fact that the full determinant det[ Φ] = det [ φi(xj; {x/j}) ] may be replaced by a product of spin- up and spin-down terms if we choose φi(xj; {x/j}) = 0 if i∈1 ...n ↑and j ∈n↑+ 1,...,n or i∈n↑+ 1,...,n and j ∈1,...,n ↑. ``` This quote from FermiNet (which the reviewed manuscript cites as a base architecture) provides a clear, standard statement of the spin-factorized determinant product used to enforce fermionic antisymmetry. Because the reviewed paper claims to “represent the molecular networks with FermiNet” and extend them to periodic systems, consistency with the baseline formulation is important for interpretability. The contrast between this explicit spin-factorized form and the reviewed paper’s ambiguous/duplicated determinant notation highlights a real specification problem. Readers cannot confidently determine whether the paper’s ansatz is a direct periodic analogue of the FermiNet factorization or something else. This matters when assessing novelty, correctness, and comparability to baselines. The paper should resolve this inconsistency by clearly defining the determinant blocks and spin treatment in the main text. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)

The manuscript’s computational claims (notably multi-node GPU parallelization and “highly efficient” training) are not reproducible from the provided code as released, because the code explicitly lacks multi-node support and hard-pins an outdated JAX stack without an executable, versioned environment specification, making independent replication of the reported large-scale results impractical. “Calculations are also ensured by efficient and massive parallelization on multiple nodes of high-performance GPUs.” This sentence frames multi-node, large-scale GPU parallelization as a key enabler of the method’s reported results and scale (e.g., 108-electron LiH). When such computational infrastructure is central to feasibility, reproducibility requires that the released code supports the same execution mode or that the paper provides a faithful reproduction recipe (container, scripts, and system requirements). Otherwise, readers cannot distinguish between an algorithmic advance and an implementation/hardware advantage that cannot be replicated. The paper also uses strong scale-related language (“highly efficient,” “massive parallelization”), which invites scrutiny of whether the software artifact matches those capabilities. If the released code does not support multi-node, reproducing the reported wall-clock feasibility and scaling claims becomes difficult. This is a material reproducibility gap because it affects whether other groups can validate and extend the approach. “Present released code doesn't support multi-node training.” This directly contradicts the multi-node parallelization claim in the paper’s narrative and indicates that a key part of the computational setup used in the reported experiments is not available to replicators. Even if the algorithm itself is correct, lack of multi-node support can prevent reproducing the flagship calculations within reasonable resource limits, especially for larger electron counts. The manuscript does not provide an alternative route (e.g., smaller-scale scripts that reproduce at least one reported figure/table) demonstrating that the method can be validated without the unavailable infrastructure. As a result, the computational evidence in the paper becomes partially non-falsifiable for typical academic settings. This is not a minor engineering detail: for QMC with neural wavefunctions, compute scale can be the dominant feasibility constraint. Therefore, the mismatch undermines reproducibility and the strength of empirical validation. ```python REQUIRED_PACKAGES = ( "absl-py", 'attrs', "dataclasses", "networkx", "scipy==1.9.3", "numpy", "ordered-set", "typing", "chex==0.1.5", "jax==0.2.26", "jaxlib==0.1.75", "pandas", "ml_collections", "pyscf", "tables", "h5py==3.2.1", "optax==0.0.9", "opt_einsum==3.3.0", ) ``` This pinned dependency set hard-codes very old versions of core numerical libraries (notably JAX/JAXLIB), which is a common source of “bit-rot” and install failure in modern environments. The paper does not provide a container image, lockfile, or validated environment spec beyond this setup.py, so even obtaining a runnable installation can be difficult for reviewers and practitioners. This is especially problematic because the paper’s central claims depend on complex-number support, automatic differentiation, and performance-critical primitives whose APIs can change across versions. The pinned versions also diverge from the paper’s narrative of easy integration and practical adoption, because users must match an old stack precisely. A reproducible artifact typically includes an environment file and a tested installation path that is robust over time. Without that, the empirical claims are harder to verify independently. “Our experiments were carried out with `jax==0.2.26` and `jaxlib==0.1.75.`” This reinforces that the released code is tied to a narrow and dated software configuration, yet the manuscript does not provide a full specification of the broader environment (Python version, CUDA/cuDNN versions, compiler/toolchain) needed to reproduce GPU results. In high-performance neural QMC, such details often determine whether results and runtimes can be replicated. The absence of an end-to-end environment specification also makes it difficult to assess whether the reported improvements are robust or contingent on a specific, potentially non-portable stack. Moreover, the paper’s claims about being “easily integrated” into other neural structures are in tension with the rigidity of the pinned environment. Even if the algorithm is conceptually general, the artifact as released is not immediately reusable without significant engineering effort. This constitutes a substantive reproducibility concern for a methods paper.

Contrary to the claim that the method outperforms many traditional ab initio methods and reaches the level of the most accurate approaches, the reported results for the Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) and Hydrogen chain are consistently worse than or merely comparable to established Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) methods from over a decade ago. "the obtained results... outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches." (Abstract) This claim is contradicted by the paper's own data when compared to the cited benchmarks. "Supplementary Table 11 | Energy per electron of HEG... r_s=1 Net: 0.530019(1), BF-DMC: 0.52989(4)" For the Homogeneous Electron Gas, the proposed method ("Net") produces higher (worse) variational energies than the Backflow Diffusion Monte Carlo (BF-DMC) results from 2006 [4] for all reported densities ($r_s = 0.5$ to $20$). Since BF-DMC is a "traditional" QMC method, the claim of outperforming such methods is factually incorrect for this system. "Supplementary Table 3 | Energy of H_{10} chain... Bond length 1.8... Net: -0.572922(1), LR-DMC(LDA): -0.57304(1)" For the Hydrogen chain ($H_{10}$), the method's energy is slightly higher (worse) than the Lattice Regularized DMC (LR-DMC) result from the benchmark paper [1]. While the results are close, they do not "outperform" the established accurate approach. References: [1] M. Motta et al., "Towards the solution of the many-electron problem in real materials: Equation of state of the hydrogen chain with state-of-the-art many-body methods", Phys. Rev. X 7, 031059 (2017). https://journals.aps.org/prx/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevX.7.031059 [4] P. López Ríos et al., "Inhomogeneous backflow transformations in quantum Monte Carlo calculations", Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.74.066701

The use of a single $2 \times 2$ supercell for graphene is insufficient to capture long-range correlations and reach the thermodynamic limit, rendering the claim of "unprecedented accuracy" for this system unsupported. "The size of supercell is $2 \times 2$." (Supplementary Note 3.1) A $2 \times 2$ supercell contains only 8 carbon atoms. For a gapless semi-metal like graphene, finite-size effects are particularly severe. Relying on such a small system size without performing a finite-size extrapolation (as was done for the Hydrogen chain) fails to demonstrate that the method can accurately describe the bulk properties of graphene. "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions" [3] Accurate calculation of 2D materials requires careful extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit. The use of Twist Averaged Boundary Conditions (TABC) helps but does not eliminate the need for larger supercells to capture long-range correlation effects, which are crucial for the claim of "ab initio calculation of real solids". References: [3] S. Chiesa et al., "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions", Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404

The paper claims the method is "highly efficient" but provides no computational cost analysis (e.g., wall-clock time, memory usage, total FLOPs), while the reported hyperparameters suggest the method is computationally expensive. "based on which we develop a highly efficient QMC method" (Abstract) The term "highly efficient" implies a competitive computational cost relative to other methods. However, the paper provides no data to support this. Neural network ansatzes in QMC are generally known to be orders of magnitude more expensive than conventional Slater-Jastrow QMC or DFT. "Training steps 2e5... Batch size 4096" (Supplementary Table 1) The requirement of 200,000 training steps with a batch size of 4096 samples implies nearly a billion evaluations of the neural network. For a solid-state system with all-electron calculations (like LiH), this represents a massive computational burden. Without a cost comparison, the claim of "high efficiency" is misleading and likely incorrect compared to the "traditional methods" the paper claims to outperform.
The construction of the algorithm is not as novel as one would have expected, but the problem treated is very important, and the results are quite carefully done and presented.

Applying neural network for ab initio calculations of real solids has been already proposed by another group (https://doi.org/10.1038/s42005-021-00609-0). The research group has extended the restricted Boltzmann Machine ansatz to real periodic systems, although it employs the second quantization and finite basis approximation. The authors should mention this work in the introduction.

The authors states that the coupled cluster theory 'face severe difficulty when applied to solid systems.' Does this simply mean that the pre-factor of computational cost is high, or does it essentially mean that there is something wrong with its application to solids? Please clarify this point.

To my knowledge, Bloch functions are for one-electron states not for correlated many-body wavefunctions. Besides, the FermiNet employs the Generalized Slater determinants and its 'molecular orbitals' actually take into account electron correlations. The authors should prove that the application of the Bloch function form is not an approximation (if it is an approximation, please explicitly mention that). Unless this is a very trivial matter, the authors should state the proof in the Appendix or Supplementary Information.
KFAC and the mechanism of using mutiple GPUs efficiently were introduced to FermiNet by the DeepMind team not by the authors. Please write so that this is clear to the reader.
There are no information on the k-points employed. The authors should describe how many k-points were used and how to generate the k-point grids.
The definition of 'Correlation error' in the Fig. 2 h is not given.
* 'ccpvdz' is not the correct spelling. cc-pVDZ is correct and should be corrected.
Please position the figure captions above each table in the Supplementary Information.

I have never seen the word 'TZ-LDA basis.' I googled it but cannot found. The authors should write so that it is clear for readers what basis functions were used for the reproducibility of the calculation.
Please specify actual computational costs of this method. For example, the wall time taken to calculate LiH and the computational resources used.
Supplementary Table 4 looks listing energies per atom. Please state this clearly.
All the statistical errors written in the Supplementary Information are '(1)' except Supplementary Tables 6, 10 and 11. Is this a coincidence?

There is no mention to the statical error in the main text. For example, cohesive energy per unit cell is listed to three decimal places in eV, but without a description of the statistical error. Is the statistical error that small?

My understanding is that as long as finite k-point sampling is used, the divergent term derived from the exchange integral should appear at the \( \Gamma \) point. Is there no need for any correction to this divergent term? (c.f. DOI:10.1063/1.1926272, DOI:0.1021/acs.jctc.7b00049)
The manuscript rests heavily on connecting software and techniques from prior authors (Refs. 10, 11, 18, 26, 31, 39); the specific originality of this manuscript is not clearly articulated.

The manuscript's claim that the method outperforms other ab initio methods is not supported by the evidence shown. For the H10 and the UEG, the result is better stated as similar performance with the state-of-the-art DMC results (rather than a claim of being better than common approaches). In the case of the UEG in particular, it is known that the DMC results are not free from error at high densities due to fixed node error. Instead, as highlighted by a recent paper (Ref 31), the most accurate approaches come from FCIQMC which are available for N=14. Similar FCIQMC results are also available for N=54, which could have been used for comparison as they show clear improvement on BF-DMC.

The manuscript's claim of improvement over the existing FermiNet papers is not supported by the evidence shown. The changes made include a different choice of PBCs and NN optimizer. To validate the effectiveness of the optimizer, we would need to see efficiency data. For the PBCs choice, we would need to see well-analyzed side by side comparisons. Instead, the manuscript does not reach a direct comparison when treating the UEG, choosing different systems than Ref 31. It is possible to infer from the data presented that this algorithm captures DMC-quality energies, while Ref 31 goes beyond DMC to FCIQMC level accuracy. Thus, this manuscript's choice of PBCs seems less accurate than FermiNet not more.

The manuscript's claim of investigating long-range effectiveness in real systems is weakened by the size and electronic structure of the systems chosen. In practice, the long-range components of the energy are being added after the fact with finite size corrections (Ref. 26). The calculations themselves are being performed on relatively small system sizes. Evidence is not presented for the convergence of long-range effects (which would, for example, require analysis of the energy with system size). Moreover, the systems shown do not include a real metal (where long-range effects really come into play) and the system sizes shown have significant gaps at the one-particle/HF level of theory. So, while the systems proposed are ab initio real solids, they are not actually treated in the same way as the state of the art, and do not go beyond what can already be done.

Comparing this paper to what is currently the state of the art in the field, there are a number of high impact publications. The best known paper is by Troyer in Science (Ref 9) and the original FermiNet paper was published in Phys Rev Research (Ref 10). The best case scenario of the technical development appears to be a more efficient version of FermiNet for solids and I would put this on a par with the original FermiNet development in terms of its specialism. In my view, this paper is better suited to a more specialized journal which would also allow the claims of the novelty of the network and any speed up to be better considered and reviewed.
