One might expect an earlier, more typical, high luminosity phase to have cleared the local ISM. Perhaps the BH has recently entered the present dense, uncleared ISM, but this seems odd.

Related to 2): looking through the appendices it appears that the authors have been somewhat cavalier in their model estimates. Given that these are intrinsically imprecise the general conclusions may be OK, but the logic seems a bit forced. For example, they use the beta_perp~2.2 of J2 in a number of equations when evaluating the properties of the shock at J1. For example the theta ~7 deg is applied to J1 to get a true core separation. These numbers are also used in the penultimate paragraph of the main article, which further obscures that this beta_perp is being applied to the pre-J1 flow. (Also the argument around Eq 13 is circular -- beta_h was just inferred for a strong shock, which assumes the high density contrast.) These examples suggest that, at a minimum the authors need to be clearer about what they have assumed in making their estimates (e.g. small J1/J2 angle deflection, no deceleration). The authors did argue that the 2017 J1 flux was responding to the 2011 core flare. That could give an effective beta_perp estimate for pre-J1 flow of 17.3lt-y/<6y >2.9. That is crude but it does apply to J1.

Still in Appendix H, I was somewhat puzzled why the authors used the monochromatic expression from Birzan et al. (Eq 16) in Eq 10. Since one sees an SED peak in the radio we have a ~bolometric luminosity so the bolometric expression from Birzan (Eq 13) would seem more appropriate; that gives L_j~3.4e45 erg/s and M_edd~0.03 for 10^9Msun, with the numbers quoted. A more careful integration of the rest frame radio luminosity should be used, though. On the other hand An et al (ref 25) give M as 4x10^9 Msun, which re-lowers the efficiency -- you might wish to use that mass in your estimates or at least mention it and give the scaling.

On the subject of the radio SED peak -- this was published in Romani 2006 AJ 132, 1959. the reference used (14 = Zhang et al 2017) largely drew the data from that paper and I don't think it materially improved the spectrum measurement on that earlier result.
The Coppejans reference in App. H should be 2016, not 2017.

Overall I think that this is a good paper, but it could be better. If, as the authors repeatedly note, measuring the polarization is a big deal, they should give an EVPA estimate. If the nascent jet/ISM shock argument is the most important result, the arguments about its energetics should clearer. Perhaps with a bit of attention to the points above these aspects could be improved. Publication would then be appropriate.

Confidence intervals for the errors need to be given: 68% or 90% or (something else? If 68%, the proper motion measurement may not be significant (enough; they are only at the ~2-sigma level (95%). In this case, reporting (upper limits may be more appropriate. 2) In Appendix C, it is said that the (positional errors are 10% and 5% of FWHM of the restoring beam for J1 and J2, (respectively. As the errors may have significant impacts on the (proper motion (and other) results, these numbers need further justifications.

The authors do not propagate measurement errors adequately when reporting other derived quantities. Some of their measurements have relatively large uncertainties (e.g., proper motion), and so their conclusions may change if considering the errors. It may be very difficult to quantify all the errors, but they need to be considered seriously when drawing conclusions.

The bulk Lorentz factor is reported to be >3.3, only an upper limit. While this is consistent with previous estimations (e.g., Zhang et al. 2017, An & Romani 2018), it alone does not rule out a possibility of a powerful jet (as opposed to the claimed ‘less powerful jet’).

For inferring the jet power and ISM density (Appendix H), the authors assume that the spectral index is 0 between 1.4 GHz and 15 GHz for J1 and/or J2. Although previous measurements of the radio spectrum seem to support this albeit a spectral break at ~10 GHz, the measurements are for C+J1+J2 (or C as it dominates) as compared to J1+J2. Is it reasonable to assume the same spectrum for J1+J2 when the variability of C+J1+J2 is different from that of J1 (and/or J2) in Table 3 and Figure A1? This may be a concern when extrapolating the 15-GHz flux density to 1.4-GHz. In particular, the authors mention a propagating shock. The propagating shock may change the radio spectral shape in time (e.g., Valtaoja et al. 1992), hence assuming a radio spectral index of 0 in 2017-2018 based on earlier data needs a justification. In this regard, (near) contemporaneous multi-frequency observations may be helpful.

The empirical relation (between L_jet and P_1.4GHz) they used in Appendix H may have a relatively large uncertainty (a few orders of magnitude scatter in the data used for deriving the relation; Birzan et al. 2008); this (and other) uncertainty needs to be considered when deriving the jet power and claiming that it is low.

The reported number n~65 cm^-3 (in the text and Appendix H) for the ISM density is too precise compared to the arguments given in Appendix H (see also comments 3-5). Shouldn’t it be a limit as is for the density contrast rho_ISM/rho_jet>11 because beta_h is only a limit?

It is said “In the highest-resolution images, a sharp (>90 degree) jet bending is seen...” in the text. As the jet bending is seen in a previous study of the same source (e.g., Zhang et al. 2017), the previous work needs to be recognized.

It is said “a prominent X-ray emission (with a strong contribution from the disk)..” in the text. It is believed that X-rays in these high-z blazars are produced mainly by the synchrotron-self-Compton process and that disks of supermassive blackholes mostly radiate in the optical band. Hence, the statement needs to be clarified.
In Appendix G above equation (8) it is said “Employing equation (7) which reduces to...”. Equation (7) does not seem to reduce to (8), so this may be a mistake.

Authors indicated J0906+6930 as the most distant known blazar, having z=5.47. However, there is a recent detection by Saxena et al. (2018, MNRAS, 480, 2733) of a jetted AGN at z=5.72. The radio spectral index suggests it could be a radio galaxy, thus with the jet viewing angle greater than ≈10°. Therefore, the authors statements are formally correct. However, I would say a few words about this other jetted AGN.

[page 1, row 19] Authors wrote: \"As jetted quasars seem to harbour heavier black holes than non jetted ones, high-redshift blazars are probes...\". The line of reasoning is inverted. High-z quasars are useful probes for the early universe because they are the brightest sources, and, therefore, it is easier to find them. But, the extreme brightness is due to: (1) the presence of a relativistic jet viewed by a small angle, which means that the electromagnetic emission is Doppler boosted (with a typical Doppler factor of 10, the luminosity of the jet is boosted to 10000 times its isotropic value); (2) as the jet power scales with the mass of the central black hole (P proportional to M^1.4, cf Heinz & Sunyaev 2003), a larger mass implies a larger observed jet power.
[page 1, row 28-29] the radio loudness is a meaningless parameter. No need to cite it.

[Section F] Doppler boosting parameter of the jet. Authors estimated the Doppler factor δ by using the brightness temperature as measured from the VLBI core size. The apparent jet speed β<sub>app</sub> is measured from VLBI observations. From δ and β<sub>app</sub>, it is then possible to calculate the bulk Lorentz factor Γ and the viewing angle θ. As the authors reported also the OVRO 15 GHz radio light curve, I suggest to calculate δ also from radio variability. An easier way is to refer to Liodakis et al. (2018, ApJ, 866, 137), who already did it and reported δ=8.91. Together with β<sub>app</sub>=2.2c measured by the authors, it results Γ=4.8 and θ=3°. Please note that Liodakis calculated the equipartition brightness temperature is a way different from usual. So, when comparing your results with him, please take into account this difference. Please note that also the main text -- not only Section F -- must be changed accordingly.

[Section H] authors calculated the jet power by using a relationship by Birzan et al. (2008). These relationships links the extended radio emission measured in the MHz frequency range with the jet kinetic power. Therefore, the application to one component detected at 15 GHz is rather risky, as it is known that extrapolating from high to low frequency could give serious errors in the estimation of the extended emission. In addition, the jet power by Birzan refers only to the kinetic component, and does not include the radiative part. The kinetic part calculated in this way is based on the cavities excavated by the jet activity during million of years and are not comparable with a year time scale as from the authors of the present essay. I suggest to adopt the relationships by Foschini (2014, Int. J. Mod. Phys. Conf. Series, 28, 1460188), which links the radiative and kinetic power to the 15 GHz core luminosity. You should apply them to the core flux, but you can also try to apply them to the component J1. Obviously, you have to address the proper caveats. A quick calculation by using the core emission, resulted in a log P<sub>rad</sub>=45.4 [erg/s] and log P<sub>kin</sub>=46.1 [erg/s], greater than the estimates from the Birzan relationship, as expected.

the last (but not least) point refers to the estimation of the mass of the central black hole and the accretion rate. Authors refer to previous works, which gave a mass of the order of 10<sup>9</sup>M<sub>Sun</sub> and accretion luminosity 0.4 times the Eddington limit. However, all the previous estimates adopted relationships made for non-jetted AGN. The presence of the Doppler boosting alters significantly the continuum emission, which implies an overestimation of the mass. Also the fit of the accretion disk suffers the same problem. As the optical spectra all show a strong Lyα emission line, I suggest to use it together the relationships by Pian et al. (2005, MNRAS, 361, 919), which take into account the Doppler boosting. Indeed, a quick calculation of the λL(1350A) by using the Pian's relationships results in a value of ≈7.4×10<sup>43</sup> erg/s to be compared with the value of 5×10<sup>47</sup> erg/s given by Romani (2004). The radius of the broad-line region (BLR) from Bentz et al. (2013), results to be 18.6 light days, which implies a luminosity of the disk of ≈3×10<sup>43</sup> erg/s. I've not found a measurement of the FWHM of Lyα, but if we assume the value of 5000 km/s from other lines, the mass of the central black hole results to be ≈ 7×10<sup>7</sup>M<sub>Sun</sub>. Therefore, I suggest to recalculate the mass and accretion rate of the central black hole by using the Lyα line parameters and Pian's relationships. Then, revise the discussion in the text according to the new values. A smaller mass of the central black hole would be more suitable for a young AGN, and also more consistent with a high-frequency peaker (HFP). Compare also with Berton et al. (2016, A&A, 591, A98).

The paper excludes MMS1 data from the observation-simulation comparison specifically because it does not show the bifurcated current structure, introducing potential confirmation bias in validating the simulation results against spacecraft observations. "Data from MMS2, MMS3, and MMS4 from 20:24:00 to 20:24:15 UT on June 17, 2017 were averaged to yield the profiles shown in Figs. 5a-e. MMS1 data were omitted because the current density did not exhibit an obvious bifurcated structure." This statement reveals that one of the four MMS spacecraft was excluded solely because its data did not conform to the expected bifurcated structure. In multi-spacecraft missions like MMS, all four spacecraft are designed to provide simultaneous measurements at different spatial locations within the same phenomenon. Excluding data from one spacecraft because it does not show the expected signature is methodologically problematic and could constitute confirmation bias. The spatial separation between spacecraft (~50 km during this period, as indicated in the MMS mission design [[1]](#citation-list)) may provide physically meaningful information about the structure's non-uniformity rather than being a reason for data exclusion. Standard multi-spacecraft analysis methods, such as the curlometer technique or multi-point timing analysis, typically require all four spacecraft measurements to properly characterize current sheet structures [[2]](#citation-list). "Apart from such reconnection-related dissimilarities, the observed and simulated profiles agree strikingly well, including the bifurcated current structure." The claim of "striking agreement" is made after excluding the spacecraft that showed contradictory evidence. If MMS1 did not show bifurcation while MMS2, 3, and 4 did, this spatial variability could indicate that the current sheet is more complex than the 1D equilibrium model predicts, or that the observed bifurcation is spatially localized rather than a universal feature. The paper does not provide any scientific justification for why MMS1 data should be excluded other than disagreement with the expected result. References: [1] [Burch, J. L., Moore, T. E., Torbert, R. B. & Giles, B. L. Magnetospheric Multiscale Overview and Science Objectives. Space Sci. Rev. 199, 5–21 (2016)](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11214-015-0164-9) [2] [Dunlop, M. W., Balogh, A., Glassmeier, K.-H. & Robert, P. Four-point Cluster application of magnetic field analysis tools: The Curlometer. J. Geophys. Res. 107, 1384 (2002)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/2001JA005088)

The particle-in-cell simulation uses a frequency ratio ωce/ωpe = 5, which is significantly higher than typical magnetotail conditions (where ωce/ωpe < 1), potentially affecting the electron dynamics critical to the bifurcation mechanism. "The simulation run with a frequency ratio of ωce/ωpe = 5 is shown in this paper for clarity of presentation; ratios as low as ωce/ωpe = 0.2 were also tried, but lower ratios simply increased the duration of plasma oscillations that either damp or travel away from the center without any noticeable effect on the core relaxation mechanism." The frequency ratio ωce/ωpe determines the relative importance of electron cyclotron motion versus plasma oscillations. In the Earth's magnetotail, typical values are ωce/ωpe ~ 0.1-0.5 [[3]](#citation-list), making the authors' choice of ωce/ωpe = 5 about 10-50 times larger than realistic values. While reduced parameters are common in PIC simulations for computational efficiency, such extreme departures from physical values require careful justification. The electron plasma frequency to cyclotron frequency ratio is a crucial parameter for accurately evaluating electron dynamics in current sheets [[4]](#citation-list). The dismissal of lower ratios as merely "increasing duration of plasma oscillations" does not adequately address whether the core physics is preserved under such extreme parameter choices. "10,000 particles were placed per cell per species, so about 6 × 10^8 particles were simulated with a mass ratio mi/me = 100." The reduced mass ratio mi/me = 100 (compared to the realistic value of ~1836) is a common practice in PIC simulations and is acknowledged as necessary for computational feasibility. However, combined with the extreme frequency ratio, the simulation operates in a parameter regime that is doubly removed from realistic magnetospheric conditions. While mass ratio reductions are generally well-understood [[5]](#citation-list), the combined effect of both reduced mass ratio and extreme frequency ratio on the orbit class transitions and equilibration dynamics has not been demonstrated to be negligible. References: [3] [Artemyev, A. V., Petrukovich, A. A., Nakamura, R. & Zelenyi, L. M. Cluster statistics of thin current sheets in the Earth magnetotail: Specifics of the dawn flank, proton temperature profiles and electrostatic effects. J. Geophys. Res. 116, A09233 (2011)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/2011JA016801) [4] [Artemyev, A. V. et al. The electron plasma frequency to cyclotron frequency ratio as a crucial parameter for electron dynamics in current sheets. Physics of Plasmas 27, 072904 (2020)](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/pop/article/27/7/072904/1061692) [5] [Birn, J. et al. Geospace Environmental Modeling (GEM) Magnetic Reconnection Challenge. J. Geophys. Res. 106, 3715–3719 (2001)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/1999JA900449)

The paper compares a 1D non-reconnecting PIC simulation equilibrium to an MMS-observed current sheet that is actively undergoing magnetic reconnection, making the comparison fundamentally inconsistent despite qualitative similarities. "The finite electron outflow veL in Fig. 5b indicates that the observed current sheet is undergoing magnetic reconnection, whereas the simulated current sheet, being one-dimensional, is not." The authors acknowledge that the observed and simulated systems are in qualitatively different physical states. Magnetic reconnection fundamentally alters the current sheet structure, particle distributions, and energy balance. Reconnection drives electron heating at the sheet center [[6]](#citation-list), creates characteristic velocity signatures, and can produce bifurcated structures through mechanisms entirely different from the orbit class transitions proposed in this paper [[7]](#citation-list). Comparing a 1D equilibrium to an actively reconnecting current sheet and attributing the differences simply to "reconnection-related dissimilarities" underestimates the fundamental incompatibility of the two systems. "Reconnection induces perpendicular electron heating at the sheet center, which explains the central increase of TeMM in Fig. 5d relative to Fig. 5j. The relative increase of TeLL at the outskirts in Fig. 5d is also attributed to reconnection-induced parallel electron heating." The authors attribute multiple discrepancies between observation and simulation to reconnection effects, which raises the question of what fraction of the observed structure is due to the proposed orbit class equilibration versus ongoing reconnection dynamics. Bifurcated current sheets have been previously explained as consequences of magnetic reconnection itself [[7]](#citation-list), [[8]](#citation-list). The paper does not provide a quantitative assessment of how to separate reconnection-induced features from equilibration-induced features in the MMS observations. References: [6] [Shay, M. A. et al. Electron heating during magnetic reconnection: A simulation scaling study. Phys. Plasmas 21, 122902 (2014)](https://pubs.aip.org/aip/pop/article/21/12/122902/282626) [7] [Gosling, J. T. & Szabo, A. Bifurcated current sheets produced by magnetic reconnection in the solar wind. J. Geophys. Res. 113, A10103 (2008)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/2008JA013473) [8] [Hoshino, M. et al. Structure of plasma sheet in magnetotail: Double-peaked electric current sheet. J. Geophys. Res. 101, 24775–24786 (1996)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/96JA02313)

The paper examines only a single type of disequilibrium (under-heated Harris sheet with T = 0.2Teq) without demonstrating that the orbit class transition mechanism applies to other realistic disequilibrated states. "The initial condition was an under-heated Harris current sheet with a temperature T = 0.2Teq where Teq = B0²/(4μ0n0kB) is the Harris equilibrium temperature." The paper's central claim is that orbit class transitions are responsible for collisionless current sheet relaxation in general, yet only one specific initial condition is tested. Realistic current sheets in space plasmas can be disequilibrated in many ways: over-heated, with density perturbations, with temperature anisotropy, with guide fields, or with asymmetric configurations. The generalizability of the orbit class transition mechanism to these other cases is not demonstrated. For instance, prior work on thin current sheets has shown that temperature anisotropy [[9]](#citation-list) and guide fields [[10]](#citation-list) significantly affect current sheet structure and bifurcation properties. "The initial temperature was set as one-fifth of the Harris equilibrium temperature, i.e., T = 0.2Teq" Choosing exactly 20% of the equilibrium temperature appears arbitrary. The paper does not explore how the equilibration process depends on the degree of disequilibrium. Whether the same orbit class transitions occur and produce similar bifurcated structures for T = 0.5Teq or T = 0.1Teq, or for over-heated cases with T > Teq, remains unexplored. Understanding this parameter dependence would be crucial for establishing the proposed mechanism as a universal explanation for bifurcated current sheets. References: [9] [Sitnov, M. I., Guzdar, P. N. & Swisdak, M. A model of the bifurcated current sheet. Geophys. Res. Lett. 30, 1712 (2003)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1029/2003GL017218) [10] [Zelenyi, L. M., Malova, H. V., Yu Popov, V., Delcourt, D. & Sharma, A. S. Nonlinear equilibrium structure of thin currents sheets: influence of electron pressure anisotropy. Nonlin. Processes Geophys. 11, 579–587 (2004)](https://npg.copernicus.org/articles/11/579/2004/)

This paper draws an overgeneralized causal conclusion that orbit-class transitions during collisionless equilibration are the origin of bifurcated current sheets in geospace, despite only demonstrating that bifurcation can arise in a specific, idealized disequilibrated Harris-sheet setup and one-dimensional PIC configuration. Current sheets are ubiquitous plasma structures that play the crucial role of being energy sources for various magnetic phenomena. Although a plethora of current sheet equilibrium solutions have been found, the collisionless process through which a disequilibrated current sheet relaxes or equilibrates remains largely unknown. Here we show, through analyses of phase-space distributions of single-particle orbit classes and particle-in-cell simulations, that collisionless transitions among the orbit classes are responsible for this process. Bifurcated current sheets, which are readily observed in geospace but whose origins remain controversial, are shown to naturally arise from the equilibration process and thus are likely to be the underlying structures in various phenomena; comparisons of spacecraft observations to particle-in-cell simulations support this fact. The quoted abstract does more than claim a mechanism for a particular numerical experiment: it frames bifurcated current sheets as “likely” underlying structures in “various phenomena” based on the presented equilibration scenario. That leap is not justified by the evidence shown in the manuscript, which focuses on an initially under-heated Harris sheet and demonstrates one pathway to bifurcation rather than ruling out alternative pathways. In common practice for space plasma structure-formation claims, one expects either (i) systematic parameter scans showing robustness to key environmental variations, or (ii) multi-event observational validation that constrains competing explanations. The paper does not provide such breadth, so the generality implied by “underlying structures in various phenomena” is not adequately supported. Because the statement is in the abstract, it strongly shapes reader interpretation and should meet a higher evidentiary bar than is demonstrated. As written, it risks being interpreted as establishing primacy rather than plausibility. In summary, the collisionless relaxation process of a disequilibrated current sheet was studied. The process is most naturally understood by orbit class transitions, which were analytically predicted and numerically verified. The relaxation mechanism was identified as the origin of bifurcated current sheets, and the significance of this identification in regards to previous explanations of bifurcated structures was discussed. This summary sentence makes an identification claim (“the origin”) rather than a conditional statement (“a possible origin under specified conditions”). Yet the manuscript’s own setup is narrowly scoped (a specific disequilibrium in a Harris-like reversal, followed by a 1D PIC relaxation), which cannot logically establish that the same mechanism dominates in the diverse magnetotail and magnetopause environments where bifurcation is reported. Strong “origin” language is typically reserved for results that demonstrate necessity (e.g., bifurcation does not occur without the mechanism) and robustness (e.g., across relevant guide fields, normal components, and dynamics), neither of which is shown explicitly here. Even if the mechanism is real and important, the paper’s evidence supports “can generate” rather than “is the origin.” This affects the reliability of the conclusions and how the work will be cited and used by others. Reframing the claim to match what is actually demonstrated would substantially strengthen the paper’s validity. They argued that such structures appear as a local or/and temporal en-hancement of the current density away from the neutral sheet, associated with current sheet thinning and ﬂapping prior to substorm onset. In that case the current sheet bifurcation may not be associated with magnetic reconnection. This external discussion explicitly documents that bifurcated profiles can emerge in association with current-sheet thinning and flapping and, importantly, that bifurcation “may not be associated with magnetic reconnection” in some cases [[1]](#cite1). While the paper under review does not claim reconnection is required, it does assert a specific equilibration pathway as “the origin,” which is in tension with literature emphasizing multiple observationally plausible pathways and transient geometries. The point is not that the present mechanism is wrong, but that the observational literature describes a broader phenomenology than a single causal channel. Therefore, claiming a singular “origin” requires more comprehensive evidence than presented. The external quote supports the criticism that the paper’s generalization exceeds what is established by its limited model and comparison. This matters for validity because causal attribution in magnetotail current-sheet morphology is known to be multi-factor and context-dependent. References: [1] [Runov, A. et al. *Local structure of the magnetotail current sheet: 2001 Cluster observations.* Annales Geophysicae 24, 247–262 (2006).](https://angeo.copernicus.org/articles/24/247/2006/angeo-24-247-2006.pdf)

This paper’s observational validation (the MMS–PIC comparison used to support the bifurcation-origin claim) is not robust because it relies on a single time interval and explicitly omits a spacecraft that does not show bifurcation, while still presenting the agreement as striking evidence for the proposed mechanism. MMS data and local LMN coordinates Data from MMS2, MMS3, and MMS4 from 20:24:00 to 20:24:15 UT on June 17, 2017 were averaged to yield the profiles shown in Figs. 5a-e. MMS1 data were omitted because the current density did not exhibit an obvious bifurcated structure. This sentence describes a selection step that materially affects the central observational comparison: one of the four MMS spacecraft is excluded specifically because it does not show the target structure. Excluding discordant measurements can be appropriate if there is a prespecified physical reason (e.g., different trajectory through a spatially structured sheet), but the paper does not present such a justification or quantify how omission changes the inferred profile. In current-sheet studies, multi-spacecraft disagreement is often informative about 3D structure, temporal evolution, or crossing geometry; discarding it without analysis can bias conclusions. Because the omitted spacecraft is part of the same tetrahedron, its non-bifurcated current density weakens the claim that the observed sheet robustly matches a bifurcated equilibrium profile. The lack of an uncertainty treatment (e.g., variability across spacecraft) further makes it difficult to judge whether the agreement is meaningful or cosmetic. This directly undermines the strength of the conclusions drawn from Fig. 5. Apart from such reconnection-related dissimilarities, the observed and simulated profiles agree strikingly well, including the bifurcated current structure. The paper characterizes the agreement as “strikingly well,” which is a strong evaluative statement, yet the comparison is qualitative and is presented for a single short interval. In the same paragraph, the authors acknowledge that the observed current sheet is reconnecting while the simulated one is not, meaning the systems differ in important physics that can affect temperature and pressure tensor structure. Under common validation standards, qualitative similarity in a few profiles is insufficient to support a mechanistic identification without quantitative metrics (e.g., normalized residuals, uncertainty bands) and evidence of robustness across events. The omission of MMS1 (documented elsewhere in the paper) further heightens the risk that the comparison is confirmation-biased. As a result, the “striking” characterization overstates what the evidence can bear. This weakens the reliability of the paper’s interpretation of the MMS event as support for the proposed equilibration mechanism. In examining AE-activity dependence (Table 1) or local plasma conditions we could not ﬁnd systematic differences between events with different types or different thicknesses of the current sheets in our limited survey. (More focused efforts with a larger database are required to reveal and quan-tify the activity dependence, if it exists at all.) This external statement highlights a key methodological norm for current-sheet morphology claims: robust inference often requires larger event databases and focused statistical efforts [[1]](#cite1). The paper under review instead uses one event as its main observational anchor for a broad mechanistic claim, and even within that event excludes one spacecraft’s measurement. The quoted literature does not invalidate single-event case studies, but it does show that systematic dependence and typology are difficult to establish without broader statistics. Therefore, presenting a single-case qualitative match as strong support (“agree strikingly well”) falls short of common practice for settling an origin controversy. The external quote supports the criticism that the paper’s validation step is underpowered relative to the scope of its conclusion. This is significant because the central claim explicitly targets an unresolved, debated observational phenomenon. References: [1] [Runov, A. et al. *Local structure of the magnetotail current sheet: 2001 Cluster observations.* Annales Geophysicae 24, 247–262 (2006).](https://angeo.copernicus.org/articles/24/247/2006/angeo-24-247-2006.pdf)

This paper contains a major internal inconsistency in the reported PIC simulation duration (Figure 3 caption versus Methods), which directly affects interpretation of relaxation timescales and prevents precise reproduction of the presented results. FIG. 3. Streak plots of variables from the particle-in-cell simulation. Streak plots of a the sheared magnetic field \( B_y \), b the current density \( J_z \), c the ion temperature \( T_i \), and d the ion density \( n_i \) from \( t = 0 \) to \( 1000 \omega_{ci}^{-1} \). This caption states that Fig. 3 spans to \(1000\,\omega_{ci}^{-1}\), implying a simulation or at least plotted window an order of magnitude longer than what is specified elsewhere. For a relaxation/equilibration study, the time horizon is not cosmetic: it determines whether the shown “equilibrium” is transient, metastable, or persistent. If the figure truly spans \(1000\,\omega_{ci}^{-1}\), it would also interact with claims about oscillations, damping, or boundary effects over long durations. Conversely, if the correct timespan is \(100\,\omega_{ci}^{-1}\), then the caption is misleading and the figure may not demonstrate long-term steadiness. Because this is the central simulation evidence for equilibration, ambiguity in the run duration materially weakens confidence in the conclusions. It also prevents others from matching outputs when attempting reproduction. The simulation time was \(t_{max} = 100\omega_{ci}^{-1}\) with a time step \(\Delta t = 7.63 \times 10^{-4}\omega_{ci}^{-1}\). The Methods section unambiguously specifies \(t_{max}=100\,\omega_{ci}^{-1}\), contradicting the Fig. 3 caption’s \(1000\,\omega_{ci}^{-1}\). This discrepancy cannot be resolved by a reader without access to the authors’ run scripts and plotting code, and it is large enough that it plausibly changes which processes could occur or be visible. In numerical plasma studies, reproducibility depends critically on consistent reporting of domain, timestep, and run duration because these define stability constraints and physical timescales. The paper’s main narrative also references equilibration by \(\sim 30\,\omega_{ci}^{-1}\), so clarifying whether the simulation ended at 100 or 1000 affects the evidentiary weight of “remains steady.” A mismatch at this level is a substantive methodology flaw, not a minor typo, because it affects the reader’s ability to interpret and reproduce the central figure. The inconsistency therefore undermines the reliability of the reported simulation-based verification. Whenever a result may be of potential interest, keep track of how it was produced. This guidance reflects a widely accepted computational-research norm that the provenance of results must be precisely recorded [[2]](#cite2). The inconsistency between caption and Methods indicates that the paper does not meet this basic standard for at least one of its key simulation outputs. In a field where many details (normalization, timestep, boundary handling, duration) affect results, ambiguous reporting makes independent verification substantially harder. This is especially consequential here because the paper’s primary claims hinge on time evolution (relaxation and equilibration) rather than static equilibria alone. The quote is not a policy statement; it is a methodological requirement to maintain validity of computational claims. The paper’s conflicting time reporting violates this expectation and is therefore a significant issue for reproducibility and interpretation. Correcting and reconciling these reported parameters is necessary for the work to be reliably assessed. References: [2] [Sandve, G. K. et al. *Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research.* PLOS Computational Biology (2013).](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003285)

This paper does not provide sufficient materials to reproduce its principal results (orbit-class population analyses and MMS comparison) because it withholds the analysis code (“upon reasonable request”) and the provided code directory is essentially an upstream dependency (PySPEDAS) rather than the paper-specific scripts needed to regenerate the figures. The codes used in the data analyses are available from Y.D.Y. upon reasonable request. This sentence establishes that the figure-generation and analysis scripts are not publicly available at publication time. In computational and data-driven plasma-physics studies, the ability to rerun the exact processing steps (including data cleaning, coordinate transforms, averaging, and plotting choices) is essential for independent reproduction and error-checking. “Upon reasonable request” creates a discretionary access barrier and is not equivalent to durable, archival availability, especially as contact details, institutional access, or responsiveness can change over time. This is particularly problematic here because the paper’s key conclusions depend on detailed phase-space diagnostics and a qualitative MMS–PIC match; small processing differences can change inferred structure. The paper does provide raw data locations, but without the scripts that map raw data to the reported profiles, full reproduction is not feasible. This therefore constitutes a major methodology transparency gap. ```toml [project] name = "pyspedas" version = "2.0.5" description = "Python Space Physics Environment Data Analysis Software (pySPEDAS)" ``` This project metadata shows that the bundled “code” is the PySPEDAS package rather than paper-specific analysis scripts. PySPEDAS is a general-purpose community framework and, by itself, does not encode the exact event selection, averaging windows, coordinate definitions, smoothing choices, or plotting steps that produce Fig. 5 and related results. Therefore, including PySPEDAS does not resolve the reproducibility problem created by withholding the authors’ own scripts. In practice, the critical missing artifact is the executable workflow that ties MMS data retrieval and processing to the plotted profiles, along with any scripts used to compute orbit-class diagnostics from simulation outputs. Without those scripts, independent researchers cannot verify whether the reported profiles are robust to reasonable analysis choices. The code block supports the criticism by demonstrating a mismatch between “code availability” needs and the material actually provided. This mismatch directly impacts reproducibility of the central claims. The Python-based Space Physics Environment Data Analysis Software (PySPEDAS) framework supports multi-mission, multi-instrument retrieval, analysis, and visualization of heliophysics time series data. This external description clarifies that PySPEDAS is an analysis framework, not a complete reproduction package for a specific paper’s figures [[5]](#cite5). The existence of a framework does not substitute for sharing the exact scripts and parameters used in a particular study, because frameworks are intentionally flexible and support many alternative workflows. For the manuscript under review, the crucial missing information is how the authors used PySPEDAS (which routines, which data products, what preprocessing, what averaging, what coordinate transformation details beyond the final LMN vectors). The quote thus supports the criticism that bundling PySPEDAS alone is insufficient for replicating the paper’s figure-level results. This is a major issue because the paper’s main validation step is a figure-level match between MMS and PIC profiles. Without the paper’s scripts, readers cannot distinguish between robust physical agreement and analysis-dependent presentation. Last, but not least, all input data, scripts, versions, parameters, and intermediate results should be made publicly and easily accessible. This is a well-established standard for computational reproducibility and directly contradicts a “reasonable request” model for key analysis code [[2]](#cite2). The paper under review does share some data locations, but it does not provide the scripts and parameters required to regenerate its main plots and derived quantities. Because the claimed mechanism is subtle (orbit-class transitions inferred from phase-space structure) and the observational validation is qualitative, independent reproduction is especially important to establish trust. The absence of publicly accessible scripts also hinders downstream reuse, extension, and verification by other groups, which is part of normal scientific practice in computational plasma physics. This quote supports the criticism by articulating what is expected for reproducibility when results depend on computational processing. The gap between this standard and the paper’s actual “code availability” materially affects the work’s reproducibility. They are also, we believe, prerequisites for reproducible computational research: if software is not version controlled, readable, and tested, the chances of its authors (much less anyone else) being able to re-create results are remote. (Source: [[3]](#cite3)) This sentence articulates a widely accepted view that software in computational science must be treated as part of the experimental method, not a peripheral convenience. In this manuscript, the core claims (orbit-class population changes and MMS–PIC profile agreement) depend on nontrivial data processing, categorization, and plotting, i.e., on software behaving correctly. Without access to the authors’ analysis scripts, readers cannot check whether numerical choices (binning, smoothing, coordinate transforms, and averaging) could produce the reported structures. This is particularly important because the paper’s interpretive claims hinge on qualitative profile features (“bifurcated current structure,” “central dip,” “density plateau”) that can be sensitive to processing details. Treating software as “apparatus” implies it should be inspectable and verifiable by peers, analogous to how instrument calibration and experimental protocols are shared. The quote therefore supports the criticism that withholding the analysis code undermines the reproducibility and auditability of the work’s central results. The files are in hdf5 format. Refer to https://smileipic.github.io/Smilei/post-processing.html for information on how to post-process the data. (Source: [[4]](#cite4)) This dataset description shows that the archived PIC outputs require post-processing steps before they become the paper’s plotted quantities and interpreted diagnostics. While sharing large raw outputs is valuable, it is not sufficient for reproducibility unless the exact post-processing workflow used to compute the paper’s figures is also provided. In this manuscript, the main mechanistic evidence involves phase-space structure and orbit-class interpretation, which depend on how distribution functions and moments are computed and visualized from the simulation outputs. If different reasonable post-processing choices (e.g., bin edges, normalization, integration ranges, or time averaging) lead to different apparent bifurcation or anisotropy patterns, then the conclusions are not independently checkable. The quote also indicates reliance on external documentation for generic post-processing, but the missing component is the paper-specific “glue” code that maps these files to Figs. 3–5 and the associated quantitative claims. Therefore, even with the Zenodo archive, the end-to-end reproduction of key results remains blocked without the authors’ scripts and configuration files. Documentation and metadata describing data products, algorithms, instrument calibrations, validation, and data quality will be provided. (Source: [[6]](#cite6)) This statement from the MMS data portal highlights that reproducible MMS analyses depend on knowing precisely which calibrated data products and algorithms were used, not just the mission and instrument names. The manuscript specifies a time interval and mentions averaging and minimum variance analysis, but it does not identify the exact MMS product levels, cadences, or any filtering and resampling steps needed to reproduce current density and pressure/temperature tensor profiles. This matters because MMS has multiple data rates and product variants, and derived quantities (like current density peaks and temperature anisotropy proxies) can depend on these choices. The fact that MMS provides extensive metadata and algorithm documentation underscores that the paper could, in principle, be fully reproducible if it published the exact processing pipeline. Instead, the paper directs readers to public data access while withholding the actual analysis scripts, leaving a critical gap between “data available” and “results reproducible.” The quote therefore supports the criticism that the methodology description is insufficiently complete for independent replication of the MMS-based evidence. References: [2] [Sandve, G. K. et al. *Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research.* PLOS Computational Biology (2013).](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003285) [3] [Wilson, G. et al. *Best Practices for Scientific Computing.* PLOS Biology (2014).](https://journals.plos.org/plosbiology/article?id=10.1371/journal.pbio.1001745) [4] [Zenodo. *Yoon et al 2021 PIC data* (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.4607112).](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4607112) [5] [spedas/pyspedas. *Python-based Space Physics Environment Data Analysis Software.*](https://github.com/spedas/pyspedas) [6] [MMS Science Data Center. *MMS public data site.*](https://lasp.colorado.edu/mms/sdc/public/)

The comparison between the 1D non-reconnecting simulation and the MMS event is invalid because the MMS event is undergoing reconnection, which is known to generate bifurcated structures independently of the proposed relaxation mechanism. "The finite electron outflow $v_{eL}$ in Fig. 5b indicates that the observed current sheet is undergoing magnetic reconnection, whereas the simulated current sheet, being one-dimensional, is not." The authors explicitly acknowledge that the observed event exhibits reconnection signatures (outflow), which are absent in the 1D simulation. This fundamental difference in physical regimes undermines the validity of the comparison. "A sharp peak in the reconnection rate... and a bifurcated current sheet... are also characteristic features of fast kinetic reconnection... [Hesse et al., 1999]" [1] Literature establishes that magnetic reconnection itself generates bifurcated current sheets (e.g., due to Hall physics and meandering orbits in the diffusion region). Since the MMS event is reconnecting, the observed bifurcation can be plausibly attributed to reconnection dynamics rather than the pre-reconnection relaxation process proposed by the authors. The paper fails to distinguish between these two causes or demonstrate why the relaxation model is a better fit than a reconnection model. References: [1] [Ma, Z. W., & Bhattacharjee, A. (2001). Multiscale modeling of magnetospheric reconnection. Journal of Geophysical Research: Space Physics.](https://deepblue.lib.umich.edu/bitstream/handle/2027.42/95438/jgra18824.pdf?sequence=1)

The "relaxed equilibrium" obtained in the 1D simulation is likely unstable to 2D instabilities (e.g., tearing, drift-kink) which are artificially suppressed in the simulation, making the equilibrium physically unrealizable or transient in real systems. "The 1D simulation domain was $10\lambda$... periodic boundary conditions were imposed for the fields in the y-direction... The simulated current sheet, being one-dimensional, is not [reconnecting]." The 1D setup enforces translational invariance in the $y$ and $z$ directions, artificially suppressing tearing modes (reconnection) and drift-kink instabilities. "With recent 3D kinetic simulation results, Ricci et al. (2004) excluded the action of reconnection as the bifurcation trigger, but privileged the role of current aligned instabilities like the lower-hybrid drift instability or the Kelvin-Helmholtz instability." [2] Previous studies indicate that current sheets are subject to various 2D/3D instabilities that can alter their structure or lead to bifurcation. By restricting the simulation to 1D, the paper ignores these dominant modes. In a real 3D system, the sheet might succumb to tearing or kinking before or during the proposed relaxation, rendering the "1D equilibrium" irrelevant. References: [2] [Ricci, P., Lapenta, G., & Brackbill, J. U. (2004). Bifurcated current sheet: Model and observations. Geophysical Research Letters.](https://hal.science/hal-00269295v1/document)

The arguments the authors give and the numerical simulations presented are convincing and novel but, it is not a priori evident for a non-expert why the subject is important to others in the space and/or plasma physics community. I would recommend that the authors address this question with more care before publication so the results can be appealing to a broad audience.

Regarding the comparison between the simulation and the MMS observations. The authors mention that the current sheet observed by MMS is significantly thinner than in the simulation. Can the authors expand on this difference? Is the current sheet close to equilibrium? Does reconnection contribute to thinning the current sheet?

On the same note, the authors should clarify how the x-axes on the two sides in Figure 5 compare. Since the speed and thickness of this current sheet are known from before (i.e., Wang et al., 2020), it should be possible to provide a conversion factor between seconds and “x/lambda” in the observations.
It would be more illustrative if the color scale in Figure 4d & 4i was centered around zero (so that 0 is white).
Line 195: It seems the variable \"Re\" for Earth radius is not explained.
Line 199: It seems the variable “de” for electron skin depth is not explained.
Line 238: “It fact” -> “In fact”

The paper makes speculative causal claims about visual memory consolidation based solely on electrophysiological findings without any behavioral or cognitive measures to support this interpretation. "Our study suggests that phase-amplitude coupling between physiologic HFO and delta waves, as rated by MI, is strengthened during development, in the occipital lobe particularly during toddlerhood and preschool. Given that occipital delta-nested HFO are believed to support visual memory consolidation, our observations imply that process may be significantly strengthened during early childhood." This statement presents a causal inference that links electrophysiological measurements (MI in the occipital lobe) to a cognitive process (visual memory consolidation) without any behavioral evidence. The study did not measure visual memory performance at any age, nor did it correlate MI values with any cognitive outcome. The logical leap from "MI increases with age in occipital lobe" to "visual memory consolidation is strengthened" requires direct behavioral validation that is absent from this study. "one possible explanation for the developmental enhancement of occipital MI is that the cerebral cortex learns to generate HFO at a preferred phase of background rhythms in an experience-dependent manner, optimizing visual memory consolidation during slow-wave sleep (Ji and Wilson, 2007; Mehta, 2007; Sasaki et al., 2010; Buzsáki and da Silva, 2012; Nagasawa et al., 2012)." The cited studies (particularly Ji and Wilson, 2007) were conducted in rodents using place cells in the hippocampus and visual cortex. The translation of these findings to human developmental changes in phase-amplitude coupling is speculative. Prior research on sleep-dependent memory consolidation in humans has emphasized the need for direct electrophysiology-behavior correlations, particularly in human intracranial studies where such correlations can be directly tested ([1]). The absence of any behavioral measures in this study undermines the validity of claims about memory consolidation. * Quote from literature: "Beyond general suppression, sleep and its disruption appear to influence AHN differentially along the dorsoventral hippocampal axis. While studies in rodents have shown that sleep deprivation preferentially impairs neurogenesis in the ventral DG... it has also been reported that REM sleep deprivation can also reduce cell proliferation in the dorsal DG and impair hippocampal-dependent spatial memory." ([2]) The literature demonstrates that linking electrophysiological markers to memory consolidation requires behavioral validation, as the relationship between neural oscillations and memory function is complex and species-specific. The paper's claims about visual memory consolidation in human children based on occipital MI measurements alone represent an overinterpretation without the necessary behavioral evidence. References: [1] [Electrophysiological markers of memory consolidation in the human hippocampus](https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2123430119) - Paller et al. PNAS 2022 [2] [Modifying Factors of Adult Hippocampal Neurogenesis](https://www.mdpi.com/2073-4409/15/1/59) - Cells 2024

The paper's emphasis on early childhood (ages 1-3.9 years) as a critical period for developmental changes in MI is undermined by analyzing only 14 patients in this age group, which provides insufficient statistical power for robust normative atlas construction. "The mixed model analysis of iEEG data of 14 children aged from 1.0 and 3.9 suggested that, as compared to the other lobes, the occipital lobe showed higher MI ≥80 Hz & 0.5-1 Hz (mixed model effect: +0.087 [95%CI: +0.078 to +0.097]; uncorrected p-value: < 0.001)" While the p-value is significant, the analysis is based on only 14 children across a 3-year developmental window. Given the rapid neurodevelopmental changes during this period and the study's goal of creating a "normative atlas," this sample size is insufficient to establish reliable developmental norms. The paper's hypothesis specifically highlights early childhood as a critical period ("pattern recognition memory is known to improve most rapidly before six years of age"), yet the data from this period is the least robust. "In a previous study, we created normative atlases of MI and HFO based on data from 47 patients aged between 4 and 19 years, which revealed a general enhancement of these measures in the occipital lobe (Kuroda et al., 2021). In the current study, we aimed to replicate this finding in a cohort of 14 children aged between 1.0 and 3.9 years and 100 individuals aged 4 years or older." The asymmetry between the sample sizes (14 children vs. 100 older individuals) creates an imbalance that makes developmental comparisons across age groups problematic. The claims about developmental trajectories being "drastic during young childhood" (as indicated by AIC comparisons of model fits) rely heavily on data from this small subsample, undermining the reliability of these conclusions. * Quote from literature: "The most important limitation of this study is the small sample size of children, which also results in reduced statistical power. We did not apply corrections for multiple comparisons because of the exploratory nature of the analysis." ([3]) This limitation from other neuroimaging studies highlights a recognized problem in the field: small pediatric samples lead to reduced statistical power and unreliable developmental inferences. For a study aiming to create "normative atlases" that will serve as clinical references, the sample of 14 young children (spanning 3 years of development) is inadequate to establish reliable age-specific norms. References: [3] [Cross-sectional study EEG connectivity patterns in children](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-33555-w_reference.pdf) - Scientific Reports 2025

The tractography analysis uses an adult-derived population-averaged DWI atlas from healthy adults to infer white matter connectivity in children as young as 1 year old, which is inappropriate given the substantial differences in white matter structure between children and adults. "We delineated white matter DWI streamlines using open-source data from 1,065 healthy participants (http://brain.labsolver.org/diffusion-mri-templates/hcp-842-hcp-1021; Yeh et al., 2018)" The HCP dataset consists of young adults (ages 22-35), not children. Using this atlas to trace white matter pathways supposedly connecting cortical regions showing developmental changes in MI in children aged 1-20 years introduces systematic bias. Pediatric white matter differs substantially from adult white matter in terms of myelination, tract volume, and connectivity patterns, particularly in children under 5 years old. "Our group validated the use of open-source DWI data by demonstrating that the inferred velocity of neural propagations induced by single-pulse electrical stimulation was similar whether using open-source or individual patient DWI data (Sonoda et al., 2021)." This validation refers to neural propagation velocity, not anatomical accuracy of tract identification in children. The validation does not address whether adult-derived tractography templates accurately represent the anatomical connectivity patterns in developing brains, which is the critical question for interpreting developmental co-growth patterns. * Quote from literature: "There have been existing atlases created based on specific populations. But they often struggle to generalize across different age groups... A future direction will be to extend the atlas to include high-quality lifespan datasets, covering a broader range of age groups, and thus enabling the investigation of WM development across the full lifespan." ([4]) Recent literature explicitly acknowledges that population-averaged tractography atlases struggle to generalize across age groups and that age-specific or lifespan atlases are needed for developmental research. The paper's use of an adult atlas to interpret findings in patients as young as 1 year old represents a methodological mismatch that undermines the validity of the tractography-based developmental interpretations shown in Figure 5. References: [4] [Cross-Population White Matter Atlas Creation for Neonatal-Adult Brain Comparison](https://arxiv.org/html/2512.20370) - arXiv 2024

The finding that HFO rate is elevated in the occipital lobe shows substantial inconsistency across the four different HFO detection methods, particularly in young children, which undermines the robustness and generalizability of the conclusions. "The mixed-model analysis of iEEG data from 14 children between the ages of 1.0 and 3.9 did not show a higher HFO rate in the occipital lobe compared to extra-occipital lobes, except when considering the HFO rate defined by the Hilbert method (HFO STE ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: +0.022 [95%CI: -0.039 to +0.082]; uncorrected p-value: 0.48; HFO SLL ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: +0.036 [95%CI: -0.46 to +0.53]; uncorrected p-value: 0.89; HFO HIL ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: +0.84 [95%CI: +0.57 to +1.1]; uncorrected p-value: < 0.001; HFO MNI ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: -0.044 [95%CI: -0.90 to +3.0 x 10⁻³]; uncorrected p-value: 0.067)." In young children, three out of four detection methods (STE, SLL, MNI) fail to show significant occipital enhancement of HFO, while only the Hilbert method shows significance. This inconsistency raises concerns about whether the reported occipital HFO enhancement is a robust biological phenomenon or an artifact of specific detection algorithms. For findings intended to serve as normative references for clinical applications, such method-dependent variability is problematic. "Another mixed-model analysis of iEEG data from 100 individuals between the ages of 4.0 and 41.5 demonstrated that the occipital lobe had a higher HFO rate compared to the extra-occipital lobes, except when considering the HFO rate defined by the MNI method (HFO STE ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: +0.14... HFO MNI ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: +0.043 [95%CI: -0.014 to +0.099]; uncorrected p-value: 0.14)." Even in the larger sample of older patients, the MNI method consistently fails to detect occipital HFO enhancement. The paper attempts to explain this as a methodological feature ("its detector being designed to be agnostic to persistent forms of high-frequency activity"), but this explanation highlights that different HFO definitions yield different biological conclusions. This undermines the paper's claim to provide generalizable normative data. * Quote from literature: "HFOs in iEEG recordings have emerged as promising clinical biomarkers for identifying epileptogenic brain regions. Characterized by brief, synchronized transients in the 80–500 Hz range, their detection has improved through... Universal automated high frequency oscillation detector for real-time, long term eeg." ([5]) The literature demonstrates ongoing efforts to standardize HFO detection precisely because different methods yield different results. The substantial method-dependent variability in this paper's findings (particularly the complete failure of MNI method to detect developmental patterns that other methods detect) suggests that the normative atlas may not be robust enough for clinical implementation without specifying which detection method should be used. References: [5] [Adaptive neuromodulation dialogues - High-frequency oscillations as biomarkers](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12715846/) - PMC 2024

The study uses number of antiseizure medications as a proxy for seizure-related cognitive burden in statistical models, but does not adequately account for direct pharmacological effects of these medications on the measured electrophysiological biomarkers (MI and HFO rate). "We considered a larger number of antiseizure medications as a surrogate of a more severe seizure-related cognitive burden since polytherapy is associated with more disabling seizures and seizure-related cognitive impairment (Kwan and Brodie, 2001; Kuroda et al., 2021). We employed this approach since no single neuropsychological assessment can quantify the severity of cognitive impairment across all age ranges." This approach conflates two distinct effects: (1) seizure-related cognitive burden and (2) direct medication effects on EEG activity. Antiseizure medications are known to directly affect EEG oscillatory activity, including high-frequency components. By treating medication count as a proxy for cognitive burden rather than as a potential direct confounder of the electrophysiological measures, the study may underestimate or misattribute medication effects on MI and HFO. "we employed mixed model analysis to control the effects of potential confounding factors on MI/HFO measures, including antiseizure medications, presence of MRI-visible lesions, and locations of SOZ." The mixed model includes medication count as a covariate, but does not account for specific medication types, doses, or their known differential effects on neuronal oscillations. Different antiseizure medications have distinct mechanisms of action (sodium channel blockers, GABA enhancers, etc.) that differentially affect high-frequency activity. The analysis treats all medications as equivalent units, which may not adequately control for pharmacological confounding. * Quote from literature: "In new onset epilepsy (NOE), antiseizure medication (ASM) is the first-line treatment... A recent study investigated global connectivity and regional connectivity to assess the impact of antiepileptic drug (AED) administered in patients with temporal lobe." ([6]) Recent literature explicitly examines how antiseizure medications affect EEG connectivity patterns, demonstrating that medication effects are a recognized confounder in iEEG studies. The failure to address specific medication effects (beyond simple count) represents a methodological limitation that could affect the interpretation of developmental changes in MI and HFO, particularly given that younger patients in the sample may have different medication profiles than older patients. References: [6] [Linking New Onset Epilepsy Risk-of-Relapse to EEG Patterns](https://journals.lww.com/clinicalneurophys/fulltext/9900/linking_new_onset_epilepsy_risk_of_relapse_to_eeg.287.aspx) - Journal of Clinical Neurophysiology 2024

This paper’s whole-cortex developmental atlases rely on mesh-point-wise univariate regressions whose inferential procedure appears to under-correct for the thousands of simultaneous mesh-point tests, undermining the validity of the reported “significant” developmental effects (Validity; Appropriate use of statistics and treatment of uncertainties; Conclusions). "We identified the mesh points where the developmental change (enhancement or diminution) of a given iEEG biomarker measure survived an FDR correction (for 18 comparisons: six iEEG measures × three types of age measures) in the resulting normative atlas." This sentence defines the multiple-comparisons correction scope as 18 comparisons, which accounts for biomarker/age-transform choices but not for the much larger number of mesh points tested across the cortical surface. Because each mesh point is itself a separate hypothesis test, controlling error rates over only 18 comparisons does not control the overall false positive rate of the atlas. In surface/voxel-wise mapping, the dominant multiplicity problem is typically the number of spatial tests (often thousands to hundreds of thousands), not just the number of model variants. As written, the procedure can label mesh points as “significant” even if the family-wise error across the surface is uncontrolled. This directly threatens the reliability of the spatial patterns that the atlas visualizes and the downstream analyses built on those “significant” points. "Univariate regression models, incorporating √age as an independent variable, found that 159 out of the 9464 cortical mesh points (1.7%) showed significantly positive regression slopes, whereas no cortical mesh points showed significantly negative regression slopes." This sentence confirms that thousands of mesh-point tests (9,464) were conducted and then thresholded into “significant” vs. non-significant sets. When so many spatial hypotheses are tested, the correction method must explicitly control error rates over those 9,464 tests (or use a spatially-aware alternative such as permutation/cluster-wise inference). Reporting a fraction of significant points without clarifying a surface-wide correction invites the possibility that many detected points are driven by chance, spatial autocorrelation, or model mis-specification. The fact that significance is summarized at the mesh-point level also implies that the atlas’ key claims (where development “occurs”) depend on this thresholding step. If the correction scope is inadequate, the reported 159 “significant” points may not be reproducible under standard whole-brain multiple-testing control. This is a major validity concern because the tractography analysis later conditions on these significant mesh points. "With at least one test performed for every voxel in an image, some correction of the thresholds is needed to control the error rates" ([1](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11906227/)). This statement captures the standard neuroimaging multiple-testing problem that also applies to cortical-surface mesh-point testing: many spatial locations each contribute at least one statistical test. The paper under review explicitly runs mesh-point-level regressions across 9,464 mesh points, which is directly analogous to voxel-wise testing. Therefore, the correction should address the multiplicity induced by the number of mesh points (the spatial tests), not merely the number of biomarker/age-model variants. Citing an FDR correction over 18 comparisons does not meet the “one test per voxel/mesh point” correction principle described here. As a result, the atlas’ displayed “significant developmental change” regions may be overstated. This mismatch between established neuroimaging practice and the paper’s stated correction scope undermines confidence in the reported atlas-level inferences. "Permutation methods can provide exact control of false positives" ([2](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24530839/)). This sentence illustrates a common and widely accepted alternative for controlling false positives in mass-univariate imaging analyses, especially when assumptions are weak or complex dependence structures exist. In the paper under review, mesh-point-wise regressions are performed across many spatial tests on data that are not naturally independent (multiple electrodes per patient and spatial smoothing/interpolation). In such contexts, permutation-based family-wise error control or other mass-univariate correction strategies are standard tools to ensure valid inference. The quote highlights that robust false-positive control is achievable and expected in typical imaging scenarios. Because the paper does not describe a surface-wide permutation/cluster correction (and instead describes an 18-comparison FDR), it is unclear that it achieves the level of false-positive control suggested here. This further supports the concern that the reported significant mesh-point findings may be inflated.

The paper’s “dynamic tractography” interpretation (white-matter pathways supporting developmental MI co-growth) is not well supported because it uses population-averaged open-source diffusion tractography (rather than age-matched or subject-specific diffusion data) and conditions connectivity on the paper’s thresholded mesh-point significance results (Validity; Conclusions; Data and methodology). "We delineated white matter DWI streamlines using open-source data from 1,065 healthy participants (http://brain.labsolver.org/diffusion-mri-templates/hcp-842-hcp-1021; Yeh et al., 2018), as previously reported (Mitsuhashi et al., 2021, 2022; Sonoda et al., 2021; Kitazawa et al., 2023; Ono et al., 2023)." This sentence shows that the tractography is not derived from the study’s own cohort, but from an external, population-averaged diffusion template. That choice can be useful for coarse anatomical illustration, but it weakens claims about developmental white-matter connectivity because the diffusion pathways are not estimated in the same individuals (or ages) from whom MI developmental slopes are measured. The approach also implicitly assumes that tract geometry/connectivity is sufficiently invariant across development to justify using a single template, yet the paper’s narrative frames the tractography as reflecting developmental neural communications. Moreover, because the diffusion data are external and averaged, subject-specific variability and age-related changes in tract properties (which are central to a developmental claim) are not represented. As a result, the tractography component is at risk of being an anatomical overlay rather than evidence for developmentally changing pathways linked to MI. "We interpret the visualized streamlines, including vertical occipital fasciculi and posterior callosal fibers, to reflect the ongoing development of large-scale neural communications based on slow wave-nested HFO before reaching the adult level." This sentence makes a strong developmental and mechanistic interpretation of the tractography visualization. However, the tractography is not measured longitudinally in the same participants and (as stated in Methods) is drawn from an open-source diffusion template rather than pediatric diffusion data aligned to each age. Consequently, the visualization can at best show that significant cortical regions (as defined by the paper’s statistical thresholding) are anatomically connected by known tracts in a template, not that those tracts are developing in tandem with MI changes. The leap from “connected in a template” to “reflect ongoing development of neural communications” is therefore not warranted by the presented methodology. This over-interpretation materially affects the Conclusions because it is used to motivate mechanistic explanations for the occipital MI increase. Without age-specific diffusion evidence, the tractography result should be framed more cautiously as a putative anatomical scaffold. "The Human Connectome Project consortium led by Washington University, University of Minnesota, and Oxford University is undertaking a systematic effort to map macroscopic human brain circuits and their relationship to behavior in a large population of healthy adults." ([4](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23684880/)) This statement establishes that the Human Connectome Project (referenced by the paper’s HCP template URL naming) is designed around healthy adults rather than children. If the diffusion template used for streamline reconstruction is derived from HCP-style adult datasets, it is not a developmental diffusion atlas and does not encode pediatric white-matter organization. Therefore, using such data to support claims about “ongoing development” from ages 1–21 is a mismatch between data provenance and the biological phenomenon being inferred. Adult population-averaged streamlines can differ from pediatric tract structure and do not capture maturational changes in tract endpoints, pruning, or microstructure. This does not invalidate the existence of the named tracts, but it does weaken developmental inferences about their role in MI changes. The quote thus supports the critique that the tractography evidence is not developmentally grounded. "Here we report an expert-vetted, population-averaged atlas of the structural connectome derived from diffusion MRI data (N = 842)." ([3](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29758339/)) This sentence clarifies that the referenced connectome resource is population-averaged rather than individualized. Population averaging can be valuable for illustrating canonical tract anatomy, but it is not a substitute for subject- and age-specific tractography when the research question is developmental change. In the reviewed paper, the key tractography claim is not merely anatomical presence but a developmental co-growth mechanism linking cortical MI changes across regions. A population-averaged atlas cannot test whether individuals with steeper occipital MI slopes also show corresponding tract differences, nor whether tract properties change with age in the relevant range. Thus, this quote supports the argument that the tractography portion is not evidentially aligned with the paper’s developmental interpretation. It reinforces that the tractography should be presented as a contextual visualization rather than causal/mechanistic support.

The paper’s primary outcome (developmental change in MI as phase–amplitude coupling) is computed and interpreted without reporting key controls (e.g., surrogate testing, checks for non-sinusoidal waveform-driven coupling, or artifact-related harmonic coupling), which can produce spurious MI and threaten the validity of the developmental MI conclusions (Validity; Data and methodology; Conclusions). "At each artifact-free nonepileptic site, we used the EEGLAB winPACT toolbox (https://sccn.ucsd.edu/wiki/WinPACT; Miyakoshi et al., 2013) to automatically compute MI; this program transforms all iEEG data points into Hilbert spectra and quantifies the strength of coupling between HFO amplitude and the instantaneous phase of slow waves." This sentence describes the computational pipeline (Hilbert-based transforms with an automated MI computation) but does not mention any validation steps to ensure that the measured coupling reflects physiological cross-frequency interaction rather than signal-shape or spectral-leakage artifacts. In PAC analyses, Hilbert-based methods can yield non-zero coupling values even when apparent coupling is produced by non-sinusoidal slow waves, sharp transients, or harmonics rather than genuine modulation. Because the paper’s key claims hinge on spatial and developmental differences in MI, any systematic difference in waveform shape across age/region could masquerade as MI change. The manuscript also does not report surrogate-based normalization (e.g., phase shuffling) in the Methods section shown, which is commonly used to establish that MI exceeds what is expected under a null of no coupling. Without such safeguards, it is unclear how much of the reported occipital MI enhancement reflects true physiological PAC. "Below, we report the normative developmental changes primarily in MI≥80 Hz & 0.5−1 Hz (Fig. 2), which is considered a surrogate marker of the delta-nested HFO that are believed to underly consolidation of long-term memory related to given sensory representations" This sentence goes beyond measurement and assigns a specific functional interpretation (“surrogate marker” for a memory-related physiological mechanism) to the MI estimate. Such interpretations require strong assurance that the MI metric is not inflated by non-neural artifacts or by waveform properties unrelated to cross-frequency interaction. Because the paper’s MI is computed on invasive clinical recordings in epilepsy patients, it is particularly important to demonstrate that sharp transients, harmonics, and residual epileptiform activity outside labeled zones are not driving MI. The paper later acknowledges that physiological HFO can “inflate” MI in occipital cortex, which further underscores the need to separate genuine coupling from properties like event rate and waveform morphology. Without explicit controls, the developmental changes in MI could partly reflect age-related changes in signal morphology or detector sensitivity rather than developmental strengthening of PAC. Therefore, the paper’s conclusions that MI-based coupling “is strengthened during development” are not sufficiently supported by the described methodology. "Neuronal oscillations with Non-sinusoidal Morphology Produce Spurious Phase-to-Amplitude Coupling" ([5](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27597822/)). This title and the associated abstract explicitly document that non-sinusoidal waveform morphology can generate spurious phase–amplitude coupling estimates. This is directly relevant because the reviewed paper’s MI is computed from Hilbert-based spectra of slow waves and HFO amplitude, a setting in which waveform asymmetries and harmonics can bias coupling metrics. The risk is especially salient in sleep iEEG, where slow waves can be highly non-sinusoidal and vary by region and age. Therefore, the reviewed paper should report diagnostic checks (e.g., waveform-shape metrics, harmonic/bicoherence assessments, or surrogate controls) to demonstrate that MI differences are not explained by non-sinusoidal morphology. The absence of such reporting leaves open a plausible alternative explanation for the observed developmental and occipital MI patterns. This undermines the validity of interpreting MI changes as strengthened physiological coupling. "it remains unclear to what extent PAC may reflect spurious cross-frequency coupling induced by physiological artifacts or rhythmic non-sinusoidal signals with higher harmonics." ([6](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33338621/)) This sentence from the broader PAC literature highlights two concrete confounds—physiological artifacts and non-sinusoidal harmonics—that can produce spurious PAC. The reviewed paper does not describe analyses that would specifically test for these confounds (e.g., distinguishing harmonic vs non-harmonic PAC, or quantifying spectral leakage/harmonicity), yet it uses MI as a core developmental biomarker. Because the main claims are regional (occipital vs others) and developmental (age dependence), any systematic regional or age-related difference in harmonic content could bias MI in the same pattern the paper attributes to development. This literature quote therefore directly motivates the need for additional controls that are not documented in the Methods section. Without addressing these established PAC pitfalls, the paper’s MI-based developmental conclusions remain vulnerable to alternative explanations. This is a major issue because MI is the central signal used to construct the principal atlas and mechanistic narrative.
