The submission relies on a protected MATLAB file (`MI_HFO_function.p`) for its core analysis, which prevents peer review of the methodology and hinders reproducibility. ```matlab MI_HFO_function(fontsize,max_age,movie_time_range,Export_Movie,frame_number,Export_Fig,Snapshot_time,Tract1,Tract2,savename); ``` The main analysis script `code.m` calls `MI_HFO_function`, which is provided as a P-code file (`MI_HFO_function.p`) in the `code/MI_HFO/demo_code/` directory. P-code files are obfuscated, executable-only files in MATLAB. This prevents reviewers and readers from inspecting the actual implementation of the Modulation Index (MI) and High-Frequency Oscillation (HFO) detection algorithms, making it impossible to verify the correctness of the code or reproduce the results from source. "The analysis codes are available at https://github.com/kaz1126/flatten_map_and_tractography." While the supplementary material mentions a GitHub repository, the code provided in the submission package itself is obfuscated. For a paper claiming to provide a "normative atlas" and "publicly available data" [[1]](#ref1), full transparency of the processing pipeline is essential. The reliance on a "black box" function for the central analysis undermines the scientific validity and reproducibility of the study. References: [1] S. Petkoski et al., "Developmental atlas of phase-amplitude coupling between physiologic high-frequency oscillations and slow waves," Preprint.

The study normalizes electrode locations from infants (as young as 1 year) to an adult brain template (`FSaverage`), which introduces significant spatial errors due to the non-linear developmental changes in cortical folding and surface area. "In order to pool sites from all patients, it was necessary to normalize the electrode locations to the standardized FSaverage brain surface (http://surfer.nmr.mgh.harvard.edu)." The `FSaverage` template is derived from adult brains. Mapping infant brains, which have significantly different gyrification patterns, surface areas, and brain volumes, to this adult template is known to introduce substantial spatial distortions [[2]](#ref2). The paper mentions using Infant FreeSurfer for reconstruction but then maps everything to the adult `FSaverage` for the atlas. This likely results in mislocalization of electrode sites, particularly in regions with high developmental plasticity, thereby compromising the accuracy of the proposed "developmental atlas". "For patients younger than two, we used the Infant FreeSurfer software package to reconstruct the surface image" While the reconstruction uses an age-appropriate tool, the subsequent normalization to an adult template negates this benefit for the group-level atlas. The spatial correspondence between a 1-year-old brain and an adult brain is not linear or simple, and using a standard adult surface registration for such a wide age range (1.0 to 41.5 years) without a specific pediatric template or more advanced registration method is a major methodological weakness. References: [2] A. Ghosh et al., "Evaluating the validity of volume-based and surface-based brain image registration for developmental cognitive neuroscience studies in children 4 to 11 years of age," Neuroimage, vol. 53, no. 1, pp. 85–93, 2010. [https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20451621/](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20451621/)

The study's conclusions regarding "HFO" developmental changes are heavily dependent on the specific detector used (Hilbert), with other detectors (MNI) showing contradictory results, suggesting that the "HFOs" in the occipital lobe may represent continuous physiological high-frequency activity rather than discrete events. "HFO MNI ≥80 Hz rate was very low in the occipital lobes throughout all ages; this MNI method-specific observation can be attributed to its detector being designed to be agnostic to persistent forms of high-frequency activity" The authors acknowledge that the MNI detector, which is designed to detect discrete events and ignore continuous activity, finds very low HFO rates in the occipital lobe. This directly contradicts the findings from the Hilbert detector, which shows enhancement. This discrepancy suggests that the signal driving the "HFO" results in the occipital lobe is likely continuous high-frequency activity (high gamma), not discrete HFOs [[3]](#ref3). "HFO HIL ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: +1.8 [95%CI: +1.7 to +1.9]; uncorrected p-value: < 0.001" The paper builds its main narrative around the Hilbert detector results ("HFO HIL ≥80 Hz rate was physiologically enhanced"). However, by conflating continuous high-frequency activity with "HFOs" (which are typically defined as transient events in epilepsy literature), the paper introduces terminological ambiguity. If the phenomenon is continuous, it should be characterized as such, rather than being presented under the umbrella of "HFOs", which implies a specific type of transient event often associated with pathology. References: [3] S. Ray and J. H. Maunsell, "Different origins of gamma rhythm and high-gamma activity in macaque visual cortex," PLoS Biology, vol. 9, no. 4, p. e1000610, 2011. [https://journals.plos.org/plosbiology/article?id=10.1371/journal.pbio.1000610](https://journals.plos.org/plosbiology/article?id=10.1371/journal.pbio.1000610)

The manuscript reports uncorrected p-values for key findings in the text, despite stating a policy of using FDR correction, which makes it difficult to assess the true statistical significance of the results given the multiple comparisons involved. "We considered an FDR-corrected p-value of less than 0.05 as significant, for comparisons of six iEEG measures." The Methods section establishes a threshold based on False Discovery Rate (FDR) correction. This is appropriate given the multiple measures and regions tested. "HFO MNI ≥80 Hz mixed model effect: -0.044 [95%CI: -0.90 to +3.0 x 10⁻³]; uncorrected p-value: 0.067" In the Results section, the authors consistently report "uncorrected p-value". For example, the p-value of 0.067 is reported as uncorrected. It is unclear if the "significant" findings (e.g., p < 0.001) remain significant after FDR correction, or if the authors are selectively reporting uncorrected values to claim trends. Standard practice requires reporting the corrected p-values or explicitly stating which values survived the correction threshold defined in the Methods. "The Spearman correlation failed to demonstrate a significant correlation between this regression slope and patient age in any lobe (uncorrected p-value: >0.05)." Reporting uncorrected p-values > 0.05 is fine to show non-significance, but for significant claims, the lack of corrected values is problematic.

The authors claim that the observed increase in occipital MI implies a strengthening of visual memory consolidation, which is an unsupported leap from structural/electrophysiological data to cognitive function without any behavioral assessment. "Given that occipital delta-nested HFO are believed to support visual memory consolidation, our observations imply that process may be significantly strengthened during early childhood." This conclusion is based entirely on the observed increase in Modulation Index (MI) and structural connectivity (white matter tracts). The study did not perform any memory tasks or behavioral assessments. Inferring a functional cognitive change ("process may be significantly strengthened") solely from resting-state/sleep electrophysiology and structural MRI is speculative. While the authors phrase it as an implication, it is presented as a major conclusion of the study ("Our study suggests that..."). "one possible explanation for the developmental enhancement of occipital MI is that the cerebral cortex learns to generate HFO... optimizing visual memory consolidation" While this is a valid hypothesis for discussion, presenting it as a primary explanation for the atlas features oversteps the data. Alternative explanations, such as simple maturation of local inhibitory networks or changes in skull conductivity/geometry affecting signal properties, are not sufficiently ruled out to prioritize a specific cognitive function like "visual memory consolidation" [[4]](#ref4). References: [4] G. Buzsáki and F. L. da Silva, "High frequency oscillations in the intact brain," Progress in Neurobiology, vol. 98, no. 3, pp. 241–249, 2012. [https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3429666/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3429666/)

The interpretation of MI as indicator for visual memory consolidation is somewhat speculative: The visual memory consolidation was not tested in these patients but only inferred from the literature. The conclusions with respect to epilepsy surgery should be more cautious.
The iEEG figures must be improved. All the values given in the legend should be presented in a table and not in the figure panel.

The authors use the same y-axis in all plots for better comparison across brain areas. This puts many points close to y=0 so that they cannot be discerned. I suggest a log10 y-scale combined with a log10 x-scale. Then the fit of a power law will appear as a straight line with the slope being the exponent. Given the three linear regressions used in this study (age, square root of age, and log10 age), this will be more illustrative. At the same time, it will allow a visual judgement of the regression, which is not possible now for many subplots.

The three patients >20years appear as outliers. Please redo all analyses without these three patients to verify that you results are valid for your core set of patients, which is aged \( \leq 20 \) years.

The authors record iEEG with 1000 Hz. This precludes analysis of spectral content >250Hz. All their text on HFO >250Hz (Fast Ripples) is unfounded speculation not based on their data. This text should simply be removed.
Please read the reference (Zweiphenning et al., 2022a) closely and summarize it correctly. The current summary is wrong.

Please remove the older reviews on HFO and replace them by (Chen Z, Maturana MI, Burkitt AN, Cook MJ, Grayden DB. High-frequency Oscillations in Epilepsy: What Have We Learned and What Needs to be Addressed. Neurology. 2021. https://doi.org/10.1212/wnl.0000000000011465). I recommend reading that review closely. While the study analyses four types of HFO for which detectors are available in the toolbox, their value for epilepsy surgery is still debated.

While the conclusions regarding epilepsy appear in the abstract, they should be made explicit at the end of the manuscript. Of course, they must be cautious and limited to the data provided in this study. Explicit conclusions will help the reader to understand the noteworthiness of the study.

The writing is generally good, but I believe it can be much more structured (adhering strictly to intro-methods-results sections and use the same structures within). I will mention some examples in my comments below.
Sentences can be shorter. E.g., words like ‘only’ or ‘in this study’ are often redundant and words like ‘as well as’ can be shortened to ‘and’
Abstract/manuscript: iEEG included only subdural (electrocorticography) recordings. This should be specified, already in the abstract

Introduction: ‘independent effects of patient and epilepsy profiles’: how is epilepsy profiles an independent effect? I could not find the definition of ‘epilepsy profiles’ in methods, but it is used several ties in abstract, introduction and results.

Introduction: ‘In addition to modeling cortical ontogenic changes, the present study also visualized 140 white matter pathways supporting the development of neural communications via delta141 nested HFO (as rated by MI≥80 Hz & 0.5-1 Hz), during slow-wave sleep. To accomplish this, we 142 utilized our novel imaging technique, dynamic tractography, which combines iEEG signals 143 with MRI tractography’ This is a methods-like description while in the introduction I want to hear why this extra step was taken and why the specific methods.
Methods: a sample rate of 1000 Hz is low to recognize HFOs>250 Hz

Methods/results: the methods describe that 114 patients will be selected and then the inclusion criteria. I want to know how these 114 patients resulted from how many patients in the beginning. It seems that the period is the strict criterium, so than 114 patients is a result from the selection.

Methods/results: To ensure the accuracy of the developmental iEEG 192 atlases, we only included artifact-free iEEG segments from 8,251 nonepileptic electrode sites 193 (mean: 72.4 per patient; range: 12 to 121;) Here again, the number of used/selected channels is a result. I want to know how many channels were discarded because of artefacts, SOZ, MRI, irritative zones.

survived an FDR correction (for 18 comparisons: six iEEG measures × three types of age measures): I wonder if an FDR is really needed here because both the iEEG measures and the types of age measures are dependant variables. It is good to reduce the change of finding false positive relations but high correction for false discoveries may yield false negative results. This should be discussed in the discussion (Wagenmakers E et al Nature 2022 one statistical analysis must not rule them all).

I have to two main criticisms to this work: 1. the absence of analysis on the pathological channels and the claim of a better definition of the EZN
2. the lack of evidence supporting the hypothesis that visual memory consolidation and the strengthen of the MI in the occipital lobe are linked

I suggest dedicating more place in the paper to the neurocognitive aspect and the hypothesis possibly associated with this finding than to the need of improving the epileptogenic biomarkers. Indeed, the HFO and MI analysis is done on the “physiological” channels, so no information is done on the real identification of the EZN in these patients. If you claim this, you should also analyse the epileptogenic channels.

For example, in the abstract and introduction you mention that this atlas could improve the “age-appropriate localization of the epileptogenic zone”, I’m not sure that your results can assure this, it is more an adding information on physiological developmental activities as no data support a better delineation of the EZN in these patients.

From a practical point of view, I find difficult to use these results in the clinical practice for the definition of the epileptogenic zone, but they are really important for the understanding of the visual memory process. As mentioned in the paper, HFO in the occipital lobe are found at a very high frequency rate in the physiological brain and it is difficult to use these markers for the definition of the EZN in this area.

The authors analysed a large cohort of patients (n=114) including very young children (n= 14 < 4 years old) studied by iEEG and operated with an Engel class I in the follow up. The brain area analysed were the “physiological” ones, so the ones not involved in the EZN.

It will be of interest to compare the MI in the “physiological” occipital lobe with the MI in the “pathological” ones and possibly with neuropsychological evaluations. Does it exist a “threshold” of MI that distinguishes the physiological from pathological channels? Do neurophysiological data correlate with neuropsychological examination?

If MI is involved in visual memory consolidation, children with visual memory impairment may possibly show modifications in this index. I think more data is needed to support your hypothesis on visual memory.

I found it limiting not to define better the criteria of \"slow wave sleep\". In animals, there is no clear consensus but in humans, it should correspond to the criteria of slow wave sleep N3 according to the AASM (American academy of sleep medicine) and specify if it is the first or second part of the night at least because the proportion of slow waves is not the same. It would also be necessary to specify on which way they determined the stage of sleep, knowing that the amplitude of the slow waves can be different according to the regions.

Data are collected during sleep; do you think that dreams rather than the sleep modifications during this early stage of life can play a role? Did you look to spindles activities? Could be MI involved in such a change? Occipito-parietal regions are also taught to be involved in consciousness (Siclari et al., Nat Neurosci 2017), do you think your findings could be somehow linked to such experiences in the young child?

It seems that the mesial part of the fronto-parietal lobes behaves differently from the lateral ones, did you try to separate these areas in your statistical analysis? In particular, hippocampus is known to have physiological ripples especially during sleep involved in memory processing, so maybe it deserves a different approach that being included in all the temporal lobe.

Limitations are represented by the subdural iEEG technique that evaluates more the cortical aspects of the brain, without a good sampling i.e. of the mesial temporal regions that are fundamental for memory consolidation; this limits to me the word “global atlas” because some important regions are not sufficiently sampled.

I could not find the ancillary analysis of the rates of HFO>150 hz and 250 Hz that could indeed be of interest regarding the difference between pathological and physiological HFO even if done on a smaller cohort of patients.
Regarding occipital HFA independently of epileptogenic area, you can cite also Melani 2013 Continuous High Frequency Activity: A peculiar SEEG pattern related to specific brain regions.
What do you mean with the word “enhanced MI?” could you better clarify?

The paper develops and validates predictive models using in vitro stool-derived microbial communities that completely lack host immune components known to be essential mediators of colonization resistance. This fundamentally limits the translational relevance of the findings to actual human gut colonization. "To systematically study colonization outcomes in complex microbial communities, we established large-scale cultivation of human stool-derived in vitro communities in multi-well plates" The in vitro experimental system captures only microbe-microbe interactions and lacks the host compartment entirely. This is a substantial simplification because colonization resistance in vivo involves bidirectional host-microbiota interactions that cannot be recapitulated in a static fermentation system. "Our results suggest that the colonization resistance of microbial communities is predictable and tunable via the data-driven approach" This conclusion overstates the applicability of the findings. The predictability demonstrated pertains to in vitro systems where only microbial competition matters, but extrapolating this to claims about "microbial communities" broadly (including in vivo scenarios) is not warranted given the experimental design. "Colonization resistance is a fundamental host defense mechanism that relies on the synergistic interaction between the gut microbiota and the host immune system to prevent enteric pathogen colonization and infection... The gut microbiota provides direct protection by occupying ecological niches, competing for nutrients, producing bacteriocins and engaging in contact-dependent inhibition... The host contributes through physical and chemical mucosal barriers as well as innate and adaptive immune responses." from [Li & Guo (2025)][1] Recent literature explicitly establishes that colonization resistance requires both microbial and host immune components working synergistically. The in vitro system used in the paper captures only the microbiota component while entirely missing the host immunity component, which includes IL-22-mediated antimicrobial peptide production, epithelial glycosylation, secretory IgA, and mucus barrier dynamics—all known to shape colonization outcomes. "ILC3 deficiency increased epithelial galactosylation, facilitating overgrowth of the mucin-degrader A. muciniphila... This reveals that ILC3-derived IL-22 controls broader glycosylation patterns that shape microbial community structure and pathogen behavior." from [Li & Guo (2025)][1] A. muciniphila, one of the two invading species tested in the paper, is specifically regulated by ILC3-IL-22 host pathways through epithelial glycosylation. The in vitro system cannot capture these dynamics, yet the paper draws conclusions about A. muciniphila colonization predictability that may not translate to in vivo contexts where host immune regulation plays a role. References: [1] Li, N. & Guo, X. (2025). The gut microbiota and host immunity synergistically orchestrate colonization resistance. *Gut Microbes*. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12773493/

The PERMANOVA analysis shows that baseline community composition explains only 2-3% of variance in colonization outcomes (R² = 0.03 for E. faecium, R² = 0.022 for A. muciniphila), yet the paper claims that colonization outcomes are "predictable" from baseline composition with AUROC values of 0.86. This apparent contradiction is not adequately addressed, raising questions about what the machine learning models are actually capturing and whether the baseline composition alone drives the observed classification performance. "Principal component analysis (PCoA) based on the Bray-Curtis dissimilarity of the compositional profiles of baseline communities. The difference between permissive and resistant communities was significant (PERMANOVA Adonis test, R² = 0.03, p < 0.0001)" An R² of 0.03 indicates that baseline community composition explains only 3% of the variance between permissive and resistant communities for E. faecium colonization. While statistical significance was achieved (p < 0.0001), the effect size is notably small. Standard interpretations suggest that R² below 0.1 indicates weak relationships. "The difference between highly permissive and lowly permissive communities was significant (PERMANOVA Adonis test, R² = 0.022, p < 0.001)" Similarly, for A. muciniphila, the baseline composition explains only 2.2% of variance between high and low permissive communities. Such small effect sizes suggest that factors other than community composition contribute substantially to colonization outcomes. "Random Forest classifier displayed the best performance in predicting whether E. faecium could successfully colonize based on the species-level community composition (AUROC=0.86)" The paper reports high classification performance (AUROC=0.86) while the PERMANOVA R² is only 0.03. While these metrics measure different aspects—AUROC measures discrimination ability while R² measures variance explained—the stark difference warrants discussion. High AUROC with low R² can occur when there are subtle but consistent differences between groups; however, this means the models may rely on small marginal differences that could be sensitive to technical variation or may not reflect ecologically meaningful predictors. "Grazing clearly affects community composition, though it accounts for a relatively small amount of the variation in composition (5.6%)." from [Applied Multivariate Statistics in R - PERMANOVA chapter][2] Standard interpretation in ecological studies considers R² values around 5-6% as explaining "a relatively small amount" of variation. The R² values in this paper (2-3%) are even smaller, yet are used to support conclusions about predictability without acknowledging this limitation. References: [2] Applied Multivariate Statistics in R - PERMANOVA. https://uw.pressbooks.pub/appliedmultivariatestatistics/chapter/permanova/

The synthetic data validation uses the Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) model, for which the authors derive an analytical linear relation between baseline composition and post-invasion abundance (achieving Spearman ρ = 1.0). This creates an idealized scenario where the ground truth has a known linear structure. The paper acknowledges this linear relation "doesn't hold for other dynamical models" yet uses this validation to support broader claims about the data-driven approach's applicability to real biological systems with unknown, likely non-linear dynamics. "The analytically derived relation can fully explain the simulated colonization outcomes in GLV model (Fig.S1, Spearman correlation ρ = 1, p < 0.001)" When the analytical derivation produces a perfect correlation (ρ = 1) with simulated data, this means the synthetic validation is essentially testing whether machine learning can learn a known linear relationship. This is a much easier task than learning from real biological systems where the underlying dynamics are unknown and likely non-linear. "Although the linear relation doesn't hold for other dynamical models, it suggests that learning the mapping for COP is feasible by the data-driven approach" The paper explicitly acknowledges the limitation that the linear relation is specific to GLV dynamics, yet extrapolates from this to claim feasibility for the general data-driven approach. This logical leap is problematic because real microbial communities may follow dynamics with higher-order interactions, metabolic cross-feeding, and other non-linear processes that violate the GLV assumptions. "We generated synthetic data of colonization outcomes using the Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) model" The GLV model assumes pairwise linear interactions and lacks higher-order interactions, metabolic modeling, or spatiotemporal dynamics present in real gut communities. While GLV is a reasonable starting point, using perfect GLV fit as primary validation may overestimate the approach's effectiveness in more complex real-world scenarios. "Coexistence in diverse communities with higher-order interactions... In the generalization of the Lotka–Volterra model we consider, every species has an abundance that is fixed by its intrinsic growth rate and its self-regulation" from [Gibbs et al. (2022)][3] Recent ecological modeling literature has demonstrated that higher-order interactions play important roles in community dynamics and coexistence that are not captured by standard GLV models. By validating on GLV synthetic data, the paper tests an idealized scenario that may not reflect the complexity of real microbial communities. References: [3] Gibbs, T., et al. (2022). Coexistence in diverse communities with higher-order interactions. *PNAS*, 119(43), e2205063119. https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2205063119

The experimental validation tests only two invading species (E. faecium and A. muciniphila), both of which have specific ecological niches and characteristics. E. faecium is a facultative anaerobe that can tolerate harsh conditions, while A. muciniphila is a specialized mucin degrader. The generalizability of the data-driven approach to other invading species with different ecological strategies remains untested. "We conducted colonization experiments for two commensal gut bacteria species Enterococcus faecium and Akkermansia muciniphila in hundreds of human stool-derived in vitro microbial communities" Testing only two species limits the ability to make general claims about the data-driven approach for predicting colonization outcomes. Different invading species may rely on different ecological mechanisms (nutrient competition, antimicrobial production, niche occupation) that could require different features for prediction. "We conducted a preliminary experiment to investigate the colonization outcome of different exogenous species (Fig.S5). We found that E. faecium, A. muciniphila, and F. nucleatum could successfully colonize in some communities... In contrast, S. salivarius, B. breve, and Lactobacillus spp. could not colonize in nearly all the communities we tested" The preliminary experiments show that different species have very different colonization abilities. The fact that several species (S. salivarius, B. breve, Lactobacillus spp.) could not colonize any communities means the machine learning approach was not tested for these species. Species-specific biological factors may determine colonization success in ways that are not generalizable across taxa. "A. muciniphila... is a specialized mucin-degrader... ILC3-derived IL-22 controls broader glycosylation patterns that shape microbial community structure and pathogen behavior" from [Li & Guo (2025)][1] A. muciniphila's ecological niche as a mucin degrader makes it unique among gut bacteria. In the in vitro system lacking mucin dynamics regulated by host signals, the factors determining A. muciniphila colonization may differ substantially from in vivo scenarios. This limits the extent to which findings about A. muciniphila colonization prediction can be generalized. References: [1] Li, N. & Guo, X. (2025). The gut microbiota and host immunity synergistically orchestrate colonization resistance. *Gut Microbes*. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12773493/

The "colonization impact" analysis, which identifies species that influence invasion outcomes, is based on perturbing trained machine learning models rather than direct experimental measurements. While the E. faecalis-E. faecium interaction was validated experimentally, the colonization impacts for most other species remain model-based inferences that could reflect artifacts of the training data or model architecture rather than genuine biological interactions. "To compute the colonization impact, i.e., the impact of a resident species on the colonization outcome of the invading species, we first trained the prediction models using all the samples. Then, we performed a thought experiment by introducing a perturbation in the abundance of the resident species" The colonization impact metric is derived from model predictions under simulated perturbations rather than actual experimental perturbations. This approach assumes that the trained model accurately captures the causal relationships between resident species and colonization outcomes, which may not be the case if the model is fitting correlational patterns. "In comparison, Clostridium symbiosum, a species predicted to have a neutral impact, did not alter the colonization of E. faecium" The experimental validation tests only one positive case (E. faecalis inhibits E. faecium) and one negative control (C. symbiosum is neutral). The paper identifies multiple species with predicted strong colonization impacts (Figure 5D-G shows rankings of many species) but only one interaction is validated, leaving the accuracy of the other predictions unknown. "The median colonization impact of a resident species across different local communities was positively correlated to its interaction strength on the exogenous species (Spearman correlation coefficient ρ = 0.73)" This correlation is derived from synthetic GLV data where interaction strengths are known by construction. In real experimental data, the true interaction strengths are unknown, so the claim that colonization impact reflects genuine ecological interactions cannot be directly verified beyond the single E. faecalis validation.

The paper claims to "infer the impact of each resident species" using the machine learning model, but this inference is based on model sensitivity analysis and lacks comprehensive experimental validation. "Learning the mapping from the baseline taxonomic profile to colonization outcomes can help us infer the impact of each resident species on the colonization of the exogenous species" The authors use a "thought experiment" (perturbation analysis) on the trained model to infer interactions. While this reveals what the *model* uses for prediction, it does not necessarily reflect true ecological interactions, as machine learning models (especially Random Forest) can rely on correlated features rather than causal drivers. "strongly interacting species could significantly alter the colonization outcomes, e.g., the presence of Enterococcus faecalis inhibits the invasion of E. faecium." The authors validate this specific interaction (Fig. 3D, Fig. S16), but they do not validate the broader set of inferred interactions. Without broader validation (e.g., using mono-colonization or pairwise assays as in [2]), the claim that the method can reliably "infer the impact of each resident species" is overreached. The method generates hypotheses, but the "inferred impact" should not be equated with confirmed ecological interaction without further proof. References: [2] Weiss, A. S. et al. In vitro interaction network of a synthetic gut bacterial community. ISME J 16, 1095-1109 (2022). https://www.nature.com/articles/s41396-021-01153-z

L. 338-340: \"We demonstrate that the data-driven approach enables accurate function prediction and system-level understanding of complex microbial communities.\" This is a big claim. Which systems-level understanding did the authors gain? They did not learn the mechanism behind the interaction between E. faecalis and E. faecium. The advantage of knowing mechanisms is that the model is better able to deal with changes in conditions, such as lower pH or different nutrient levels. A machine learning approach may work in a given situation but will have to be retrained when circumstances, such as the medium, change. This weakness of the data-driven approach is not discussed.

The gLV simulations deviate from the real case in an important point: the exogenous species may have been already present in the stool communities, perhaps below detection level. In that case, not the colonisation itself is of interest but the boost given to the \"exogenous\" species (which may be due to some density-dependent mechanism). This issue needs to be discussed. Another problem is that no noise was added to the simulated data. However, sequencing comes with large technical variation, so it is important to assess the robustness of the machine learning tools to such variation.

Another limitation to be discussed is the presence of mucin in liquid form. I assume this was necessary to enable the growth of A. muciniphila, but it deviates from the biology of the intestinal system, where A. muciniphila prefers to colonise (solid) mucin and other gut bacteria prefer the lumen. It would be of interest to find out to what extent A. muciniphila's ability to colonise the tested communities depends on the mucin concentration in the medium.
To what extent maybe simpler statistical models (including LASSO regression) still predict the data? Are there less data-intensive methods that could still provide accurate predictions?
Why does random-forest performs poorly in the regression task with the Lotka-Volterra simulations but it is the best-performing algorithm in real communities? Any hypotheses?
L100- I wonder if there is a way to map these variables in figure 1 for extra clarity.
L170 - State in the Lotka-Volterra results that random forest performs poorly at all sizes.
Figure 1. Make invading species clearer, maybe the lines of other species in the community should be shades of gray.
Figure 2B. Is this real data? Which communities are these?

Throughout the work, the authors use Receiver Operator Characteristic (ROC) curves to evaluate the accuracy of their colonization classifiers, and conclude that classification is accurate when the area under the curve ROC curve (AUROC) is greater than 0.8 (line 155).

While the use of these methods is standard in machine learning, I worry that using unqualified terms like “accurately predict” / “successfully predict” / “predictable” as a stand in for this technical definition might be confusing for microbial ecologists, who might be more inclined to interpret them according to their standard English usage.

What the manuscript strongly shows is that the trained classifiers are better than random guessing (AUROC=0.5), but there is a large gap between that and “successful prediction”. I think the manuscript would be improved if the authors could revise their language throughout the work to make their accuracy limitations more clear. E.g., a plain reading of the current version of the abstract might give the impression that there are no accuracy limitations at all. The use of “predictable” in the section heading on line 196 is similarly confusing, as is the use of “can solve the classification problem” on line 233 . The results are already impressive, so some extra clarity on this front would be helpful for avoiding confusion within the field.

In addition to clarifying the language, it would be helpful to provide some guidance for how ROC curves and AUROC scores translate to accuracy in real experiments. E.g. what is the best total accuracy (true positives + true negatives / total) that can be achieved for a given classifier, regardless of its internal threshold? (this would work well for balanced examples like the one here, but would need to be tweaked for less permissive colonization)

Likewise, to get some intuition for the AUC scores / accuracy scores, it could be useful to compare the machine learning methods to an even simpler classifier based on total community diversity. Given the results of Figs 3B and 4B, this naïve classifier might do pretty well...

For the quantitative prediction task (predicting the relative abundance at steady state), the authors use the Pearson correlation as a measure of accuracy. I was confused the correlation metric was used, rather than raw sum of squared differences between the observed and predicted values. (The latter would seem to more closely match what one would be looking for in an experiment.)\n\nAlso, I couldn’t tell from the methods whether the R^2 values were calculated from the relative abundances or their log transformed values; Fig 4H-J suggests the latter but would be good to clarify.
I couldn’t tell from the methods how the perturbations used to define “colonization impact” (CI) scores were chosen. What were the magnitudes and/or signs of the perturbations?

It is known that GLV models exhibit limit cycles and chaotic dynamics, so that they do not always have a steady state. How would you apply the method in that case? Were the parameters chosen in a special way to avoid this behavior?
I couldn’t tell how the self-interaction terms were chosen in the GLV model. Please clarify.

The GLV model works with absolute abundances, while the data are compositional (relative abundance). Could this influence the differences between the colonization interactions in Fig. 5B? Ideally, the authors could convert their GLV abundances into relative abundances to make their simulations more like their data. Is the analytical result in Eq. 4 still valid in the compositional domain?
Similar results to Eq. 4 have been utilized in cavity method approaches to GLV models (e.g. Bunin Phys Rev E 2017). These should be cited around line 158-160, along with the new derivation.

If I understand correctly, the result in follows almost immediately from the definition of the GLV model (similar to Eq. 2), if we make the additional assumption that A is invertible. This seems like a pretty strong assumption, so it might be best to say that *some* GLV models satisfy this derivation. Are the parameter chosen in a special way here to ensure that A is invertible?
The use of \"established\" on line 174 does not seem appropriate, given that similar experiments were performed in the cited reference. I’d suggest changing this to something like “used” instead.

The use of the term “the data driven approach” is confusing, since there are many possible ways to use data to inform prediction. I’d suggesting changing this to “data driven approaches” or “our data-driven approach”, depending on the context, so that readers will know what is being referred to.

Related to the previous point, several earlier studies have used data-driven approaches to predict competition and colonization gut microbiome-like communities (e.g. Buffie et al Nature 2015, Venturelli et al Mol Sys Biol 2018). They used slightly more parameterized models (e.g. GLV in this case) but are still conceptually related to the approaches present work. These contributions should be cited somewhere in the introduction (e.g. around line 80).

The paper directly compares computationally predicted bulk conductivity from kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulations with experimental total ionic conductivity measured from polycrystalline pellets, without adequately addressing that experimental measurements include grain boundary contributions that are not modeled in the simulations. "our measured experimental conductivity (see Figure 4b) of ~ 0.1 S cm⁻¹ at 573 K is in excellent agreement with our computed value of ~ 0.2 S cm⁻¹" This comparison treats computational and experimental values as directly comparable despite fundamental methodological differences. The kMC simulations model only bulk (grain interior) diffusion in idealized perfect crystals, while the experimental impedance spectroscopy measurements on polycrystalline pellets include both bulk and grain boundary contributions. The supplementary materials reveal pellet compacities of only 69-77% (Table S6), indicating significant porosity and grain boundary density. Standard practice in the solid electrolyte field is to separate bulk and grain boundary contributions when comparing with computational results, as grain boundaries can significantly reduce measured conductivity, particularly in NASICON-type materials. * Quote from supplementary materials (Table S6): "Compacity (%) x=1.5: 69.16; x=2.0: 71.21; x=2.4: 76.63" The low compacity values (less than 80%) indicate that the experimental pellets have significant porosity and grain boundary volume. In polycrystalline ceramics with such densification levels, the grain boundary contribution to total resistance can be substantial. The apparent "agreement" between computed (~0.2 S/cm) and experimental (~0.1 S/cm) values may be coincidental, as bulk conductivity in fully dense samples could be even higher than the experimental total conductivity, potentially indicating a larger discrepancy from computational predictions than suggested. "The fitting of Nyquist plot was carried out using ZView software. For x = 1.5, the equivalent circuit used was based on semicircle phenomena... Applying the same equivalent circuit for other samples (x = 2.0 and x = 2.4) was not satisfactory, thus a one-phenomena Debye Model for solid electrolyte between two ionically blocking electrodes is used instead." The use of different equivalent circuits for different compositions suggests that the impedance spectra could not be consistently analyzed to separate bulk and grain boundary contributions. The one-phenomena Debye model used for x = 2.0 and 2.4 compositions does not separate these contributions, yet the paper claims excellent agreement with bulk conductivity calculations. Ma et al. [[1]](#citation-1) demonstrated that for NASICON materials, bulk grain conductivity can exceed 0.01 S/cm at room temperature, significantly higher than total conductivity measured from typical polycrystalline samples. References: [1] Ma, Q. et al. Room temperature demonstration of a sodium superionic conductor with grain conductivity in excess of 0.01 S cm⁻¹ and its primary applications in symmetric battery cells. [*J. Mater. Chem. A* **7**, 7766–7776 (2019)](https://doi.org/10.1039/C9TA00048H)

The kinetic Monte Carlo simulations are exclusively based on the high-temperature rhombohedral (R3̄c) NASICON structure, yet experimental measurements at compositions x = 2.0 and x = 2.4 show clear monoclinic-to-rhombohedral phase transitions at ~433 K that significantly affect activation energies, creating a fundamental mismatch between simulation conditions and experimental reality. "In the rhombohedral high-temperature NASICON structure (R3̄c), there are two independent and partially occupied sodium sites, Na(1) and Na(2)." The paper's theoretical framework and kMC model are explicitly built on the rhombohedral structure. This is appropriate for high-temperature simulations but not for comparing with experimental data measured across the phase transition. The monoclinic phase (C2/c) has different Na site orderings, local environments, and transport pathways that are not captured by the R3̄c-based model. "Both compositions at x = 2.0 and x = 2.4 show two linear domains with different activation energies displayed in Figure 4b. Thus, the Arrhenius plots of Figure 4b reproduce the documented monoclinic-to-rhombohedral phase transition upon heating for the compositions Na₃Zr₂Si₂P₁O₁₂, and Na₃.₄Zr₂Si₂.₄P₀.₆O₁₂ at ~ 433 ± 15 K." The experimental data clearly shows two distinct activation energy regimes (0.38 eV below and 0.22 eV above ~433 K) corresponding to the monoclinic and rhombohedral phases respectively. However, the kMC simulations were performed at 373, 473, and 573 K using only the R3̄c structure. At 373 K, the experimental samples are in the monoclinic phase with higher activation energy, but simulations model the rhombohedral phase. This fundamental inconsistency undermines direct comparisons between computed and measured conductivity at lower temperatures. * Quote from Figure 4a caption: "In panel a, X-Ray diffraction pattern (dark blue, orange, and red circles), and Le Bail fitting (solid lines in cyan, dark brown and dark red) for synthesized Na₁₊ₓZr₂SiₓP₃₋ₓO₁₂ (x = 1.5, 2.0, and 2.4)" The XRD patterns confirm that at room temperature, compositions x = 2.0 and 2.4 adopt the monoclinic C2/c structure, not the rhombohedral R3̄c structure used in simulations. While phase transitions in NASICON are well-documented [[2]](#citation-2), the computational model does not account for the different ionic transport characteristics in the low-temperature monoclinic phase, which is structurally distinct and shows different Na migration pathways. References: [2] Jolley, A. G., Taylor, D. D., Schreiber, N. J. & Wachsman, E. D. Structural Investigation of Monoclinic-Rhombohedral Phase Transition in Na3Zr2Si2PO12 and Doped NASICON. [*J. Am. Ceram. Soc.* **98**, 2902–2907 (2015)](https://doi.org/10.1111/jace.13692)

The kinetic Monte Carlo simulations employ a fixed attempt frequency (ν = 0.5 × 10¹³ Hz) without justification, sensitivity analysis, or acknowledgment that this parameter directly affects the absolute values of predicted diffusivity and conductivity by orders of magnitude. * Quote from code (run_kmc.py, line 16): `v = 0.5*10**13` The attempt frequency is hard-coded as a constant value of 5 THz across all compositions and temperatures. In transition state theory, the attempt frequency (also called the pre-exponential factor or vibrational prefactor) relates to phonon frequencies at the transition state and can vary significantly between different materials, compositions, and even different migration paths within the same material. The absolute diffusivity D and conductivity σ scale linearly with this parameter, meaning any error in the attempt frequency propagates directly to all predicted transport properties. * Quote from Methods section: "We implemented a rejection-free kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulation scheme in an in-house-code, as described in Refs.³⁹,⁴⁰" While the paper references the theoretical framework for kMC, it does not discuss how the attempt frequency was determined or validated. The referenced works (Van der Ven et al. [[3]](#citation-3)) describe the methodology but the specific choice of 5 THz for NASICON is not justified. Studies on ionic conductors show that attempt frequencies can range from 10¹² to 10¹⁴ Hz depending on the system, and this variation alone could shift predicted conductivity by up to an order of magnitude. Without uncertainty quantification for this parameter, the reported "excellent agreement" with experiment cannot be rigorously evaluated. "The grey band in panel b shows the interval of confidence of our predictions at 573 K, which is lower in magnitude than the variance of Na-ion conductivities observed experimentally" While the paper presents confidence intervals for conductivity predictions, these reflect only the statistical variance from different Si/P configurations, not systematic uncertainties from model parameters like the attempt frequency. A proper uncertainty analysis would need to propagate errors from all assumed parameters. He et al. [[4]](#citation-4) established rigorous procedures for quantifying statistical variances in diffusion property calculations, emphasizing that absolute values are sensitive to multiple computational parameters beyond just configurational sampling. References: [3] Van der Ven, A., Ceder, G., Asta, M. & Tepesch, P. D. First-principles theory of ionic diffusion with nondilute carriers. [*Phys. Rev. B* **64**, 184307 (2001)](https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevB.64.184307) [4] He, X., Zhu, Y., Epstein, A. & Mo, Y. Statistical variances of diffusional properties from ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. [*npj Comput. Mater.* **4**, 18 (2018)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41524-018-0074-y)

The paper performs extensive kMC simulations across 11 Na compositions spanning the full range of 0 ≤ x ≤ 3, but experimental validation is limited to only three compositions (x = 1.5, 2.0, 2.4), leaving the majority of the computational predictions, particularly at low Na content (x < 1.5) where conductivity is orders of magnitude lower, without experimental verification. "In total, more than 2,000 kMC simulations were performed to predict the Na⁺ diffusivities and conductivities at selected temperatures of 373, 473 and 573 K of 50 NASICON models (with varying Si/P configurations) at 11 distinct Na concentrations." The computational study systematically covers 11 compositions (x = 0.03, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.1, 2.4, 2.7, 2.97), representing a significant investment in computational resources. However, this extensive compositional mapping is validated experimentally at only three points (x = 1.5, 2.0, 2.4), all in the high-conductivity regime. The predictions for low-Na compositions where conductivity is predicted to be 4-6 orders of magnitude lower remain entirely unvalidated. "We have synthesized different compositions of the NASICON, namely, Na₂.₅Zr₂Si₁.₅P₁.₅O₁₂, Na₃Zr₂Si₂P₁O₁₂, and Na₃.₄Zr₂Si₂.₄P₀.₆O₁₂ and tested their Na-ion transport characteristics." The three experimentally studied compositions all fall within the high-conductivity regime (x = 1.5 to 2.4). Key predictions about the sharp rise in conductivity from x = 0 to x ≈ 2 and the drop at x > 2.4 are not experimentally validated in this work. While low-conductivity compositions are more challenging to synthesize phase-pure and to measure accurately, the paper's strong conclusions about the composition-conductivity relationship across the full range would benefit from broader experimental validation. Prior literature exists for end-member compositions [[5]](#citation-5) but direct comparison under identical synthesis and measurement conditions is lacking. "Our model also agrees with previous measurements¹⁸,¹⁹,³⁰,³² which report a low conductivity and diffusivity in Na₁Zr₂P₃O₁₂." For the low-Na end-member, the paper relies on literature comparison rather than direct experimental validation in this study. Given the paper's emphasis on the large variability in reported conductivity values across different studies ("Figure S6 reveals a significant spread of experimentally measured ionic conductivities from different reports"), validating the full compositional range under controlled conditions would strengthen the conclusions about the optimal x ~ 2.4 composition. References: [5] Goodenough, J. B., Hong, H. Y.-P. & Kafalas, J. A. Fast Na⁺-ion transport in skeleton structures. [*Mater. Res. Bull.* **11**, 203–220 (1976)](https://doi.org/10.1016/0025-5408(76)90077-5)

This paper’s central “bridge the gap” claim is undermined because it compares kMC-predicted **bulk** conductivities against experimentally extracted **total** pellet conductivities without consistently separating bulk vs grain-boundary/contact contributions, weakening the validity of the reported quantitative agreement. Figure 2 | Computed Na-ion transport properties of Na_{1+x}Zr_2Si_xP_{3-x}O_{12} bulk based on kinetic Monte Carlo simulations. This explicitly frames the simulated conductivities as **bulk** properties, i.e., an intrinsic crystal-level transport prediction. Bulk conductivity is not directly comparable to a pellet’s apparent conductivity unless microstructural contributions (grain boundaries, porosity, contacts) are removed or modeled. The paper later states it fits impedance data with different equivalent circuits across compositions, which can alter what component is being extracted. Without a clear, composition-consistent decomposition of bulk vs non-bulk impedance features, agreement may reflect compensating errors rather than a validated model. In solid electrolyte practice, reporting and comparing “bulk” and “total” conductivities requires careful impedance interpretation and often separate semicircle attribution. As written, the comparison risks overstating the strength of the theory–experiment reconciliation. An example of a Nyquist plot used to extract the total ionic conductivity from the sintered pellets of the NASICON compositions at variable temperatures (373 K, 473 K, and 573 K) is given in Figure 4c. This sentence states the experimental quantity being extracted is **total ionic conductivity** from sintered pellets, which generally includes multiple resistive contributions (bulk, grain boundary, contact) depending on microstructure and fitting model. Total pellet conductivity is known to be sensitive to density/porosity and grain-boundary chemistry, so it is not equivalent to single-crystal/bulk transport. The paper does not show a consistent procedure that isolates the bulk arc for every composition/temperature (and it even changes the circuit model across x), making the extracted “total” values non-uniform in meaning. Because the simulations intentionally omit grain boundaries and porosity, comparing them directly to total pellet values conflates fundamentally different transport regimes. This is especially consequential because the paper’s main narrative hinges on quantitative agreement “in line with our predictions.” The evidence therefore supports that the reported agreement is not methodologically well-grounded.
x Mass (g) Thickness (cm) Surface area (cm2) Compacity (%)
1.5 0.16 0.23 0.31 69.16
2 0.16 0.23 0.30 71.21
2.4 0.15 0.20 0.29 76.63
These reported pellet “Compacity (%)” values (≈69–77%) indicate the samples are not fully dense, implying significant porosity and potentially large tortuosity effects. In solid electrolytes, porosity and imperfect densification can reduce the measured macroscopic conductivity and distort impedance spectra (e.g., depressed semicircles, broadened distributions). This makes it even less defensible to treat the extracted pellet conductivity as a direct proxy for intrinsic bulk transport. Moreover, the compacity differs across compositions, creating a confounding variable precisely where the paper claims composition-driven transport optimization. Without a correction for porosity/density or a microstructure-controlled comparison (e.g., similar relative density across samples), the composition trend in “total conductivity” may partially reflect processing artifacts. As a result, the bridge between computed bulk and measured pellet transport is not demonstrated with the rigor needed for strong conclusions. Na3+xZr2Si2+xP1−xO12 was the very first series of NASICONs discovered some 40 years ago; however, separation of bulk conductivity from total conductivity at room temperature is still problematic. This statement from an external NASICON conductivity study directly highlights that separating bulk from total conductivity is a recognized, nontrivial challenge in this material family ([1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2019/ta/c9ta00048h)). The reviewed paper’s own methodology explicitly uses “total ionic conductivity” from pellets, yet it uses the term “bulk” for its kMC outputs and treats agreement as validation. The external quote underscores that careful impedance deconvolution is required to make bulk-level statements from polycrystalline ceramics. By not presenting a consistent bulk/grain-boundary separation (or quantifying how circuit choice affects extracted values), the reviewed paper falls short of established caution in the literature. This matters because their claims extend beyond qualitative trends to numerical conductivities and “excellent agreement.” The discrepancy between best practice and the paper’s comparison approach supports the criticism as a major validity issue.

This paper’s temperature-dependent mechanistic conclusions for NASICON are not fully reliable because the kMC simulations freeze the host framework and appear to use a single lattice model, while the experiments explicitly exhibit a monoclinic-to-rhombohedral phase transition around 433 K that can change diffusion topology and barriers. In the rhombohedral high-temperature NASICON structure (\( R\overline{3}c \)), there are two independent and partially occupied sodium sites, Na(1) and Na(2). This text sets the structural basis of the transport discussion in the **high-temperature rhombohedral** phase, which is appropriate above the known transition temperature. However, the paper also evaluates and compares transport at 373 K, which is below the reported transition for x = 2.0 and 2.4 compositions (and thus likely in the monoclinic regime). If the computational lattice model remains rhombohedral across all temperatures, then the predicted low-temperature conductivities and inferred mechanisms are structurally inconsistent with the experimental state. Such a mismatch can alter not only quantitative values but also qualitative site connectivity and bottleneck geometry. Because the paper attributes trends to local polyanion environments, any structural-symmetry change is a major confounder. Therefore, anchoring the model in the high-T structure while interpreting sub-transition data raises a significant validity concern. Thus, the Arrhenius plots of Figure 4b reproduce the documented monoclinic-to-rhombohedral phase transition upon heating for the compositions Na<sub>3</sub>Zr<sub>2</sub>Si<sub>2</sub>P<sub>1</sub>O<sub>12</sub>, and Na<sub>3.4</sub>Zr<sub>2</sub>Si<sub>2.4</sub>P<sub>0.6</sub>O<sub>12</sub> at ~ 433 ± 15 K. This sentence confirms the experiments traverse a crystallographic phase transition within the paper’s own temperature window (373–573 K). A structural phase transition commonly changes site energies, migration pathways, and potentially the dominant conduction bottlenecks, meaning transport parameters are not expected to be smoothly extrapolatable without explicitly modeling the correct phase. The paper uses this transition to interpret two Arrhenius domains and activation energies, which indicates that structure is central to the experimental interpretation. Yet the computational description provided does not show a corresponding phase-aware modeling strategy (e.g., separate kMC lattices/parameters for monoclinic vs rhombohedral). As a result, the claimed agreement across temperatures may be coincidental or limited to the high-T regime. This is a major issue because the paper’s conclusions and proposed optimization strategies are framed as temperature-general within the studied window. During the kMC simulation, only Na ions can hop while the remaining atoms are frozen. Freezing the framework is a strong modeling approximation: it removes lattice vibrations, local relaxations around migrating ions, and any coupling between polyanion configurations and framework distortions. While lattice kMC can be appropriate when the host is rigid and a single phase is assumed, this paper’s experimental section explicitly involves a phase transition and discusses temperature-dependent activation energies. In such cases, framework degrees of freedom and symmetry changes can be essential to transport, so a frozen-host approximation may miss key physics at least in one regime. Additionally, freezing all non-Na atoms implicitly assumes that the migration bottleneck geometry is temperature-invariant, which is generally less justified near transitions. The paper does not provide an analysis showing that this approximation remains valid across both monoclinic and rhombohedral regimes. Hence, this sentence directly supports the criticism that the mechanistic conclusions across temperature are not fully justified. ```python prim_fname = '../inputs/prim.json' ... events_initialized = kmc.initialization(occ,prim_fname,fitting_results,fitting_results_site,events,supercell_shape,v,T) ``` This code snippet indicates the kMC simulation is initialized from a single structural template file (`prim.json`) rather than switching lattice models by temperature/phase. If the primitive lattice is fixed, then the simulation’s site network and geometric constraints are fixed as well, which is inconsistent with a scenario where the experimental crystal symmetry changes near 433 K. In other words, the code operationalizes a single-lattice, frozen-framework diffusion model across the temperature range. That choice may still reproduce some averaged conductivities, but it limits mechanistic inference about how transport changes across phases. Because the paper argues for reconciling theory and experiment over a range of temperatures, the phase-insensitive model is a substantial methodological gap. The code therefore provides concrete evidence that the phase-transition physics is unlikely to be captured as modeled. ```json "title" : "NASICON_prim", "lattice_vectors" : [ [4.593150 ,2.651856 , 7.393667], [-4.593150, 2.651856 , 7.393667], [-0.000000, -5.303713, 7.393667] ] ``` This JSON defines a single set of lattice vectors for the simulation lattice, reinforcing that the host geometry is fixed in the kMC runs. When a material undergoes a monoclinic-to-rhombohedral transition, the lattice parameters and symmetry relations change, which can alter diffusion channels and site connectivities. A fixed-lattice approach cannot represent such changes unless separate lattices are explicitly used and swapped by temperature. The paper does not describe such a two-lattice treatment, but instead reports simulations at 373, 473, and 573 K using the same kMC workflow. Therefore, the underlying structure used for transport at 373 K may not correspond to the experimentally present monoclinic phase for key compositions. This supports the criticism that the temperature-dependent conclusions are not fully validated. KMCLib - a general framework for lattice kinetic Monte Carlo (KMC) simulations This external description emphasizes that “lattice kMC” frameworks are explicitly built around a fixed set of lattice sites and events ([4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/leetmaa/KMCLib/master/README.md)). The reviewed paper likewise uses a lattice-event approach with predefined Na(1)↔Na(2) hops, which is consistent with standard lattice kMC practice. However, this also clarifies the limitation: such models do not naturally incorporate structural phase transitions unless the practitioner constructs separate lattices and event catalogs. Since the reviewed paper’s experimental behavior includes a documented phase transition within the modeled temperature range, the burden is on the authors to show phase-aware modeling or justify why a single lattice suffices. The lack of such justification makes the phase omission a significant threat to validity. The external quote thus supports the claim by situating the paper’s approach within a class of methods known to require special handling for phase changes.

The paper’s headline conductivity claims are potentially misleading because the abstract reports ~0.1 S cm^-1 for Na_{3.4}Zr_2Si_{2.4}P_{0.6}O_{12} without stating the temperature, while the main text indicates similarly large values only at elevated temperature (e.g., 573 K), weakening the robustness of the conclusions about “highest ionic conductivities” for practical solid-state battery conditions. We performed impedance spectroscopy of samples with varying Na compositions revealing that the highest ionic conductivity (\(~0.1\ S\ cm^{-1}\)) is achieved in Na_{3.4}Zr_2Si_{2.4}P_{0.6}O_{12}, in line with our predictions (\(~0.2\ S\ cm^{-1}\)). The abstract presents a very large conductivity value in S cm^-1 without specifying the temperature, which is critical because ionic conductivity in solids is strongly temperature dependent and commonly reported at a standard reference temperature (often ~25 °C) when discussing practical relevance. Without a temperature qualifier, a reader can reasonably interpret ~0.1 S cm^-1 as near-room-temperature performance, which would be extraordinary for oxide NaSICON ceramics. The main text later anchors comparable magnitudes at much higher temperatures, indicating that the abstract’s phrasing lacks essential context. Because the paper motivates the work partly by solid-state battery relevance, temperature ambiguity can materially distort the perceived significance. This is not a cosmetic issue: it affects whether the claimed performance is immediately actionable for typical operating conditions. Therefore, the omission undermines the clarity and can inflate the perceived impact. Nonetheless, for Na\(_3\)Zr\(_2\)Si\(_2\)P\(_1\)O\(_{12}\) our measured experimental conductivity (see Figure 4b) of ~ 0.1 S cm\(^{-1}\) at 573 K is in excellent agreement with our computed value of ~ 0.2 S cm\(^{-1}\). This sentence explicitly ties the ~0.1 S cm^-1 experimental value to **573 K**, which is far above room temperature. This directly shows that at least one of the headline-scale conductivities discussed in the paper occurs only at elevated temperature. When juxtaposed with the abstract’s temperature-free claim, it suggests the abstract compresses temperature-dependent performance into a single number without proper context. This matters because a conductivity of 0.1 S cm^-1 at 573 K does not imply similar performance at 298 K, given typical Arrhenius behavior and the paper’s own discussion of activation energies and phase transitions. As a result, the paper’s “highest ionic conductivity” statement is not robustly interpretable for practical conditions. The evidence supports that the narrative could overstate practical significance unless temperatures are made explicit. Measurements were recorded every 50 K from 323 K to 623 K (15 minutes soaking each time), and then cooled down every 50 K from 623 K to 323 K. This methods detail shows that the experimental impedance spectroscopy campaign is explicitly a **high-temperature** measurement protocol spanning 323–623 K. Given this, any claim of “highest ionic conductivity” should be reported together with the temperature (or as a room-temperature extrapolation with uncertainty), because the measured values are inherently temperature-indexed. The abstract does not clarify whether its 0.1 S cm^-1 refers to 373 K, 473 K, 573 K, or another temperature, even though the paper later emphasizes multiple Arrhenius regimes and a phase transition near 433 K. This ambiguity makes it hard to evaluate the claimed agreement between experiment and theory and to compare to the broader literature. In a field where room-temperature conductivity is often the benchmark for applicability, omitting temperature is a major interpretability flaw. The methods quote therefore supports that the headline performance statement is insufficiently grounded. ... using a solution-assisted solid-state reaction for preparation of Na3+xZr2Si2+xP1−xO12, a total conductivity of 5 × 10−3 S cm−1 was achieved for Na3.4Zr2Si2.4P0.6O12 at 25 °C... This external sentence provides a clear example of standard reporting practice: conductivity is explicitly stated **at 25 °C** and distinguished as “total conductivity” ([1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2019/ta/c9ta00048h)). It also gives a room-temperature benchmark for the same nominal composition family (including Na3.4Zr2Si2.4P0.6O12), reinforcing that temperature context substantially changes how large a reported conductivity is. The reviewed paper’s abstract does not provide an equivalent temperature qualifier, making its headline number hard to interpret relative to established room-temperature baselines. While different processing routes can yield different conductivities, the key issue here is that the external literature communicates temperature and measurement type explicitly. This contrast supports the criticism that the reviewed paper’s abstract overstates or at least obscures practical performance. The external quote therefore strengthens the case that the paper’s presentation could mislead readers about room-temperature applicability.

The paper’s conclusion that diffusivity/conductivity “cannot be used to identify” phase separation is not sufficiently supported because it asserts that a predicted ~1-order-of-magnitude signature is “within the error” without presenting quantified experimental uncertainties, statistical confidence intervals, or a sensitivity analysis showing detectability limits. ... this dip in ion transport properties approximately accounts for one order of magnitude, well within the error of the available experiments and our calculations, thus making it challenging to detect the signature of phase separation via diffusivity/conductivity measurements or calculations. This statement makes a strong detectability claim (“challenging to detect”) while only qualitatively referencing “the error” without quantifying it. In solid-state ionics, whether a one-order-of-magnitude change is detectable depends on the actual experimental uncertainty (including pellet-to-pellet variability, fitting ambiguity, and microstructure effects) and on the computational statistical uncertainty across sampled configurations. The paper does not provide error bars on the key transport plots nor a formal uncertainty propagation that would justify the detectability conclusion. Moreover, the computational model’s uncertainty is not transparently translated into confidence bounds on the phase-separation dip (e.g., distribution over configurations for the phase-separated ensembles). As a result, the inference that conductivity cannot identify phases is not robustly established. Because this conclusion is extended beyond NASICON (“may also be valid for other solid electrolytes and electrodes”), the lack of quantified uncertainty becomes a major issue. Our calculations suggest that Na diffusivity and conductivity cannot be used to identify the underlying phases within the NASICON electrolyte. This is an unusually strong negative conclusion (“cannot be used”) that would normally require a careful power/detectability analysis demonstrating that, given realistic experimental noise and model uncertainty, transport signatures are statistically indistinguishable. The paper instead provides a qualitative argument and does not report, for example, the variance of conductivity across the phase-separated configurations relative to the solid-solution configurations. In practical terms, even if the average dip is ~10×, detectability could still be possible with controlled microstructure and repeated measurements—unless proven otherwise. The statement also risks conflating “difficult under typical measurement scatter” with “in principle impossible,” which are scientifically different. Without quantified uncertainties, the conclusion is not reliably supported by the presented evidence. This weakens the robustness of the paper’s broader guidance on identifying phase separation. The grey band in panel b shows the interval of confidence of our predictions at 573 K, which is lower in magnitude than the variance of Na-ion conductivities observed experimentally (Figure S6). While this mentions an “interval of confidence,” the paper does not define how the confidence interval is computed (e.g., percentile range across 50 configurations, standard error of the mean, bootstrapping, or another method). Without a definition, it is unclear whether the grey band is a statistically meaningful uncertainty estimate that can be used to support detectability claims. Additionally, the phrase “variance ... observed experimentally” is not accompanied by experimental error bars or a measurement model explaining what portion of scatter is noise versus systematic differences across studies. For detectability of phase separation, the relevant comparison is not just “variance across literature reports,” but uncertainty under controlled conditions for the same processing and microstructure. Therefore, even where uncertainty language appears, it is insufficiently operationalized to justify the strong inference made. This supports the criticism that the statistical treatment is inadequate for the claimed conclusion. ... the diffusional properties obtained from AIMD simulations are often plagued by poor statistics. This external sentence highlights a general, well-known issue in computational ion-transport estimates: limited sampling can lead to unreliable diffusivities and conductivities unless statistics are carefully handled ([2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41524-018-0074-y)). Although the reviewed paper uses kMC rather than AIMD and emphasizes large sampling, the same principle applies when making detectability claims based on differences that are only ~10×. To argue that a one-order-of-magnitude dip is “within error,” the authors need to clearly quantify statistical uncertainty in their kMC averages and compare it to experimental uncertainty under comparable conditions. The external quote underscores why simply asserting “within error” is inadequate: statistical reliability must be demonstrated, not assumed. This is especially important because the paper’s conclusion is negative and generalized beyond the specific system. Therefore, the external evidence supports the need for more rigorous uncertainty quantification than what is provided.

The paper claims that their KMC model predicts an ionic conductivity of approximately 0.2 S/cm (200 mS/cm) for the optimal composition. However, the provided code yields values around 0.002 S/cm (2 mS/cm) at the same temperature (573 K), which is two orders of magnitude lower. "highest ionic conductivity (~0.1 S cm^{-1}) is achieved in Na_{3.4}Zr_2Si_{2.4}P_{0.6}O_{12}, in line with our predictions (~0.2 S cm^{-1})." The authors claim a theoretical prediction of ~0.2 S/cm. ```python # Output from run_kmc.py for x=2.4 at 573 K # Conductivity 1.753E+00 ``` The code output is in mS/cm (as confirmed by the unit conversion factor `1.602*10**11` in `kmc.py`). The value `1.753` corresponds to 1.753 mS/cm, or 0.001753 S/cm. This is approximately 100 times lower than the claimed 0.2 S/cm. This huge discrepancy suggests either a flaw in the model implementation provided, incorrect parameters (e.g., attempt frequency), or a misrepresentation of the theoretical results in the text.

The paper claims to achieve the "highest ionic conductivity (~0.1 S cm^{-1})" for NASICON. However, this value (likely at 300°C/573 K based on context) is not higher than values reported in early literature, such as 0.2 S/cm at 300°C reported by Hong in 1976. "highest ionic conductivity (~0.1 S cm^{-1}) is achieved in Na_{3.4}Zr_2Si_{2.4}P_{0.6}O_{12}" The authors present 0.1 S/cm as a record-breaking value. "The conductivity of Na_{3}Zr_{2}Si_{2}PO_{12} at 300°C is 0.2 (ohm-cm)^{-1}." [1] Hong (1976) reported a conductivity of 0.2 S/cm at 300°C for the standard NASICON composition ($x=2$). The value reported in this manuscript (0.1 S/cm) is actually lower than this 40-year-old benchmark. Even if the authors refer to total conductivity vs. grain conductivity, the claim of "highest ionic conductivity" without qualification is misleading given the established literature values. References: [1] Hong, H. Y.-P. "Crystal structures and crystal chemistry in the system Na_{1+x}Zr_2Si_xP_{3-x}O_{12}." Materials Research Bulletin 11, no. 2 (1976): 173-182. https://doi.org/10.1016/0025-5408(76)90073-8

The abstract claims that the KMC simulations map the compositional space "over an unprecedented time range – in the realm of milliseconds". However, the provided code and parameters run for only 3000 KMC passes, which corresponds to a physical time scale of microseconds, not milliseconds. "More than 2,000 distinct ab initio-based kinetic Monte Carlo simulations have been used to map the statistically vast compositional space of NASICON over an unprecedented time range – in the realm of milliseconds" The authors claim to reach millisecond time scales. ```python kmc_pass = 3000 # ... # Output from run_kmc.py # Pass Time # 99 6.068E-08 ``` In the provided code, `kmc_pass` is set to 3000. The output shows that 100 passes correspond to approximately 60 nanoseconds ($6 \times 10^{-8}$ s). Extrapolating linearly, 3000 passes would correspond to roughly 1.8 microseconds ($1.8 \times 10^{-6}$ s). To reach 1 millisecond ($10^{-3}$ s), the simulation would need to run for over a billion passes, which is not reflected in the provided code configuration. The claim of "milliseconds" appears to be exaggerated by three orders of magnitude based on the provided evidence.

The measurement and analysis of the presented impedance results is not correct. It is unreasonable that for impedance measurements using ion-blocking electrodes the impedance spectra do not start at the origin (0,0) of the Nyquist impedance plot. The main example for this error is on Figure 4C, for the spectrum at 373 K, in which the blue semicircle extends from 25 Ohms to ca. 145 Ohms. Since the authors mention that they measured up to frequencies of 30 MHz, there is no reason for all the spectra to not start at the origin. A possible source for the offset is that the electrolyte is reacting with the gold contact materials. The formation of such Na-Au alloys is possible at the temperatures reported here, so the authors should check if the electrodes have been affected by the measurement conditions. The other explanation for the offset of the blue semicircle is that this corresponds to the grain boundary resistance of the sample. Since the samples tested are not dense (the authors report pellet densities in the range of 70-77%), it is likely that grain boundary resistances are the main contributor to the impedance spectra. If the explanation for the offset is the second one, then it is a big problem for the manuscript because the authors make direct comparisons of the theoretically obtained ionic conductivities and those obtained experimentally. Such a direct comparison is only possible with fully dense samples, impedance data single crystals or when the impedance data is analyzed considering grain (bulk) and grain boundary resistivities.

Since the impedance data analysis is flawed and it serves to support one of the key findings of the manuscript, namely “we have demonstrated that first principles- based kMC can access significantly longer time scales in the millisecond range and larger length scales (a total of 21,504 atom supercells in the present work), enabling us to approach experimental time and space resolutions and establish a robust link between measurements and theory”, I cannot recommend the manuscript for publication as is.

The authors should discuss the limitation of their method for long-range diffusion analyses since the method does not incorporate the effect of grain boundaries.
