This is of particular interest when trying to understand experimental results where the material’s microstructure can dominate the transport properties. Specifically in their work, where the authors compare their bulk (intra-grain) predicted transport properties with their experimental results, which seems to include both, bulk and grain boundaries (inter-grain) conductivity results. The authors could measure their transport properties at lower temperatures, which usually helps to decouple bulk and grain boundary resistances to Na+ transport. This would be highly important, given that the authors have employed different synthetic methodologies and obtained different relative densities, which impacts the total conductivity reported. SEM images should be included to compare if microstructures are still comparable.

The reported kMC method only allows for Na+ to move, constraining the rest of the atoms to fix positions. Considering that for other polyanion systems have been reported polyanion rotations during Na+ diffusion – so-called paddle-wheel effect, do the authors anticipate any limitation or their current methodology to this particular system or other sulphide systems where they expected to extend this methodology?

For the NBE method the authors have employed a temperature of 0 K, while with other methods and techniques temperatures above 300 K have been used. Do the authors expect a constant Arrehneious behaviour of the transport properties over the whole range of temperatures reported to make these results comparable?

This reviewer has some concerns regarding the temperature choices for their theoretical calculations. The authors use the structure obtained at 1500 K to mimic that of the maximum temperature employed during experimental synthesis. In most cases, however, materials are cooled down to ambient temperature naturally, allowing the crystal structure to relax. This also seems to be the case of their experimental condition – no quenching protocol is mentioned. The authors should discuss this point and recalculate it as necessary. Additionally, the authors must perform Rietveld refinements, rather than Le Bail fittings, to obtain full structural information, importantly Na distribution within Na(1) and Na(2) sites, stoichiometry as well as quantification of ZrO2 impurity presence. Particularly, the presence of this impurity is concerning in their Na = 3 sample, where a non-negligible amount or ZrO2 is detected, which could affect the total transport properties of the material. Additionally, the authors’ claim on phase purity of these compounds should be revised.

It would be helpful for the reader to include in the supporting information the structural parameters for the models employed. Considering these are quenched structures at 1500 K, which is the symmetry of their model used for the diffusion calculations? This is an important parameter considering the monoclinic to rhombohedral transition of the NASICON system at some compositions at temperatures below the quenching point selected. Additionally, Na+ occupancies across different sites could vary with temperature.
This reviewer also recommends the authors to calculate/show the windows sizes difference for Na+ diffusion when replacing P5+ with Si4+, and correlate these with their results/conclusions.

The authors should include in the abstract the temperature at which the reported conductivity is obtained. Furthermore, the authors claim high conductivity (page 28) of their sample with 0.1 mS cm-1 at 200 oC, considering the current state-of-the-art in the field this claim should be revised.
The authors should also consider including the effect of the higher or optimal Na stoichiometry when discussing the positive effect of P5+ substitution with Si4+ on page 26.

Structures with charge-neutral vacancies at initial and final images in the diffusion path were fully optimized until the interatomic forces were less than 0.01 eV/Å and stresses were less than 0.29 GPa. Stress tensor looks high, not zero. Normally it should be 0.05 GPa or less than this.

The paper reports benchmark performance metrics as single point estimates without standard deviations, confidence intervals, or statistical tests, despite the inherently stochastic nature of LLM outputs and despite the methodology section indicating each task was evaluated multiple times. This undermines the reliability and interpretability of the claimed performance differences between GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo-0125. "GPT-4o exhibits exceptional proficiency in documentation-centric operations, achieving a 92% success rate, complemented by robust execution in analysis (71%) and computational tasks (70%)." The paper reports precise percentages (92%, 71%, 70%) without any measure of variance or uncertainty. Given that LLM outputs are inherently stochastic, and that the code repository shows each task was run 3 times, these percentages should be accompanied by standard deviations or confidence intervals. Without uncertainty estimates, readers cannot assess whether the reported differences between models are statistically significant or simply within the range of random variation. This is particularly problematic for making claims about model superiority. "To ensure robustness, we manually evaluated all questions using GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo-0125, verifying the output log files and AFM software results multiple times in collaboration with different researchers to eliminate potential human errors." While the methodology mentions "multiple times" evaluation, there is no quantification of variance across these runs in the reported results. The JSON data files in the code repository (e.g., question_1.json) contain three separate evaluation results for each task, but the paper does not aggregate these with proper statistical measures. Best practices in LLM evaluation recommend reporting uncertainty through credible intervals or standard deviations across runs to enable meaningful model comparison. "Pass@$k$ is widely used to report performance for LLM reasoning, but it often yields unstable, misleading rankings, especially when the number of trials (samples) is limited and compute is constrained. We present a principled Bayesian evaluation framework that replaces Pass@$k$ and average accuracy over $N$ trials (avg@$N$) with posterior estimates of a model's underlying success probability and credible intervals, yielding stable rankings and a transparent decision rule for differences." [[1]](#citation-1) This recent work on LLM evaluation methodology (ICLR 2026) explicitly recommends uncertainty quantification through Bayesian credible intervals rather than point estimates. The AILA paper's lack of uncertainty measures contradicts emerging best practices in the LLM evaluation literature. The paper makes comparative claims (e.g., "80% versus 0% success") that would benefit significantly from proper statistical treatment to determine whether observed differences are meaningful. References: [1] [Don't Pass@k: A Bayesian Framework for Large Language Model Evaluation](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PTXi3Ef4sT) - ICLR 2026 Conference Submission

The benchmark evaluation is limited to only two models (GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo-0125) from a single provider (OpenAI), despite the stated goal of establishing benchmarking standards for autonomous laboratories. This severely limits the generalizability of findings regarding LLM capabilities for self-driving laboratory applications. "Systematic evaluation of AILA using two advanced closed source language models—GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo-0125—unveils distinctive execution patterns and operational efficacies." The benchmark claims to evaluate AI agents for autonomous microscopy but only tests two models from the same provider. The code repository contains folders for additional models (Claude-3.5-sonnet, Llama-3.3-70B-versatile) indicating these were tested but not reported. This selective reporting raises questions about why these results were excluded and whether they might contradict the paper's conclusions. "To enhance large language models (LLMs) for chemistry problem solving, several LLM-based agents augmented with tools have been proposed, such as ChemCrow and Coscientist. Our error analysis with a chemistry expert suggests that: For specialized chemistry tasks, such as synthesis prediction, we should augment agents with specialized tools; however, for general chemistry questions like those in exams, agents' ability to reason correctly with chemistry knowledge matters more, and tool augmentation does not always help." [[2]](#citation-2) Related work on LLM-based scientific agents (ChemCrow, Coscientist) typically evaluates multiple model families to establish general conclusions about agent capabilities. The AILA paper's restriction to OpenAI models prevents conclusions about whether observed behaviors (e.g., the "sleepwalking" phenomenon) are model-specific or generalizable to other LLM architectures. This limitation is particularly important given that different model families may exhibit fundamentally different instruction-following behaviors. "We present the first systematic evaluation of instruction compliance across 13 major LLMs using real-world enterprise scenarios." [[3]](#citation-3) Contemporary benchmarking studies in the LLM field typically evaluate a diverse set of models (this paper evaluates 13 models across 8 families) to establish generalizable conclusions. The AILA paper's two-model comparison falls short of this standard, limiting the scientific value of its benchmarking contribution and preventing meaningful conclusions about LLM capabilities for laboratory automation across the broader landscape of available models. References: [2] [Tooling or Not Tooling? The Impact of Tools on Language Agents](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12765490/) - PMC 2025 [3] [The Instruction Gap: LLMs get lost in Following Instruction](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.03269v1) - arXiv 2025

Analysis of the released AFMBench dataset reveals that only 43% of the 100 benchmark tasks contain actual evaluation results, with 57% marked as "NaN" (missing data). This incomplete dataset undermines the reproducibility of the reported metrics and raises questions about the reliability of the benchmark's conclusions. "AFMBench comprises 100 expertly curated experimental tasks (see S3.1 in Supplementary Information for a few examples of tasks; all the tasks are available in the GitHub repo) manually designed to rigorously evaluate autonomous AFM operations across multiple dimensions of complexity." The paper claims 100 curated tasks, but verification of the released data (code/Data/afm_qs/*.json) shows that only 43 questions have actual results recorded for both GPT-4o and GPT-3.5, with 57 questions containing "NaN" values for both models. The script verification_code_claude-opus-4-5-20251101/check_benchmark_data.py confirms: "Total questions: 100; GPT-4o complete results: 43; GPT-4o NaN results: 57". This means the reported performance metrics are derived from less than half of the claimed benchmark. "All the tasks in AFMBench, along with the complete log files of the responses for each of the tasks from GPT-4o and GPT-3.5 are available at: https://github.com/M3RG-IITD/AILA" The claim of "all the tasks" being available is misleading since the majority of tasks lack evaluation results. For example, question_50.json, question_51.json, and question_73.json all show "result": [{"GPT-4": "NaN", "GPT-3.5": "NaN"}]. Without complete data, the reported accuracy percentages (92% for documentation, 71% for analysis) cannot be independently verified or reproduced by other researchers. "Analysis of the task architecture reveals distinct patterns in resource utilization and operational complexity. In Figure 2A, tool coordination requirements highlight a systematic preference for sophisticated workflows, with 69% of tasks demanding multi-tool integration, while 31% operate through single-tool protocols." These architectural statistics describe the design of all 100 tasks, but the actual performance evaluation appears to be based on only 43 completed tasks. It remains unclear whether the evaluated subset is representative of the full benchmark distribution across complexity levels and task types, potentially biasing the reported performance metrics toward easier or more tractable tasks.

The paper introduces the term "sleepwalking" as a novel phenomenon where LLM agents perform unauthorized actions, claiming it is "reported for the first time to the best of the authors' knowledge." However, this claim is based on only 3 instances from GPT-4o's instruction adherence errors, which represents an insufficient sample size to establish a new phenomenon and distinguish it from well-documented instruction-following failures in the LLM literature. "A critical finding emerged regarding GPT4o's instruction adherence. In one of the three recorded errors, GPT-4o exceeded its designated operational limits, performing actions that were not authorized by the provided guidelines." The "sleepwalking" phenomenon is based on just 3 error cases from GPT-4o, which represents 15% of GPT-4o's total 20% error rate (i.e., approximately 3 cases out of 100 tasks). Claiming to identify a new phenomenon from such a small sample is statistically problematic. The described behavior—LLMs performing additional actions beyond instructions—is consistent with well-documented instruction-following failures rather than a distinct phenomenon requiring new terminology. "Similar to the observation of 'hallucination' in LLMs, these results present a unique challenge—SDLs tend to take arbitrary actions potentially based on memory rather than following the instructions, referred to hereafter as 'sleepwalking'. These issues are especially critical in sensitive experimental settings..." The proposed distinction between "sleepwalking" and existing concepts like hallucination or instruction non-compliance is not clearly established. The paper speculates that actions are "potentially based on memory" without providing mechanistic evidence. The LLM evaluation literature has extensively documented instruction-following failures including "procedural violations" and "deviations from specified response patterns" as general LLM limitations rather than phenomena specific to laboratory contexts. "This phenomenon, which we term the 'instruction gap,' represents a fundamental barrier to reliable LLM deployment in production systems. [...] Our findings reveal the 'instruction gap' - a fundamental challenge where models excel at general tasks but struggle with precise instruction adherence required for enterprise deployment." [[3]](#citation-3) Recent work characterizes LLM instruction-following failures as a well-documented "instruction gap" phenomenon across multiple model families and domains. The behaviors described as "sleepwalking" in the AILA paper—performing actions beyond instructions, deviating from specified protocols—align with documented patterns of instruction compliance failures in enterprise RAG systems. Introducing new terminology for what appears to be an established phenomenon, based on only 3 observations, risks fragmenting the literature unnecessarily. References: [3] [The Instruction Gap: LLMs get lost in Following Instruction](https://arxiv.org/html/2601.03269v1) - arXiv 2025

The benchmark employs a binary correct/incorrect classification scheme for evaluation that oversimplifies the assessment of complex experimental tasks, preventing nuanced understanding of partial successes, near-misses, and the degree to which errors affect experimental outcomes. "To assess the evaluation of questions in terms of accuracy, we classified the answers provided by AILA into two categories: fully correct answers and incorrect or partially correct answers. A fully correct answer was considered accurate, while any incorrect or incomplete response was deemed incorrect." The binary classification system collapses important distinctions between different types and severities of errors. A task where the agent correctly sets 4 out of 5 parameters would be scored identically to a task where it fails completely. This binary approach is particularly problematic for multi-step experimental tasks where partial completion may still provide scientific value. Modern LLM evaluation frameworks increasingly adopt graded scoring rubrics to capture performance nuances. "We present a principled Bayesian evaluation framework [...] giving closed-form expressions for the posterior mean and uncertainty of any weighted rubric and enabling the use of prior evidence when appropriate. [...] The framework [...] naturally extends to graded, rubric-based evaluations." [[1]](#citation-1) Contemporary LLM evaluation methodology recommends graded, rubric-based assessments that can capture partial correctness and varying degrees of task completion. The binary scoring in AFMBench prevents meaningful analysis of where and how LLMs succeed or fail within complex multi-step experimental workflows, limiting the diagnostic value of the benchmark for identifying specific areas for improvement. "Evaluation outcomes are modeled as categorical (not just 0/1) with a Dirichlet prior." Best practices in LLM benchmarking, as evidenced by recent methodological work, recognize that categorical or continuous evaluation outcomes better capture the complexity of task performance than binary measures. For AFM operations involving multiple parameters and sequential steps, a graded evaluation would provide more actionable insights for framework development. References: [1] [Don't Pass@k: A Bayesian Framework for Large Language Model Evaluation](https://openreview.net/forum?id=PTXi3Ef4sT) - ICLR 2026 Conference Submission

The paper’s AFMBench “efficiency” metric is not a valid measure of experimental efficiency because it excludes AFM scan time and excludes time for incorrect answers, yet the paper uses the resulting numbers to support claims about time savings in real experiments. “To ensure precise efficiency measurements, scanning time for images and the time taken by questions with incorrect answers were excluded from the analysis.” Excluding scan time removes a central part of AFM experiment duration and breaks any end-to-end interpretation of efficiency. Excluding time for incorrect answers introduces survivorship bias by omitting recovery and rework costs that drive real throughput. These choices can make a less reliable agent appear faster by discarding slow failures and retries from the timing statistics. The paper does not justify why a laboratory-assistant efficiency metric should ignore physical actuation time and the cost of incorrect actions. Without an end-to-end timing analysis, the efficiency results cannot support claims about practical experimental speed. “This automation significantly reduces the time and effort compared to manual execution.” This claim concerns an iterative experiment that repeatedly captures images and analyzes results, so scan time and re-scans are central to total wall-clock time. Because the paper’s efficiency metric explicitly removes scan time, it cannot substantiate overall time reduction for the physical workflow. Excluding time for incorrect answers also makes comparisons asymmetric because manual execution necessarily includes troubleshooting and corrective actions. The paper therefore conflates LLM interaction latency with experimental throughput. A valid time-savings claim would require reporting end-to-end time including acquisition, waiting, retries, and recoveries. “End-to-end benchmarks provide an all-inclusive evaluation that extends beyond the boundaries of the ML model itself. Rather than focusing solely on a machine learning model’s computational efficiency or accuracy, these benchmarks encompass the entire pipeline of an AI system.” [[1]](#ref1) AFMBench is framed as a real-world benchmark that physically executes tasks on AFM hardware, which aligns with end-to-end evaluation principles. The cited guidance emphasizes measuring the entire pipeline, which in AFM includes scanning and hardware wait times. By excluding scan time, the paper’s efficiency metric becomes primarily an interaction-time metric rather than experimental efficiency. By excluding incorrect-answer time, it also fails to capture robustness-related overheads that matter operationally. This mismatch undermines the paper’s interpretation of “efficiency” as experimental efficiency. References: [1] [ML Systems Book — “Benchmarking AI”](https://mlsysbook.ai/book/contents/core/benchmarking/benchmarking.html)

The paper’s safety/alignment claims (e.g., “sleepwalking” and unauthorized actions) are not causally supported because the implementation executes model-supplied Python via `exec()` without sandboxing and the supplementary logs show AFM-control code executed through the “Image Analyzer” tool, undermining the intended safety boundaries. “It is worth noting that AI-agent based on GPT-4o, a model known for its reasoning capabilities, exhibit tendencies to perform actions beyond what is instructed, termed as “sleepwalking”.” This statement attributes unsafe actions primarily to the LLM agent, but in instrument-control settings the system’s tool design is a major determinant of safety outcomes. If tools permit arbitrary code execution, unauthorized actions become an architectural inevitability when the model errs. The paper does not separate failures due to model instruction non-adherence from failures enabled by permissive execution pathways. Without that separation, the evidence cannot support the implied causal interpretation that “sleepwalking” is chiefly an LLM property. This weakens the validity of the paper’s safety conclusions and generalization to other SDLs. ```python if dynamic_code: # Safely execute the dynamic code try: exec(dynamic_code) print("Dynamic code executed successfully.") ``` The code executes untrusted dynamic code using `exec()` without restrictions, despite labeling it “Safely execute.” In a lab automation context, this makes the tool capable of arbitrary actions permitted by the Python environment, including direct hardware control. Such a design means safety relies on perfect model adherence, which the paper itself shows is not reliable. The architecture therefore lacks enforceable safety boundaries at the tool interface. This undermines the paper’s framing of observed incidents as mainly an alignment phenomenon rather than a missing sandbox/guardrail problem. “This function executes arbitrary code. Calling it with user-supplied input may lead to security vulnerabilities.” [[3]](#ref3) The Python documentation explicitly warns that `exec()` on user-supplied input can create security vulnerabilities, which directly applies when model-generated strings are executed as code. In AILA, the model is effectively the “user” producing executable content, so the warning maps directly to the system’s core control mechanism. The paper does not describe compensating controls such as allowlisted command schemas, command validation, sandboxing, or human approval. Therefore, unsafe actions are plausibly explained by permissive execution design rather than being evidence of an LLM-specific safety failure mode. This substantially weakens the validity of the paper’s causal framing of safety issues. References: [3] [Python Documentation — Built-in Functions: `exec()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#exec)

The paper’s Methods mis-specify “average friction” and RMS roughness and omit essential calibration details for quantitative friction in physical units, undermining the validity and reproducibility of the mechanical-property measurement component. “Average friction was calculated using the following formula: \[ F_{ave} = \frac{1}{2} \times (f_{ij} - b_{ij}) \]” This equation defines a per-pixel half-difference and does not define an average over the image because it lacks any aggregation across \(i,j\). Since the paper reports a scalar “average friction,” the written definition is incomplete or inconsistent with the reported quantity. This is not a minor notation issue because it changes what would be computed by readers implementing the Methods. The manuscript does not specify an aggregation convention elsewhere, so reproductions could diverge. This undermines methodological transparency for a key benchmark output. “\[ R_{rms} = \frac{1}{M.N} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (z_{ij} - \bar{z})^2 \]” The RMS roughness equation omits the square root and therefore describes a mean squared deviation rather than a root-mean-square quantity. This changes units and can materially change reported values and interpretations. Because roughness is used as an output in benchmark tasks and case studies, a definitional error affects the validity of conclusions about analysis correctness. Even if the implementation uses the correct formula, the manuscript becomes non-reproducible for readers relying on the written Methods. This is a substantive methodological flaw in the paper’s quantitative analysis description. ```python friction = 0.5 * (data['Image']['Forward']['Friction force'] - data['Image']['Backward']['Friction force']) average_friction = np.mean(friction) ... rms_roughness = np.sqrt(total_sum / (M * N)) ``` The released code computes “average friction” as a mean across pixels, contradicting the manuscript’s friction equation that lacks any averaging operator. The code computes RMS roughness with an explicit square root, contradicting the manuscript’s RMS equation. These inconsistencies mean the paper’s Methods are not a reliable specification of the implemented analysis. For a benchmark paper, alignment between written methodology and executable code is essential for reproducibility. The mismatch raises doubt about whether the manuscript’s reported formulas were validated against the implementation. “Therefore, proper calibration of the normal and lateral force constants of the cantilever is crucial in order to extract quantitative, accurate, reliable, and reproducible lateral force values from AFM manipulation experiments.” [[4]](#ref4) This AFM methods statement highlights that quantitative lateral-force reporting depends on calibration, which is directly relevant when reporting friction in nN. The paper’s Methods describe array operations on a friction channel but do not document how lateral sensitivity and torsional constants are handled to ensure physical units are meaningful. Without calibration documentation, friction values may be instrument- and session-dependent and not reproducible across setups. Software verification does not substitute for force calibration unless calibration parameters are specified. This omission undermines the validity of the paper’s “mechanical property measurement” component. “The most common surface roughness parameters were evaluated: Ra (average roughness), Rq (root mean square roughness), and Rz (maximum height of the profile).” [[5]](#ref5) This standard definition reinforces that RMS roughness is explicitly a “root mean square” quantity, consistent with requiring a square root in its definition. The manuscript’s RMS formula omits that root, creating a definitional mismatch with common practice. Because roughness values are used as outputs for benchmark tasks, definitional correctness is essential for meaningful evaluation and comparison. Even if the code computes RMS correctly, the written Methods still mislead readers and impede independent replication. This supports the claim that the methodology is not adequately specified. References: [4] [Çiçek et al., “Quantitative estimation of nanoparticle/substrate adhesion by atomic force microscopy” (Beilstein J. Nanotechnol., 2026; PMCID: PMC12766167)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12766167/) [5] [Tunca et al., “Comparison of Mechanical, Surface, and Chemical Properties…” (Turk J Orthod., 2025; PMCID: PMC12758443)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12758443/)

The AFMBench performance conclusions are not statistically robust because the paper reports point-estimate success rates without confidence intervals or run-to-run variance and uses manual binary scoring without reporting inter-rater reliability or adjudication procedures. “GPT-4o exhibits exceptional proficiency in documentation-centric operations, achieving a 92% success rate, complemented by robust execution in analysis (71%) and computational tasks (70%).” These success rates are reported as point estimates without any confidence intervals, even though each category has a finite and sometimes small number of tasks. Without uncertainty, readers cannot assess whether differences between categories or models are meaningful or within expected sampling variability. The paper also does not report repeated runs per task, which is important for stochastic LLM agents and tool-driven workflows. As a result, the robustness of conclusions about comparative strengths and weaknesses is unclear. Statistical uncertainty is especially important when using the benchmark to motivate broader design claims. “To assess the evaluation of questions in terms of accuracy, we classified the answers provided by AILA into two categories: fully correct answers and incorrect or partially correct answers.” A binary rubric can be reasonable, but many AFMBench tasks are multi-step and may admit borderline outcomes that require explicit adjudication rules. The paper notes that “multiple researchers were involved,” but it does not report inter-rater agreement, blinding, or how disagreements were resolved. Without that information, reported accuracy can depend on subjective judgment and may not reproduce across evaluators or labs. This is a major reproducibility issue for a benchmark positioned as a standard evaluation suite. The absence of rater-reliability reporting undermines confidence in the reported success rates. “Running each benchmark 5-10 times and reporting statistical measures beyond simple means (including standard deviations or 95% confidence intervals) quantifies result stability…” [[1]](#ref1) Agentic systems are probabilistic and can show meaningful run-to-run variability, which makes single-run reporting fragile. The cited guidance reflects common benchmarking practice: report dispersion and confidence intervals to distinguish real effects from noise. The paper reports single percentages and average times, so it is unclear whether findings are stable under resampling or minor environment changes. This matters because the paper draws conclusions about error modes and model differences that could change with repeated trials. Without multi-run and uncertainty reporting, the conclusions are less reliable. “The Wilson method for calculating confidence intervals for proportions … is based on inverting the hypothesis test…” [[2]](#ref2) AFMBench accuracy is fundamentally a proportion (successes out of tasks) and is naturally accompanied by confidence intervals under standard statistical reporting. The NIST handbook demonstrates that well-established methods exist for proportion confidence intervals, reinforcing that uncertainty reporting is expected. For small subsets (e.g., 10 calculation tasks), confidence intervals can be wide and could materially alter interpretation of performance gaps. The paper’s omission therefore leaves key claims statistically under-supported. This weakens the reliability of comparative conclusions. References: [1] [ML Systems Book — “Benchmarking AI”](https://mlsysbook.ai/book/contents/core/benchmarking/benchmarking.html) [2] [NIST/SEMATECH e-Handbook — “7.2.4.1. Confidence intervals”](https://www.itl.nist.gov/div898/handbook/prc/section2/prc241.htm)

The paper overgeneralizes AFMBench-derived results to other disciplines and claims “universal compatibility with API-enabled AFM systems” without evidence, despite the demonstrated system being tied to a specific vendor stack and benchmark task design. “Our implementation utilizes the DriveAFM instrument (Nanosurf), which is accessed through a Python-based API architecture and designed for universal compatibility with API-enabled AFM systems.” This is a strong portability claim that would require showing the same framework operating across multiple AFM vendors and APIs. The paper does not provide cross-instrument validation, a compatibility matrix, or an explicit vendor-agnostic abstraction layer. In practice, “API-enabled” does not imply a common command model, safety interlocks, or file formats across vendors. Therefore, the evidence in the manuscript does not support “universal compatibility” beyond the demonstrated Nanosurf setup. This overstatement weakens the credibility of the paper’s generality claims. “This work’s implications transcend materials characterization. The empirically validated principles—modular architecture (69% multi-tool efficiency), strategic agent deployment (83:17 ratio), and cross-domain integration (80% hybrid task success)—establish design parameters for autonomous laboratories across disciplines.” The reported ratios are descriptive properties of the authors’ curated AFMBench task set and their specific agent/tool decomposition, not established invariants of autonomous lab design. Extrapolating them to other modalities requires evidence that tasks, tools, and constraints are comparable across domains. The paper does not present cross-domain benchmarks, deployments on other instruments, or replications in other labs. Without such evidence, treating these numbers as cross-disciplinary “design parameters” is not justified. This weakens the paper’s broader claims of significance. “**Fallacy:** *Benchmark performance directly translates to real-world application performance.*” [[1]](#ref1) The paper uses AFMBench performance statistics to motivate broad conclusions about autonomous laboratories across disciplines. The cited warning highlights that benchmark-to-deployment transfer is often unreliable due to differences in environment, workloads, and constraints. This risk is amplified when transferring from AFM to unrelated lab modalities with different actuation and safety requirements. Without cross-domain validation, benchmark-derived ratios should not be treated as general prescriptions for SDL design. This directly supports the critique that the paper’s generalization exceeds its evidence. References: [1] [ML Systems Book — “Benchmarking AI”](https://mlsysbook.ai/book/contents/core/benchmarking/benchmarking.html)

The paper selectively reports results only for GPT-4o and GPT-3.5, omitting data for Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Llama 3.3 70B which are present in the repository and potentially offer significant insights. "Systematic evaluation of AILA using two advanced closed source language models—GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo-0125—unveils distinctive execution patterns and operational efficacies." The paper explicitly states it evaluates only these two models. However, the accompanying code repository contains full evaluation results for other models. ``` code/Results/Multi-Agent/Claude-3.5-sonnet code/Results/Multi-Agent/Llama-3.3-70B-versatile ``` The existence of these directories in the repository confirms that experiments were conducted with Claude 3.5 Sonnet and Llama 3.3 70B. ``` Sheet names: ['Sheet1', 'gpt_4o_1', 'gpt-35', 'claude', 'Sheet3', 'Sheet2', 'Llama', ...] ``` The raw results file (`Result_raw_metrices.xlsx`) contains detailed scoring for these omitted models. For instance, the `claude` sheet contains 300 rows of evaluation data. Omitting these results, especially when Claude 3.5 Sonnet is a strong competitor in coding and reasoning tasks, undermines the transparency and comprehensiveness of the benchmark. It prevents the reader from understanding how open-weights models (Llama) or other state-of-the-art closed models (Claude) compare to the GPT series in this domain.

The benchmark (AFMBench) relies on manual evaluation, which is not scalable, introduces subjectivity, and hinders reproducibility by the broader community. "To ensure robustness, we manually evaluated all questions using GPT-4o and GPT-3.5-turbo-0125, verifying the output log files and AFM software results multiple times in collaboration with different researchers to eliminate potential human errors." Manual evaluation is prone to human error and inconsistency, despite the authors' efforts. More importantly, it makes the benchmark difficult to scale or use for automatic regression testing of new models. "A fully correct answer was considered accurate, while any incorrect or incomplete response was deemed incorrect." This binary or qualitative classification by humans lacks the precision of automated metrics. In contrast, other scientific benchmarks like SciBench [1] or ChemCrow [2] often employ automated validation (e.g., checking against ground truth values or successful execution of a known reaction path) to ensure objective and reproducible scoring. "Given that some questions require manual inspection of the AFM software to verify whether specific parameters are set correctly..." This dependency on manual inspection of the GUI/software state indicates a lack of programmatic verification (e.g., via API state checks), which is a standard practice in software and agent benchmarking. References: [1] Wang, X., et al. "SciBench: Evaluating College-Level Scientific Problem-Solving Abilities of Large Language Models." arXiv preprint arXiv:2307.10635 (2023). https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.10635 [2] Bran, A. M., et al. "ChemCrow: Augmenting large-language models with chemistry tools." Nature Machine Intelligence (2024). https://www.nature.com/articles/s42256-024-00832-8

The system exhibits a high error rate in code generation, which is a critical bottleneck for an autonomous lab assistant and suggests that the current framework is not yet reliable for autonomous operation. "GPT-4o exhibits a total error rate of 20%, with errors distributed across three primary categories: code generation (60%), agent/tool selection (25%), and instruction adherence (15%)." This statistic implies that 12% of all tasks (60% of the 20% error rate) fail due to code generation issues even with the most advanced model. "GPT-3.5-turbo-0125 demonstrates a markedly higher total error rate of 45%, with errors concentrated in two categories: code generation (66.7%) and agent/tool selection (33.3%)." The high failure rate in code generation across both models indicates a fundamental weakness in the "Code Executor" tool or the prompt engineering strategy. For a system claiming to be an "Artificially Intelligent Lab Assistant" capable of "Autonomous Microscopy Experiments", such a high failure rate in its primary actuation mechanism (code execution) severely limits its practical autonomy and reliability compared to more robust systems demonstrated in domains like chemistry [3]. References: [3] Boiko, D. A., et al. "Autonomous chemical research with large language models." Nature 624, 570–578 (2023). https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06792-0

The agent exhibits dangerous "sleepwalking" behavior where it performs unrequested actions, posing a significant risk to hardware, which is not adequately addressed by the proposed safety framework. "In one of the three recorded errors, GPT-4o exceeded its designated operational limits, performing actions that were not authorized by the provided guidelines. For instance, it carried out potentially risky tip movements while it was only instructed to scan the surface" Unintended physical movements in AFM can destroy the cantilever or damage the sample, which are costly incidents. "In another case, GPT-4o was instructed to capture an image and calculate surface friction. While the image was captured correctly, the system failed to analyse it and switches the AFM to the lateral force mode instead of following clear instructions for a specific task" This unpredictability, termed "sleepwalking" by the authors, highlights a critical safety gap. While the authors mention a "safety framework" involving restricted access, the fact that the agent can still execute valid but unrequested commands (like switching modes or moving tips) within the allowed API subset shows that the safeguards are insufficient for autonomous deployment. This aligns with broader concerns about hallucination and alignment in LLMs [4], but the physical consequences here are more severe. References: [4] Huang, L., et al. "A survey on hallucination in large language models: Principles, taxonomy, challenges, and open questions." ACM Transactions on Information Systems (2023). https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/3617023

The paper claims to present "Autonomous Microscopy Experiments", but the demonstrated tasks are primarily single-step or limited multi-step workflows initiated by explicit natural language commands, which constitutes automation rather than true autonomy. "Upon receiving the query, AILA dissects it into sequential objectives: image acquisition via AFM-HA followed by DHA-led analysis." The workflow is reactive to user queries rather than proactive. True autonomy in the context of self-driving labs often implies the ability to generate hypotheses, plan experiments over multiple iterations, and self-correct without human intervention [3]. "AFMBench comprises 100 expertly curated experimental tasks... manually designed to rigorously evaluate autonomous AFM operations" The tasks described (e.g., "Set image size", "Calculate roughness") are instruction-following tasks. While valuable, framing this as "autonomous experimentation" overstates the system's agency. It is more accurately described as a natural language interface for laboratory automation. References: [3] Boiko, D. A., et al. "Autonomous chemical research with large language models." Nature 624, 570–578 (2023). https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06792-0

Many AFM operation software or packages already integrate data analysis functionalities. What is the rationale for including a separate data handler tool in AILA? Both AFM experiments and data analysis can be done directly using the same vendor software or package, particularly for the examples provided in this manuscript.
What additional or advanced data analysis capabilities does the data handler offer beyond those already existing in AFM vendor software or package?

The inclusion of a safety framework is a great development. However, if it is constrained to predefined documentation, it may limit the flexibility for designing experiments not covered in the documentation, particularly for new experiment designs.

What is the generic algorithm used for optimizing imaging parameters? Also, if a different prompt or a sample with distinct morphology is used in the experiment, does AILA select a different optimization algorithm?

Similar questions, how consistent is the edge detection algorithm applied by AILA? Does the selection of the algorithm depend on real-time results, or is the same method consistently applied across different samples?

The consistency and adaptability of algorithm selection might directly impact the reliability of AILA. A more detailed discussion on how AILA ensures robustness in algorithm selection and data analysis should be added.

Prior research has explored the use of LLMs, such as ChatGPT, for AFM operations. The authors should acknowledge and discuss these efforts, such as https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/2632-2153/ad52e9/meta. Previous work has also identified similar challenges, such as LLMs performing well in single-step experiments but struggling with multi-step processes.
In addition, given that this manuscript focuses on LLM-driven autonomous AFM, prior efforts in autonomous AFM development should be properly referenced.

This manuscript presents a dynamic routing framework that allows the agent to choose the next agent to use. However, the author didn’t discuss how such a dynamic multiagent system actually work and how it performs comparing to single-agent scenario.
It is relatively strange to discuss the performance of different LLMs before introducing how it actually solves AFM problems.

AFM is an analytical method, and it is used for material analysis to reveal structure-property relationships. However, the authors focused on developing LLM system to improve the autonomy of the AFM analysis, but didn’t give real-world examples show how it actually perform AFM analysis, and gives the analysis conclusions like human experts, which is the ultimate goal of such analysis method. It is suggested that authors should add 1-2 real-world examples.

In Figure 3 and lines 226-233, the authors discuss the temporal efficiency of various LLMs. Is the task completion time mentioned here the sum of the LLM’s response time and the tool execution time? If so, considering that the authors interact with the LLM through network APIs, the network environment of the AILA system and the resources available on the LLM servers could impact the LLM’s response time. Therefore, this comparison of results may not accurately reflect the LLM’s performance and may need more rigorous handling.

The authors mention that Claude-3.5-sonnet exhibits subpar agentic capabilities, primarily due to agent and tool selection errors. This issue may arise from the integration challenges between Anthropic’s Claude series models and frameworks like LangChain and LangGraph, especially concerning functionalities like function calls. These integrations might not fully leverage Claude-3.5-sonnet’s inherent strengths. I hope the authors can further analyze the reasons for Claude 3.5-sonnet’s poor performance from this perspective. Additionally, Anthropic’s recent introduction of the Model Context Protocol (MCP) offers a standardized method for connecting AI models with external data sources and tools, potentially enhancing agentic performance. Integrating MCP with Claude has demonstrated improved multi-agent applications, such as Manus. I also hope the authors can conduct research and discussion on this.

The authors mention safety alignment in SDLs and implement strict operational boundaries to mitigate safety risks. I suggest that after identifying risky actions, an additional human review mechanism could be introduced. For instance, if an expert detects an issue, they could suggest corrections (input via natural language), enabling a “human-in-the-loop” interaction that ensures continuous, safe operation of the workflow, rather than directly terminating the task when candidate actions beyond operational boundaries.

The level of detail and readability of the Supplementary Materials need improvement. (1) A table of contents is missing. (2) The output logs from the AILA system are too lengthy and appear to be directly copied from terminal outputs. These should be organized and refined to enhance readability. (3) The detailed process and results of expert evaluation of AILA’s performance on AFMBench, including how researchers determined whether the answers provided by AILA were entirely correct, incorrect, or partially correct, should be included in the supplementary materials and referenced in the main text. If too many details, representative examples can be included.

However, experimentally, the scope of the current framework is limited to scans in one location and very standardized analysis. From an applied AI perspective, the agent and tool sets are also highly task-specific and their performance depends greatly on the specificity of the prompts. With the addition of artifact/anomaly detection tools such as those developed by the autonomous microscopy works cited, as well as an additional agent that iteratively debugs generated code to control the instrument, the agentic framework has the potential to be applicable to real-world experiments.
In the evaluation of agent performance, is each task given to each model once? Or if the exact same tasks are repeated, how consistent is the agent response each time?

The current focus seems to be on the agents completing the task in one go. If an agent cannot complete the task, it knows, with appropriate prompting, to output NEED HELP to invoke the other agent. Is there a way for the user to provide additional context in such situations, and for the user’s feedback to be stored to improve the agent?

In Table S2, we see detailed vs. concise prompts for the same task. The concise prompt fails but the detailed prompt is a step-by-step guide for the agents. What is the advantage in using an agentic framework here when presumably an LLM with access to tools can follow instructions and execute?

Relatedly, the structure of the framework seems to be two agents, which each have access to two tools. How does the performance compare to if one agent was given all tools? Or if there was an agent for each tool?

More generally, the distinction between tool and agent is not clear. The authors themselves seem to use the two interchangeably, see for e.g. line 701. Figure 2 and the discussion around it centers on this distinction.

Regarding model-specific limitations, could iterative debugging improve results for cases where the failure mode is code generation? I suggest some quantification of the distribution of types of bugs and a clarification of how that may be addressed beyond needing an entirely different LLM.

There is a lot of emphasis placed on the model evaluation. At the rate of current progress, models become obsolete in a few months. E.g. Claude 3.5 and GPT-3.5 have been succeeded by newer models.\n\na. It would be nice to see a focus on evaluation metrics rather than specific models

One of the critical results presented is the demonstration of experimental workflows beyond single scan acquisition. In figure 5, the prompt given is “Optimize the values of the P, I, and D gains using a genetic algorithm. Do not correct the baseline.” This is quite specific. Is there any analysis on whether the agentic framework could complete the task without being told to optimize the PID, but rather more generically with instructions to optimize for image clarity and/or stability between scans? Or without specifying the algorithm to be used?

The first three discussion paragraphs summarize and repeat many points that were scattered throughout the results section. I suggest a more in-depth discussion of the last two points instead, as they seem more relevant to addressing strengths (modularity) and limitations (limited functionality) of the framework.

AFM parameter optimization:

a. Not clear if the genetic algorithm code was provided or written by the LLM. If the former, why not evaluate the models on their ability to write code to solve problems?

b. What happens if you add a code writer in addition to a code executor?
Does the current workflow call predefined code blocks from the documentation?
How can others evaluate their models or agents on this benchmark? By definition, the benchmark should be accessible to anyone.

The paper claims to demonstrate end-to-end reproducibility of the Hosny et al. study, but the validation is limited to only one of the seven independent datasets used in the original work. The original multi-cohort study validated across 7 datasets from 5 institutions (1,194 NSCLC patients), while this replication only uses a subset of the NSCLC-Radiomics dataset (N=206 subjects). This significantly narrows the evidence supporting the claim that cloud infrastructure enables reproducible AI pipelines. "Besides its impact on the field, another reason we chose this study for our use case was the fact that the AI pipeline was validated using an independent cohort now hosted by the IDC, i.e., the NSCLC-Radiomics dataset, allowing us to use the platform to reproduce the AI pipeline validation." The authors explicitly acknowledge selecting the original study based on data availability rather than comprehensively reproducing the full validation scope. The NSCLC-Radiomics dataset was just one of seven external validation cohorts in the Hosny et al. study. True reproducibility would require validating across multiple cohorts to ensure the pipeline generalizes, yet this paper only demonstrates data fetching and processing on a single cohort. This limitation is significant because the original study's key contribution was demonstrating multi-cohort validation—an aspect not reproduced here. "we compared our cloud-based pipeline with the original results shared by Hosny et Al. on a subset of the NSCLC-Radiomics dataset" The term "subset" further indicates that the validation is not even on the complete NSCLC-Radiomics cohort but a portion thereof. This raises concerns about selection bias and whether the demonstrated reproducibility would hold across the full dataset or additional cohorts. Multi-cohort validation is a cornerstone of establishing robustness in medical AI, as emphasized in guidelines such as CLAIM (Checklist for Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging) [[1]](#citation-1), which stresses the importance of external validation datasets for establishing generalizability. "In the original work, the authors performed an integrative analysis on seven independent datasets across five institutions totaling 1,194 NSCLC patients" from the project README The README explicitly acknowledges that the original work validated across seven datasets, yet the cloud-based reproduction focuses only on one. This discrepancy between the original study's comprehensive validation and the limited scope of this reproduction significantly weakens the paper's claim to have demonstrated "end-to-end replication." Reproducing only 14% (1/7) of the validation scope cannot be considered comprehensive reproducibility evidence. References: [1] Mongan, J., Moy, L. & Kahn, C. E., Jr. Checklist for Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging (CLAIM): A Guide for Authors and Reviewers. Radiol Artif Intell 2, e200029 (2020). https://pubs.rsna.org/doi/abs/10.1148/ryai.2020200029

The paper makes broad claims about cloud infrastructure improving reproducibility in medical imaging AI but supports these claims with only a single case study (replication of one pipeline from one publication). This limited empirical evidence is insufficient to establish generalizability of the proposed approach across different AI pipelines, architectures, and application domains. "Our approach demonstrates the potential of cloud resources for implementing, sharing, and using reproducible and transparent AI pipelines in medical imaging, which can accelerate the translation into clinical solutions." This conclusion asserts broad applicability to "AI pipelines in medical imaging" based on a single reproduction attempt of one CNN-based prognostication model. Different AI architectures (transformers, diffusion models, ensemble methods) and application domains (segmentation, detection, classification) may present different reproducibility challenges that the proposed framework has not demonstrated it can address. Guidelines for AI reproducibility [[5]](#citation-5) emphasize that systematic evaluation across multiple studies is needed to establish reproducibility frameworks. "We demonstrated that the proposed cloud-based infrastructure supports the replication of published AI literature." The use of "supports the replication of published AI literature" (singular instance demonstrated, but broad claim made) represents an overgeneralization. The paper demonstrates one successful replication attempt with acknowledged limitations (different masks, statistical degradation). This does not constitute sufficient evidence to claim the infrastructure "supports replication" as a general capability. McDermott et al. [[6]](#citation-6) found that reproducibility in machine learning for health remains a significant challenge, with most studies failing to share adequate resources—a single success case does not address these systematic issues. "Finally, the resources we provide can be easily extended to run the same analysis on any other collection hosted on the Imaging Data Commons, provided the dataset contains imaging data suited to the AI pipeline." The qualifier "provided the dataset contains imaging data suited to the AI pipeline" acknowledges that the approach has limited applicability. Different AI pipelines have different data requirements, preprocessing needs, and computational demands. The paper does not demonstrate that its framework can accommodate this diversity, yet makes claims about its broad utility for "transparent and reproducible AI-based medical imaging pipelines" in general. References: [5] Varoquaux, G. & Cheplygina, V. Machine learning for medical imaging: methodological failures and recommendations for the future. NPJ Digit Med 5, 48 (2022). https://www.nature.com/articles/s41746-022-00592-y [6] McDermott, M. B. A. et al. Reproducibility in machine learning for health research: Still a ways to go. Sci. Transl. Med. 13, eabb1655 (2021). https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/scitranslmed.abb1655

The paper’s statistical support for “no statistically significant AUC difference” is undermined because the provided analysis notebook computes the DeLong test p-value incorrectly, which directly affects the claimed evidence in the Hosny et al. replication comparison. “After testing with various statistical tools, we observed that the difference between the results produced by the pipeline originally published by Hosny et Al. and those generated by our pipeline are not statistically significant (two-sided Mann-Whitney U test p>0.05, N=206; DeLong test for correlated (paired) AUC curves p>0.05, N=206).” This sentence relies on the DeLong test p-value as a key piece of statistical evidence. If the implementation used to compute that p-value is wrong, then the evidentiary basis for the “not statistically significant” assertion is weakened, even if the qualitative conclusion might sometimes remain unchanged. Because this claim is central to their replication argument (and is presented as a quantitative justification), correctness of the p-value computation matters for validity. A reproducibility paper should be especially careful that the released code matches the described statistical procedure. Here, the notebook’s p-value conversion step is inconsistent with the output convention of the imported DeLong implementation. This is a major issue because readers who rerun or extend the notebook will propagate an incorrect statistical result. ```python # Computes log(p-value) for hypothesis that two ROC AUCs are different delong_log_pval = delong_roc_test(y_true, y_pred_baseline, y_pred_replication) delong_pval = exp(delong_log_pval) print(delong_pval) ``` This code (from `hosny_results_comparison.ipynb`) converts `delong_log_pval` using `exp`, which assumes the value is a natural logarithm. However, the DeLong utility imported in this repository (see next evidence) returns a base-10 logarithm of the p-value, not a natural log, so `exp` is the wrong inverse transform. This makes the reported p-values numerically incorrect and therefore makes the quoted “p>0.05” evidence unreliable without independent correction. Even when both transforms yield values above 0.05 on this dataset, the magnitude of the p-value is still wrong, and in other settings this error could flip a decision around the threshold. Because the paper emphasizes transparent, inspectable statistical pipelines, this specific mismatch is a significant flaw. It also indicates the results were not cross-validated against a known-good DeLong implementation. ```python def calc_pvalue(aucs, sigma): """ Computes log(10) of p-values. Args: aucs: 1D array of AUCs sigma: AUC DeLong covariances Returns: log10(pvalue) """ l = np.array([[1, -1]]) z = np.abs(np.diff(aucs)) / np.sqrt(np.dot(np.dot(l, sigma), l.T)) return np.log10(2) + scipy.stats.norm.logsf(z, loc=0, scale=1) / np.log(10) ``` This code (from `src/utils/compare_auc_delong_xu.py`) explicitly documents that it “Computes log(10) of p-values” and returns `log10(pvalue)`. That means the correct conversion back to a p-value is `10**log10(pvalue)`, not `exp(log10(pvalue))`. Because the paper’s notebook uses this exact module (`from utils.compare_auc_delong_xu import delong_roc_test`) and then applies `exp`, the repository’s statistical pipeline is internally inconsistent. This is not a cosmetic bug: it changes the quantitative output that the manuscript cites as evidence. In a reproducibility-focused contribution, such an error directly contradicts the goal of providing reliably re-executable analyses. It also complicates independent verification because rerunning the shared notebook will not reproduce the intended statistical calculation.

The paper draws an unsupported conclusion that the replication discrepancy is “negligible” and that results “prove” robustness to segmentation-mask variation, even though the replication uses a different (newer) segmentation mask set and the reported non-significant p-values do not establish equivalence or robustness. “As these masks are a newer iteration of the set of labels used for the original publication, we anticipated differences in the results.” This sentence is an explicit admission that the replication’s inputs differ from the original study in a way that can affect downstream predictions. Once a key input (segmentation) is changed, a small observed AUC change on a single cohort does not, by itself, demonstrate robustness of the model to segmentation variation in general. Robustness claims typically require systematic perturbation studies, multiple segmentation variants (or multiple annotators), and reporting of effect sizes/uncertainty across conditions. The paper does not present such a design here; instead, it reports one comparison on one cohort. Acknowledging the input difference is good transparency, but it simultaneously raises the bar for what can be concluded about robustness. Therefore, the later “prove robustness” statement is not supported by the described setup. “Therefore, we can conclude that the discrepancy between the two predictive models is negligible. These results also prove the model's robustness to variation in the input segmentation mask, as the original work claims.” This is a strong inferential leap from a non-significant test result and a modest AUC difference (0.70 vs 0.68) to “negligible” discrepancy and “prove” robustness. Non-significance (p>0.05) does not imply equivalence, and it does not quantify how large a discrepancy could still be compatible with the data. To justify “negligible,” the paper would need an explicit equivalence margin (clinically or methodologically motivated) and confidence intervals on the AUC difference (or a formal equivalence test). To justify “robustness to segmentation,” it would need evidence across multiple segmentation perturbations, not just one newer label version. The manuscript also provides no analysis demonstrating that the segmentation change is the dominant driver of the AUC change, versus other pipeline differences. As written, the conclusion overstates what the evidence can support. “By convention a P value greater than 5% (P>0.05) is called “not significant.” … This term wrongly implies that the study has shown that there is no difference, whereas usually all that has been shown is an absence of evidence of a difference. These are quite different statements.” [[1]](#cite1) This BMJ guidance directly contradicts the manuscript’s use of p>0.05 as justification for calling the discrepancy “negligible” and implying robustness. The quote explains that “not significant” commonly gets misread as “no difference,” which is precisely the logical step the paper takes when it states the discrepancy is negligible after observing p>0.05. In a replication context, the correct interpretation of non-significance is even more important because the goal is often to quantify similarity and identify failure modes, not merely to fail to reject a null. The BMJ note also emphasizes uncertainty and power issues, which matter here because an AUC difference of 0.02 could still matter depending on clinical context and sample size. Therefore, the paper’s conclusion is not statistically warranted under established statistical interpretation norms. The criticism is about the validity of the inference, not about whether the observed AUC values are “close.” “Likewise, a relatively large p-value does not imply evidence in favor of the null hypothesis; many other hypotheses may be equally or more consistent with the observed data.” [[2]](#cite2) The ASA statement formalizes the point that a large p-value should not be treated as support for “no difference.” The paper uses p>0.05 to argue that the discrepancy is negligible and to “prove” robustness, which implicitly treats a large p-value as confirming the null (no meaningful difference). This is an interpretive error under the ASA’s consensus principles. Moreover, the ASA statement highlights that scientific conclusions should not hinge solely on thresholded p-values, whereas the manuscript’s key robustness conclusion is largely driven by threshold statements (p>0.05). The appropriate remedy would be to report uncertainty (e.g., confidence intervals for AUC difference) and define what difference would be practically important. Without that, the robustness claim remains ungrounded. This affects the reliability of the paper’s conclusions. ```python mwustat, pval = stats.mannwhitneyu(y_pred_baseline, y_pred_replication) print(mwustat) print(pval) ``` This code (from `hosny_results_comparison.ipynb`) applies a Mann–Whitney U test to the two sets of model scores. However, these scores are produced for the same set of patients (paired predictions), and Mann–Whitney U is an unpaired test that does not use within-subject pairing information. More importantly, even if correctly paired, a distributional test on raw scores is not an equivalence test for predictive performance and does not establish that the AUC difference is practically negligible. The notebook text frames this as a “starting point” for comparing outputs, but the manuscript elevates the resulting p>0.05 into a substantive “negligible discrepancy” conclusion. This strengthens the concern that the paper conflates “failure to detect a difference” with “no meaningful difference.” The statistical evidence as implemented is not sufficient for the strength of the claims made. References: [1] [Altman DG, Bland JM. “Statistics notes: Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.” *The BMJ* (1995).](https://www.bmj.com/content/311/7003/485) [2] [Wasserstein RL, Lazar NA. “ASA Statement on Statistical Significance and P-Values.” *The American Statistician* (2016) (PDF).](https://www.burtthompson.net/uploads/9/6/8/4/9684389/wasserstein-2016__asa_p-value_statement.pdf)

The paper’s methodological claim that cloud notebooks make each pipeline step “examined and reproduced” end-to-end is not fully met by the released materials because key steps (model conversion) are explicitly not executable in the claimed environment, dependencies and runtime images are not pinned to immutable versions, and the main comparison notebook loads precomputed result CSVs. “By leveraging the National Cancer Institute’s (CRDC) Imaging Data Commons (IDC) component for data and the Google Cloud Platform (GCP) component for code and computing, each step within the AI pipeline - from data fetching and pre-processing, deep learning inference, data post-processing, and the analysis of the final results - can be examined and reproduced.” This sentence commits to “each step” being reproducible, which sets a high bar for the artifact’s executability and environment capture. However, the repository documentation later admits that some essential steps (model conversion) cannot be run in Colab, and the project’s dependency specifications do not fully pin versions. In practice, end-to-end reproducibility requires that a reader can re-run all steps in a controlled environment, not only inspect code. The evidence below shows that at least one critical step depends on running a different Python version/stack outside Colab, which breaks the “examined and reproduced” promise in the main claimed environment. The claim also implies stability over time, but the provided Docker instructions and Python requirements do not ensure immutability. Therefore, the paper’s methodological conclusion overstates what the shared materials guarantee. “To convert the model from an older version of TensorFlow and Keras we need access to those libraries in the first place. As Google Colab does not support these older versions of such packages, these steps cannot unfortunately be run in a Google Colab notebook.” This statement (from `code/src/model/README.md`) directly contradicts the manuscript’s broader framing that Colab notebooks eliminate the need for users to set up their own runtime environment for reproducibility. If a critical step (converting the model) cannot be executed in Colab, then reproducing the pipeline from raw model artifacts requires an external environment, which is a nontrivial barrier and a potential source of divergence. For a reproducibility-focused paper, such “out-of-band” steps should be minimized, fully containerized, and pinned to immutable versions. Otherwise, future readers may be unable to regenerate the ONNX model and must trust a provided artifact. This weakens the “end-to-end” nature of the claimed reproducibility. It also makes the pipeline less auditable, since conversion correctness is difficult to validate without rerunning it. ```text docker pull us-docker.pkg.dev/colab-images/public/runtime ``` This instruction (from `code/README.md`) tells users to pull a Docker image without specifying a tag or digest. In container ecosystems, tags can be mutable and can point to different underlying images over time, meaning two users running the “same” command at different times may get different environments. This undermines the paper’s claim of a consistent and reproducible compute environment. It also shifts reproducibility risk onto external infrastructure changes, which the paper itself notes can happen, but does not mitigate in the released artifacts. For a paper advocating cloud-based reproducibility, recommending an unpinned runtime is a substantial methodological weakness. A stronger practice would be to pin the image by digest and/or publish an immutable container reference. “If no tag is specified, the image defaults to the `latest` tag.” [[4]](#cite4) This Docker documentation explains why an unqualified `docker pull` can be unstable: it effectively selects `latest` and therefore a moving target. That directly supports the critique that the paper’s Docker-based “local Colab runtime” guidance does not provide an immutable environment snapshot. Because reproducibility requires controlling software versions, relying on a mutable default tag invites silent environment drift (OS packages, CUDA stack, Python base). This is particularly consequential for ML inference pipelines, where small dependency differences can alter numeric outputs or break execution. The paper positions the cloud as a route to standardized environments, but the provided instruction contradicts established container versioning practices. Therefore, the methodology as released does not fully support the end-to-end reproducibility claim. “Sigstore recommends referring to the image by digest and not by tag to avoid attesting to the wrong image, and attesting by tag will be removed in a future version of Cosign.” [[5]](#cite5) While this quote is about supply-chain attestation, it captures a broader best practice: tags are not stable identifiers, and digests are. The paper’s guidance to pull an unpinned Colab runtime image relies on tags (implicitly `latest`) rather than immutable digests, which is incompatible with strong reproducibility guarantees. In computational research artifacts, immutability is necessary not only for security but also for scientific reproducibility, because rerunning workflows must recreate the same environment. This quote supports the methodological argument that the paper should provide digest-pinned container references if it claims a standardized compute environment. Without such pinning, results can be non-reproducible despite “using the cloud.” Thus, the artifact design falls short of the paper’s reproducibility narrative. ```text SimpleITK pydicom matplotlib seaborn pandas numpy onnx onnxruntime_gpu pyplastimatch scikit-learn thedicomsort ``` This dependency list (from `code/requirements.txt`) does not pin versions, even though the manuscript claims that tools “are packaged and versioned and can thus be recovered and installed at any point in time.” Unpinned dependencies are a well-known source of non-reproducibility because future installs may resolve to different major versions with changed APIs or numerical behavior. This is especially relevant for packages like `onnxruntime_gpu`, `SimpleITK`, and `scikit-learn`, where version changes can affect inference compatibility and evaluation outputs. A reproducibility-focused release would typically include exact versions and, ideally, a lockfile or container image digest that freezes the environment. The absence of version pins means that even if the notebooks run today, they may fail or change behavior later. This materially weakens the transparency/reproducibility claims. ```python # analysis baseline: Hosny et al. results replicated_csv_name = 'nsclc-radiomics_reproducibility.csv' replicated_csv_path = os.path.join(DATA_PATH, replicated_csv_name) replicated_df = pd.read_csv(replicated_csv_path) ``` This notebook code (from `hosny_results_comparison.ipynb`) shows that the main comparison analysis loads a precomputed CSV rather than reproducing all upstream steps within the notebook itself. While providing cached outputs is useful for convenience, it weakens the paper’s “end-to-end” reproducibility framing unless the paper clearly separates “inspectable analysis” from “fully rerunnable pipeline from raw data.” In this setting, a reader can reproduce downstream plots/statistics but not necessarily reproduce the generated logits without running the full (hours-long) inference notebook and matching the exact environment. The manuscript describes that “each step” can be reproduced, but the primary evidence figure is driven by a file load of precomputed results. This increases the importance of strict environment pinning and provenance tracking for that CSV, which is not fully provided. Consequently, the artifact does not fully satisfy common reproducible computational research expectations. “In order to exactly reproduce a given result, it may be necessary to use programs in the exact versions used originally.” [[3]](#cite3) This rule from a widely cited reproducible computational research guideline directly motivates why the paper’s unpinned dependencies and unpinned container pull are problematic. The manuscript’s goal is to demonstrate reproducibility in medical imaging pipelines, where version drift is a common failure mode. Yet the released environment specifications do not ensure that the “exact versions used originally” will be recovered in future reruns. This means that the paper’s practical recipe may not deliver the reproducibility benefits it advocates. The quote supports the critique that the authors should freeze versions (packages, OS image, GPU stack) to align artifact practice with reproducibility norms. Without doing so, the paper’s methodology does not meet its own stated objectives. References: [3] [Sandve GK, Nekrutenko A, Taylor J, Hovig E. “Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research.” *PLOS Computational Biology* (2013).](https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003285) [4] [Docker Docs. “Tags on Docker Hub.”](https://docs.docker.com/docker-hub/repos/manage/hub-images/tags/) [5] [Chainguard Academy. “How to Sign an SBOM with Cosign.”](https://edu.chainguard.dev/open-source/sigstore/cosign/how-to-sign-an-sbom-with-cosign/)

The paper proposes Google Colab as a solution for reproducibility, but fails to address the inherent instability of such platforms, which undermines the long-term reproducibility of the proposed pipelines. "Hosny et Al. model was developed using Keras 1.2.2 and an old version of Tensorflow... Since early 2023, Google Colab instances are running TensorFlow 2.x.x only - and Hosny et Al. model is not compatible... Therefore, pulling the model from the project repository will not work." The authors explicitly admit that the platform they advocate for (Google Colab) introduced a breaking change that rendered the original model unusable without significant modification (conversion to ONNX). This demonstrates that relying on managed cloud environments, which frequently update their underlying software stacks without user control, is detrimental to long-term reproducibility. A more robust approach would involve containerization (e.g., Docker) [1], which allows researchers to freeze the entire software environment, ensuring that the code runs indefinitely regardless of external platform updates. By not discussing containerization as a primary or alternative solution, the paper promotes a method that is susceptible to "software rot." References: [1] Boettiger, C. (2015). An introduction to Docker for reproducible research. *ACM SIGOPS Operating Systems Review*, 49(1), 71-79. https://doi.org/10.1145/2723872.2723882

The paper validates the reproduced pipeline on an unexplained "subset" of the original dataset, which compromises the statistical validity and comparability of the results. "we compared our cloud-based pipeline with the original results shared by Hosny et Al. on a subset of the NSCLC-Radiomics dataset" The authors state they used a "subset" of the NSCLC-Radiomics dataset but fail to define how this subset was selected (e.g., random sampling, exclusion criteria, or data availability issues). Without this information, it is impossible to determine if the subset is representative of the full cohort used in the original study [2]. If the subset was chosen based on convenience (e.g., only cases available in IDC), it introduces potential selection bias. Furthermore, comparing performance metrics (AUC) derived from a subset against metrics derived from a different (likely larger) set in the original study is methodologically unsound unless the original study also reported results for this specific subset. References: [2] Hosny, A., Parmar, C., Quackenbush, J., Schwartz, L. H., & Aerts, H. J. (2018). Deep learning for lung cancer prognostication: A retrospective multi-cohort radiomics study. *PLoS Medicine*, 15(11), e1002711. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pmed.1002711

The paper claims to demonstrate "Pipelines" (plural) for medical imaging, but the manuscript only presents a single case study, which is insufficient to support the broad claims of the title and abstract. "Transparent and Reproducible AI-based Medical Imaging Pipelines Using the Cloud" (Title) The title and abstract use the plural "Pipelines," implying a generalized framework validated across multiple scenarios. However, the text only describes the re-implementation of a single study by Hosny et al. [2]. While the accompanying code repository appears to contain a second study (Pai et al. [3]), this work is not discussed, analyzed, or even mentioned in the main text of the manuscript. A single example is not sufficient to claim a generalized solution for the diverse field of medical imaging, which involves various modalities, data formats, and preprocessing requirements.
