The paper’s headline conductivity claims are potentially misleading because the abstract reports ~0.1 S cm^-1 for Na_{3.4}Zr_2Si_{2.4}P_{0.6}O_{12} without stating the temperature, while the main text indicates similarly large values only at elevated temperature (e.g., 573 K), weakening the robustness of the conclusions about “highest ionic conductivities” for practical solid-state battery conditions. We performed impedance spectroscopy of samples with varying Na compositions revealing that the highest ionic conductivity (\(~0.1\ S\ cm^{-1}\)) is achieved in Na_{3.4}Zr_2Si_{2.4}P_{0.6}O_{12}, in line with our predictions (\(~0.2\ S\ cm^{-1}\)). The abstract presents a very large conductivity value in S cm^-1 without specifying the temperature, which is critical because ionic conductivity in solids is strongly temperature dependent and commonly reported at a standard reference temperature (often ~25 °C) when discussing practical relevance. Without a temperature qualifier, a reader can reasonably interpret ~0.1 S cm^-1 as near-room-temperature performance, which would be extraordinary for oxide NaSICON ceramics. The main text later anchors comparable magnitudes at much higher temperatures, indicating that the abstract’s phrasing lacks essential context. Because the paper motivates the work partly by solid-state battery relevance, temperature ambiguity can materially distort the perceived significance. This is not a cosmetic issue: it affects whether the claimed performance is immediately actionable for typical operating conditions. Therefore, the omission undermines the clarity and can inflate the perceived impact. Nonetheless, for Na\(_3\)Zr\(_2\)Si\(_2\)P\(_1\)O\(_{12}\) our measured experimental conductivity (see Figure 4b) of ~ 0.1 S cm\(^{-1}\) at 573 K is in excellent agreement with our computed value of ~ 0.2 S cm\(^{-1}\). This sentence explicitly ties the ~0.1 S cm^-1 experimental value to **573 K**, which is far above room temperature. This directly shows that at least one of the headline-scale conductivities discussed in the paper occurs only at elevated temperature. When juxtaposed with the abstract’s temperature-free claim, it suggests the abstract compresses temperature-dependent performance into a single number without proper context. This matters because a conductivity of 0.1 S cm^-1 at 573 K does not imply similar performance at 298 K, given typical Arrhenius behavior and the paper’s own discussion of activation energies and phase transitions. As a result, the paper’s “highest ionic conductivity” statement is not robustly interpretable for practical conditions. The evidence supports that the narrative could overstate practical significance unless temperatures are made explicit. Measurements were recorded every 50 K from 323 K to 623 K (15 minutes soaking each time), and then cooled down every 50 K from 623 K to 323 K. This methods detail shows that the experimental impedance spectroscopy campaign is explicitly a **high-temperature** measurement protocol spanning 323–623 K. Given this, any claim of “highest ionic conductivity” should be reported together with the temperature (or as a room-temperature extrapolation with uncertainty), because the measured values are inherently temperature-indexed. The abstract does not clarify whether its 0.1 S cm^-1 refers to 373 K, 473 K, 573 K, or another temperature, even though the paper later emphasizes multiple Arrhenius regimes and a phase transition near 433 K. This ambiguity makes it hard to evaluate the claimed agreement between experiment and theory and to compare to the broader literature. In a field where room-temperature conductivity is often the benchmark for applicability, omitting temperature is a major interpretability flaw. The methods quote therefore supports that the headline performance statement is insufficiently grounded. ... using a solution-assisted solid-state reaction for preparation of Na3+xZr2Si2+xP1−xO12, a total conductivity of 5 × 10−3 S cm−1 was achieved for Na3.4Zr2Si2.4P0.6O12 at 25 °C... This external sentence provides a clear example of standard reporting practice: conductivity is explicitly stated **at 25 °C** and distinguished as “total conductivity” ([1](https://pubs.rsc.org/en/content/articlelanding/2019/ta/c9ta00048h)). It also gives a room-temperature benchmark for the same nominal composition family (including Na3.4Zr2Si2.4P0.6O12), reinforcing that temperature context substantially changes how large a reported conductivity is. The reviewed paper’s abstract does not provide an equivalent temperature qualifier, making its headline number hard to interpret relative to established room-temperature baselines. While different processing routes can yield different conductivities, the key issue here is that the external literature communicates temperature and measurement type explicitly. This contrast supports the criticism that the reviewed paper’s abstract overstates or at least obscures practical performance. The external quote therefore strengthens the case that the paper’s presentation could mislead readers about room-temperature applicability.

The paper’s conclusion that diffusivity/conductivity “cannot be used to identify” phase separation is not sufficiently supported because it asserts that a predicted ~1-order-of-magnitude signature is “within the error” without presenting quantified experimental uncertainties, statistical confidence intervals, or a sensitivity analysis showing detectability limits. ... this dip in ion transport properties approximately accounts for one order of magnitude, well within the error of the available experiments and our calculations, thus making it challenging to detect the signature of phase separation via diffusivity/conductivity measurements or calculations. This statement makes a strong detectability claim (“challenging to detect”) while only qualitatively referencing “the error” without quantifying it. In solid-state ionics, whether a one-order-of-magnitude change is detectable depends on the actual experimental uncertainty (including pellet-to-pellet variability, fitting ambiguity, and microstructure effects) and on the computational statistical uncertainty across sampled configurations. The paper does not provide error bars on the key transport plots nor a formal uncertainty propagation that would justify the detectability conclusion. Moreover, the computational model’s uncertainty is not transparently translated into confidence bounds on the phase-separation dip (e.g., distribution over configurations for the phase-separated ensembles). As a result, the inference that conductivity cannot identify phases is not robustly established. Because this conclusion is extended beyond NASICON (“may also be valid for other solid electrolytes and electrodes”), the lack of quantified uncertainty becomes a major issue. Our calculations suggest that Na diffusivity and conductivity cannot be used to identify the underlying phases within the NASICON electrolyte. This is an unusually strong negative conclusion (“cannot be used”) that would normally require a careful power/detectability analysis demonstrating that, given realistic experimental noise and model uncertainty, transport signatures are statistically indistinguishable. The paper instead provides a qualitative argument and does not report, for example, the variance of conductivity across the phase-separated configurations relative to the solid-solution configurations. In practical terms, even if the average dip is ~10×, detectability could still be possible with controlled microstructure and repeated measurements—unless proven otherwise. The statement also risks conflating “difficult under typical measurement scatter” with “in principle impossible,” which are scientifically different. Without quantified uncertainties, the conclusion is not reliably supported by the presented evidence. This weakens the robustness of the paper’s broader guidance on identifying phase separation. The grey band in panel b shows the interval of confidence of our predictions at 573 K, which is lower in magnitude than the variance of Na-ion conductivities observed experimentally (Figure S6). While this mentions an “interval of confidence,” the paper does not define how the confidence interval is computed (e.g., percentile range across 50 configurations, standard error of the mean, bootstrapping, or another method). Without a definition, it is unclear whether the grey band is a statistically meaningful uncertainty estimate that can be used to support detectability claims. Additionally, the phrase “variance ... observed experimentally” is not accompanied by experimental error bars or a measurement model explaining what portion of scatter is noise versus systematic differences across studies. For detectability of phase separation, the relevant comparison is not just “variance across literature reports,” but uncertainty under controlled conditions for the same processing and microstructure. Therefore, even where uncertainty language appears, it is insufficiently operationalized to justify the strong inference made. This supports the criticism that the statistical treatment is inadequate for the claimed conclusion. ... the diffusional properties obtained from AIMD simulations are often plagued by poor statistics. This external sentence highlights a general, well-known issue in computational ion-transport estimates: limited sampling can lead to unreliable diffusivities and conductivities unless statistics are carefully handled ([2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41524-018-0074-y)). Although the reviewed paper uses kMC rather than AIMD and emphasizes large sampling, the same principle applies when making detectability claims based on differences that are only ~10×. To argue that a one-order-of-magnitude dip is “within error,” the authors need to clearly quantify statistical uncertainty in their kMC averages and compare it to experimental uncertainty under comparable conditions. The external quote underscores why simply asserting “within error” is inadequate: statistical reliability must be demonstrated, not assumed. This is especially important because the paper’s conclusion is negative and generalized beyond the specific system. Therefore, the external evidence supports the need for more rigorous uncertainty quantification than what is provided.

The paper directly compares computationally predicted bulk conductivity from kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulations with experimental total ionic conductivity measured from polycrystalline pellets, without adequately addressing that experimental measurements include grain boundary contributions that are not modeled in the simulations. "our measured experimental conductivity (see Figure 4b) of ~ 0.1 S cm⁻¹ at 573 K is in excellent agreement with our computed value of ~ 0.2 S cm⁻¹" This comparison treats computational and experimental values as directly comparable despite fundamental methodological differences. The kMC simulations model only bulk (grain interior) diffusion in idealized perfect crystals, while the experimental impedance spectroscopy measurements on polycrystalline pellets include both bulk and grain boundary contributions. The supplementary materials reveal pellet compacities of only 69-77% (Table S6), indicating significant porosity and grain boundary density. Standard practice in the solid electrolyte field is to separate bulk and grain boundary contributions when comparing with computational results, as grain boundaries can significantly reduce measured conductivity, particularly in NASICON-type materials. * Quote from supplementary materials (Table S6): "Compacity (%) x=1.5: 69.16; x=2.0: 71.21; x=2.4: 76.63" The low compacity values (less than 80%) indicate that the experimental pellets have significant porosity and grain boundary volume. In polycrystalline ceramics with such densification levels, the grain boundary contribution to total resistance can be substantial. The apparent "agreement" between computed (~0.2 S/cm) and experimental (~0.1 S/cm) values may be coincidental, as bulk conductivity in fully dense samples could be even higher than the experimental total conductivity, potentially indicating a larger discrepancy from computational predictions than suggested. "The fitting of Nyquist plot was carried out using ZView software. For x = 1.5, the equivalent circuit used was based on semicircle phenomena... Applying the same equivalent circuit for other samples (x = 2.0 and x = 2.4) was not satisfactory, thus a one-phenomena Debye Model for solid electrolyte between two ionically blocking electrodes is used instead." The use of different equivalent circuits for different compositions suggests that the impedance spectra could not be consistently analyzed to separate bulk and grain boundary contributions. The one-phenomena Debye model used for x = 2.0 and 2.4 compositions does not separate these contributions, yet the paper claims excellent agreement with bulk conductivity calculations. Ma et al. [[1]](#citation-1) demonstrated that for NASICON materials, bulk grain conductivity can exceed 0.01 S/cm at room temperature, significantly higher than total conductivity measured from typical polycrystalline samples. References: [1] Ma, Q. et al. Room temperature demonstration of a sodium superionic conductor with grain conductivity in excess of 0.01 S cm⁻¹ and its primary applications in symmetric battery cells. [*J. Mater. Chem. A* **7**, 7766–7776 (2019)](https://doi.org/10.1039/C9TA00048H)

The kinetic Monte Carlo simulations employ a fixed attempt frequency (ν = 0.5 × 10¹³ Hz) without justification, sensitivity analysis, or acknowledgment that this parameter directly affects the absolute values of predicted diffusivity and conductivity by orders of magnitude. * Quote from code (run_kmc.py, line 16): `v = 0.5*10**13` The attempt frequency is hard-coded as a constant value of 5 THz across all compositions and temperatures. In transition state theory, the attempt frequency (also called the pre-exponential factor or vibrational prefactor) relates to phonon frequencies at the transition state and can vary significantly between different materials, compositions, and even different migration paths within the same material. The absolute diffusivity D and conductivity σ scale linearly with this parameter, meaning any error in the attempt frequency propagates directly to all predicted transport properties. * Quote from Methods section: "We implemented a rejection-free kinetic Monte Carlo (kMC) simulation scheme in an in-house-code, as described in Refs.³⁹,⁴⁰" While the paper references the theoretical framework for kMC, it does not discuss how the attempt frequency was determined or validated. The referenced works (Van der Ven et al. [[3]](#citation-3)) describe the methodology but the specific choice of 5 THz for NASICON is not justified. Studies on ionic conductors show that attempt frequencies can range from 10¹² to 10¹⁴ Hz depending on the system, and this variation alone could shift predicted conductivity by up to an order of magnitude. Without uncertainty quantification for this parameter, the reported "excellent agreement" with experiment cannot be rigorously evaluated. "The grey band in panel b shows the interval of confidence of our predictions at 573 K, which is lower in magnitude than the variance of Na-ion conductivities observed experimentally" While the paper presents confidence intervals for conductivity predictions, these reflect only the statistical variance from different Si/P configurations, not systematic uncertainties from model parameters like the attempt frequency. A proper uncertainty analysis would need to propagate errors from all assumed parameters. He et al. [[4]](#citation-4) established rigorous procedures for quantifying statistical variances in diffusion property calculations, emphasizing that absolute values are sensitive to multiple computational parameters beyond just configurational sampling. References: [3] Van der Ven, A., Ceder, G., Asta, M. & Tepesch, P. D. First-principles theory of ionic diffusion with nondilute carriers. [*Phys. Rev. B* **64**, 184307 (2001)](https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevB.64.184307) [4] He, X., Zhu, Y., Epstein, A. & Mo, Y. Statistical variances of diffusional properties from ab initio molecular dynamics simulations. [*npj Comput. Mater.* **4**, 18 (2018)](https://doi.org/10.1038/s41524-018-0074-y)

The paper performs extensive kMC simulations across 11 Na compositions spanning the full range of 0 ≤ x ≤ 3, but experimental validation is limited to only three compositions (x = 1.5, 2.0, 2.4), leaving the majority of the computational predictions, particularly at low Na content (x < 1.5) where conductivity is orders of magnitude lower, without experimental verification. "In total, more than 2,000 kMC simulations were performed to predict the Na⁺ diffusivities and conductivities at selected temperatures of 373, 473 and 573 K of 50 NASICON models (with varying Si/P configurations) at 11 distinct Na concentrations." The computational study systematically covers 11 compositions (x = 0.03, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.1, 2.4, 2.7, 2.97), representing a significant investment in computational resources. However, this extensive compositional mapping is validated experimentally at only three points (x = 1.5, 2.0, 2.4), all in the high-conductivity regime. The predictions for low-Na compositions where conductivity is predicted to be 4-6 orders of magnitude lower remain entirely unvalidated. "We have synthesized different compositions of the NASICON, namely, Na₂.₅Zr₂Si₁.₅P₁.₅O₁₂, Na₃Zr₂Si₂P₁O₁₂, and Na₃.₄Zr₂Si₂.₄P₀.₆O₁₂ and tested their Na-ion transport characteristics." The three experimentally studied compositions all fall within the high-conductivity regime (x = 1.5 to 2.4). Key predictions about the sharp rise in conductivity from x = 0 to x ≈ 2 and the drop at x > 2.4 are not experimentally validated in this work. While low-conductivity compositions are more challenging to synthesize phase-pure and to measure accurately, the paper's strong conclusions about the composition-conductivity relationship across the full range would benefit from broader experimental validation. Prior literature exists for end-member compositions [[5]](#citation-5) but direct comparison under identical synthesis and measurement conditions is lacking. "Our model also agrees with previous measurements¹⁸,¹⁹,³⁰,³² which report a low conductivity and diffusivity in Na₁Zr₂P₃O₁₂." For the low-Na end-member, the paper relies on literature comparison rather than direct experimental validation in this study. Given the paper's emphasis on the large variability in reported conductivity values across different studies ("Figure S6 reveals a significant spread of experimentally measured ionic conductivities from different reports"), validating the full compositional range under controlled conditions would strengthen the conclusions about the optimal x ~ 2.4 composition. References: [5] Goodenough, J. B., Hong, H. Y.-P. & Kafalas, J. A. Fast Na⁺-ion transport in skeleton structures. [*Mater. Res. Bull.* **11**, 203–220 (1976)](https://doi.org/10.1016/0025-5408(76)90077-5)

Each of the disorders is associated with a phenotypic profile (symptoms + neurocognitive disturbances). The study is somewhat limited for not incorporating these. For example, are there common/overlapping neurocognitive disturbances that correspond to the overlapping neurobiological changes. I assume ENIGMA has this information per disorder, that could be incorporated alongside the cortical thickness. This would extend the paper more deeply into translational territory and would be stronger than the superficial “reverse inference” observations on the top of page 8, second column. Likewise, are there symptom- or cognitive features that associate more strongly with the “molecular-correlated” disorders on Fig. 3 (ADHD, MDD – e.g. attention) versus those with a “connectivity” profile (SCZ – e.g. executive functioning). If this is to be deferred to future work, it should in the least be addressed in the Discussion.

Most of the disorders – some more than others – are quite heterogenous over time (recent onset versus chronic), across age, and across patients (e.g. unipolar versus melancholic versus bipolar depression). Most of the patients in the clinical ENIGMA cohorts are quite stable and have been on pharmacological therapies, many of which have impacts on cortical thickness (e.g. anti-psychotics [1]). MDD, SCZ and ADHD in particular are not unitary entities across time or patients and their representation as singular entities in the various figures is a translational limitation. This approach is not at all unique to the present paper, but a widespread limitation so there is no line to be drawn in the sand here: Again, this could be addressed with some additional analyses (such as a break-out analysis of medication, age or time since illness onset analyses on at least one diagnostic category) or simply acknowledged in the Discussion

A methodological limitation is that the core method essentially derives from “correlations of correlations”, limiting insights into deeper mechanistic causes. I think the existing “dig down” analyses are elucidating and largely overcome this weakness, but I would avoid statements like “regional molecular vulnerability and macroscale brain network architecture interact to drive the spatial patterning of cortical abnormalities in multiple disorders” which is something that could hardly have a counter-factual.

The main marker of neurobiological perturbation is cortical thickness: While this is a good choice, it is arguably not that surprising to see molecular associations stronger than connectomic ones, given that the former impact directly on cortical activity, function and integrity. But does intra-cortical pathology hold a privileged position when considering the pathogenesis of brain disorders? Some might argue that many disorders arise primarily in white matter connections, and that these then propagate to cortical grey matter. This is a relatively minor point but might warrant a brief note in the paper.
I thought it was only the analysis pipelines that were harmonized across ENIGMA not the image acquisition (p9) which were quite site specific (but I could be wrong!).
p4: Why “embryonic” stage. Wiring abnormalities can arise across any neurodevelopmental stage.

The MRI data used to reconstruct the normative functional and structural connectivity data is not particularly state of the art (long TR, 32 channel head coil, single-shell, deterministic tractography etc). I doubt this will impact on the bottom-line findings, but I am not sure why the authors did not choose a more advanced HCP-like data set to work with.

The authors note the limitations of the 68-region DK cortical atlas – and the rationale for using it. But the regions in this atlas differ highly in extent and it would be worth checking that regions that dominate the cross-diagnostic findings are not simple larger in extent and therefore more likely to overlap by chance, and more likely to have higher SnR for all the analysis steps.

A point perhaps for another occasion, but I am a bit sceptical about the now widely used spin test to represent nulls for spatial correlations between data sets: While it is crucial that spatial correlations be preserved in nulls, the process of conflated and then collapsing the cortical surface will introduce geometric distortions that over-estimate the true underlying null variance: spatial correlations from highly curved regions will be compressed onto the inflated representation which are then spun and mapped onto flatter regions. While it seems likely that spatial correlations will be preserved ‘on average’ the variance (the width of the null distribution) will be a composite of the true variance plus these geometric effects. A more accurate null could be obtained through whitening then resampling on the naïve cortical geometry. I do not think this is an issue for the present paper (because if anything a wider null means a more conservative test), but hope this limitation either be rebutted or acknowledged in the corresponding Methods section of the ms (p12).

For some analyses, a considerable number of tests have been carried out (for example those depicted in Figure 2). I understand that correcting for multiple comparisons could be problematic here (metrics are not independent) but, if no correction procedure is used I think it should be at least acknowledged on the limitation section.

Is the epicentre likelihood related to Euclidean distance? I found intriguing that the highest epicentre likelihood is located at motor (and parietal) cortex. I would have expected that association regions and hubs will constitute these epicentres. As authors point out “In particular, regions that are highly connected and potentially important for communication tend to be disproportionately affected by disease [26, 114]”. I wonder whether I am misinterpreting the metric or whether there the distance effect on the connectivity matrix influences the epicentre likelihood. Interestingly, authors discuss this finding in the discussion but arguments are focused on previous evidence showing that sensory-motor cortex is a plausible epicentre. Could you please discuss whether this is specific to primary cortex? or rather there is also evidence of the opposite (epicentres predominantly in associations cortices). Note: Looking at Figure S7, I think that one possibility is that this is driven by the epilepsy (which is duplicated). Given that these maps show considerable differences in regional distribution across conditions, is the average a meaningful metric?

The disorder similarity matrix showed in Figure 5a looks very similar to a Structural Covariance Network. Given that it is computed as “pairwise correlation of regional cortical thickness” (according to figure 5a legend) I wonder whether the associations described later are structural-Covariance-related (e.g. as described in Romero-Garcia et al. 2018, Neuroimage) rather than disorder-related. Would it make sense to compute the disorder similarity as the correlation between “cortical thinning (for example, the difference with controls or the T-scores)” rather than using raw cortical thickness? Note: The Disorder similarity methods section states that “we correlated the abnormality vectors” so authors may already have gone down this route. If that’s the case, it will be just a matter of rephrasing this part of Figure 5a legend) and no discussion or analysis would be required.

Explicitly mention that the Lausanne dataset has been used elsewhere (e.g. Bazinet et al. 2021, Neuroimage). Could authors briefly mention why deterministic rather than probabilistic tractography was used?

The quantification of myelin content using MRI is still under debate. For example, to my knowledge, there are no articles correlating myelin histology with T1w/T2w ratio. For other approaches such as Magnetization Transfers, R2 ranges between 0.4 and 0.7 approximately. I understand that this is not within the scope of this manuscript (it doesn’t even show a significant association) but I recommend to the authors that they add a one or two lines outlining this limitation of MRI-based myelin markers (see Van der Weijden et al. 2021, Neuroimage, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1053811920310466?via=ihub, for an interesting discussion.).

I am intrigued that Schizophrenia and Schizotypy have such a differentiated influence of molecular vs. connectivity predictors. It is mentioned on the results but it would be interesting to mention on the discussion
Genetic sex has an impact on brain development. Should sex-stratified analysis be done? This may improve power by deconfounding, or lead to some further insight.

The extent to which the global hypothesis is refuted by this work depends much on the quality of the global phenotypes. For example, if the noise level is higher in the global phenotypes, then they will be found to be less of a contributor to cortical abnormality even if a "noise free" version of the global phenotypes is actually more predictive. Can the authors somehow assess the noise level in the local - vs- global phenotypes (maybe with a simulation?) Or possibly repeat the construction of the global attributes using a different preprocessing pipeline?

This might be a good validation of the methodology: can the authors look at pairwise genetic correlation between the pathologies and conditions they study, and see if the pathologies and conditions that share genetic architecture significantly are also close together in the Figure 3 axis? This seems like a good control (towards gaining the sort of confidence I asked for in the previous point) and if the global attributes are robust they should emerge correlated in traits that have similar genetic etiology.
Some short forms such as pd for Parkinson's and 22q for 22q11.2 deletion are inconsistently used (i.e., sometimes the short form is used othertimes not, which could be confusing).

If it's possible, the title could be changed so that it's more clear that "shape" is a verb, for example "Cross-disorder cortical abnormalities are shaped by ..." I know that's longer, maybe there's another way. As it is, it is a bit hard to parse.
On page 3, there is a typo "cortical abnormaliity" should be "cortical abnormality"

For Figure 5: x-axis should be on same scale across all except panel a. Pearson's r in some panels looks like < -1, is that because of jitter? panel b: The authors claim this is a normal distribution, can they provide a ks test? panel g: the top 2 and bottom 2 should likely be switched to match caption. Finally, significant correlation is not a contrast to low correlation. None of these are highly correlated (but, they are indeed all positively correlated).
Typo in caption of Figure 1: "inhibitory".

The paper claims to demonstrate "network spreading" of cortical abnormalities but uses only cross-sectional data, which cannot distinguish between actual spreading processes and other explanations for spatial correlations. The methodology (correlation between regional abnormality and mean neighbor abnormality) only demonstrates spatial association, not mechanistic spreading. "This suggests a network spreading phenomenon where focal pathology or perturbation propagates to connected regions, resulting in cortical abnormality that is correlated with the underlying connection patterns." This statement implies a causal, mechanistic spreading process ("propagates"), yet the cross-sectional node-neighbor correlation analysis cannot establish temporal dynamics of spreading. The observed spatial correlation could arise from multiple alternative mechanisms including shared genetic vulnerability, common environmental exposures, simultaneous developmental insults, or confounding by unmeasured variables. Critically, demonstrating actual "propagation" requires longitudinal data showing that abnormalities in one region precede and predict subsequent abnormalities in connected regions. "We hypothesized that abnormalities in such molecular mechanisms at the regional level may spread trans-synaptically" Trans-synaptic spreading is a mechanistic process that has been established in neurodegenerative diseases through longitudinal studies and experimental manipulations in animal models. The term "trans-synaptic" implies a specific biological mechanism (protein transfer across synapses), but the cross-sectional correlation analysis used here cannot provide evidence for this mechanism. Previous studies that have established trans-synaptic spreading used longitudinal PET imaging (e.g., Vogel et al. 2020 [[1]](#citation-list)) or postmortem sequential staging (Braak staging), not cross-sectional correlations. * Quote from Brown et al. (2019) [[2]](#citation-list): "Here we evaluated this transneuronal degeneration hypothesis by attempting to predict future atrophy in a longitudinal cohort of patients with behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia" This study by Brown et al., which is cited by the reviewed paper, explicitly uses longitudinal data to test whether neurodegeneration "progresses along connectional pathways." The authors of that study chose a longitudinal design specifically because cross-sectional node-neighbor correlations cannot establish spreading dynamics. The reviewed paper's cross-sectional approach is methodologically weaker for testing the same hypothesis. * Quote from Markello & Misic (2021) [[3]](#citation-list): "Technological and data sharing advances have led to a proliferation of high-resolution structural and functional maps of the brain... however, most standard methods for statistical inference fail to account for their spatial properties." This paper (by authors including the corresponding author of the reviewed paper) notes that spatial correlations between brain maps are subject to multiple confounds. While the reviewed paper uses spin tests to control for spatial autocorrelation, this still cannot distinguish true spreading from co-occurring vulnerability at spatially proximate regions, as both would produce similar node-neighbor correlation patterns. References: [1] Vogel, J. W. et al. (2020). Spread of pathological tau proteins through communicating neurons in human Alzheimer's disease. Nature Communications, 11, 2612. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-15701-2 [2] Brown, J. A. et al. (2019). Patient-Tailored, Connectivity-Based Forecasts of Spreading Brain Atrophy. Neuron, 104(5), 856–868. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0896627319307433 [3] Markello, R. D. & Misic, B. (2021). Comparing spatial null models for brain maps. NeuroImage, 236, 118052. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1053811921003293

All molecular predictors (gene expression, receptor densities, metabolism, synapse density, myelination) and connectivity predictors are derived exclusively from healthy adult participants, yet these are used to predict and explain cortical abnormalities in patient populations where these features may be substantially altered by disease processes. "Gene expression data was collected by the Allen Human Brain Atlas as described in Hawrylycz et al." The Allen Human Brain Atlas is derived from postmortem brain tissue of donors without known neurological or psychiatric disorders. However, the disorders studied in this paper (schizophrenia, depression, epilepsy, Parkinson's disease, etc.) are known to involve altered gene expression patterns in the brain. Using healthy-derived gene expression to predict patient abnormalities assumes that the spatial pattern of gene expression is preserved in disease, which may not be valid for many of the studied conditions. "Receptor densities were estimated using PET tracer studies for a total of 18 receptors and transporters, across 9 neurotransmitter systems." The PET receptor density maps are from healthy volunteers, but many disorders studied involve receptor alterations. For example, dopamine receptors are altered in Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and ADHD; serotonin receptors are altered in depression and OCD. Using healthy receptor profiles to predict abnormalities in these disorders is circular if the diseases involve changes to the very receptor systems being used as predictors. "The data used was structural and functional connectivity data for 69 healthy adults (25 males, 28.8 ± 9.1 years of age) from the Lausanne University Hospital" The structural and functional connectomes used to assess "connectomic vulnerability" are from healthy young adults, yet diseases like schizophrenia, epilepsy, and Parkinson's are associated with altered structural and functional connectivity. Hansen et al. (2022) [[4]](#citation-list) themselves noted that "only seven proteins... show significant and large relationships with the expression of their corresponding genes," highlighting that even in healthy brains, the relationship between molecular measures can be complex. In disease states, these relationships may be further disrupted. * Quote from the paper's limitations: "both local biological and global connectivity predictors are derived from state-of-the-art open-access datasets in healthy participants, but they do not capture individual variability or changes across the lifespan" While the authors acknowledge this as a limitation, they do not discuss how disease-related changes to molecular and connectivity profiles might specifically bias their results. For example, if receptor densities are systematically altered in patient populations, the correlations observed between healthy receptor profiles and patient abnormalities may reflect confounding by regional vulnerability rather than causal mechanisms. References: [4] Hansen, J. Y., Markello, R. D., Tuominen, L. et al. (2022). Correspondence between gene expression and neurotransmitter receptor and transporter density in the human brain. NeuroImage, 264, 119671. https://juser.fz-juelich.de/record/910494/files/Hansen_etal_NeuroImage_2022.pdf

The paper repeatedly uses causal and mechanistic language ("drive," "shape," "propagate," "govern") to describe relationships that are purely correlational, which may mislead readers about the strength of evidence for mechanistic claims. "We find that regional molecular vulnerability and macroscale brain network architecture interact to drive the spatial patterning of cortical abnormalities in multiple disorders." The word "drive" implies causation, but the analysis consists of linear regression models showing correlation between molecular/connectomic predictors and cross-sectional cortical abnormality patterns. Correlation cannot establish that molecular vulnerability "drives" abnormality patterns; other confounding factors could explain both the predictor patterns and the abnormality patterns simultaneously. "We consistently find that local attributes govern both disorder-specific abnormalities and cross-disorder similarity more than global connectivity and regional dynamics." The term "govern" implies control or regulation, suggesting a causal relationship. However, the dominance analysis used merely decomposes variance explained in a regression model. Higher explained variance does not establish that local attributes causally "govern" abnormalities—it only shows stronger statistical association. "molecular mechanisms interact with the structural and functional architectures of the brain to guide cross-disorder abnormality patterns" "Interact" and "guide" suggest mechanistic processes, but the evidence presented is a correlation between molecular R² and node-neighbor correlation across disorders. This is a meta-analytic correlation across 13 data points (disorders), not a demonstration of molecular-connectomic interaction at the mechanistic level. "connection patterns among vulnerable regions further amplify this vulnerability" "Amplify" suggests an active process where connectivity increases vulnerability, but the cross-sectional data cannot determine whether connectivity amplifies vulnerability or whether vulnerable regions happen to be connected for reasons unrelated to amplification. The temporal ordering required to establish amplification (vulnerability → spreading → increased vulnerability) cannot be inferred from cross-sectional data.

The distance-dependent cross-validation method (75% training, 25% test based on spatial proximity) may not fully address spatial autocorrelation issues, as test regions adjacent to the training boundary will share autocorrelation structure with training regions, potentially inflating model performance estimates. "Each model was cross-validated using a distance-dependent method. For 1000 randomly chosen source regions, the model was fit on a training set, composed of the 75% of regions closest to the source region. Next, cortical abnormality values were predicted on the remaining 25% of regions" This cross-validation scheme selects the spatially closest 75% of regions for training, meaning the test set comprises only the most distant 25% of regions. However, regions at the boundary between training and test sets will have substantial spatial autocorrelation with training regions. With only 68 cortical regions in the Desikan-Killiany atlas, a 75/25 split results in approximately 51 training and 17 test regions, where test regions near the boundary may have highly correlated values with adjacent training regions. * Quote from Alexander-Bloch et al. (2018) [[5]](#citation-list): "On testing for spatial correspondence between maps of human brain structure and function" This foundational paper on spatial autocorrelation in brain maps (cited by the reviewed paper) emphasizes that spatial proximity creates dependencies that standard statistical methods cannot handle. The reviewed paper's distance-dependent cross-validation creates a sharp spatial boundary, but brain map autocorrelation functions typically decay gradually with distance. Regions in the test set that are only slightly farther than the 75th percentile distance will still share substantial autocorrelation with training regions. * Quote from code file 01_regression_and_dominance.py, lines 54-55: ```python train_idx = idx[:int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords)))] test_idx = idx[int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords))):] ``` The code shows that training and test indices are determined by a hard cutoff at the 75th percentile of distance from the source region. This binary split does not account for the continuous nature of spatial autocorrelation, where regions just beyond the cutoff (in the test set) may have values highly predicted by regions just before the cutoff (in the training set). More sophisticated approaches such as spatial block cross-validation or leave-one-out with spatial buffers would better address this limitation. References: [5] Alexander-Bloch, A. F. et al. (2018). On testing for spatial correspondence between maps of human brain structure and function. NeuroImage, 178, 540–551. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1053811918304968

This paper makes a potentially invalid and insufficiently documented dataset claim that all ENIGMA-derived cortical abnormality maps come from adult patients, even though at least some of the underlying ENIGMA morphometry mega-/meta-analyses explicitly span childhood through adulthood; this undermines the validity of cross-disorder comparisons and the robustness of downstream interpretations that implicitly assume a single life-stage. “All cortical abnormality maps were collected from adult patients, following identical processing protocols, for a total of over 21 000 scanned patients against almost 26 000 controls.” This sentence asserts an adult-only sampling frame as a core premise for comparability across the 13 disorder maps, but the paper does not provide disorder-by-disorder age ranges or cite adult-only ENIGMA subgroup analyses for neurodevelopmental conditions. Because cortical thickness and its disorder-associated deviations are strongly age-dependent, mixing developmental stages can change both spatial patterns and effect sizes, which would directly affect the paper’s cross-disorder similarity analyses and regression fits. The claim is also used to justify that the maps are “comparable,” so if it is inaccurate or only partially true, it becomes a validity issue rather than a minor reporting gap. The manuscript does not describe any harmonized restriction to adult cohorts, nor any sensitivity analysis for age-stratified ENIGMA maps. As a result, readers cannot verify whether the analyzed ADHD/ASD/OCD-related maps reflect adult-only case-control effects or a mixture of ages. This ambiguity makes the paper’s cross-disorder conclusions harder to interpret and reproduce. “The values for each map are z-scored effect sizes (Cohen's d) of cortical thickness in patient populations versus healthy controls.” This methods statement clarifies that the disorder maps are summary effect sizes but does not specify whether they were computed within an adult-only subset or pooled across ages in the contributing ENIGMA cohorts. Without explicit age-range documentation per disorder, it is impossible to reconcile the adult-only claim with the known design of several ENIGMA working-group analyses. Because the paper later correlates and regresses these maps against molecular and connectomic predictors, any age-related heterogeneity in the source effect-size maps can propagate into both the estimated “predictability” and the identified “epicentres.” Standard practice in large-scale ENIGMA morphometry work is to stratify or model age effects when the sample spans development, precisely because age modifies case-control effects. The absence of such information here prevents an informed assessment of whether age is a confound or a controlled design feature. This weakens confidence that the comparisons across disorders are on equal footing. “The sample comprised 1,571 patients with ASD and 1,651 healthy control subjects (age range, 2-64 years) from 49 participating sites.” [[2]](#ref2) This sentence from the ENIGMA ASD working-group abstract directly contradicts an adult-only characterization for at least one disorder category the paper includes (ASD). It indicates that the underlying ASD morphometry analysis spans early childhood through older adulthood, which makes “adult patients” an inaccurate shorthand unless the reviewed paper specifically selected an adult-only ASD derivative map. The reviewed manuscript does not explain any such selection, transformation, or filtering, so readers are left to assume the default ENIGMA map is used. If the ASD map includes ages 2–64, then its spatial pattern can reflect a mixture of developmental trajectories rather than a single adult phenotype. This is especially important because the reviewed paper interprets spatial patterning mechanistically (molecular vulnerability, epicentres), which could be age-contingent. Therefore, the adult-only claim materially affects the validity of the paper’s multi-disorder framework. “Analyses were performed separately for children, adolescents, and adults, using linear mixed-effects models adjusting for age, sex, and site (and intracranial volume for subcortical and surface area measures).” [[3]](#ref3) This sentence from an ENIGMA cross-disorder morphometry comparison (ADHD/ASD/OCD working groups) shows that ENIGMA analyses in these neurodevelopmental disorders explicitly treat age group as a key analytic axis rather than assuming adulthood. It implies that pooled analyses across age can mask or alter effects, which is precisely why stratification is performed. The reviewed manuscript includes ADHD and ASD but does not report which age-stratified estimates (if any) were used to create its disorder maps. If the authors used pooled maps, then their own adult-only assertion is inconsistent with typical ENIGMA practice and potentially incorrect. If they used adult-only maps, the omission of this detail is still a major reproducibility gap because it determines which ENIGMA outputs were analyzed. In either case, the paper’s central “single multi-disorder framework” is undermined by unclear age composition for key disorders. References: [2] [van Rooij, D., Anagnostou, E., Arango, C., et al. Cortical and Subcortical Brain Morphometry Differences Between Patients With Autism Spectrum Disorder and Healthy Individuals Across the Lifespan: Results From the ENIGMA ASD Working Group. *Am J Psychiatry* (2018).](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29145754/) [3] [Boedhoe, P. S. W., van Rooij, D., Hoogman, M., et al. Subcortical Brain Volume, Regional Cortical Thickness, and Cortical Surface Area Across Disorders: Findings From the ENIGMA ADHD, ASD, and OCD Working Groups. *Am J Psychiatry* (2020).](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32539527/)

This paper reports a 1,000-iteration distance-dependent cross-validation protocol for validating multilinear models, but the accompanying code implements a different procedure (deterministic leave-one-node-as-source splitting), which undermines the reliability of the reported generalization results and the conclusions drawn from model performance comparisons. “Briefly, for each of 1 000 iterations, the 75% of regions closest in Euclidean distance to a randomly chosen source node were selected as the training set, and the remaining 25% of regions as the test set.” This sentence describes a stochastic validation scheme with 1,000 random source nodes, which would yield a large distribution of train/test splits and therefore a stable estimate of generalization performance. Because this paper uses cross-validation to support claims that certain predictor families (e.g., receptor profiles) best “predict” disorder maps, the exact validation protocol materially affects conclusions. If the procedure is implemented differently, the reported cross-validation distributions (e.g., medians and quartiles in Fig. S3) may not correspond to what the code actually computes. The mismatch also prevents readers from reproducing the reported validation statistics by running the shared scripts. In structured spatial data, small implementation changes can meaningfully alter estimated performance because nearby parcels are statistically dependent. Therefore, a discrepancy between Methods and code is a significant methodology and reproducibility problem. ```python def cv_slr_distance_dependent(X, y, coords, train_pct=.75, metric='rsq'): P = squareform(pdist(coords, metric="euclidean")) train_metric = [] test_metric = [] for i in range(len(y)): distances = P[i, :] # for every node idx = np.argsort(distances) train_idx = idx[:int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords)))] test_idx = idx[int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords))):] ... return train_metric, test_metric ``` This function iterates over `for i in range(len(y))`, meaning the number of splits equals the number of brain regions (68 here), not 1,000 random iterations as stated in the paper. Because each region is used once as the “source node,” the procedure is deterministic given the coordinates; there is no random sampling of source nodes and no 1,000-iteration Monte Carlo estimate. That difference changes the interpretation of variability in cross-validated performance (it becomes “across source nodes” rather than “across repeated random splits”). It also means the paper’s stated computational procedure and the released implementation are not the same, which harms reproducibility. Given the small sample size (68 parcels), the difference between 68 and 1,000 splits is not superficial: it affects the stability of reported summary statistics and any narrative about robustness. As a result, the cross-validation evidence supporting key performance comparisons is not reliably documented. “Ecological data often show temporal, spatial, hierarchical (random effects), or phylogenetic structure. Modern statistical approaches are increasingly accounting for such dependencies. However, when performing cross-validation, these structures are regularly ignored, resulting in serious underestimation of predictive error.” [[1]](#ref1) This passage explains why validation procedures must be carefully aligned with the dependence structure of the data—precisely the situation for cortical maps with strong spatial autocorrelation. The reviewed paper’s cross-validation is intended to mitigate spatial dependence via distance-based splitting, but a misdescribed or mis-implemented scheme can easily yield overly optimistic performance estimates. Because the paper’s headline claims rely on comparing predictive strength across different feature families, underestimating predictive error can lead to overconfident conclusions about which predictors “best” explain disease patterns. The Roberts et al. discussion is general (ecological data) but directly applicable to spatially structured brain parcellation data where neighboring parcels are dependent. It underscores that the details of the splitting procedure are not cosmetic; they determine whether the validation meaningfully tests extrapolation across space. Therefore, the Methods–code mismatch is a significant issue rather than a minor documentation error. “the reported statistical significance of these correlation is in some cases greatly inflated by failing to take into consideration the spatial non- independence of brain maps.” [[5]](#ref5) This statement highlights a core risk in brain-map analyses: naive correlation-based evaluation can produce inflated inferences when spatial dependence is not properly handled. In the reviewed work, cross-validation is a key safeguard intended to demonstrate that regression fits are not merely exploiting spatial smoothness. If the cross-validation protocol is misreported relative to what was executed, then the paper’s evidence that it has controlled dependence-driven optimism becomes weaker. Even when spin tests are used for some p-values, validation metrics (train/test correlations) can still be biased if the splitting scheme does not enforce the intended independence. This makes an accurate, code-matched description of cross-validation essential to the paper’s methodological credibility. The cited literature supports the principle that spatial non-independence can inflate correlation-based assessments, increasing the stakes of any CV mismatch. Consequently, the discrepancy threatens the robustness of the paper’s performance-based conclusions. References: [1] [Roberts, D. R., Bahn, V., Ciuti, S., et al. Cross-validation strategies for data with temporal, spatial, hierarchical, or phylogenetic structure. *Ecography* (2017).](https://www.wsl.ch/lud/biodiversity_events/papers/Roberts_et_al-2017-Ecography.pdf) [5] [Alexander-Bloch, A., Shou, H., Liu, S., et al. On testing for spatial correspondence between maps of human brain structure and function. *NeuroImage* (2018).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6095687/)

This paper’s description of the connectomic predictor set is internally inconsistent (claiming nine predictors while enumerating seven), and it also contains conflicting parcellation statements for at least one molecular predictor; these inconsistencies materially reduce methodological transparency and hinder exact reproduction of the regression and dominance analyses. “A total of nine global connectome predictors were used in the multilinear model to represent the influence that global connectivity has on disorder-specific cortical morphology.” This sentence specifies the dimensionality of the connectomic feature set, which is essential for reproducing regression fits, dominance analysis (which scales with 2^p − 1 submodels), and adjusted R^2 values. However, the paper’s subsequent list of connectomic predictors in the same section contains only seven distinct measures, creating a direct inconsistency in the core model specification. Because dominance analysis partitions model fit across predictors, the number of predictors is not a minor detail; it changes the combinatorics of submodels and the meaning of “dominance.” It also affects adjusted R^2, which explicitly depends on p through the degrees-of-freedom correction. Without knowing whether p=7 or p=9 was used, a reader cannot re-run the analysis and expect agreement with reported dominance patterns. This undermines transparency and reproducibility of one of the paper’s main analytic pillars. “Connectivity predictors: strength = sum of weighted connections; betweenness = fraction of all shortest paths traversing region i; closeness = mean shortest path length between region i and all other regions; Euclidean distance = mean Euclidean distance between region i and all other regions; participation coefficient = diversity of connections from region i to the seven Yeo-Krienen resting-state networks [130]; clustering = fraction of triangles including region i; mean first passage time = average time for a random walker to travel from region i to any other region in the network.” This figure caption enumerates exactly seven connectivity predictors, which conflicts with the Methods claim of “nine” predictors. The discrepancy is problematic because readers rely on such lists to understand which aspects of network embedding were tested and which were not. It also impacts interpretability: if additional predictors were included but not described, then the narrative about “centrality” being less dominant than “number of connections” may omit relevant variables. Conversely, if only these seven were used, then the “nine” count is simply incorrect and signals unreliable method reporting. In either case, the mismatch reduces confidence in the precision of the model specification presented to the reader. Given that the paper compares “molecular” versus “connectomic” predictability, miscounting features can also bias perceived fairness of that comparison. ```python # disorder maps + predictors ct = np.genfromtxt(path+'data/enigma_ct.csv', delimiter=',') bio = np.genfromtxt(path+'data/local_biol_predictors.csv', delimiter=',') conn = np.genfromtxt(path+'data/' + dataset + '/global_conn_predictors.csv', delimiter=',') ... bio_predictor_names = np.load(path+'data/bio_predictor_names.npy') conn_predictor_names = np.load(path+'data/conn_predictor_names.npy') ... predictors = [bio, conn, conn_bin, conn_fc, np.concatenate((bio, conn), axis=1)] predictor_names = [bio_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names, np.concatenate((bio_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names))] ``` The released analysis script operationalizes the connectomic predictor set via a single matrix `conn` and a corresponding `conn_predictor_names` array, and then uses this set repeatedly across the main and supplementary variants. This code structure implies that the analysis hinges on a fixed, explicitly enumerated set of connectomic variables, so any mismatch in the manuscript’s stated number of predictors directly affects reproducibility. The script does not document any hidden additional connectomic variables that would reconcile “nine” with “seven,” suggesting the manuscript may contain an incorrect count. Since dominance analysis and cross-validation are executed in loops over these matrices, the predictor dimensionality is baked into the computations. Therefore, inconsistency between the written Methods and the implementation is consequential for anyone attempting to replicate results from the provided codebase. This evidence supports the criticism that the methodological reporting is not sufficiently precise for exact reproduction. “All analyses were conducted using the 68-region Desikan Killiany parcellation [19, 29], as this is the native and only available representation of ENIGMA datasets.” This sentence asserts a single parcellation basis for all analyses, which is important because both predictors and outcome maps must be aligned region-by-region for regression. Yet the Methods later describe at least one predictor as being “parcel-lated into 68 cortical regions according to the Lausanne anatomical atlas [19],” which is a different naming/atlas convention within the same manuscript. If this is not merely a wording/OCR issue, it implies a potential atlas mismatch or an undocumented mapping step between parcellations. Such mapping can alter spatial smoothness and correlations, affecting regression and dominance results. Even if the actual underlying processing used a consistent 68-region atlas, the manuscript’s inconsistent descriptions prevent readers from knowing what was done. This ambiguity is significant because parcellation alignment is a core prerequisite for validity of the statistical comparisons.

This paper overgeneralizes its “cross-disorder epicentre” conclusion in the abstract and narrative, because epicentre likelihood is explicitly computed only for the subset of disorders that pass a network-spreading significance filter and is then averaged—so the reported epicentres are not established for all thirteen disorders. “Finally, we find that cross-disorder abnormalities are consistently subtended by a small subset of network epicentres in bilateral sensory-motor, medial temporal lobe, precuneus, and superior parietal cortex.” This sentence frames the epicentre result as a general property of “cross-disorder abnormalities,” which a reader would reasonably interpret as applying across the full set of 13 disorders analyzed in the paper. However, the Methods/Results later qualify that epicentre likelihood is only computed for disorders that exhibit significant node–neighbour correlations, meaning the epicentre map is conditional on a selection criterion. As a result, the abstract’s phrasing risks overstating the scope of the finding and conflating “cross-disorder” with “across the selected network-spreading subset.” This matters because epicentres are used to support mechanistic claims about network-mediated amplification of pathology, and overgeneralizing them could mislead readers about universality across psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative conditions. The paper does not present an epicentre analysis for non-network-spreading disorders or quantify uncertainty about the epicentre locations when the inclusion criterion changes. Therefore, the conclusion as written is stronger than what the implemented analysis supports. “Epicentre likelihood was only calculated for brain maps with significant correlation between their node and neighbour abnormality (network spreading disorders). This list comprised of: 22q11.1 deletion syndrome, ADHD, ASD, right and left temporal epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and schizotypy (Fig. S7). We averaged epicentre likelihood of these seven disorders to produce a map of epicentre likelihood across disorders that demonstrate network spreading disorder-specific cortical morphology (Fig. 4c, right).” This passage explicitly defines the epicentre analysis as conditional on a statistical filter and lists only seven included disorders, which directly conflicts with a blanket “cross-disorder” framing across all thirteen disorders. Because the selection criterion is based on a significance test, the subset is not fixed a priori and is sensitive to methodological choices (e.g., null model, connectivity matrix variant), which can affect the resulting averaged epicentre map. Averaging only over this subset also means that disorders without significant network spreading do not contribute to the reported epicentre locations, so the result cannot be interpreted as a pan-disorder epicentre map. This is a substantial limitation because the paper studies disorders with different etiologies and expected propagation mechanisms, and the subset criterion implicitly favors conditions whose maps align with the chosen network-spreading metric. The manuscript does not present alternative summaries (e.g., per-disorder epicentres across all 13, or a weighted analysis accounting for uncertainty). Thus, the strong abstract claim is not fully supported by the described analysis. ```python idx = np.where(pspin['fc-also'] < 0.05)[0] if 4 and 5 in idx: # both epilepsies epilepsy_mean = np.mean(epi[:, [4, 5]], axis=1).reshape(-1, 1) idx = np.delete(idx, (idx == 4) | (idx == 5)) epi_subset = np.concatenate((epi[:, idx], epilepsy_mean), axis=1) # epicentre likelihood (mean) epi_summary = np.mean(epi_subset, axis=1) ``` The code implements the subset-selection described in the paper by selecting disorders with `pspin['fc-also'] < 0.05` and then computing epicentre summaries only over `epi_subset`. This confirms that the reported “cross-disorder” epicentre map is not computed over all disorders but only over those meeting the network-spreading criterion, with additional ad hoc handling of epilepsy subtypes. Because the subset is determined after significance testing, the resulting epicentre map is subject to selection effects and cannot be interpreted as an unconditional summary across the 13 disorders. The extra step of collapsing two epilepsy maps also changes the effective weighting of disorders in the mean, which should be explicitly reflected in any general claim about epicentres. While such choices may be defensible, they need to be matched by appropriately scoped language in the conclusions. As written, the abstract statement is not aligned with the conditional and selective procedure actually used.

This paper’s connectomic vulnerability and network-spreading inferences are weakened because the structural connectome used to compute all connectivity predictors and neighbour-weighted measures is derived from a small sample of healthy young adults, while the analyzed disorders span conditions with markedly different age distributions and known age-related changes in structural connectivity. “Structural and functional data were collected ... on \( n = 70 \) healthy young adults (16 females, \( 25.3 \pm 4.9 \) years).” This sentence makes clear that all connectomic predictors and network-spreading computations are anchored to a single connectome estimate from young adults. Yet the paper’s disorder set includes conditions with typical onset and progression well outside this age range (e.g., Parkinson’s disease) as well as neurodevelopmental disorders, meaning the normative connectome used for “vulnerability” does not match the life-stage of the patient populations represented in the ENIGMA effect-size maps. Because graph metrics (strength, centrality, participation, etc.) depend on the underlying connectivity matrix, age-related differences in structural connectivity can alter which regions are labeled as hubs or epicentres. The paper acknowledges in general terms that predictors do not capture lifespan changes, but it still draws broad conclusions about connectome architecture interacting with local vulnerability across disorders. Without demonstrating robustness to alternative connectomes (e.g., older-adult connectomes, age-stratified connectomes, or disorder-relevant cohorts), the connectomic conclusions risk being artifacts of the chosen young-adult reference network. This is a substantive limitation because it affects not just effect size but the spatial ranking of key regions. Therefore, the connectomic vulnerability claims are not as robust as the manuscript’s general framing suggests. “Third, both local biological and global connectivity predictors are derived from state-of-the-art open-access datasets in healthy participants, but they do not capture individual variability or changes across the lifespan—both of which are key factors in neurological, psychiatric, and neurodevelopmental disorders.” This limitation statement correctly identifies a central threat to validity: the predictors are fixed, healthy reference maps that ignore both individual variability and developmental/aging effects. However, the main text still interprets correlations with these predictors as reflecting mechanisms of “vulnerability” and uses the same young-adult connectome to define neighbour exposure and epicentres. The fact that the authors flag the issue does not resolve it, because no analysis is provided to quantify how sensitive results are to using different age-matched connectomes or to plausible lifespan-related changes in network topology. In many disorders, both connectivity and molecular features change with age, treatment, and disease stage, so a young-adult baseline may systematically mischaracterize where network exposure is highest. This limitation thus directly impacts the strength and generality of the paper’s conclusions, not merely their scope. A robust approach would include sensitivity analyses or stratified reference networks. “Results revealed more pronounced age-related decreases in structural connectivity for sensorimotor, ventral attention, and sub-cortical networks ...” [[6]](#ref6) This sentence provides direct evidence that structural connectivity exhibits systematic age-related decreases across the lifespan, affecting multiple networks—including sensorimotor systems that the reviewed paper later highlights as epicentres. If structural connectivity changes with age, then graph metrics and neighbour-weighted exposure estimates computed on a young-adult connectome may not transfer to older adult populations or to disorders with different age distributions. This is particularly relevant because the reviewed paper interprets network spreading and epicentre likelihood as reflecting connectome architecture’s role in shaping disorder patterns. The cited study indicates that the underlying structural connectivity substrate is not stationary across adulthood, challenging the assumption that a single young-adult connectome provides a universally valid “vulnerability” scaffold. Consequently, the paper’s connectomic inferences could be biased toward young-adult hub structure rather than disorder-relevant network organization. This evidence supports the criticism that lifespan mismatch is a significant methodological and interpretive issue. “The original Human Connectome Project yielded a rich data set on structural and functional connectivity in a large sample of healthy young adults ... This dataset will provide a unique resource for examining how brain organization and connectivity changes across typical aging ...” [[4]](#ref4) This passage underscores two points relevant to the reviewed paper’s design choices: (i) high-quality connectome datasets are often young-adult–centered, and (ii) connectivity is expected to change across typical aging sufficiently to motivate dedicated aging cohorts. The reviewed paper uses a young-adult connectome as a fixed scaffold to interpret multiple disorders, but the cited overview highlights that extending connectome analyses across age is a primary research goal precisely because the connectome evolves. This supports the argument that applying a young-adult connectome indiscriminately to disorders spanning the lifespan requires explicit justification and robustness checks. In particular, disorders like Parkinson’s disease occur in older populations where age-related connectivity differences are nontrivial, potentially shifting which regions are central or well-connected. Without accounting for these changes, the reviewed paper’s connectomic vulnerability claims may not generalize to the populations that generate the disorder maps. Thus, the external literature reinforces that lifespan mismatch is a substantial concern for validity. References: [4] [Bookheimer, S. Y., Salat, D. H., Terpstra, M., et al. The Lifespan Human Connectome Project in Aging: An overview. *NeuroImage* (2019).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6649668/) [6] [Merenstein, A., Song, S., et al. High-resolution structural connectivity mediates age-related differences in functional connectivity and fluid cognition. *Brain Communications* (2025).](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41113674/)

The paper overgeneralizes the findings on "network spreading" and "epicentres" to "cross-disorder abnormalities" in general, despite the analysis showing significance for only a subset of disorders (7 out of 13). "Finally, we find that cross-disorder abnormalities are consistently subtended by a small subset of network epicentres in bilateral sensory-motor, medial temporal lobe, precuneus, and superior parietal cortex." This statement in the abstract implies a universal finding across the studied disorders. However, the results section specifies that "mean epicentre likelihood was calculated for all seven disorders that show a significant correlation between regional and neighbour abnormality". My re-analysis confirms that "network spreading" (correlation between node and neighbour abnormality) is statistically significant ($p < 0.05$) for only 7 disorders (22q, ADHD, ASD, Epilepsy RTLE, Epilepsy LTLE, Bipolar, Schizotypy). Major disorders such as Schizophrenia ($p = 0.13$), Depression ($p = 0.07$), and OCD ($p = 0.08$) are excluded from this "consistent" finding. Presenting this as a general "cross-disorder" phenomenon without explicitly stating the exclusion of nearly half the disorders (including major ones) in the abstract is misleading.

The paper infers "network spreading" and identifies "epicentres" based solely on static cross-sectional correlations between regional abnormality and neighbour abnormality, which is insufficient to establish a dynamic spreading mechanism. "A disorder whose cortical morphology demonstrates network spreading was defined as one whose regional abnormality pattern is correlated to mean neighbour abnormality, weighted by structural connectivity and functional connectivity." A high correlation between a region's atrophy and its neighbours' atrophy (spatial autocorrelation on the network) can arise from shared vulnerability (e.g., spatially correlated gene expression or shared developmental inputs) without any dynamic spreading process [1]. Inferring a "spreading" mechanism and using terms like "epicentre" implies a causal, dynamic process that propagates pathology from a source. This cannot be rigorously established from static cross-sectional data alone, without longitudinal tracking or fitting a dynamic diffusion model [2]. The paper acknowledges "interaction" but the terminology strongly suggests a specific dynamic mechanism that is not directly tested. References: [1] Fornito, A., Zalesky, A., & Breakspear, M. (2015). The connectomics of brain disorders. *Nature Reviews Neuroscience*, 16(3), 159-172. https://www.nature.com/articles/nrn3901 [2] Raj, A., Kuceyeski, A., & Weiner, M. (2012). A network diffusion model of progression predicts longitudinal patterns of atrophy and metabolism in Alzheimer's disease. *Cell Systems*, 73(6), 1204-1215. https://www.cell.com/neuron/fulltext/S0896-6273(12)00976-3

The use of healthy adult connectomes and molecular maps to predict abnormalities in developmental disorders (e.g., ASD, ADHD) assumes a normative architecture that may not exist in these patients, ignoring disease-specific alterations. "...we defined the connectivity fingerprint of a region by calculating ... of a weighted structural connectivity matrix from 70 healthy adults..." The study uses a "normative" connectome from healthy adults to define vulnerability for all disorders, including neurodevelopmental ones like ASD and ADHD. However, literature suggests that these disorders are characterized by fundamental alterations in the structural connectome itself [3, 4]. By assuming that the "vulnerability" is defined by the healthy architecture, the study ignores the possibility that the disease-specific connectome architecture is the primary driver of the abnormalities. This is a significant limitation for the developmental disorders included in the study, as the "healthy" vulnerability map may not reflect the actual network constraints present in these patients.

Lines 172-175: The results indicate that the PM2.5-induced oxidative stress (OS) potency could mainly be attributed to PM2.5-bound toxic metals. Calculating PM2.5-induced OS potency for selected metals and PAHs is based on reference models, which can be found in the study by Jin et al. (2019). That study provided EC1.5 and REP for valence metals. However, ICP-MS does not analyze metal valence. The authors should clarify the OS potency concentrations from metals and make assumptions if necessary. In addition, the calculations of OP are not transparent the authors only presented references.
Have any prior experimental studies investigated the toxic effects of industrial PM2.5? It’s necessary to compare the findings with earlier research.

Numerous studies in environmental epidemiology have investigated the impact of PM2.5 compositions on public health. It is essential for authors to analyze and contrast the variations between their study and previous epidemiology research in this field.

Line 62-63 “while many of the major PM components, including inorganic sulfate and nitrate, are commonly considered innocuous species17.”, the author should double check the sentence. The reference is out of date and there are many epidemiology studies found the toxic of inorganic sulfate. Modifying the acidity and alkalinity levels could potentially impact the toxicity of metals present in PM2.5.

Line 140-142 “Here, the targeted chemicals are focused on metals and PAHs, which are commonly regarded as key toxic components associated with the biological effects of PM2.5.”, please explain why black carbon or elemental carbon is not a targeted chemicals in this studies?
It is recommended to include the proportion of certain key constituents, such as inorganic sulfate and nitrate, in Supplementary Table 4.
What methodology did the author employ to compute the information presented in Supplementary Figure 7a?

While I agree with the authors that the toxicity of particulate matter varies with source, the sampling collection and handling methods used in this work are not appropriate for the toxicity tests they perform. The sampling methods documented in the supplementary materials fails to collect the condensible particulate matter, which is where much of the most toxic components result in the ambient air. What is required is a dilution sampling system to allow the reaction and condensation closer to what happens once the particles, vapors, and gases are released into the atmosphere. One appropriate method is described here: England GC, Watson JG, Chow JC, Zielinska B, Chang MC, Loos KR, Hidy GM. Dilution-based emissions sampling from stationary sources: Part 1--Compact sampler methodology and performance. J Air Waste Manag Assoc. 2007 Jan;57(1):65-78. doi: 10.1080/10473289.2007.10465291. PMID: 17269232.

A second problem with the methods is that no mention is made of post collection sample handing to protect the sample acidity from neutralization. A proper storage would place samples collected into containers with citric acid coated material to prevent neutralization of acids, such as sulfuric acid, until use in the toxicological tests, also under ammonia free conditions. This is especially important in fossil fuel combustion particulate matter, high in acidic sulfur, which is known to activate transition metals by making them bioavailable (e.g., see: Fang, T.; Guo, H.; Zeng, L.; Verma, V.; Nenes, A.; Weber, R.J. Highly Acidic Ambient Particles, Soluble Metals, and Oxidative Potential: A Link between Sulfate and Aerosol Toxicity. Environmental Science & Technology 2017, 51, 2611-2620, doi:10.1021/acs.est.6b06151).

The role of sulfates is dismissed as relatively non-toxic by the authors in their discussion, but this is not a full consideration of its role. In isolation, it is true that its toxicity is low, but in combination with metals (as always occurs in the real world) acidic sulfur has an important role, as indicated by the epidemiological evidence, which the authors fail to acknowledge. For example, see: Weichenthal S, Lavigne E, raub A, Umbrio D, You H, Pollitt K, Shin T, Kulka R, Stieb DM, Korsiaik J, Jessiman B, JBrook JR, Hatzopoulou M, Evans G, Burnett RT, Association of Sulfur, Transition Metals, and the Oxidative Potential of Outdoor PM2.5 with Acute Cardiovascular Events: A Case-Crossover Study of Canadian Adults. Environmental Health Perspectives. Environ Health Perspect 129(10). (2021). doi: 30 10.1289/EHP9449.

The paper proposes a "health-oriented" air pollution control strategy based on in vitro oxidative stress (EC₁.₅) and cytotoxicity (IC₂₀) potency values, assuming these metrics are proportional to actual human health outcomes.
