Because cohesive energies are differences between large numbers, careful control of consistency between the atomic and bulk computations is needed to avoid spurious agreement. In the reviewed manuscript, the isolated-atom terms are not computed within the same reported workflow, so the cohesive energies are not fully “closed” results of the proposed method. The citation also underscores why importing atomic energies is not a minor bookkeeping choice: it can dominate the error budget for the derived quantity. Therefore, presenting cohesive-energy agreement as a primary accuracy demonstration requires more methodological closure than the paper currently provides. This gap materially weakens the strength of the paper’s validation evidence. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf) [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [4] E. H. Megchiche, C. Mijoule, M. Amarouche, and J.-C. Barthelat. *First-principles calculations of the vacancy formation energy in nickel* (Algerian Journal of Advanced Materials, 2006). [https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document](https://hal.science/hal-04289125/document)

The finite-size control for key solid benchmarks (graphene and LiH) is not validated via systematic convergence studies (supercell size and twist/k-point sampling), yet the manuscript draws strong conclusions about near-experimental accuracy; this is a major methodological gap because residual finite-size errors in Coulomb systems can be comparable to (or larger than) the claimed error bars. “The calculations are performed on a \( 2 \times 2 \) supercell of graphene using twist average boundary condition (TABC) [25] in conjunction with structure factor S(k) correction [26] (see Supplementary Fig. 3) to reduce the finite-size error.” This sentence shows that the graphene cohesive-energy claim is based on a single, small supercell (2×2) augmented by correction schemes. For graphene-like systems, finite-size effects can be subtle because the system is not a simple gapped insulator and long-range Coulomb correlations remain important. Merely stating that TABC and an S(k) correction are used does not establish that the residual finite-size bias is below 0.1 eV/atom. The manuscript does not report a convergence check with respect to supercell size (e.g., 2×2 vs 3×3 vs 4×4) or twist-mesh density. Without such a study, the magnitude of the remaining finite-size error is unknown, making the subsequent accuracy conclusion fragile. Given that the main comparative statement is “within 0.1 eV/atom,” the absence of a demonstrated finite-size error bound is a significant methodological omission. “A 3×3 Monkhorst-Pack mesh in the first Brillouin zone of the supercell reciprocal space with Γ point centered is used to approximate the twist average integral, which reads” This indicates that twist averaging is approximated with a modest 3×3 mesh for the supercell. For high-accuracy claims, the adequacy of the twist mesh should be justified by explicit convergence tests, because twist integration error is a controlled numerical approximation. The supplement gives a specific quadrature rule, but does not report how the result changes with a denser mesh (e.g., 4×4 or 6×6) or with different symmetry handling. In addition, for quantities like cohesive energy, the twist-integration error propagates into an energy difference, which may amplify small biases. Without reporting the mesh-convergence behavior, it is not possible to know whether the reported “finite-size error corrected” cohesive energy is stable at the claimed accuracy. This undermines the credibility of the graphene validation as presented. “Using our neural network, we first simulate the equation of state of LiH on a \( 2 \times 2 \times 2 \) supercell, as shown in Fig. 2f. In addition, we employ a standard finite-size correction based on Hartree-Fock calculations of a large supercell (see Supplementary Fig. 5).” This sentence shows that LiH results are primarily based on a 2×2×2 QMC supercell, with an a posteriori Hartree–Fock-based correction rather than an explicit many-body size extrapolation. In Coulomb systems, the leading finite-size effects include both single-particle shell effects and many-body long-range correlation contributions; HF corrections address only part of the problem. The paper does not show QMC energies for multiple LiH supercell sizes (beyond a single 3×3×3 point at one lattice constant) to empirically demonstrate the corrected equation-of-state curve is converged. Because the Birch–Murnaghan fit and derived properties (bulk modulus, cohesive energy) are sensitive to small systematic shifts, the lack of a multi-size convergence assessment is a substantial gap. As a result, the reported agreement with experimental thermodynamic quantities cannot be robustly attributed to the method rather than finite-size modeling choices. “Twist averaging serves to make the decay more regular but does not reduce its overall magnitude that is determined by a charge interacting with the correlation hole of its own image in the nearby supercell.” [[2]](#ref2) This statement from the foundational TABC literature emphasizes that twist averaging primarily regularizes shell effects but does not, by itself, eliminate the dominant long-range Coulomb finite-size contributions. This is directly relevant because the reviewed manuscript leans on TABC (graphene) and HF-based corrections (LiH) to claim near-experimental accuracy from small supercells. The quote highlights that additional many-body finite-size corrections (beyond twist averaging) are typically required to control the Coulomb-image/correlation-hole contributions. While the manuscript mentions an S(k) correction for graphene, it does not provide a quantitative residual-error analysis or show that the correction is sufficient for the claimed accuracy. Therefore, the literature suggests that the paper’s finite-size treatment may be incomplete without further evidence. This undermines the strength of the conclusions drawn from the finite supercell calculations. “Finite-size corrections, both on kinetic and potential energy, can be calculated within a single simulation.” [[3]](#ref3) This quote reflects a well-established viewpoint in QMC finite-size analysis: both kinetic and potential energy contributions can require explicit many-body finite-size corrections grounded in long-wavelength physics. The reviewed manuscript does include some correction ideas (TABC, structure factor correction, HF empirical correction), but it does not demonstrate that these corrections collectively bound residual finite-size error below the levels needed for its strongest claims. In particular, for LiH the manuscript emphasizes HF-based correction, which is not the same as a many-body correction of both kinetic and potential contributions. For graphene, the small twist grid and single supercell size leave open whether both classes of finite-size errors are controlled. The quoted literature makes clear that robust finite-size handling is a central methodological requirement, not an optional detail. Without convergence evidence, the manuscript’s accuracy claims remain under-justified. References: [2] C. Lin, F. H. Zong, and D. M. Ceperley. *Twist-averaged boundary conditions in continuum quantum Monte Carlo algorithms* (Phys. Rev. E 64, 016702). [https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf](https://people.physics.illinois.edu/ceperley/papers/157.pdf) [3] S. Chiesa, D. M. Ceperley, R. M. Martin, and M. Holzmann. *Finite-Size Error in Many-Body Simulations with Long-Range Interactions* (Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404). [https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404)

The manuscript reports many high-precision quantitative outcomes (e.g., “within 0.1 eV/atom,” “error of less than 1%”) without clearly defining statistical uncertainties, their estimators, or how uncertainties propagate through derived metrics like cohesive energies and “correlation error,” which weakens the reliability of the conclusions. “Supplementary Figure 2 | \(2 \times 2\) Graphene training curve. For clarity, at each iteration number, we show the median energy per primitive cell over the last 10% of iteration.” Reporting a “median … over the last 10%” is a visualization choice that does not, by itself, provide a statistically interpretable uncertainty estimate for the final energies. Medians suppress outliers but do not quantify sampling variance, autocorrelation effects, or residual optimization drift, all of which are central in VMC-style estimations. Because the paper’s main claims hinge on small energy differences (sub-eV per atom) and on “outperforming” baselines, the uncertainty model needs to be explicit and standard. The paper does not accompany these median curves with defined error bars or confidence intervals. Without an explicit estimator (e.g., mean ± standard error with reblocking), a reader cannot assess whether apparent improvements exceed stochastic noise. This directly weakens confidence in the reported accuracy margins. ```html -76.15588(6) -76.24949(5) -76.26314(5) ``` The parenthetical notation strongly suggests an uncertainty is being reported, but the manuscript does not define what “(6)” or “(5)” means (e.g., one standard error of the mean, one standard deviation, or an error from fitting). In QMC practice, different conventions exist, and the distinction matters when comparing to claimed sub-0.1 eV/atom accuracy. Moreover, twist averaging and structure-factor corrections introduce additional sources of uncertainty and bias beyond the raw Monte Carlo sampling noise. The table as presented also does not state whether these uncertainties are computed after equilibration, reblocking, or accounting for autocorrelation. Without an explicit definition, the reported precision cannot be reliably interpreted. This is a significant issue because the paper repeatedly uses precise numerical comparisons to support strong conclusions. “Overall, our neural network performs very well, with an error of less than 1% in a wide range of density (see Supplementary Table 11).” This claim depends on a “correlation error” metric defined using multiple energies (HF, a DMC reference, and the network’s estimate). Such derived ratios can be sensitive to small denominators (e.g., at high density where correlation energies are small) and to uncertainties in each contributing term. The manuscript does not provide a propagation-of-uncertainty analysis for this percentage, nor does it state whether the “<1%” bound holds within statistical confidence. If the underlying energies have correlated Monte Carlo noise (common in reusing walkers, or in shared correction schemes), naive error reporting can be misleading. In addition, finite-size and fixed-node biases can dominate over sampling error for some regimes, but these are not separated. As a result, the “<1%” statement is hard to interpret as a robust quantitative guarantee. This weakens the reliability of the conclusions drawn from the HEG comparison. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` After network optimization, we run O(105) MCMC steps and calculate the mean local energy every 10 steps. The energy and associated standard error are estimated using a standard approach to account for correlations. 60 ``` This quote exemplifies common practice in neural-network VMC papers: energies are reported with a clearly stated “standard error” estimate that accounts for correlated samples. The reviewed manuscript does not provide a comparable definition of its parenthetical uncertainties, nor does it state how autocorrelation is handled in its estimates. Because QMC sampling is correlated by construction, omission of correlation-aware error estimation can materially understate uncertainty. The quote is not presented to require identical methods, but to illustrate the field’s baseline expectation for uncertainty reporting when making strong quantitative claims. Without similarly explicit uncertainty definitions, the reviewed paper’s precision claims are less credible. This affects both interpretability and reproducibility. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)

The manuscript’s formal description of the solid wavefunction ansatz is internally inconsistent between the main-text expression and the provided algorithm, making it difficult to verify correctness (e.g., spin structure and determinant factorization) and undermining reproducibility of the central methodological contribution. “\(\Psi(\mathbf{r}) = \mathrm{Det}_1[e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r}_1}u_{mol}^\uparrow(d)]\mathrm{Det}_1[e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{r}_1}u_{mol}^\downarrow(d)] .\)” This equation indicates a product of (at least) two determinants associated with spin-up and spin-down channels, aligning with standard QMC antisymmetry enforcement. However, the notation is ambiguous (both are labeled “Det\_1”), and it does not clearly specify whether the determinants are block-diagonalized by spin or full determinants over all electrons. In periodic systems with complex-valued orbitals, these details matter for both the correctness of antisymmetry constraints and the implementation of phase factors. Because the method’s novelty is in extending molecular neural wavefunctions to periodic boundary conditions, the wavefunction definition must be unambiguous. Any inconsistency in determinant structure directly affects the interpretation of how fermionic antisymmetry is ensured. This ambiguity is magnified because subsequent algorithmic details are presented as a concrete implementation. “28: \( \Psi = \mathrm{Det}[\phi^\dagger] \mathrm{Det}[\phi^\dagger] \)” The algorithmic line shown here appears to form the wavefunction by multiplying two identical determinants, without specifying distinct spin channels. Taken literally, this differs from the main-text description of separate spin-up and spin-down determinants and would be conceptually incorrect for a typical spin-separated ansatz. Even if this is intended as shorthand, the manuscript does not clarify how spin is represented in \(\phi\) or why the product involves two identical terms. Since Algorithm 1 is presented as the “overall sketch of the neural network,” readers should be able to map it directly to an implementable procedure. Internal inconsistencies in the method specification impede verification by other groups and weaken confidence that the written method matches what was executed. Given that the wavefunction definition is the core of the paper, this is a significant reproducibility and correctness concern. ```python def logdet_matmul(xs: Sequence[jnp.ndarray], w: Optional[jnp.ndarray] = None) -> jnp.ndarray: """Combines determinants and takes dot product with weights in log-domain. Args: xs: FermiNet orbitals in each determinant. Either of length 1 with shape (ndet, nelectron, nelectron) (full_det=True) or length 2 with shapes (ndet, nalpha, nalpha) and (ndet, nbeta, nbeta) (full_det=False, determinants are factorised into block-diagonals for each spin channel). Returns: sum_i w_i D_i in the log domain, where w_i is the weight of D_i, the i-th determinant (or product of the i-th determinant in each spin channel, if full_det is not used). """ ``` The released implementation explicitly distinguishes two cases: a single full determinant over all electrons or a product of spin-channel determinants when factorized. This illustrates that the determinant factorization and spin handling are subtle and explicitly parameterized in code. When contrasted with the manuscript’s Algorithm 1 line that multiplies two identical determinants, it reinforces that the paper’s written description does not clearly convey the actual determinant structure needed for correctness. Moreover, because the paper positions its architecture as an extension of FermiNet to periodic systems, readers expect consistency between described mathematical form and code-level semantics. If the manuscript does not precisely describe whether it uses “full_det” or spin-block determinants in each experiment, reproducing and comparing results becomes harder. This is not merely a presentation issue: determinant structure can change expressivity, optimization behavior, and even numerical stability. The mismatch therefore undermines the clarity and verifiability of the central methodological claim. (excerpt below) [[1]](#ref1) ``` Eq. 7 uses the fact that the full determinant det[ Φ] = det [ φi(xj; {x/j}) ] may be replaced by a product of spin- up and spin-down terms if we choose φi(xj; {x/j}) = 0 if i∈1 ...n ↑and j ∈n↑+ 1,...,n or i∈n↑+ 1,...,n and j ∈1,...,n ↑. ``` This quote from FermiNet (which the reviewed manuscript cites as a base architecture) provides a clear, standard statement of the spin-factorized determinant product used to enforce fermionic antisymmetry. Because the reviewed paper claims to “represent the molecular networks with FermiNet” and extend them to periodic systems, consistency with the baseline formulation is important for interpretability. The contrast between this explicit spin-factorized form and the reviewed paper’s ambiguous/duplicated determinant notation highlights a real specification problem. Readers cannot confidently determine whether the paper’s ansatz is a direct periodic analogue of the FermiNet factorization or something else. This matters when assessing novelty, correctness, and comparability to baselines. The paper should resolve this inconsistency by clearly defining the determinant blocks and spin treatment in the main text. References: [1] D. Pfau, J. S. Spencer, A. G. D. G. Matthews, and W. M. C. Foulkes. *Ab-Initio Solution of the Many-Electron Schrödinger Equation with Deep Neural Networks* (arXiv:1909.02487v3). [https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1909.02487.pdf)

The manuscript’s computational claims (notably multi-node GPU parallelization and “highly efficient” training) are not reproducible from the provided code as released, because the code explicitly lacks multi-node support and hard-pins an outdated JAX stack without an executable, versioned environment specification, making independent replication of the reported large-scale results impractical. “Calculations are also ensured by efficient and massive parallelization on multiple nodes of high-performance GPUs.” This sentence frames multi-node, large-scale GPU parallelization as a key enabler of the method’s reported results and scale (e.g., 108-electron LiH). When such computational infrastructure is central to feasibility, reproducibility requires that the released code supports the same execution mode or that the paper provides a faithful reproduction recipe (container, scripts, and system requirements). Otherwise, readers cannot distinguish between an algorithmic advance and an implementation/hardware advantage that cannot be replicated. The paper also uses strong scale-related language (“highly efficient,” “massive parallelization”), which invites scrutiny of whether the software artifact matches those capabilities. If the released code does not support multi-node, reproducing the reported wall-clock feasibility and scaling claims becomes difficult. This is a material reproducibility gap because it affects whether other groups can validate and extend the approach. “Present released code doesn't support multi-node training.” This directly contradicts the multi-node parallelization claim in the paper’s narrative and indicates that a key part of the computational setup used in the reported experiments is not available to replicators. Even if the algorithm itself is correct, lack of multi-node support can prevent reproducing the flagship calculations within reasonable resource limits, especially for larger electron counts. The manuscript does not provide an alternative route (e.g., smaller-scale scripts that reproduce at least one reported figure/table) demonstrating that the method can be validated without the unavailable infrastructure. As a result, the computational evidence in the paper becomes partially non-falsifiable for typical academic settings. This is not a minor engineering detail: for QMC with neural wavefunctions, compute scale can be the dominant feasibility constraint. Therefore, the mismatch undermines reproducibility and the strength of empirical validation. ```python REQUIRED_PACKAGES = ( "absl-py", 'attrs', "dataclasses", "networkx", "scipy==1.9.3", "numpy", "ordered-set", "typing", "chex==0.1.5", "jax==0.2.26", "jaxlib==0.1.75", "pandas", "ml_collections", "pyscf", "tables", "h5py==3.2.1", "optax==0.0.9", "opt_einsum==3.3.0", ) ``` This pinned dependency set hard-codes very old versions of core numerical libraries (notably JAX/JAXLIB), which is a common source of “bit-rot” and install failure in modern environments. The paper does not provide a container image, lockfile, or validated environment spec beyond this setup.py, so even obtaining a runnable installation can be difficult for reviewers and practitioners. This is especially problematic because the paper’s central claims depend on complex-number support, automatic differentiation, and performance-critical primitives whose APIs can change across versions. The pinned versions also diverge from the paper’s narrative of easy integration and practical adoption, because users must match an old stack precisely. A reproducible artifact typically includes an environment file and a tested installation path that is robust over time. Without that, the empirical claims are harder to verify independently. “Our experiments were carried out with `jax==0.2.26` and `jaxlib==0.1.75.`” This reinforces that the released code is tied to a narrow and dated software configuration, yet the manuscript does not provide a full specification of the broader environment (Python version, CUDA/cuDNN versions, compiler/toolchain) needed to reproduce GPU results. In high-performance neural QMC, such details often determine whether results and runtimes can be replicated. The absence of an end-to-end environment specification also makes it difficult to assess whether the reported improvements are robust or contingent on a specific, potentially non-portable stack. Moreover, the paper’s claims about being “easily integrated” into other neural structures are in tension with the rigidity of the pinned environment. Even if the algorithm is conceptually general, the artifact as released is not immediately reusable without significant engineering effort. This constitutes a substantive reproducibility concern for a methods paper.

Contrary to the claim that the method outperforms many traditional ab initio methods and reaches the level of the most accurate approaches, the reported results for the Homogeneous Electron Gas (HEG) and Hydrogen chain are consistently worse than or merely comparable to established Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) methods from over a decade ago. "the obtained results... outperform many traditional ab initio methods and reach the level of the most accurate approaches." (Abstract) This claim is contradicted by the paper's own data when compared to the cited benchmarks. "Supplementary Table 11 | Energy per electron of HEG... r_s=1 Net: 0.530019(1), BF-DMC: 0.52989(4)" For the Homogeneous Electron Gas, the proposed method ("Net") produces higher (worse) variational energies than the Backflow Diffusion Monte Carlo (BF-DMC) results from 2006 [4] for all reported densities ($r_s = 0.5$ to $20$). Since BF-DMC is a "traditional" QMC method, the claim of outperforming such methods is factually incorrect for this system. "Supplementary Table 3 | Energy of H_{10} chain... Bond length 1.8... Net: -0.572922(1), LR-DMC(LDA): -0.57304(1)" For the Hydrogen chain ($H_{10}$), the method's energy is slightly higher (worse) than the Lattice Regularized DMC (LR-DMC) result from the benchmark paper [1]. While the results are close, they do not "outperform" the established accurate approach. References: [1] M. Motta et al., "Towards the solution of the many-electron problem in real materials: Equation of state of the hydrogen chain with state-of-the-art many-body methods", Phys. Rev. X 7, 031059 (2017). https://journals.aps.org/prx/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevX.7.031059 [4] P. López Ríos et al., "Inhomogeneous backflow transformations in quantum Monte Carlo calculations", Phys. Rev. E 74, 066701 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.74.066701

The use of a single $2 \times 2$ supercell for graphene is insufficient to capture long-range correlations and reach the thermodynamic limit, rendering the claim of "unprecedented accuracy" for this system unsupported. "The size of supercell is $2 \times 2$." (Supplementary Note 3.1) A $2 \times 2$ supercell contains only 8 carbon atoms. For a gapless semi-metal like graphene, finite-size effects are particularly severe. Relying on such a small system size without performing a finite-size extrapolation (as was done for the Hydrogen chain) fails to demonstrate that the method can accurately describe the bulk properties of graphene. "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions" [3] Accurate calculation of 2D materials requires careful extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit. The use of Twist Averaged Boundary Conditions (TABC) helps but does not eliminate the need for larger supercells to capture long-range correlation effects, which are crucial for the claim of "ab initio calculation of real solids". References: [3] S. Chiesa et al., "Finite-size error in many-body simulations with long-range interactions", Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 076404 (2006). https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.97.076404

The paper claims the method is "highly efficient" but provides no computational cost analysis (e.g., wall-clock time, memory usage, total FLOPs), while the reported hyperparameters suggest the method is computationally expensive. "based on which we develop a highly efficient QMC method" (Abstract) The term "highly efficient" implies a competitive computational cost relative to other methods. However, the paper provides no data to support this. Neural network ansatzes in QMC are generally known to be orders of magnitude more expensive than conventional Slater-Jastrow QMC or DFT. "Training steps 2e5... Batch size 4096" (Supplementary Table 1) The requirement of 200,000 training steps with a batch size of 4096 samples implies nearly a billion evaluations of the neural network. For a solid-state system with all-electron calculations (like LiH), this represents a massive computational burden. Without a cost comparison, the claim of "high efficiency" is misleading and likely incorrect compared to the "traditional methods" the paper claims to outperform.
The construction of the algorithm is not as novel as one would have expected, but the problem treated is very important, and the results are quite carefully done and presented.

Applying neural network for ab initio calculations of real solids has been already proposed by another group (https://doi.org/10.1038/s42005-021-00609-0). The research group has extended the restricted Boltzmann Machine ansatz to real periodic systems, although it employs the second quantization and finite basis approximation. The authors should mention this work in the introduction.

The authors states that the coupled cluster theory 'face severe difficulty when applied to solid systems.' Does this simply mean that the pre-factor of computational cost is high, or does it essentially mean that there is something wrong with its application to solids? Please clarify this point.

To my knowledge, Bloch functions are for one-electron states not for correlated many-body wavefunctions. Besides, the FermiNet employs the Generalized Slater determinants and its 'molecular orbitals' actually take into account electron correlations. The authors should prove that the application of the Bloch function form is not an approximation (if it is an approximation, please explicitly mention that). Unless this is a very trivial matter, the authors should state the proof in the Appendix or Supplementary Information.
KFAC and the mechanism of using mutiple GPUs efficiently were introduced to FermiNet by the DeepMind team not by the authors. Please write so that this is clear to the reader.
There are no information on the k-points employed. The authors should describe how many k-points were used and how to generate the k-point grids.
The definition of 'Correlation error' in the Fig. 2 h is not given.
* 'ccpvdz' is not the correct spelling. cc-pVDZ is correct and should be corrected.
Please position the figure captions above each table in the Supplementary Information.

I have never seen the word 'TZ-LDA basis.' I googled it but cannot found. The authors should write so that it is clear for readers what basis functions were used for the reproducibility of the calculation.
Please specify actual computational costs of this method. For example, the wall time taken to calculate LiH and the computational resources used.
Supplementary Table 4 looks listing energies per atom. Please state this clearly.
All the statistical errors written in the Supplementary Information are '(1)' except Supplementary Tables 6, 10 and 11. Is this a coincidence?

There is no mention to the statical error in the main text. For example, cohesive energy per unit cell is listed to three decimal places in eV, but without a description of the statistical error. Is the statistical error that small?

My understanding is that as long as finite k-point sampling is used, the divergent term derived from the exchange integral should appear at the \( \Gamma \) point. Is there no need for any correction to this divergent term? (c.f. DOI:10.1063/1.1926272, DOI:0.1021/acs.jctc.7b00049)
The manuscript rests heavily on connecting software and techniques from prior authors (Refs. 10, 11, 18, 26, 31, 39); the specific originality of this manuscript is not clearly articulated.

The manuscript's claim that the method outperforms other ab initio methods is not supported by the evidence shown. For the H10 and the UEG, the result is better stated as similar performance with the state-of-the-art DMC results (rather than a claim of being better than common approaches). In the case of the UEG in particular, it is known that the DMC results are not free from error at high densities due to fixed node error. Instead, as highlighted by a recent paper (Ref 31), the most accurate approaches come from FCIQMC which are available for N=14. Similar FCIQMC results are also available for N=54, which could have been used for comparison as they show clear improvement on BF-DMC.

The manuscript's claim of improvement over the existing FermiNet papers is not supported by the evidence shown. The changes made include a different choice of PBCs and NN optimizer. To validate the effectiveness of the optimizer, we would need to see efficiency data. For the PBCs choice, we would need to see well-analyzed side by side comparisons. Instead, the manuscript does not reach a direct comparison when treating the UEG, choosing different systems than Ref 31. It is possible to infer from the data presented that this algorithm captures DMC-quality energies, while Ref 31 goes beyond DMC to FCIQMC level accuracy. Thus, this manuscript's choice of PBCs seems less accurate than FermiNet not more.

The manuscript's claim of investigating long-range effectiveness in real systems is weakened by the size and electronic structure of the systems chosen. In practice, the long-range components of the energy are being added after the fact with finite size corrections (Ref. 26). The calculations themselves are being performed on relatively small system sizes. Evidence is not presented for the convergence of long-range effects (which would, for example, require analysis of the energy with system size). Moreover, the systems shown do not include a real metal (where long-range effects really come into play) and the system sizes shown have significant gaps at the one-particle/HF level of theory. So, while the systems proposed are ab initio real solids, they are not actually treated in the same way as the state of the art, and do not go beyond what can already be done.

Comparing this paper to what is currently the state of the art in the field, there are a number of high impact publications. The best known paper is by Troyer in Science (Ref 9) and the original FermiNet paper was published in Phys Rev Research (Ref 10). The best case scenario of the technical development appears to be a more efficient version of FermiNet for solids and I would put this on a par with the original FermiNet development in terms of its specialism. In my view, this paper is better suited to a more specialized journal which would also allow the claims of the novelty of the network and any speed up to be better considered and reviewed.

The paper claims that "no significant differences between the two groups exposed at 11.7T and 0T could be identified" in safety-related measurements, yet no statistical power analysis is provided to determine whether the sample size of 20 subjects per group was adequate to detect meaningful differences, raising concerns about whether the absence of statistical significance reflects true safety or simply insufficient power. "Twenty volunteers (24.2±4.8 year old, 8 males) were scanned at 11.7T and performed the tests mentioned below. Another set of twenty volunteers (23.6±6.0 year old, 7 males) was exposed to a 0T, nocebo, field and underwent the same tests (except genotoxicity)." The study uses only 20 subjects per group for safety assessment. For a first-in-human safety study at such an unprecedented field strength, this sample size is notably small. Without a power analysis, readers cannot determine whether the study was adequately powered to detect clinically meaningful differences in physiological, vestibular, or cognitive measures between groups. The absence of detectable differences could reflect either true safety equivalence or simply Type II error due to inadequate statistical power. "In all tests performed throughout the protocol, no significant differences between the two groups exposed at 11.7T and 0T could be identified (extended data Fig. 3 and Table 1)." The authors interpret the lack of statistical significance as evidence for safety. However, in statistical hypothesis testing, failure to reject the null hypothesis does not confirm the null hypothesis—particularly when power is unknown. Standard practice in safety studies would include equivalence or non-inferiority analyses with predefined margins, or at minimum, report power calculations showing adequate sensitivity to detect effects of concern. "Methods: A total of 29 subjects were exposed to a 10.5 T MRI field and underwent vestibular, cognitive, and physiological testing before, during, and after exposure; for 26 subjects, testing and exposure were repeated within 2-4 weeks of the first visit." [[1]](#citation-1) The comparable 10.5T safety study by Grant et al. (2020) used a larger sample size (29 subjects) and implemented a repeated-measures design where 26 subjects underwent testing twice with 2-4 weeks separation. This design provides substantially more statistical power through within-subject comparisons and enables assessment of short-term versus longer-term effects. The current study's single-visit design with fewer subjects represents a less rigorous approach to safety assessment. References: [1] Grant, A., Metzger, G.J., Van de Moortele, P.F., et al. "10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function." Magnetic Resonance Imaging 73, 163-176 (2020). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/

The paper claims superior signal-to-noise ratio at 11.7T compared to 3T and 7T, but provides only visual comparison of images acquired from different subjects using different RF coil configurations, without any quantitative SNR measurements to support the claim. "A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T on different volunteers. The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T" The authors explicitly acknowledge using "different volunteers" for the cross-field comparison. Inter-subject variability in brain anatomy, tissue composition, and physiological noise is well-documented in MRI literature and can significantly confound comparisons. While matching acquisition parameters is appropriate, subject-to-subject variability can introduce substantial differences in image quality that are unrelated to field strength. A rigorous comparison would require within-subject measurements or use of standardized phantoms. "Acquisitions were performed with 1Tx (body coil)-32Rx, 8Tx-32Rx and 8Tx-32Rx head coils at 3T, 7T and 11.7T respectively." The RF coil configurations differ significantly across field strengths (1Tx body coil at 3T versus 8Tx dedicated head coils at 7T and 11.7T). Transmit coil efficiency, receive coil geometry, and channel count all substantially affect SNR independent of field strength. The use of a body coil for transmission at 3T versus optimized parallel transmit head coils at higher fields introduces additional confounds beyond field strength that are not accounted for in the comparison. "The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T, which can be traded for higher spatial (or temporal) resolutions" Despite claiming SNR gains, the paper provides no quantitative SNR measurements. Standard MRI literature reports SNR values using established methodologies (e.g., signal from tissue regions divided by noise from background or difference images). The claim of "supralinear gain in sensitivity" referenced from prior work [[2]](#citation-2) was based on quantitative measurements. The current paper's visual comparison in Figure 2C, while visually compelling, does not provide the quantitative evidence needed to substantiate SNR claims or enable comparison with published values at other field strengths. "The SNR was measured at the center of a spherical water saline phantom with a gradient‐recalled echo sequence. Measurements were performed at NeuroSpin at 3, 7, and 11.7 T." [[3]](#citation-3) Notably, the same research group published a rigorous quantitative SNR comparison (Le Ster et al., 2022) using phantom measurements with controlled experimental conditions across field strengths. This methodology, which yielded precise SNR scaling data (B₀^1.94±0.16), would have been directly applicable to the current in vivo study. The absence of similar quantitative measurements in the current paper, despite the group's demonstrated capability, represents a missed opportunity to provide objective evidence for the claimed SNR benefits. References: [2] Pohmann, R., Speck, O., Scheffler, K. "Signal-to-noise ratio and MR tissue parameters in human brain imaging at 3, 7, and 9.4 tesla using current receive coil arrays." Magnetic Resonance in Medicine 75, 801-809 (2016). https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/mrm.25677 [3] Le Ster, C., Grant, A., Van de Moortele, P.F., et al. "Magnetic field strength dependent SNR gain at the center of a spherical phantom and up to 11.7T." Magnetic Resonance in Medicine 88, 2131-2138 (2022). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35849739/

This paper draws overly strong conclusions about human safety/tolerability at 11.7T from a small, short-term, endpoint-limited protocol, which weakens the validity of the safety claims made in the Abstract and Main text. "To assure the safety of human imaging at such high field strength, we performed physiological, vestibular, behavioral and genotoxicity measurements on the volunteers. The data shows no evidence of adverse effects." This pair of sentences is used to justify a broad safety conclusion, but “no evidence of adverse effects” is a non-equivalence statement that depends strongly on sample size, endpoint sensitivity, and follow-up duration. The described measures target a subset of plausible acute and subacute effects, but do not address detection of rare events or delayed outcomes, which is central when translating a first-in-human ultra-high-field exposure into a generalized “safety” assertion. The paper does not report an a priori safety endpoint hierarchy, minimally important differences, or any power/sensitivity analysis for the bioeffects outcomes, so readers cannot assess what magnitude of harm the study could have detected. In MRI safety contexts, the field’s novelty (11.7T whole-body) raises the bar for evidentiary strength, because the appropriate inference is typically “no concerning signal observed under this protocol” rather than “safe” in general. As written, the conclusion materially overreaches what the reported study design can support. "Twenty volunteers (24.2±4.8 year old, 8 males) were scanned at 11.7T and performed the tests mentioned below." A cohort of 20 healthy young adults is a reasonable starting point for feasibility, but it is not sufficient to substantiate broad claims about safety and innocuousness of 11.7T scanning, particularly for outcomes that may be infrequent or heterogeneous across populations. The inclusion criteria (18–40 years) also intentionally narrow the population, which further limits external validity of the “humans can safely tolerate” implication. The manuscript does not provide a rationale that this N is adequate for the multiple physiological, vestibular, cognitive, and genotoxicity endpoints it assesses. Without a quantitative sensitivity/power discussion, the negative findings are hard to interpret because the study could simply be underpowered for clinically meaningful or rare effects. In safety-focused first-in-human work, it is standard to explicitly frame conclusions as preliminary and bounded by the sample and follow-up. "Randomised trials offer the best approach for providing safety data as well as efficacy data, although they cannot detect rare harms." [[5]](#cite-5) This statement highlights a core limitation of using small human studies to infer safety: even rigorous randomized designs are structurally limited in detecting rare adverse events. The present paper’s safety assessment is based on two cohorts of 20 participants and is not positioned as an adequately powered harms-detection study, making the “no adverse effects” framing particularly vulnerable to false reassurance. The issue is amplified because the paper does not quantify uncertainty around adverse-event rates (e.g., confidence bounds) or specify which harms were actively adjudicated versus passively reported. The quoted guidance is consistent with common practice that early studies can screen for frequent/large effects but cannot establish absence of uncommon harms. Therefore, the manuscript’s broad safety conclusion is not justified by its study scale. "Authors should particularly avoid the common error of interpreting a non-significant result as indicating equivalence of interventions." [[5]](#cite-5) The paper repeatedly interprets “no significant differences” between 11.7T and 0T as evidence for innocuousness, but the statistical non-significance of group differences does not establish equivalence without an explicit equivalence/non-inferiority design and margins. The manuscript does not report equivalence margins, confidence intervals for group differences, or an analysis plan tailored to equivalence, so its interpretation risks precisely the error described in the CONSORT elaboration. This is not a cosmetic reporting issue: it directly affects whether the central safety conclusion is logically warranted. In safety studies, equivalence-style reasoning is often crucial because the claim of “no effect” must be defined quantitatively. The absence of that framework makes the conclusion unreliable. "Likewise, a relatively large p-value does not imply evidence in favor of the null hypothesis; many other hypotheses may be equally or more consistent with the observed data." [[2]](#cite-2) The manuscript’s safety narrative leans heavily on finding no statistically significant differences between groups and then describing the impact of field exposure as “negligible,” but the cited principle warns against that inference. A large p-value is compatible with a wide range of effect sizes when the study is small or measurements are noisy, which is exactly the situation for many physiological and behavioral endpoints in N=20 cohorts. The paper does not present confidence intervals for key endpoints or a structured equivalence analysis, so the reader cannot tell whether clinically meaningful differences have been excluded. In MRI safety evaluation, the magnitude and uncertainty of effects matter at least as much as whether a threshold is crossed, because small effects may still accumulate or interact with other stressors. This mismatch between statistical interpretation best practices and the manuscript’s conclusions undermines the robustness of its “no adverse effects” messaging. "A modified testing protocol informed by these results was devised with the goal of permitting increased enrollment while providing continued monitoring to evaluate field effects." [[4]](#cite-4) This prior ultra-high-field study (10.5T) illustrates a more cautious and methodologically standard posture: initial observations are treated as preliminary signals that motivate larger enrollment and continued monitoring, rather than being used to broadly confirm safety. The contrast matters because the paper under review claims that its “safety and imaging data” confirm applicability and that “humans can safely tolerate” 11.7T, despite having a comparable or smaller cohort than other UHF safety pilots. The quoted sentence evidences a field norm that early UHF human exposure studies remain vigilant to subtle or low-frequency effects and explicitly plan follow-on monitoring to strengthen inference. That norm is especially relevant at 11.7T, where multiple physiological systems (vestibular, cognitive, cardiovascular, thermal) could be affected. Compared to this practice, the reviewed paper’s conclusion is stronger than its evidentiary base. References: [2] Wasserstein RL, Lazar NA. *The ASA’s statement on p-values: context, process, and purpose*. The American Statistician (2016). https://sciences.ucf.edu/biology/d4lab/wp-content/uploads/sites/23/2017/03/WassersteinLazar2016.pdf [4] Grant A, Metzger GJ, Van de Moortele P-F, et al. *10.5 T MRI static field effects on human cognitive, vestibular, and physiological function* (Abstract). PubMed (PMID: 32822819). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32822819/ [5] Moher D, Hopewell S, Schulz KF, et al. *CONSORT 2010 Explanation and Elaboration: updated guidelines for reporting parallel group randomised trials* (PDF). BMJ (2010). https://www.bmj.com/content/bmj/340/bmj.c869.full.pdf

This paper’s conclusion that 11.7T provides a higher SNR (and related contrast-to-noise benefits) is not robustly supported because the 3T/7T/11.7T comparisons are performed on different subjects and different transmit/receive configurations, and no quantitative SNR/CNR measurement protocol is reported for the comparison. "A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T on different volunteers. The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T..." The paper uses a cross-subject comparison to argue a field-strength-driven SNR gain, but SNR is sensitive to subject anatomy/positioning, motion, and physiological noise, so “different volunteers” introduces an uncontrolled confound. The text also limits “same protocol” to a subset of parameters, leaving other contributors (coil loading, shimming success, flip-angle accuracy, reconstruction settings) potentially different across sites/systems. Because the comparison is not within-subject and the paper does not present quantitative SNR estimation (with uncertainties), the evidence is largely qualitative and cannot cleanly attribute observed differences to B0. In standard MRI methodology, field-strength SNR comparisons are typically designed to isolate B0 effects by holding subjects and coil geometry as constant as possible. As written, the paper’s phrasing overstates causal attribution from an under-controlled comparison. "Acquisitions were performed with 1Tx (body coil)-32Rx, 8Tx-32Rx and 8Tx-32Rx head coils at 3T, 7T and 11.7T respectively." This sentence directly indicates that the transmit chain differs substantially across field strengths (body-coil transmit at 3T versus multi-channel transmit at 7T/11.7T), which alters flip-angle uniformity and achievable signal even at fixed nominal sequence parameters. Coil design is a major determinant of receive sensitivity and parallel imaging behavior, so changing coil type makes it difficult to attribute differences to B0 alone. The paper does not provide a calibration/normalization method to separate coil-related gains from field-strength gains (e.g., intrinsic SNR modeling, flip-angle correction, or controlled phantom normalization). In ultra-high field, coil performance and B1 inhomogeneity are known to be major limiting factors, making this confound especially consequential. Therefore, the claimed field-strength “gain in SNR” is not methodologically well supported. "The same human subjects were scanned at all three field strengths, using identical sequence parameters and similar 31- or 32-channel receive coil arrays." [[3]](#cite-3) This published field-strength comparison provides a clear methodological contrast: it removes major confounds by scanning the same subjects and using matched acquisition parameters and comparable receive arrays. That design choice is widely regarded as necessary to make credible statements about how SNR scales with B0 in vivo, because it limits inter-subject variability and coil-related effects. The reviewed paper explicitly does the opposite (different subjects and different transmit strategies), so its evidence does not reach the rigor level commonly expected for quantitative SNR claims. Importantly, the literature example shows that controlling these factors is feasible and standard for SNR scaling studies, undermining any implication that qualitative cross-subject figures are sufficient. This gap weakens the reliability of the manuscript’s interpretation. "The g-factors did not show the expected decrease, indicating a dominating role of coil design." [[3]](#cite-3) This result emphasizes that hardware (especially coil design) can dominate performance metrics even when field strength changes, which directly bears on the reviewed paper’s use of different coil/transmit configurations at 3T/7T/11.7T. If coil design can dominate acceleration penalties and effective sensitivity, then attributing observed image differences to B0 without a controlled design is unreliable. The reviewed paper does not report g-factor, SNR maps, or reconstruction comparability across systems, so the potential coil-dominance confound remains unaddressed. This is a major issue because the paper’s broader claims about “high signal and contrast to noise ratio” at 11.7T implicitly point to B0 as the primary cause. The literature indicates that without controlling or quantifying coil effects, such causal claims are not robust. References: [3] Pohmann R, Speck O, Scheffler K. *Signal-to-noise ratio and MR tissue parameters in human brain imaging at 3, 7, and 9.4 tesla using current receive coil arrays* (Abstract). PubMed (PMID: 25820458). https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25820458/

The manuscript’s methodological reporting is insufficient to evaluate whether the claimed SAR mitigation and pTx safety control are robust across subjects, because key safety-relevant quantities (e.g., achieved SAR margins, hotspot validation, and sensitivity to motion) are not presented for the 11.7T pTx protocols. "We deployed parallel transmission tools to mitigate the radiofrequency field inhomogeneity problem and tame the specific absorption rate." This statement links the main technical contribution (pTx) to a core human safety requirement (SAR control), but it is presented as a high-level assertion without the quantitative details necessary for independent evaluation. At ultra-high field, SAR limitations and local hotspots are among the primary safety constraints, so a reader needs to see at least the operational SAR limits used, how conservatism was set, and how compliance was verified in vivo. The paper later mentions virtual observation points and constrained pulse design, but does not provide the actual SAR values (global/head SAR and local 10g SAR), margins to limits, or per-subject distributions that would substantiate “tamed” SAR. Because the safety conclusion depends on this control being reliable for all scanned subjects, the lack of quantitative reporting undermines the credibility of the safety claim. This is a methodological transparency gap that directly affects validity. "The GRAPE pulse was a universal RF pulse [28] and was designed offline on a database constituted of the 6 first subject field maps." A universal pulse designed from a limited database can be practical, but it raises two safety-relevant questions: how robust is it to out-of-distribution anatomy/positioning, and how conservative are the SAR constraints under variability. The manuscript does not report any validation results showing that the universal pulse maintained SAR compliance and flip-angle targets across the remaining subjects beyond the six used to design it. In pTx, performance robustness and SAR robustness are not guaranteed to coincide; a pulse can appear to work (acceptable flip-angle error) while still creating higher local SAR hotspots in some anatomies. Without reporting robustness statistics or worst-case analyses, readers cannot judge whether the approach generalizes safely beyond the design set. This is particularly important because the paper’s conclusions generalize to “humans” rather than to the six-map database. "It enables spatial field manipulation, but at the same time, it may create hotspots by focusing the RF fields. This means that accurate subject-specific SAR measurement is mandatory to operate pTx appropriately." [[1]](#cite-1) This safety review summarizes a widely accepted concern in high-field pTx: hotspot creation is an intrinsic risk, and safe operation depends on accurate (often subject-specific) SAR assessment. The reviewed paper does not report subject-specific SAR verification, nor does it describe any empirical validation (e.g., temperature monitoring or phantom verification) that would allow independent assessment of SAR modeling accuracy at 11.7T. While the paper mentions virtual observation points, it does not show how conservative the VOP set was, what head models were used, or whether model mismatch was accounted for. Given the “mandatory” nature of accurate SAR assessment emphasized in the literature, omission of these details is a major methodological gap rather than a minor reporting choice. Consequently, the reader cannot verify that the pTx approach was safely controlled across participants. "They compared conventional quadrature excitation and multi-spoke parallel-transmit excitation after RF shimming and found that the local SAR increased by more than 100% in 1 and 4 cm3 for quadrature and parallel-transmit excitation, respectively, in the worst case where the head model was shifted by 20 and 10 mm in right and posterior directions, respectively." [[1]](#cite-1) This quoted finding highlights that relatively plausible head-position changes can dramatically increase local SAR under pTx, which is directly relevant for a 90-minute session where motion is likely. The reviewed paper acknowledges motion problems for image quality but does not connect motion to SAR safety, and it does not report how head stabilization and motion were handled from a safety standpoint. Without a motion-sensitivity analysis or conservative SAR margins that explicitly account for potential motion, the claim that SAR was “tamed” is not fully supported. In ultra-high-field first-in-human studies, demonstrating safety robustness to realistic deviations (including motion) is an important part of safety reporting. The absence of this analysis reduces confidence that the protocol is safe beyond idealized conditions. References: [1] Okada T, Akasaka T, Thuy DHD, Isa T. *Safety for Human MR Scanners at 7T*. Magnetic Resonance in Medical Sciences (2022). https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9618930/

The paper relies on qualitative statements to assess image quality and SNR gain, lacking quantitative metrics essential for validating the performance of the new 11.7T scanner. "The presented in vivo human brain images acquired at 11.7T thereby reveal good signal homogeneity consistent with the simulations, without severe RF field inhomogeneity artefacts." "Good" is a subjective descriptor. The authors do not present B1+ maps or quantitative homogeneity metrics (e.g., coefficient of variation) for the in vivo data to objectively demonstrate the performance of the parallel transmission system at 11.7T. "The comparison highlights the gain in signal:noise ratio (SNR) at 11.7T, which can be traded for higher spatial (or temporal) resolutions..." No SNR values, maps, or measurements are provided to quantify this claimed gain. A scientific comparison of field strengths requires quantitative data to substantiate claims of improvement. "Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) maps were calculated... The SNR increased supralinearly with field strength..." [2] Previous studies comparing field strengths (e.g., Pohmann et al.) provide detailed quantitative SNR maps and measurements to validate expected gains, setting a standard that this paper fails to meet. References: [2] Pohmann, R., Speck, O., & Scheffler, K. (2016). Signal-to-noise ratio and MR tissue parameters in human brain imaging at 3, 7, and 9.4 Tesla using current receive coil arrays. Magnetic Resonance in Medicine, 75(2), 801-809. https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/mrm.25677

Given the unprecedented field strength, the description of safety monitoring, particularly regarding RF heating (SAR), is insufficient. "The specific absorption rate (SAR), also growing with Bo [10], was tamed with virtual observation points [11] and dedicated RF pulse design algorithms [12]." While the method is named, no data is shown on the actual SAR levels monitored during the scans or the validation of the Virtual Observation Points (VOP) model at 11.7T. Given the high risk of local heating at this frequency, detailed safety data is crucial. "The data shows no evidence of adverse effects." The paper focuses on physiological and behavioral side effects but omits the technical safety data (e.g., temperature simulations vs. measurements) that would prove the RF safety of the imaging session itself.

The provided code repository only contains scripts for the behavioral task (ANT), while the core technical innovation (pTx pulse design and reconstruction) is not shared. "We tackled this problem by deploying parallel transmission hardware and software..." The paper attributes the success of the imaging to this software, yet it is not provided. ``` ANT-R task ============= ... `ant-r-classic.py` is a script implementing the ANT-R task described in Fan, J. et al (2009). ``` The `README.md` in the provided code repository confirms that the code is exclusively for the "Attentional Network Task" (behavioral), not for the MRI pulse design or image reconstruction. "The code repository... contains several Python scripts implementing variants of the ANT task." The absence of the imaging code prevents reproduction of the pTx pulses and image reconstruction, which are the primary technical contributions enabling the 11.7T images.

The comparison with 7T is limited to a single T2*-weighted protocol and does not benchmark against state-of-the-art 7T imaging. "A 2D T2*-weighted acquisition was repeated with the same protocol (resolution, bandwidth, acquisition time, number of receive elements) at 3T and 7T..." Using the "same protocol" across field strengths is often suboptimal because relaxation times (T1, T2*) change with field strength. A fair comparison would use protocols optimized for each field strength to demonstrate the true potential of 11.7T over 7T. "Images indicate sufficient SNR to boost further spatial resolution or decrease acquisition time in the future." Without a comparison to optimized 7T images (which can also achieve very high resolution), the claim that 11.7T provides a unique advantage is not fully supported by the evidence presented.

In order to maximize the impact of this scientific report, it is important to provide open access to the initial data presented in figure 2. This will pique the curiosity of the community and greatly increase the number of citations for this study. Although the authors have stated that \"All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the paper and its Extended Method,\" I was unable to locate this information in the materials provided for review. Sharing data is now more accessible than ever through platforms like neurovault.org, which I highly recommend to the authors.

In essence, sharing the neuroimaging data displayed in figure 2 will enable the community to adopt this technological advancement with enthusiasm, scrutinize the accuracy of the findings, and pave the way for future research and discoveries. I strongly urge you to consider sharing this valuable data.

While the image resolution is certainly impressive, similar resolutions have been demonstrated using lower field strengths (7T), albeit with longer scan times, see for example Feinberg et al, “Next generation MRI scanner” Nature Methods 20, 2023. doi: 10.1038/s41592-023-02068-7.
Show images of all subjects, e.g. in the supplementary materials, to demonstrate the robustness of results or at least discuss issues with robustness at this stage.

Show results of other relevant contrasts (T1, DWI, MR angiography) and methods that are very likely to profit from 11.7T (e.g. MRS, fMRI) as well, or at least discuss issues that arise with these and other important MR contrasts.

While I understand that the authors have 3T and 7T scanners available at their facility. I would argue that the relevant reference point would be the human imaging results obtained at the field strength of 10.5T. A more detailed comparison with published findings at this field strength (closest to the one used here) would be highly relevant.

The comparison with 3T and 7T results seems biased as it is unclear if the parameters used were optimized at the lower field strengths. Acquisition parameters have not been provided, only stated that \"similar scans were performed\".

While the authors briefly mention next steps in the technical developments, I miss a more detailed discussion of how much these will improve SNR (compared to theoretical possible SNR) and resolution (e.g. with the improved motion mitigation strategies) and other aspects.
I miss a clearer and more detailed discussion about what the current results and future developments mean for scientific/neuroscience and potentially clinical applications.

A few times ISMRM abstracts are used as references, but these seem only accessible to registrants of the conference. While most people in the field will likely have/get access, this is not the case for the average reader.
The project name Iseult is used throughout the manuscript, but it is unclear what the specific meaning is?
I think the work would benefit from a better description of state-of-the-art resolution compared to the new scanner.

In that sense I was also somewhat surprised that the authors didn’t attempt to break any “records” for resolution. For instance, doi: 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2013.08.013 acquired images at 9.4T with 0.13x0.13x0.8 mm^3 and doi: 10.1371/journal.pone.0133921 presented 0.44 mm isotropic resolution. Without robbing any novelty from the current work, it may be beneficial to at least discuss these previous works and state the highest state-of-the-art resolutions in other fields (perhaps also vis-a-vis scan time and motion effects).

In Fig 2 it is unclear which exact conditions were used in each of the 3/7/11.7 T measurements. Would they be expected to only vary in resolution (i.e. were TE/TR adjusted for the different field T2(*/T1) or were they performed under the same conditions (TE/TR)? If the latter, contrast is expected to vary, and to make a good comparison it is recommended to match the contrast conditions while only varying resolution if possible. Please clarify.

The paper draws conclusions about the magnetic field structure being consistent with a "shock-compressed magnetic field model" despite explicitly not performing absolute EVPA calibration, which is a prerequisite for such physical interpretations in VLBI polarimetry. "As the observing period was only 2 hours, the primary focus was on inferring the intensity and the location of the polarised emission; calibration of absolute electric vector position angle (EVPA) was not attempted since it would decrease already short total exposition on the target source." The paper explicitly acknowledges that absolute EVPA calibration was not performed. Standard VLBI polarimetry methodology requires EVPA calibration to establish the absolute orientation of the electric field vectors, which is essential for relating the observed polarization pattern to the underlying magnetic field structure. Without this calibration, the observed EVPA values have an unknown offset that renders physical interpretation of magnetic field geometry unreliable. "While specific orientation of the EVPA cannot be treated physically due to the absence of its calibration, the orderly distributed electric vectors are consistent with the shock-compressed magnetic field model." Despite the acknowledged absence of EVPA calibration, the paper still draws a physical conclusion about consistency with the shock-compressed magnetic field model. The "orderly distribution" of uncalibrated EVPAs cannot be reliably used to infer magnetic field topology because the relationship between EVPA and magnetic field direction depends on the absolute calibration. Without knowing the true EVPA values, one cannot determine whether the magnetic field is parallel or perpendicular to the jet axis, nor whether it is toroidal, poloidal, or randomly oriented. "Absolute calibration of the electric position vector angle (EVPA) was applied using single dish observations from the Effelsberg telescope on the same day as our observations... The error in the EVPA calibration is estimated to be around 10 degrees." [[1]](#citation-list) This quote from a recent space VLBI study of 3C 279 demonstrates the standard practice in the field: absolute EVPA calibration is performed using contemporaneous single-dish observations, and even then has uncertainties of ~10 degrees. The J0906+6930 paper lacks this calibration step entirely, making the magnetic field interpretations significantly less reliable than those achieved in similar studies. References: [1] [Fuentes et al. (2025), "Revisiting 3C 279 jet morphology with space VLBI at 26 GHz," Astronomy & Astrophysics](https://www.aanda.org/articles/aa/pdf/2025/12/aa55188-25.pdf)

The paper derives two Doppler factor estimates (δ = 6.1 ± 0.8 from brightness temperature and δ_var = 4.8 from variability) that differ by ~27%, yet uses only the higher value for deriving physical parameters (Lorentz factor, viewing angle) without adequately addressing this discrepancy or incorporating it into the uncertainty analysis. "The T_B of the J0906+6930 core is (30.2 ± 4.0) × 10^10 K with a consequent Doppler factor δ = T_B/T_B,eq of 6.1 ± 0.8" This Doppler factor is derived by assuming the source is exactly at equipartition (T_B,eq = 5 × 10^10 K), and that any excess brightness temperature is entirely due to Doppler boosting. This assumption may not hold precisely, as intrinsic brightness temperatures can vary depending on the source conditions and magnetic field configuration. "The corresponding Doppler factor is δ_var = ∛(T_B,var/T_B,eq) = 4.8" The variability-based Doppler factor of 4.8 is approximately 21% lower than the brightness temperature-based value of 6.1. This ~1.3 difference is outside the quoted error bars of ±0.8 for the first estimate, suggesting the methods may be probing different aspects of the source or that systematic uncertainties are underestimated. "This value is consistent but marginally lower than that derived from the above VLBI model fitting." The paper dismisses the 27% discrepancy as "consistent but marginally lower" without providing statistical justification. When two independent methods give systematically different results, this typically indicates unaccounted systematic uncertainties. The paper then proceeds to use only δ = 6.1 for calculating Γ = 3.6 and θ = 6.8°, without propagating the uncertainty from the δ_var = 4.8 value into the final parameter estimates. Using δ = 4.8 instead would yield Γ ≈ 3.3 and θ ≈ 9°, which would affect the conclusions about the jet's power and nascent nature.

The negative proper motion of component J1 (μ = -0.006 ± 0.004 mas/yr, corresponding to -0.8 ± 0.5 c) has a statistical significance of only ~1.5σ and is consistent with zero within errors, yet is used as key evidence supporting the jet-ISM interaction and "stationary" jet component interpretation. "The proper motion of J1 is −0.006 ± 0.004 mas yr^−1 (–0.8±0.5 c, Figure 3), consistent with a scenario involving jet–ISM interaction and the subsequent jet bending." The quoted proper motion has an uncertainty of ±0.004 mas/yr, giving a signal-to-noise ratio of only 1.5 (0.006/0.004). At this significance level, the measurement is consistent with zero motion, positive motion (outward), or even the reported negative motion (inward toward core). A negative proper motion would imply the jet component is moving backward toward the core, which is physically unusual and not discussed in the paper. "The position of J1 (projected distance ~5.3 pc away from the core) is nearly stationary between 2004 and 2018, consistent with a jet beam encountering dense surrounding ISM." While "nearly stationary" is a reasonable interpretation given the low significance of the measured motion, the paper uses this observation as primary evidence for jet-ISM interaction. The statistical evidence is weak: at 1.5σ, there is approximately a 13% probability that the true proper motion is positive (outward motion) rather than negative or zero. More conservative language acknowledging this substantial uncertainty would be appropriate. * Quote (from Supplementary Table 5): "μ(R) = −0.006±0.004" for J1, and "The significance of μ(Dec, J2) is larger than 3σ" The paper appropriately notes that J2's proper motion is significant at >3σ, contrasting with J1's measurement. However, the subsequent physical interpretations rely heavily on J1's behavior despite its low statistical significance. The MOJAVE survey [[2]](#citation-list), which provides systematic proper motion measurements for a large sample of AGN jets, typically identifies reliable kinematics from measurements with higher statistical significance. Drawing firm physical conclusions from individual component motions at only 1.5σ significance should be approached with appropriate caveats. References: [2] [Lister, M. L. et al. (2019), "MOJAVE XVII. Jet Kinematics and Parent Population Properties of Relativistically Beamed Radio-Loud Blazars," The Astrophysical Journal, 874, 43](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2019ApJ...874...43L)

The ISM density estimate (n_e ≥ 26.6 cm^−3) depends critically on the jet luminosity calculation, which uses empirical scaling relations with quoted uncertainties of ±2 in logarithm (a factor of 100), yet this substantial uncertainty is not adequately propagated to the derived ISM properties. "log L_jet,rad = (12 ± 2) + (0.75 ± 0.04) log L_radio" and "log L_jet,kinetic = (6 ± 2) + (0.90 ± 0.04) log L_radio" The intercepts in these empirical relations have uncertainties of ±2 in log space, meaning the jet luminosity estimates from these relations have intrinsic uncertainties of a factor of ~100. This is an enormous systematic uncertainty that dominates the error budget but is not propagated through the subsequent calculations of ISM density. "It must be noted that the empirical relations have a large scatter mainly due to the diversity in the sources constituting the inference, ranging from radio-loud AGN to X-ray binaries, and having to properly account for spectral state transitions, variability and systematics from comparison of data from different databases. The uncertainty on the jet power may then be underestimated." The paper acknowledges this limitation in a single sentence but does not quantify how this affects the downstream conclusions. The ISM density calculation (n_e ≥ 26.6 cm^−3) is presented as a constraint without confidence intervals that incorporate the ±2 log uncertainty in the jet luminosity relations. Given the factor of ~100 uncertainty in jet power, the ISM density constraint could plausibly range over two orders of magnitude. "ρ_ISM ≥ 2.4 × 10^−26 g cm^−3 which corresponds to a number density n_e ≥ 26.6 cm^−3" This lower limit is presented as a firm physical constraint used to support the jet-ISM interaction scenario. However, given the factor of ~100 uncertainty in the jet luminosity, and the momentum flux balance equation's dependence on L_jet, the true lower limit on n_e could be significantly different. The interpretation that "a relatively lighter jet susceptible to interaction and bending owing to a relatively denser medium" is weakened by these unquantified systematic uncertainties.

This paper’s quantitative claims about the jet power relative to Eddington are internally inconsistent, weakening the validity of the central conclusion that J0906+6930 hosts a “less powerful” jet undergoing a transition in feedback mode. “The lower Lorentz factor and slower jet component are consistent with the less powerful nature of the J0906+6930 jet (jet/Eddington luminosity \( \sim 0.004 \), see Methods jet and ISM properties).” This sentence makes the jet/Eddington ratio a key quantitative support for the paper’s “less powerful jet” interpretation, which is then used to motivate broader physical conclusions about feedback mode transitions. Because the paper relies on this ratio for interpretation rather than treating it as a side calculation, any inconsistency in how it is computed directly affects the soundness of the conclusions. The cited value (\(\sim 0.004\)) implicitly requires a consistent definition of both \(L_{\rm jet}\) and \(L_{\rm Edd}\), and clarity about whether beaming corrections are applied. If different sections use different definitions without reconciliation, readers cannot tell which physical regime the source is claimed to occupy. This is a major issue because jet power is central to the claimed evolutionary scenario. “The jet luminosity is taken as the addition of the radiative and kinetic contributions and is \( L_{\text{jet}} = 2.8 \times 10^{46} \) erg s\(^{-1}\), and is \( 2.2 \times 10^{43} \) erg s\(^{-1}\) using \( \delta = 6.0 \) as inferred in Appendix F. This is ~0.02 \( L_{\text{edd}} \), where \( L_{\text{edd}} = (1.3 \times 10^{47} \) erg s\(^{-1}\))\( m_g \) is the Eddington luminosity and using \( m_g = 4.4 \times 10^{-2} \) as inferred from Appendix H.” This passage simultaneously reports two values of \(L_{\rm jet}\) that differ by about three orders of magnitude, but then asserts a single ratio to \(L_{\rm Edd}\) (“~0.02”) without specifying which \(L_{\rm jet}\) value is used. Moreover, using the paper’s stated \(L_{\rm Edd}=1.3\times 10^{47}\,m_g\) erg s\(^{-1}\) and \(m_g=4.4\times 10^{-2}\) implies \(L_{\rm Edd}\approx 5.7\times 10^{45}\) erg s\(^{-1}\); then \(2.8\times 10^{46}\) erg s\(^{-1}\) corresponds to \(\sim 4.9\,L_{\rm Edd}\), while \(2.2\times 10^{43}\) erg s\(^{-1}\) corresponds to \(\sim 3.9\times 10^{-3}\,L_{\rm Edd}\). Neither matches “~0.02,” and only the smaller value is near the \(\sim 0.004\) ratio claimed in the main text. Because the paper explicitly acknowledges large scatter in the empirical jet-power relations, the lack of a consistent, transparent ratio calculation makes the inferred jet weakness and feedback-transition narrative unreliable. This is not a minor numerical typo: it changes whether the jet is interpreted as sub-Eddington, near-Eddington, or super-Eddington.

This paper’s interpretation of parsec-scale jet kinematics (including an inward/negative proper motion for J1 and derived Lorentz factor/viewing angle) is not robust because it depends on low-significance linear trends and assumes a stationary, well-defined core despite explicit evidence of core blending and structural variability. “Combining the archival and new data, the proper motion of J1 is \( -0.006 \pm 0.004 \) mas yr\(^{-1}\) (–0.8±0.5 c, Figure 3), consistent with a scenario involving jet–ISM interaction and the subsequent jet bending.” The reported J1 proper motion is only about 1.5σ from zero, so it is statistically consistent with no motion (or modest outward motion) at conventional significance thresholds. Treating such a marginal trend as “consistent with” a specific physical mechanism (jet–ISM interaction producing inward motion) overstates what the data can support, especially given sparse sampling over long time baselines. Because the claimed low bulk Lorentz factor and “less powerful jet” interpretation depend on the jet-speed estimates, the weakness of the proper-motion significance propagates into key conclusions. In VLBI kinematics studies, low-significance motions are typically reported as upper limits or treated cautiously unless supported by clear component tracking across many epochs. Here the paper’s narrative uses J1’s sign (negative) as qualitative evidence, which is not statistically justified. “Doppler beaming in the relativistic jet can cause the core brightness temperature (\( T_B \)) to exceed the theoretical limits set by equipartition between the kinetic and magnetic energy densities, assumed to be \( T_{B,\text{eq}} = 5 \times 10^{10} \) K\(^{32}\). The \( T_B \) of the J0906+6930 core is \( (30.2 \pm 4.0) \times 10^{10} \) K with a consequent Doppler factor \( \delta = T_B/T_{B,\text{eq}} \) of 6.1 ± 0.8 (see Methods Doppler boosting parameters).
