The word "drive" implies causation, but the analysis consists of linear regression models showing correlation between molecular/connectomic predictors and cross-sectional cortical abnormality patterns. Correlation cannot establish that molecular vulnerability "drives" abnormality patterns; other confounding factors could explain both the predictor patterns and the abnormality patterns simultaneously. "We consistently find that local attributes govern both disorder-specific abnormalities and cross-disorder similarity more than global connectivity and regional dynamics." The term "govern" implies control or regulation, suggesting a causal relationship. However, the dominance analysis used merely decomposes variance explained in a regression model. Higher explained variance does not establish that local attributes causally "govern" abnormalities—it only shows stronger statistical association. "molecular mechanisms interact with the structural and functional architectures of the brain to guide cross-disorder abnormality patterns" "Interact" and "guide" suggest mechanistic processes, but the evidence presented is a correlation between molecular R² and node-neighbor correlation across disorders. This is a meta-analytic correlation across 13 data points (disorders), not a demonstration of molecular-connectomic interaction at the mechanistic level. "connection patterns among vulnerable regions further amplify this vulnerability" "Amplify" suggests an active process where connectivity increases vulnerability, but the cross-sectional data cannot determine whether connectivity amplifies vulnerability or whether vulnerable regions happen to be connected for reasons unrelated to amplification. The temporal ordering required to establish amplification (vulnerability → spreading → increased vulnerability) cannot be inferred from cross-sectional data.

The distance-dependent cross-validation method (75% training, 25% test based on spatial proximity) may not fully address spatial autocorrelation issues, as test regions adjacent to the training boundary will share autocorrelation structure with training regions, potentially inflating model performance estimates. "Each model was cross-validated using a distance-dependent method. For 1000 randomly chosen source regions, the model was fit on a training set, composed of the 75% of regions closest to the source region. Next, cortical abnormality values were predicted on the remaining 25% of regions" This cross-validation scheme selects the spatially closest 75% of regions for training, meaning the test set comprises only the most distant 25% of regions. However, regions at the boundary between training and test sets will have substantial spatial autocorrelation with training regions. With only 68 cortical regions in the Desikan-Killiany atlas, a 75/25 split results in approximately 51 training and 17 test regions, where test regions near the boundary may have highly correlated values with adjacent training regions. * Quote from Alexander-Bloch et al. (2018) [[5]](#citation-list): "On testing for spatial correspondence between maps of human brain structure and function" This foundational paper on spatial autocorrelation in brain maps (cited by the reviewed paper) emphasizes that spatial proximity creates dependencies that standard statistical methods cannot handle. The reviewed paper's distance-dependent cross-validation creates a sharp spatial boundary, but brain map autocorrelation functions typically decay gradually with distance. Regions in the test set that are only slightly farther than the 75th percentile distance will still share substantial autocorrelation with training regions. * Quote from code file 01_regression_and_dominance.py, lines 54-55: ```python train_idx = idx[:int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords)))] test_idx = idx[int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords))):] ``` The code shows that training and test indices are determined by a hard cutoff at the 75th percentile of distance from the source region. This binary split does not account for the continuous nature of spatial autocorrelation, where regions just beyond the cutoff (in the test set) may have values highly predicted by regions just before the cutoff (in the training set). More sophisticated approaches such as spatial block cross-validation or leave-one-out with spatial buffers would better address this limitation. References: [5] Alexander-Bloch, A. F. et al. (2018). On testing for spatial correspondence between maps of human brain structure and function. NeuroImage, 178, 540–551. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1053811918304968

This paper makes a potentially invalid and insufficiently documented dataset claim that all ENIGMA-derived cortical abnormality maps come from adult patients, even though at least some of the underlying ENIGMA morphometry mega-/meta-analyses explicitly span childhood through adulthood; this undermines the validity of cross-disorder comparisons and the robustness of downstream interpretations that implicitly assume a single life-stage. “All cortical abnormality maps were collected from adult patients, following identical processing protocols, for a total of over 21 000 scanned patients against almost 26 000 controls.” This sentence asserts an adult-only sampling frame as a core premise for comparability across the 13 disorder maps, but the paper does not provide disorder-by-disorder age ranges or cite adult-only ENIGMA subgroup analyses for neurodevelopmental conditions. Because cortical thickness and its disorder-associated deviations are strongly age-dependent, mixing developmental stages can change both spatial patterns and effect sizes, which would directly affect the paper’s cross-disorder similarity analyses and regression fits. The claim is also used to justify that the maps are “comparable,” so if it is inaccurate or only partially true, it becomes a validity issue rather than a minor reporting gap. The manuscript does not describe any harmonized restriction to adult cohorts, nor any sensitivity analysis for age-stratified ENIGMA maps. As a result, readers cannot verify whether the analyzed ADHD/ASD/OCD-related maps reflect adult-only case-control effects or a mixture of ages. This ambiguity makes the paper’s cross-disorder conclusions harder to interpret and reproduce. “The values for each map are z-scored effect sizes (Cohen's d) of cortical thickness in patient populations versus healthy controls.” This methods statement clarifies that the disorder maps are summary effect sizes but does not specify whether they were computed within an adult-only subset or pooled across ages in the contributing ENIGMA cohorts. Without explicit age-range documentation per disorder, it is impossible to reconcile the adult-only claim with the known design of several ENIGMA working-group analyses. Because the paper later correlates and regresses these maps against molecular and connectomic predictors, any age-related heterogeneity in the source effect-size maps can propagate into both the estimated “predictability” and the identified “epicentres.” Standard practice in large-scale ENIGMA morphometry work is to stratify or model age effects when the sample spans development, precisely because age modifies case-control effects. The absence of such information here prevents an informed assessment of whether age is a confound or a controlled design feature. This weakens confidence that the comparisons across disorders are on equal footing. “The sample comprised 1,571 patients with ASD and 1,651 healthy control subjects (age range, 2-64 years) from 49 participating sites.” [[2]](#ref2) This sentence from the ENIGMA ASD working-group abstract directly contradicts an adult-only characterization for at least one disorder category the paper includes (ASD). It indicates that the underlying ASD morphometry analysis spans early childhood through older adulthood, which makes “adult patients” an inaccurate shorthand unless the reviewed paper specifically selected an adult-only ASD derivative map. The reviewed manuscript does not explain any such selection, transformation, or filtering, so readers are left to assume the default ENIGMA map is used. If the ASD map includes ages 2–64, then its spatial pattern can reflect a mixture of developmental trajectories rather than a single adult phenotype. This is especially important because the reviewed paper interprets spatial patterning mechanistically (molecular vulnerability, epicentres), which could be age-contingent. Therefore, the adult-only claim materially affects the validity of the paper’s multi-disorder framework. “Analyses were performed separately for children, adolescents, and adults, using linear mixed-effects models adjusting for age, sex, and site (and intracranial volume for subcortical and surface area measures).” [[3]](#ref3) This sentence from an ENIGMA cross-disorder morphometry comparison (ADHD/ASD/OCD working groups) shows that ENIGMA analyses in these neurodevelopmental disorders explicitly treat age group as a key analytic axis rather than assuming adulthood. It implies that pooled analyses across age can mask or alter effects, which is precisely why stratification is performed. The reviewed manuscript includes ADHD and ASD but does not report which age-stratified estimates (if any) were used to create its disorder maps. If the authors used pooled maps, then their own adult-only assertion is inconsistent with typical ENIGMA practice and potentially incorrect. If they used adult-only maps, the omission of this detail is still a major reproducibility gap because it determines which ENIGMA outputs were analyzed. In either case, the paper’s central “single multi-disorder framework” is undermined by unclear age composition for key disorders. References: [2] [van Rooij, D., Anagnostou, E., Arango, C., et al. Cortical and Subcortical Brain Morphometry Differences Between Patients With Autism Spectrum Disorder and Healthy Individuals Across the Lifespan: Results From the ENIGMA ASD Working Group. *Am J Psychiatry* (2018).](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29145754/) [3] [Boedhoe, P. S. W., van Rooij, D., Hoogman, M., et al. Subcortical Brain Volume, Regional Cortical Thickness, and Cortical Surface Area Across Disorders: Findings From the ENIGMA ADHD, ASD, and OCD Working Groups. *Am J Psychiatry* (2020).](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32539527/)

This paper reports a 1,000-iteration distance-dependent cross-validation protocol for validating multilinear models, but the accompanying code implements a different procedure (deterministic leave-one-node-as-source splitting), which undermines the reliability of the reported generalization results and the conclusions drawn from model performance comparisons. “Briefly, for each of 1 000 iterations, the 75% of regions closest in Euclidean distance to a randomly chosen source node were selected as the training set, and the remaining 25% of regions as the test set.” This sentence describes a stochastic validation scheme with 1,000 random source nodes, which would yield a large distribution of train/test splits and therefore a stable estimate of generalization performance. Because this paper uses cross-validation to support claims that certain predictor families (e.g., receptor profiles) best “predict” disorder maps, the exact validation protocol materially affects conclusions. If the procedure is implemented differently, the reported cross-validation distributions (e.g., medians and quartiles in Fig. S3) may not correspond to what the code actually computes. The mismatch also prevents readers from reproducing the reported validation statistics by running the shared scripts. In structured spatial data, small implementation changes can meaningfully alter estimated performance because nearby parcels are statistically dependent. Therefore, a discrepancy between Methods and code is a significant methodology and reproducibility problem. ```python def cv_slr_distance_dependent(X, y, coords, train_pct=.75, metric='rsq'): P = squareform(pdist(coords, metric="euclidean")) train_metric = [] test_metric = [] for i in range(len(y)): distances = P[i, :] # for every node idx = np.argsort(distances) train_idx = idx[:int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords)))] test_idx = idx[int(np.floor(train_pct * len(coords))):] ... return train_metric, test_metric ``` This function iterates over `for i in range(len(y))`, meaning the number of splits equals the number of brain regions (68 here), not 1,000 random iterations as stated in the paper. Because each region is used once as the “source node,” the procedure is deterministic given the coordinates; there is no random sampling of source nodes and no 1,000-iteration Monte Carlo estimate. That difference changes the interpretation of variability in cross-validated performance (it becomes “across source nodes” rather than “across repeated random splits”). It also means the paper’s stated computational procedure and the released implementation are not the same, which harms reproducibility. Given the small sample size (68 parcels), the difference between 68 and 1,000 splits is not superficial: it affects the stability of reported summary statistics and any narrative about robustness. As a result, the cross-validation evidence supporting key performance comparisons is not reliably documented. “Ecological data often show temporal, spatial, hierarchical (random effects), or phylogenetic structure. Modern statistical approaches are increasingly accounting for such dependencies. However, when performing cross-validation, these structures are regularly ignored, resulting in serious underestimation of predictive error.” [[1]](#ref1) This passage explains why validation procedures must be carefully aligned with the dependence structure of the data—precisely the situation for cortical maps with strong spatial autocorrelation. The reviewed paper’s cross-validation is intended to mitigate spatial dependence via distance-based splitting, but a misdescribed or mis-implemented scheme can easily yield overly optimistic performance estimates. Because the paper’s headline claims rely on comparing predictive strength across different feature families, underestimating predictive error can lead to overconfident conclusions about which predictors “best” explain disease patterns. The Roberts et al. discussion is general (ecological data) but directly applicable to spatially structured brain parcellation data where neighboring parcels are dependent. It underscores that the details of the splitting procedure are not cosmetic; they determine whether the validation meaningfully tests extrapolation across space. Therefore, the Methods–code mismatch is a significant issue rather than a minor documentation error. “the reported statistical significance of these correlation is in some cases greatly inflated by failing to take into consideration the spatial non- independence of brain maps.” [[5]](#ref5) This statement highlights a core risk in brain-map analyses: naive correlation-based evaluation can produce inflated inferences when spatial dependence is not properly handled. In the reviewed work, cross-validation is a key safeguard intended to demonstrate that regression fits are not merely exploiting spatial smoothness. If the cross-validation protocol is misreported relative to what was executed, then the paper’s evidence that it has controlled dependence-driven optimism becomes weaker. Even when spin tests are used for some p-values, validation metrics (train/test correlations) can still be biased if the splitting scheme does not enforce the intended independence. This makes an accurate, code-matched description of cross-validation essential to the paper’s methodological credibility. The cited literature supports the principle that spatial non-independence can inflate correlation-based assessments, increasing the stakes of any CV mismatch. Consequently, the discrepancy threatens the robustness of the paper’s performance-based conclusions. References: [1] [Roberts, D. R., Bahn, V., Ciuti, S., et al. Cross-validation strategies for data with temporal, spatial, hierarchical, or phylogenetic structure. *Ecography* (2017).](https://www.wsl.ch/lud/biodiversity_events/papers/Roberts_et_al-2017-Ecography.pdf) [5] [Alexander-Bloch, A., Shou, H., Liu, S., et al. On testing for spatial correspondence between maps of human brain structure and function. *NeuroImage* (2018).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6095687/)

This paper’s description of the connectomic predictor set is internally inconsistent (claiming nine predictors while enumerating seven), and it also contains conflicting parcellation statements for at least one molecular predictor; these inconsistencies materially reduce methodological transparency and hinder exact reproduction of the regression and dominance analyses. “A total of nine global connectome predictors were used in the multilinear model to represent the influence that global connectivity has on disorder-specific cortical morphology.” This sentence specifies the dimensionality of the connectomic feature set, which is essential for reproducing regression fits, dominance analysis (which scales with 2^p − 1 submodels), and adjusted R^2 values. However, the paper’s subsequent list of connectomic predictors in the same section contains only seven distinct measures, creating a direct inconsistency in the core model specification. Because dominance analysis partitions model fit across predictors, the number of predictors is not a minor detail; it changes the combinatorics of submodels and the meaning of “dominance.” It also affects adjusted R^2, which explicitly depends on p through the degrees-of-freedom correction. Without knowing whether p=7 or p=9 was used, a reader cannot re-run the analysis and expect agreement with reported dominance patterns. This undermines transparency and reproducibility of one of the paper’s main analytic pillars. “Connectivity predictors: strength = sum of weighted connections; betweenness = fraction of all shortest paths traversing region i; closeness = mean shortest path length between region i and all other regions; Euclidean distance = mean Euclidean distance between region i and all other regions; participation coefficient = diversity of connections from region i to the seven Yeo-Krienen resting-state networks [130]; clustering = fraction of triangles including region i; mean first passage time = average time for a random walker to travel from region i to any other region in the network.” This figure caption enumerates exactly seven connectivity predictors, which conflicts with the Methods claim of “nine” predictors. The discrepancy is problematic because readers rely on such lists to understand which aspects of network embedding were tested and which were not. It also impacts interpretability: if additional predictors were included but not described, then the narrative about “centrality” being less dominant than “number of connections” may omit relevant variables. Conversely, if only these seven were used, then the “nine” count is simply incorrect and signals unreliable method reporting. In either case, the mismatch reduces confidence in the precision of the model specification presented to the reader. Given that the paper compares “molecular” versus “connectomic” predictability, miscounting features can also bias perceived fairness of that comparison. ```python # disorder maps + predictors ct = np.genfromtxt(path+'data/enigma_ct.csv', delimiter=',') bio = np.genfromtxt(path+'data/local_biol_predictors.csv', delimiter=',') conn = np.genfromtxt(path+'data/' + dataset + '/global_conn_predictors.csv', delimiter=',') ... bio_predictor_names = np.load(path+'data/bio_predictor_names.npy') conn_predictor_names = np.load(path+'data/conn_predictor_names.npy') ... predictors = [bio, conn, conn_bin, conn_fc, np.concatenate((bio, conn), axis=1)] predictor_names = [bio_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names, np.concatenate((bio_predictor_names, conn_predictor_names))] ``` The released analysis script operationalizes the connectomic predictor set via a single matrix `conn` and a corresponding `conn_predictor_names` array, and then uses this set repeatedly across the main and supplementary variants. This code structure implies that the analysis hinges on a fixed, explicitly enumerated set of connectomic variables, so any mismatch in the manuscript’s stated number of predictors directly affects reproducibility. The script does not document any hidden additional connectomic variables that would reconcile “nine” with “seven,” suggesting the manuscript may contain an incorrect count. Since dominance analysis and cross-validation are executed in loops over these matrices, the predictor dimensionality is baked into the computations. Therefore, inconsistency between the written Methods and the implementation is consequential for anyone attempting to replicate results from the provided codebase. This evidence supports the criticism that the methodological reporting is not sufficiently precise for exact reproduction. “All analyses were conducted using the 68-region Desikan Killiany parcellation [19, 29], as this is the native and only available representation of ENIGMA datasets.” This sentence asserts a single parcellation basis for all analyses, which is important because both predictors and outcome maps must be aligned region-by-region for regression. Yet the Methods later describe at least one predictor as being “parcel-lated into 68 cortical regions according to the Lausanne anatomical atlas [19],” which is a different naming/atlas convention within the same manuscript. If this is not merely a wording/OCR issue, it implies a potential atlas mismatch or an undocumented mapping step between parcellations. Such mapping can alter spatial smoothness and correlations, affecting regression and dominance results. Even if the actual underlying processing used a consistent 68-region atlas, the manuscript’s inconsistent descriptions prevent readers from knowing what was done. This ambiguity is significant because parcellation alignment is a core prerequisite for validity of the statistical comparisons.

This paper overgeneralizes its “cross-disorder epicentre” conclusion in the abstract and narrative, because epicentre likelihood is explicitly computed only for the subset of disorders that pass a network-spreading significance filter and is then averaged—so the reported epicentres are not established for all thirteen disorders. “Finally, we find that cross-disorder abnormalities are consistently subtended by a small subset of network epicentres in bilateral sensory-motor, medial temporal lobe, precuneus, and superior parietal cortex.” This sentence frames the epicentre result as a general property of “cross-disorder abnormalities,” which a reader would reasonably interpret as applying across the full set of 13 disorders analyzed in the paper. However, the Methods/Results later qualify that epicentre likelihood is only computed for disorders that exhibit significant node–neighbour correlations, meaning the epicentre map is conditional on a selection criterion. As a result, the abstract’s phrasing risks overstating the scope of the finding and conflating “cross-disorder” with “across the selected network-spreading subset.” This matters because epicentres are used to support mechanistic claims about network-mediated amplification of pathology, and overgeneralizing them could mislead readers about universality across psychiatric, neurodevelopmental, and neurodegenerative conditions. The paper does not present an epicentre analysis for non-network-spreading disorders or quantify uncertainty about the epicentre locations when the inclusion criterion changes. Therefore, the conclusion as written is stronger than what the implemented analysis supports. “Epicentre likelihood was only calculated for brain maps with significant correlation between their node and neighbour abnormality (network spreading disorders). This list comprised of: 22q11.1 deletion syndrome, ADHD, ASD, right and left temporal epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and schizotypy (Fig. S7). We averaged epicentre likelihood of these seven disorders to produce a map of epicentre likelihood across disorders that demonstrate network spreading disorder-specific cortical morphology (Fig. 4c, right).” This passage explicitly defines the epicentre analysis as conditional on a statistical filter and lists only seven included disorders, which directly conflicts with a blanket “cross-disorder” framing across all thirteen disorders. Because the selection criterion is based on a significance test, the subset is not fixed a priori and is sensitive to methodological choices (e.g., null model, connectivity matrix variant), which can affect the resulting averaged epicentre map. Averaging only over this subset also means that disorders without significant network spreading do not contribute to the reported epicentre locations, so the result cannot be interpreted as a pan-disorder epicentre map. This is a substantial limitation because the paper studies disorders with different etiologies and expected propagation mechanisms, and the subset criterion implicitly favors conditions whose maps align with the chosen network-spreading metric. The manuscript does not present alternative summaries (e.g., per-disorder epicentres across all 13, or a weighted analysis accounting for uncertainty). Thus, the strong abstract claim is not fully supported by the described analysis. ```python idx = np.where(pspin['fc-also'] < 0.05)[0] if 4 and 5 in idx: # both epilepsies epilepsy_mean = np.mean(epi[:, [4, 5]], axis=1).reshape(-1, 1) idx = np.delete(idx, (idx == 4) | (idx == 5)) epi_subset = np.concatenate((epi[:, idx], epilepsy_mean), axis=1) # epicentre likelihood (mean) epi_summary = np.mean(epi_subset, axis=1) ``` The code implements the subset-selection described in the paper by selecting disorders with `pspin['fc-also'] < 0.05` and then computing epicentre summaries only over `epi_subset`. This confirms that the reported “cross-disorder” epicentre map is not computed over all disorders but only over those meeting the network-spreading criterion, with additional ad hoc handling of epilepsy subtypes. Because the subset is determined after significance testing, the resulting epicentre map is subject to selection effects and cannot be interpreted as an unconditional summary across the 13 disorders. The extra step of collapsing two epilepsy maps also changes the effective weighting of disorders in the mean, which should be explicitly reflected in any general claim about epicentres. While such choices may be defensible, they need to be matched by appropriately scoped language in the conclusions. As written, the abstract statement is not aligned with the conditional and selective procedure actually used.

This paper’s connectomic vulnerability and network-spreading inferences are weakened because the structural connectome used to compute all connectivity predictors and neighbour-weighted measures is derived from a small sample of healthy young adults, while the analyzed disorders span conditions with markedly different age distributions and known age-related changes in structural connectivity. “Structural and functional data were collected ... on \( n = 70 \) healthy young adults (16 females, \( 25.3 \pm 4.9 \) years).” This sentence makes clear that all connectomic predictors and network-spreading computations are anchored to a single connectome estimate from young adults. Yet the paper’s disorder set includes conditions with typical onset and progression well outside this age range (e.g., Parkinson’s disease) as well as neurodevelopmental disorders, meaning the normative connectome used for “vulnerability” does not match the life-stage of the patient populations represented in the ENIGMA effect-size maps. Because graph metrics (strength, centrality, participation, etc.) depend on the underlying connectivity matrix, age-related differences in structural connectivity can alter which regions are labeled as hubs or epicentres. The paper acknowledges in general terms that predictors do not capture lifespan changes, but it still draws broad conclusions about connectome architecture interacting with local vulnerability across disorders. Without demonstrating robustness to alternative connectomes (e.g., older-adult connectomes, age-stratified connectomes, or disorder-relevant cohorts), the connectomic conclusions risk being artifacts of the chosen young-adult reference network. This is a substantive limitation because it affects not just effect size but the spatial ranking of key regions. Therefore, the connectomic vulnerability claims are not as robust as the manuscript’s general framing suggests. “Third, both local biological and global connectivity predictors are derived from state-of-the-art open-access datasets in healthy participants, but they do not capture individual variability or changes across the lifespan—both of which are key factors in neurological, psychiatric, and neurodevelopmental disorders.” This limitation statement correctly identifies a central threat to validity: the predictors are fixed, healthy reference maps that ignore both individual variability and developmental/aging effects. However, the main text still interprets correlations with these predictors as reflecting mechanisms of “vulnerability” and uses the same young-adult connectome to define neighbour exposure and epicentres. The fact that the authors flag the issue does not resolve it, because no analysis is provided to quantify how sensitive results are to using different age-matched connectomes or to plausible lifespan-related changes in network topology. In many disorders, both connectivity and molecular features change with age, treatment, and disease stage, so a young-adult baseline may systematically mischaracterize where network exposure is highest. This limitation thus directly impacts the strength and generality of the paper’s conclusions, not merely their scope. A robust approach would include sensitivity analyses or stratified reference networks. “Results revealed more pronounced age-related decreases in structural connectivity for sensorimotor, ventral attention, and sub-cortical networks ...” [[6]](#ref6) This sentence provides direct evidence that structural connectivity exhibits systematic age-related decreases across the lifespan, affecting multiple networks—including sensorimotor systems that the reviewed paper later highlights as epicentres. If structural connectivity changes with age, then graph metrics and neighbour-weighted exposure estimates computed on a young-adult connectome may not transfer to older adult populations or to disorders with different age distributions. This is particularly relevant because the reviewed paper interprets network spreading and epicentre likelihood as reflecting connectome architecture’s role in shaping disorder patterns. The cited study indicates that the underlying structural connectivity substrate is not stationary across adulthood, challenging the assumption that a single young-adult connectome provides a universally valid “vulnerability” scaffold. Consequently, the paper’s connectomic inferences could be biased toward young-adult hub structure rather than disorder-relevant network organization. This evidence supports the criticism that lifespan mismatch is a significant methodological and interpretive issue. “The original Human Connectome Project yielded a rich data set on structural and functional connectivity in a large sample of healthy young adults ... This dataset will provide a unique resource for examining how brain organization and connectivity changes across typical aging ...” [[4]](#ref4) This passage underscores two points relevant to the reviewed paper’s design choices: (i) high-quality connectome datasets are often young-adult–centered, and (ii) connectivity is expected to change across typical aging sufficiently to motivate dedicated aging cohorts. The reviewed paper uses a young-adult connectome as a fixed scaffold to interpret multiple disorders, but the cited overview highlights that extending connectome analyses across age is a primary research goal precisely because the connectome evolves. This supports the argument that applying a young-adult connectome indiscriminately to disorders spanning the lifespan requires explicit justification and robustness checks. In particular, disorders like Parkinson’s disease occur in older populations where age-related connectivity differences are nontrivial, potentially shifting which regions are central or well-connected. Without accounting for these changes, the reviewed paper’s connectomic vulnerability claims may not generalize to the populations that generate the disorder maps. Thus, the external literature reinforces that lifespan mismatch is a substantial concern for validity. References: [4] [Bookheimer, S. Y., Salat, D. H., Terpstra, M., et al. The Lifespan Human Connectome Project in Aging: An overview. *NeuroImage* (2019).](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6649668/) [6] [Merenstein, A., Song, S., et al. High-resolution structural connectivity mediates age-related differences in functional connectivity and fluid cognition. *Brain Communications* (2025).](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41113674/)

The paper overgeneralizes the findings on "network spreading" and "epicentres" to "cross-disorder abnormalities" in general, despite the analysis showing significance for only a subset of disorders (7 out of 13). "Finally, we find that cross-disorder abnormalities are consistently subtended by a small subset of network epicentres in bilateral sensory-motor, medial temporal lobe, precuneus, and superior parietal cortex." This statement in the abstract implies a universal finding across the studied disorders. However, the results section specifies that "mean epicentre likelihood was calculated for all seven disorders that show a significant correlation between regional and neighbour abnormality". My re-analysis confirms that "network spreading" (correlation between node and neighbour abnormality) is statistically significant ($p < 0.05$) for only 7 disorders (22q, ADHD, ASD, Epilepsy RTLE, Epilepsy LTLE, Bipolar, Schizotypy). Major disorders such as Schizophrenia ($p = 0.13$), Depression ($p = 0.07$), and OCD ($p = 0.08$) are excluded from this "consistent" finding. Presenting this as a general "cross-disorder" phenomenon without explicitly stating the exclusion of nearly half the disorders (including major ones) in the abstract is misleading.

The paper infers "network spreading" and identifies "epicentres" based solely on static cross-sectional correlations between regional abnormality and neighbour abnormality, which is insufficient to establish a dynamic spreading mechanism. "A disorder whose cortical morphology demonstrates network spreading was defined as one whose regional abnormality pattern is correlated to mean neighbour abnormality, weighted by structural connectivity and functional connectivity." A high correlation between a region's atrophy and its neighbours' atrophy (spatial autocorrelation on the network) can arise from shared vulnerability (e.g., spatially correlated gene expression or shared developmental inputs) without any dynamic spreading process [1]. Inferring a "spreading" mechanism and using terms like "epicentre" implies a causal, dynamic process that propagates pathology from a source. This cannot be rigorously established from static cross-sectional data alone, without longitudinal tracking or fitting a dynamic diffusion model [2]. The paper acknowledges "interaction" but the terminology strongly suggests a specific dynamic mechanism that is not directly tested. References: [1] Fornito, A., Zalesky, A., & Breakspear, M. (2015). The connectomics of brain disorders. *Nature Reviews Neuroscience*, 16(3), 159-172. https://www.nature.com/articles/nrn3901 [2] Raj, A., Kuceyeski, A., & Weiner, M. (2012). A network diffusion model of progression predicts longitudinal patterns of atrophy and metabolism in Alzheimer's disease. *Cell Systems*, 73(6), 1204-1215. https://www.cell.com/neuron/fulltext/S0896-6273(12)00976-3

The use of healthy adult connectomes and molecular maps to predict abnormalities in developmental disorders (e.g., ASD, ADHD) assumes a normative architecture that may not exist in these patients, ignoring disease-specific alterations. "...we defined the connectivity fingerprint of a region by calculating ... of a weighted structural connectivity matrix from 70 healthy adults..." The study uses a "normative" connectome from healthy adults to define vulnerability for all disorders, including neurodevelopmental ones like ASD and ADHD. However, literature suggests that these disorders are characterized by fundamental alterations in the structural connectome itself [3, 4]. By assuming that the "vulnerability" is defined by the healthy architecture, the study ignores the possibility that the disease-specific connectome architecture is the primary driver of the abnormalities. This is a significant limitation for the developmental disorders included in the study, as the "healthy" vulnerability map may not reflect the actual network constraints present in these patients.

Lines 172-175: The results indicate that the PM2.5-induced oxidative stress (OS) potency could mainly be attributed to PM2.5-bound toxic metals. Calculating PM2.5-induced OS potency for selected metals and PAHs is based on reference models, which can be found in the study by Jin et al. (2019). That study provided EC1.5 and REP for valence metals. However, ICP-MS does not analyze metal valence. The authors should clarify the OS potency concentrations from metals and make assumptions if necessary. In addition, the calculations of OP are not transparent the authors only presented references.
Have any prior experimental studies investigated the toxic effects of industrial PM2.5? It’s necessary to compare the findings with earlier research.

Line 62-63 “while many of the major PM components, including inorganic sulfate and nitrate, are commonly considered innocuous species17.”, the author should double check the sentence. The reference is out of date and there are many epidemiology studies found the toxic of inorganic sulfate. Modifying the acidity and alkalinity levels could potentially impact the toxicity of metals present in PM2.5.

Line 140-142 “Here, the targeted chemicals are focused on metals and PAHs, which are commonly regarded as key toxic components associated with the biological effects of PM2.5.”, please explain why black carbon or elemental carbon is not a targeted chemicals in this studies?
It is recommended to include the proportion of certain key constituents, such as inorganic sulfate and nitrate, in Supplementary Table 4.
What methodology did the author employ to compute the information presented in Supplementary Figure 7a?

While I agree with the authors that the toxicity of particulate matter varies with source, the sampling collection and handling methods used in this work are not appropriate for the toxicity tests they perform. The sampling methods documented in the supplementary materials fails to collect the condensible particulate matter, which is where much of the most toxic components result in the ambient air. What is required is a dilution sampling system to allow the reaction and condensation closer to what happens once the particles, vapors, and gases are released into the atmosphere. One appropriate method is described here: England GC, Watson JG, Chow JC, Zielinska B, Chang MC, Loos KR, Hidy GM. Dilution-based emissions sampling from stationary sources: Part 1--Compact sampler methodology and performance. J Air Waste Manag Assoc. 2007 Jan;57(1):65-78. doi: 10.1080/10473289.2007.10465291. PMID: 17269232.

A second problem with the methods is that no mention is made of post collection sample handing to protect the sample acidity from neutralization. A proper storage would place samples collected into containers with citric acid coated material to prevent neutralization of acids, such as sulfuric acid, until use in the toxicological tests, also under ammonia free conditions. This is especially important in fossil fuel combustion particulate matter, high in acidic sulfur, which is known to activate transition metals by making them bioavailable (e.g., see: Fang, T.; Guo, H.; Zeng, L.; Verma, V.; Nenes, A.; Weber, R.J. Highly Acidic Ambient Particles, Soluble Metals, and Oxidative Potential: A Link between Sulfate and Aerosol Toxicity. Environmental Science & Technology 2017, 51, 2611-2620, doi:10.1021/acs.est.6b06151).

The role of sulfates is dismissed as relatively non-toxic by the authors in their discussion, but this is not a full consideration of its role. In isolation, it is true that its toxicity is low, but in combination with metals (as always occurs in the real world) acidic sulfur has an important role, as indicated by the epidemiological evidence, which the authors fail to acknowledge. For example, see: Weichenthal S, Lavigne E, raub A, Umbrio D, You H, Pollitt K, Shin T, Kulka R, Stieb DM, Korsiaik J, Jessiman B, JBrook JR, Hatzopoulou M, Evans G, Burnett RT, Association of Sulfur, Transition Metals, and the Oxidative Potential of Outdoor PM2.5 with Acute Cardiovascular Events: A Case-Crossover Study of Canadian Adults. Environmental Health Perspectives. Environ Health Perspect 129(10). (2021). doi: 30 10.1289/EHP9449.

The study uses methanol-water sonication extraction to prepare PM₂.₅ samples for toxicity testing, but this methodology can significantly alter the chemical composition and measured toxicity of samples compared to ambient PM₂.₅. This extraction-method dependence, documented in the literature and even in a paper cited by the authors (Roper et al. 2019), raises concerns about whether the observed toxicity differences between industrial sectors reflect true differences in PM₂.₅ composition or artifacts of the extraction procedure interacting differently with sector-specific PM₂.₅. "The PM₂.₅ filter samples were sonicated in methanol and ultra-purified water, and the obtained PM₂.₅ extracts were then dried under purified nitrogen." The paper describes their extraction method but does not discuss how this choice may affect their results or compare their method to alternatives. Given that different industrial PM₂.₅ sources have substantially different chemical compositions (e.g., ISI PM₂.₅ has much higher metal content), the extraction method may recover different fractions of toxic constituents from each source type, potentially biasing the inter-sector comparisons. * Quote from Roper et al. (2019), cited as reference 41 in the paper: "Significant differences were observed between extraction methods for concentrations of elements and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) of the PM2.5 tested following its preparation for biological response studies... The impact of these chemical differences on sensitive endpoints of zebrafish development was investigated. Significant differences in the percent incidence and timing of mortality were associated with PM2.5 extraction method." [[3]](#citation-3) This paper, which the authors cite, explicitly demonstrates that extraction method significantly affects both chemical recovery and measured toxicity of PM₂.₅ samples. Notably, the study found that water and methanol extraction methods showed substantially different element recovery profiles compared to organic solvent extractions. Since ISI PM₂.₅ is metal-rich while power/cement PM₂.₅ has different compositions, the methanol-water extraction may preferentially recover metal species, potentially amplifying the apparent toxicity difference of ISI PM₂.₅. * Quote from Roper et al. (2019): "For total elements detected in the PM2.5 extracts... the order of extraction method yield from highest to lowest concentration was: water (5.90 ± 0.023), methanol (4.94 ± 0.020), DCM (1.41 ± 0.016)... The chemical profiles resulting from the different extraction methods in this study all had an elevated contribution of hydroxy-PAHs compared to the Ambient SOP Sample." [[3]](#citation-3) The literature shows that extraction solvent choice dramatically affects element recovery (up to 18-fold differences between methods) and alters PAH profiles. Since the paper under review found that toxic metals contributed approximately 84% of the OS potency in ISI PM₂.₅, the extraction method's efficiency in recovering metals is critical. The authors do not discuss how their chosen method may interact with the distinct chemical profiles of different industrial sources. References: [3] Roper, C. et al. (2019). PM2.5 Filter Extraction Methods: Implications for Chemical and Toxicological Analyses. *Environmental Science & Technology* 53(1), 434-442. https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6652177/

This paper defines toxic units (TUs) as the reciprocal of sample-level EC\_1.5/IC\_20 endpoints but then aggregates provincial TUs as the reciprocal of an emission-weighted average of EC\_1.5/IC\_20, a non-equivalent transformation that can substantially bias toxic equivalent values and therefore the sector-ranking and policy conclusions derived from toxic potency-adjusted exposure (Supplementary Note 9). Toxic units (TUs), including estimation of PM_{2.5}-induced OS and CT potency values, can be estimated as follows: TUOS_{j,s} = \frac{1}{(\mathrm{EC}_{1.5})_{j,s}} and TUCT_{j,s} = \frac{1}{(\mathrm{IC}_{20})_{j,s}}, where j and s denote sample (j) and industry source (s), respectively, EC_{1.5} is the endpoint of the PM_{2.5}-induced OS potency, indicating the concentration causing a 1.5-fold increase in intracellular ROS formation relative to that in the control group, IC_{20} is the endpoint of the PM_{2.5}-induced CT potency, indicating the concentration causing 20% inhibition of the cell viability relative to that in the control group. This passage unambiguously defines TU as an inverse mapping from an endpoint concentration (EC\_1.5 or IC\_20) to a “toxic unit.” Once TU is defined as 1/EC or 1/IC, any aggregation across processes or samples that aims to represent a mixture or a sector should be done in TU-space (i.e., by combining 1/EC values with appropriate weights). Otherwise, the aggregation no longer corresponds to the stated definition of TU and will systematically deviate whenever EC/IC varies across components. Because the paper’s central premise is “unequal toxicities,” substantial variation is expected, making this discrepancy practically important rather than a negligible algebraic detail. The rest of the analysis (toxic equivalents, TPAE, and control rankings) depends on these aggregated toxicity metrics, so any bias here propagates directly into headline results. The average provincial TUs of industrial PM_{2.5} emissions can be estimated as follows: TUOS_{i,s} = \frac{1}{\sum (\mathrm{EC}_{1.5})_{j,s} \times \mathrm{WF}_{j,s}} and TUCT_{i,s} = \frac{1}{\sum (\mathrm{IC}_{20})_{j,s} \times \mathrm{WF}_{j,s}}, where WF is the weighing factor, estimated based on the mass fraction of PM_{2.5} emissions of each individual manufacturing process (or each individual fuel-fired unit) to the total PM_{2.5} emission amount of all manufacturing processes (or all fuel-fired units). This formula computes TU as the reciprocal of a weighted *arithmetic mean* of EC\_1.5/IC\_20, which is generally not equal to the weighted mean of the reciprocals (the TU values) implied by the earlier TU definition. The difference can be large under heterogeneity (e.g., mixing a highly potent component with a weakly potent one), exactly the regime the paper emphasizes. Because the weighting factor WF is emission-mass-based, the natural toxicity-weighted mixture metric—consistent with a linear “per mass” potency framing—would be Σ WF·(1/EC) rather than 1/Σ WF·EC. The paper does not justify why the nonlinear reciprocal-of-mean is the correct mixture rule for toxicity units, nor does it provide a sensitivity analysis showing the ranking is invariant to this choice. As a result, the quantitative toxic equivalents and exposure-risk reductions can be artifacts of this aggregation choice rather than robust reflections of underlying toxicity measurements. TPAE<sub>OS</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> were estimated as TPAE<sub>OS</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × OS<sub>i,s</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × CT<sub>i,s</sub>, respectively, where OS<sub>i,s</sub> and CT<sub>i,s</sub> are the toxic equivalent values of the PM<sub>2.5</sub>-induced OS and CT potency, respectively, originating from each industry sector (s) in each geographic unit (i). This shows that the toxic equivalent values (OS\_{i,s}, CT\_{i,s}) are direct multiplicative factors in the paper’s population-relevant risk index (TPAE). Therefore, any bias in TU aggregation that feeds into OS/CT equivalents will proportionally bias TPAE and any reported “times higher” reductions. Because the core policy recommendation is based on relative reductions in population-weighted TPAE under alternative sector priorities, even moderate systematic distortion of OS/CT equivalents can flip or exaggerate the comparison. The formulation also implies a linear scaling of “risk” with these equivalence factors, so the exact construction of OS/CT equivalents is not a cosmetic methodological choice but a structural determinant of results. The paper provides no validation that its particular TU aggregation rule preserves ordering under realistic intra-sector variability, which undermines confidence in the downstream comparisons. The toxic equivalent (TEQ) is operationally defined by the sum of the products of the concentration of each congener multiplied by its TEF value. This statement describes the standard logic of toxicity-equivalency aggregation: overall mixture toxicity is represented as a *linear* sum of component contributions in the form Σ(concentration × equivalency factor), rather than by applying a nonlinear reciprocal transformation after averaging. While the paper’s pollutants and endpoints differ (industrial PM vs. dioxin congeners), the key methodological parallel is the mixture-combination principle used to build a single index from heterogeneous components. If the paper intends TUs/TEs to play an analogous “equivalency factor” role, a linear mass-weighted combination is the default and widely used approach in regulatory and risk-assessment settings. The paper’s reciprocal-of-weighted-mean construction deviates from this norm without justification, which is especially concerning because it can systematically downweight extreme toxicities. This mismatch strengthens the concern that the paper’s aggregation step is not only internally inconsistent but also out of line with established equivalency frameworks. [\[3\]](#ref3)

This paper makes strong policy claims about “health-oriented” control and “exposure risks” using toxic potency-adjusted exposure (TPAE) that is built from in vitro A549 oxidative-stress/cytotoxicity endpoints measured on extracted primary stack PM, without demonstrating that TPAE is a valid surrogate for real-world human health outcomes or that its assumed linear scaling supports sector-priority decisions. Compared with the current mass-based control strategy (prioritizing implementation of ultralow emission standards in the power sector), the proposed health-oriented control strategy (priority control of the ISI sector) could generate 5.4 times higher reduction in population-weighted toxic potency-adjusted PM<sub>2.5</sub> exposure among polluting industries in China. This sentence elevates “toxic potency-adjusted PM\_2.5 exposure” to the central outcome for comparing policies and motivates a sector-priority shift. However, “toxic potency-adjusted exposure” is not a standard epidemiologic effect metric (e.g., attributable mortality or morbidity), and the paper does not show how a given percentage reduction in TPAE maps to an expected reduction in health outcomes. Without such calibration, the “5.4 times higher reduction” is only meaningful within the authors’ constructed index, not necessarily for human health. The claim is especially consequential because it frames existing regulations as mis-prioritized and proposes an “urgently required” alternative; that level of prescription typically requires outcomes that are validated against human data or at least mechanistically linked through well-supported dose–response relationships. As written, the paper risks over-interpreting an index built from in vitro endpoints as if it were a health-impact function. Human epithelial A549 cell lines were adopted to examine PM<sub>2.5</sub>-related biological effects, including PM<sub>2.5</sub>-induced oxidative stress (OS) potency and cytotoxicity (CT) potency. This establishes that the toxicity signal driving the policy analysis is derived from a single in vitro cell line assay platform, rather than from in vivo or epidemiologic evidence. A549 assays are useful for mechanistic screening, but translating their dose–response endpoints to population-level exposure risk is nontrivial because real exposures involve complex deposition, clearance, metabolism, and systemic responses not represented in monoculture assays. The paper also uses solvent extraction (“sonicated in methanol and ultra-purified water”) before testing, which can change the bioavailable fraction of components relative to inhalation exposure and complicate interpretability across sources. Critically, the paper does not demonstrate that the relative ranking of source toxicities in A549 assays persists under more realistic exposure models or across multiple relevant endpoints (e.g., inflammation, cardiovascular pathways). Because TPAE is built from these endpoints, the absence of validation threatens the robustness of the downstream sector-priority recommendation. TPAE<sub>OS</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> were estimated as TPAE<sub>OS</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × OS<sub>i,s</sub> and TPAE<sub>CT</sub> = C<sub>i,s</sub> × CT<sub>i,s</sub>, respectively, where OS<sub>i,s</sub> and CT<sub>i,s</sub> are the toxic equivalent values of the PM<sub>2.5</sub>-induced OS and CT potency, respectively, originating from each industry sector (s) in each geographic unit (i). This construction implicitly assumes that “health-relevant risk” scales linearly with modeled concentration (C\_{i,s}) and a single scalar toxic-equivalence multiplier (OS\_{i,s} or CT\_{i,s}) derived from in vitro endpoints. In practice, health impact assessment typically requires an exposure–response function (often nonlinear and outcome-specific) and baseline risk information; a simple product index can be informative for ranking hypotheses but is not automatically a valid risk surrogate. The paper does not provide any empirical evidence that a unit change in OS-equivalent or CT-equivalent translates into proportional changes in morbidity/mortality, nor does it evaluate whether the chosen endpoints represent the dominant pathways of PM\_2.5 health effects. Because the policy comparisons and cost-effectiveness conclusions are made “per unit of toxic potency-adjusted mass,” any misspecification of this index can materially alter which sector appears most beneficial to control. The linear product form therefore requires external validation that is not shown in the manuscript. We point out here the potential dangers of exclusive use of an in vitro screening approach in view of experience with other particles, suggesting that particles can cause a rather narrow range of effects on cells in culture which does not reflect the range of different pathogenic effects they cause in vivo. This review directly cautions against using in vitro oxidative-stress-type screens as a stand-alone basis for hazard ranking across particle types, because similar cellular oxidative stress responses can correspond to very different in vivo pathologies. That warning applies to this paper’s strategy of using A549-based OS/CT potency endpoints as the core toxicity input for nationwide policy ranking. Even if the assays are executed carefully, the quote highlights that the mapping from in vitro response to in vivo disease outcomes can be weak or ambiguous without supporting toxicokinetic and in vivo evidence. The paper’s central claim is not merely mechanistic (that industrial PM differs in potency), but prescriptive (that control priorities should change to protect health), which requires stronger validation than a screening endpoint alone. The external critique therefore strengthens the concern that the paper’s “health-oriented” index may not reliably predict actual health benefits from sector-specific controls. [\[1\]](#ref1) Health impact assessments typically use a single log-linear CR function per health outcome to estimate counts of avoided human health effects resulting from air quality improvements. This statement summarizes a widely used practice in policy-relevant health impact assessment: converting exposure reductions into avoided health outcomes through concentration–response (CR) functions grounded in epidemiology. In contrast, the paper’s TPAE does not specify a CR function relating its toxicity-weighted exposure to concrete outcomes (e.g., mortality), and therefore cannot be directly interpreted as “health effects” avoided. Because the paper claims “cost-effective reductions in particulate exposure risks,” readers would reasonably expect an outcome-linked risk model or at least a demonstration that TPAE is predictive of outcome changes. The absence of such linkage means that the comparative effectiveness ratios (e.g., “5.4 times higher reduction”) may not correspond to proportional differences in avoided disease burden. This discrepancy undermines the robustness of the conclusions for real-world health-oriented regulation, even if TPAE is a useful exploratory index for hypothesis generation. [\[5\]](#ref5)

This paper’s sector-contribution estimates and “health-oriented” control prioritization are potentially biased because they explicitly omit source-specific secondary aerosol formation and fugitive emissions from key industrial processes and, in attribution scenarios, remove only primary PM<sub>2.5</sub> emissions; this can materially change both exposure burdens and the ranking of which sector should be prioritized. Source-specific secondary and fugitive aerosols emission from manufacturing iron/steel and cement processes were not considered, which could result in underestimation of the adverse impacts associated with exposure to specific source-emitted aerosols. This admission directly affects the paper’s central comparison of sector priorities because excluding secondary and fugitive components changes both the magnitude and spatial distribution of exposure attributable to each sector. Industrial sectors differ substantially in precursor emissions (e.g., SO\_2, NO\_x, VOCs, NH\_3 interactions) and in fugitive dust contributions; omitting these components can therefore alter relative burdens, not just absolute totals. The claim that ISI should be prioritized over power based on toxic potency-adjusted exposure reductions depends on accurate sector-attributable concentrations, which are compromised if key aerosol pathways are missing. Importantly, the limitation is not a marginal refinement: secondary formation often dominates ambient PM\_2.5 composition in many regions, and industrial precursors are major drivers of that formation. Without quantifying the direction and magnitude of this omission, the policy ranking is not reliably supported. Emission inventories excluding primary PM<sub>2.5</sub> emissions from power, cement, and ISI sectors, as obtained with the baseline scenarios, were used to simulate the NoELE, NoCI, and NoISI scenarios, respectively. This sentence indicates that sector attribution is performed by “zeroing out” only primary PM\_2.5 emissions, not the gaseous precursors that form secondary PM\_2.5 attributable to the same sectors. As a result, the computed “ambient PM\_2.5 concentration attributable to the power, cement, and ISI sectors” will systematically miss secondary contributions from those sectors and can misrepresent sector responsibility for population exposure. This is particularly problematic for comparing sectors because power and heavy industry have distinct precursor profiles and stack heights, affecting downwind secondary production differently. The paper’s toxicity-weighting then multiplies these potentially incomplete concentrations by toxicity equivalents, compounding errors in both concentration and toxicity dimensions. Therefore, the attribution approach is not aligned with the paper’s stated goal of assessing health-relevant impacts of industrial emissions in the atmosphere. As for the population-weighted TPAE, we limited our uncertainty analysis to the emission inventory owing to the near-linear response of the ambient PM_{2.5} concentration to the primary PM_{2.5} emission. This sentence reflects an analytical framing in which primary PM\_2.5 emissions are treated as the dominant driver of the modeled concentration responses relevant for TPAE. However, secondary aerosol formation is explicitly acknowledged elsewhere as omitted, so assuming a near-linear response to primary PM\_2.5 is not a safe basis for concluding that ignoring these pathways does not affect rankings. Even within primary PM\_2.5, the relationship between emissions and population-weighted concentration can be nonlinear due to meteorology, plume rise, and chemistry affecting particle dynamics, which would be handled by the chemical transport model rather than assumed. By tying both attribution and uncertainty choices to primary PM\_2.5 only, the paper structurally limits its ability to support conclusions about comprehensive industrial PM\_2.5 exposure burdens. This weakens the validity of using the resulting TPAE reductions as the decisive basis for sector-priority policy. Secondary fine particles - Fine particles that form in complicated reactions in the atmosphere of chemicals, such as sulfur dioxides and nitrogen oxides, that are emitted from power plants, industries, and automobiles. This definition highlights that secondary fine particles arise from atmospheric reactions of emitted precursor gases and that power plants and industries are explicitly among the sources of those precursors. Therefore, a sector-attribution framework that removes only primary PM\_2.5 emissions will, by construction, omit an important class of PM\_2.5 that is causally attributable to the same sectors through precursor emissions. Because the paper’s objective is “health-oriented air pollution control,” omitting secondary PM\_2.5 undermines the completeness of exposure assessment and may skew which sectors appear most beneficial to control. The quote reinforces that secondary PM\_2.5 is not an exotic niche component but a recognized and policy-relevant pathway for how industrial activities create fine-particle exposures. Consequently, the paper’s conclusions about optimal sector prioritization are not adequately supported without incorporating (or bounding) secondary PM\_2.5 contributions. [\[2\]](#ref2)

The acquisition of crystal structures of protein-ligand complexes is a crucial aspect in the process of structure modification, as it provides essential binding information. Unfortunately, this study is deficient in the availability of pertinent data pertaining to crystal structures.
The directing binding of AVX420 with cPLA2α was not characterized.

In Scheme 1A, the mention of a "potential increase in plasma stability through the introduction of a methyl group" did not find support in the subsequent experimental findings. The compound AVX420, used for further studies, exhibits a mere addition of one oxygen atom compared to AVX235. Thus, additional exploration is required to obtain further insights into the optimization of the compound structure.

The authors explain the non-selectivity of compounds with or without α-methyl for GVIA iPLA2 and GV sPLA2 with the same description, "MINIMAL inhibition" (on page 8 line 8 and line 16), but note that there is a disparity in inhibition rates between these two classes of compounds (Table 1).
Further pathway experiments and in vivo and PK studies are required to validate the mechanism and ascertain the effects.

Johansen modified the structure of GK470, resulting in the improved activity of compound GK420. The related studies demonstrated that AVX420 induced the apoptosis in tumor cells through upregulation of intracellular oxidative stress response and transcriptional signaling pathways. However, this manuscript was deficient in the crucial animal experiments and pertinent preclinical study, such as pharmacokinetics and bioavailability. Hence, in my opinion, the manuscript did not meet the criteria for publication in Nature Communications.

The new compounds were tested against GV and GVIA recombinant PLA2s using a valid vitro assay, showing a relatively low/minimal level of inhibition for these structurally distinct PLA2s. Nevertheless, given the number of existing PLA2s enzymes in the superfamily it is still possible that the compound inhibits other PLA2 isoforms and even that the inhibition profile for the tested ones is different when used in cells and tissues. Moreover, the compound could inhibit other unrelated enzymes when used in vivo. I wonder if the authors have compared the effects of GK420 (on cellular eicosanoid release and on cancer cell viability/proliferation) with the effects of gene manipulation of cPLA2? Does depletion of cPLA2 result in similar alterations in cell viability, ROS production, eicosanoid generation? This would help strengthen the idea that the identified mechanism of action of the compound in cancer cells is dependent on its selective inhibition of cPLA2.

The authors have convincingly demonstrated that GK420 induces ROS production and cell death in a number of hematological cancer cell lines. They describe the phenotypic and genetic changes leading to cancer cell death. In principle, as the authors state, cPLA2 could promote cancer cell proliferation through the mitogenic action of eicosanoids, such as PGE2. But cPLA2 also has some other functions, not related to lipid mediator production. Does the mechanism of action of GK420 on cancer cells depend on its capacity to inhibit cPLA2-induced AA release coupled with lipid mediator generation and signalling?

Combining their own screening data with available datasets the authors found links between the action of cPLA2 and epigenetic regulation. I wonder if genetic manipulation of cPLA2 in some of the identified sensitive cell lines would lead to alterations of the epigenetic mechanisms as predicted by these in silico analyses? It would also be very interesting to see if this potential epigenetic control is linked with the cPLA2-AA signaling axis or perhaps with other cPLA2 functions not related to lipid mediator signalling.

Correlations between cPLA2 inhibitor activity and gene expression data suggested that cPLA2-induced cancer cell death is linked with the induction of oxidative stress and that the resistance to the inhibitor could be mediated by Nrf2 signaling. Have the authors tried to target Nrf2 in combination with cPLA2 genetic manipulation in representative resistant vs sensitive cell lines to confirm the suggested mechanism? Does cPLA2 inhibition induce ER stress and alter Nrf2 signaling in relevant cell models? Do these events precede the measured rise in oxidative stress? Similarly, an important added value to this work would be if the authors could connect the dots between cPLA2 and ATF4, PRC2/mTOR signaling by cellular mechanistic studies using genetic manipulation of cPLA2 and analyses of ATF4 and mTOR/PRC2 signaling at the protein level.

This paper makes mechanistic claims that cPLA2α inhibition (AVX420) triggers leukemia cell death through loss of mTOR/PRC2 (EZH2/H3K27me3)-dependent transcriptional repression and an ATF4-dependent stress program, but the presented evidence is largely correlational/computational and does not establish causality in the leukemia models. "Unsupervised hierarchical clustering of the drug correlations revealed an interesting similarity between the action of cPLA2α inhibitors and multiple histone methyltransferase inhibitors (**Fig. S3**), specifically drugs targeting EZH2, the catalytically active component of the polycomb group repressive complex 2 (PRC2). The finding suggests that cPLA2α may play a novel role in epigenetic silencing of transcription in cancer." This is a similarity-of-profiles argument (drug–drug correlation and clustering) rather than a direct measurement of PRC2/EZH2 activity, H3K27me3, or chromatin changes following AVX420. Such analyses can be useful for hypothesis generation, but they are not sufficient to claim that AVX420 functionally converges on PRC2 in the treated leukemia cells. The manuscript does not, in the main text, show biochemical readouts (e.g., EZH2 activity assays) or chromatin assays (e.g., H3K27me3 ChIP-seq) that would directly test the proposed epigenetic step. Without perturbation experiments (e.g., EZH2 inhibition/overexpression, PRC2 component knockdown, or resistant alleles) demonstrating necessity/sufficiency, the “suggests” framing is still used later to support a concrete mechanistic model. Because this mechanism is central to the paper’s “novel function” claim, the lack of causal evidence meaningfully weakens the validity of the mechanistic conclusions. "Given our findings showing a correlation between PRC2 activity and sensitivity to cPLA_{2α} inhibition, we instead propose that cPLA_{2α} could be important for maintaining H3K27 trimethylation- dependent transcriptional suppression of certain ATF4 target genes" This explicitly grounds the mechanism in correlation and a proposal, rather than direct demonstration that AVX420 decreases H3K27 trimethylation or derepresses PRC2 targets via PRC2 loss-of-function. The paper uses “PRC2 activity” as inferred from gene-set enrichment and overlaps, but does not show that PRC2 marks or PRC2 occupancy change at implicated loci in the AVX420-treated leukemia cells. The leap from enriched signatures to a specific chromatin mark mechanism is substantial, particularly because ATF4 targets can be induced through multiple upstream pathways unrelated to PRC2. The same section argues against canonical ISR translation control, but does not directly test alternative explanations (e.g., transcription factor activation downstream of ROS). As written, the mechanistic chain “cPLA2α → mTOR/PRC2 → H3K27me3 → ATF4-target derepression → death” remains speculative. That makes the paper’s mechanistic novelty claim less robust than standard causal practice in chemical biology. "AVX420 initiates an ATF4-dependent but ISR-independent transcriptional program to initiate cell death." The heading states ATF4-dependence, but in the surrounding results the primary support is that several induced genes are described as “established targets of ATF4.” Inferring transcription-factor dependence from target-gene lists is not equivalent to demonstrating dependence, especially when ROS can activate multiple stress-responsive regulators with overlapping target sets. The manuscript does not provide (in the visible Methods/Results) functional perturbations of ATF4 (e.g., knockdown/CRISPR or pharmacologic ISR modulation) showing that AVX420-induced death requires ATF4. Moreover, it does not show ATF4 protein induction, nuclear localization, or DNA binding changes that would support a direct ATF4 mechanism. As a result, the “ATF4-dependent” phrasing overstates what is demonstrated and risks misattributing the proximate death pathway. This is a conclusions issue because the ATF4/ISR framing is used to interpret transcriptomics and connect to the proposed epigenetic model. "Providing one chemical probe for a given protein is also insufficient. Each protein should be targeted by another equally well-characterized ‘orthogonal’ chemical probe having a completely different chemical structure which would reduce the probability of having common off-targets." ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4706458/)) The paper’s central mechanistic claims rely heavily on inhibitor-based phenotypes and on correlations across three inhibitors, but it does not demonstrate that the key downstream epigenetic/ATF4 conclusions hold under orthogonal target validation strategies. While AVX002 is a different chemical class, the manuscript still does not establish that the PRC2/EZH2-linked transcriptional effects and ROS-dependent death are abolished when cPLA2α function is genetically removed or when target engagement is otherwise verified in the sensitive leukemia lines. Chemical-probe best practice emphasizes reducing common off-target explanations via orthogonal probes and appropriate controls; similarity of IC50 patterns alone does not rule out shared liabilities (e.g., redox effects) that could converge on stress pathways. This matters particularly because the paper proposes a “hitherto unexplored function” of cPLA2α in epigenetic adaptation, which requires unusually strong linkage of phenotype to target. The quote highlights that without such orthogonal corroboration, mechanistic inference from small-molecule phenotypes can be misleading. Therefore, the paper’s mechanistic interpretation falls short of common field standards for target-to-phenotype causal attribution.

This paper overstates the selectivity of the thiazolyl ketones (including the lead GK420/AVX420) for cPLA2α based on limited counter-screening and on inhibition percentages that show substantial activity on other PLA2 enzymes, undermining the validity of the “selective inhibitor” premise used throughout the biological interpretation. "All the new analogs mentioned above can be considered selective inhibitors of cPLA2α, because all present minimal inhibition of GVIA iPLA2 and GV sPLA2 at high concentrations (0.091 mole fraction), as shown in Table 1." This sentence makes a strong interpretive claim (“selective inhibitors”) and anchors it to Table 1 values at a single, high mole fraction. However, “minimal inhibition” is not consistent with several of the listed percentages, including substantial inhibition of GV sPLA2 for the lead-like compounds. Because the paper uses selectivity to justify that downstream cellular phenotypes reflect cPLA2α inhibition (rather than PLA2 cross-reactivity), any overstatement here threatens internal validity. Moreover, selectivity over only two other PLA2s does not address broader off-target risks typical for small molecules, but even within this narrow panel the data show non-trivial inhibition. When the premise is “selective,” readers will reasonably infer that cross-family inhibition is negligible across the tested panel, which is not supported by the presented numbers. This mismatch is significant because selectivity is repeatedly used to justify mechanistic conclusions and therapeutic relevance. GK420 99
0.0016 ± 0.0002 0 46 For GK420, Table 1 reports 46% inhibition of GV sPLA2 at the stated high concentration (0.091 mole fraction). A near-half inhibition at a single dose is difficult to reconcile with the phrase “minimal inhibition,” and it suggests that GK420 is not cleanly selective within the reported PLA2 panel. Even if the authors consider 46% “acceptable,” that threshold should be justified, because partial inhibition of another PLA2 could plausibly influence lipid signaling and downstream stress phenotypes. The issue is amplified because the biological conclusions (ROS induction, transcriptional programs, and cell death) are sensitive to perturbations in lipid metabolism and inflammatory mediators. Without a clearer selectivity window quantified across concentrations (e.g., IC50s for iPLA2 and sPLA2), the claim of selectivity remains under-supported. This weakens confidence that AVX420’s cancer-cell phenotypes are attributable specifically to cPLA2α. "Again, α-methyl substituted analogs can be considered selective inhibitors of cPLA2α, because all present minimal inhibition of GVIA iPLA2 and GV sPLA2 at high concentrations (0.091 mole fraction) (Table 1)." The “again” indicates the authors are extending the same selectivity interpretation to another subset of compounds, reinforcing that selectivity is central to the narrative. However, the α-methyl analogs include compounds with majority inhibition of iPLA2 and/or sPLA2 at the same dose, which contradicts the stated rationale. This is not a cosmetic inconsistency; it suggests a systematic overinterpretation of the counter-screening results. Because these compounds are then used as comparators (e.g., to argue shared cPLA2α-specific sensitivity signals in the CCLS), inflated selectivity claims can propagate into downstream association analyses. In chemical biology, such interpretive inflation is a known source of false mechanistic attribution when compounds have secondary activities. The paper would need either stronger selectivity evidence or more cautious language and additional controls. GK439 97
0.0029 ± 0.0004 67 53 GK439 shows 67% inhibition of GVIA iPLA2 and 53% inhibition of GV sPLA2 at the single reported high dose, which is clearly not “minimal.” Even if GK439 is not the lead, the paper’s argument for target-specific commonalities across multiple inhibitors depends on these analogs being functionally selective for cPLA2α. When comparator compounds substantially inhibit other PLA2s, shared sensitivity patterns could reflect shared polypharmacology rather than shared cPLA2α engagement. This is a direct threat to the validity of the CCLS-derived pathway associations claimed to represent “specific to cPLA2α inhibition.” At minimum, these data require dose–response selectivity metrics for iPLA2 and sPLA2 (not just percent inhibition at one condition) before labeling the set “selective.” Without this, the biochemical foundation for downstream claims is weaker than standard expectations. "Only high-quality chemical probes generate meaningful biological data." ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4706458/)) The paper positions AVX420 as a selective tool to uncover a new cPLA2α function, which is exactly the type of situation where chemical-probe quality is decisive. If selectivity is uncertain even within the narrow enzyme family panel, then mechanistic interpretations from phenotypic readouts (ROS, transcriptional changes, death) become ambiguous. The quoted best-practice statement is not about demanding perfection, but about aligning strength of biological claims with strength of probe characterization. Here, the manuscript draws strong mechanistic implications (epigenetic adaptation to ROS) but provides selectivity evidence that is inconsistent with its own “minimal inhibition” wording and lacks broader profiling. This mismatch increases the risk that off-target activities contribute to the observed phenotypes. Consequently, the conclusions about cPLA2α-specific vulnerabilities in acute leukemias are less reliable.

This paper bases key genomic and pathway conclusions on a 66-cell-line drug sensitivity screen, but essential experimental/procedural details and analysis specifics are deferred to supplementary materials, limiting reproducibility and making the robustness of the inferred “molecular vulnerabilities” difficult to assess.
